Author ’s Note
As a product designer with over a decade of experience, I understand the
challenges of creating user-frien dly software that not only captivates users
but also drives business success. It is a journey that can be both exhilarating
and overwhelming.
This book is crafted with a singular purpose: to empower product owners
like you. By the time you ﬁnish reading, you will be equipped to
conﬁdently navigate conversations with design teams, identi fy potential
issues in your product, and understand how each team member contributes
to a well-designed product that makes a real UX impact.
Instead of dry theory , each chapter draws from real-world experiences -
mistakes I have made or learned about during my 10+ years designing for
clients. This practical, learning-by-doing approach will help you recognize
and avoid common pitfalls in SaaS product design.
We will dive deep into the intricacies of SaaS design - from user
onboarding and data security to everything in between. Usin g relatable
examples and accessible langu age, I will guide you through complex
concepts, making them resonate with your own experiences.
Imagine you are building an app for planning dream vacations. We will
explore how to hook users from the start with effective onboarding, and
then delve into crucial elements like navigation, visual design, and branding
that keep users coming back. We will tackle the pain points head-on,
transforming your product into a joy to use.
But we will not stop there. We will cover critical aspects like performance,
personalization, accessibility , data security , analytics, and ROI. We will
highlight comm on pitfalls in these areas because every user deserves a
tailored, secure, and efﬁcient experience.
Throughout the book, you will ﬁnd real-life examples, hands- on tips, and
self-evaluation questionnaires. These tools will help you assess your
product against industry best practices and proactively identi fy areas for
improvement.
My goal is to empower you with the knowledge and tools to create
exceptional SaaS products - products that do not just satisfy users, but
delight them; products that drive business growth. And I aim to do this in a
way that is accessible, even if you are not technically inclined.
So, grab your favorite note-takin g tool and prepare for a journey of learning
through real-world tribulations. This book will transform your perspective
on product design and equip you to create software that people truly love.
Let us demystify the art of UX design for SaaS together . Are you ready?
Happy reading and designing!
Tulasi Krishna
About Author
I am Tulasi Krishna Penumarthy , a Product Designer with over a decade of
experience in the tech industry . My journey began as a Software Engineer at
Syntel Inc in 2011, where I gained valuable insights into corporate
dynamics and team collaboration.
My entrepreneurial spirit led me to co-found two startups. My ﬁrst venture
was a software development and marketing services company co-founded
with my college buddies, followed by ApnaLoanBazaar , where I
contributed to product design and development. These experiences, coupled
with my work assisting other startups, allowed me to discover my true
passion and talent for UI/UX design.
In 2015,  I founded DevForce Soft Labs Pvt Ltd, initially to support my self-
coded product, Skillholic. The company soon pivoted to become a full-scale
UI/UX design agency , which I have led for over eight years. DevForce has
served clients across India, USA, UK, Australia, Qatar , and Germany ,
specializing in industries such as SaaS, Finance, Healthcare, EdTech, AI,
Automotive, and EV .
My diverse background spans software development  & QA,
entrepreneurship, and design leadership, giving me a unique perspective on
product design and SaaS. This book, my ﬁrst, is born from my passion for
sharing knowled ge and empowering others in the dynamic ﬁeld of SaaS
product design. My goal is to provide SaaS product owners and designers
with proven methods, saving them from reinventing the wheel.
For those seeking to dive deepe r into the Art Of UX, I invite you to visit
artofux.com. You can also connect with me on Twitter/X @DesignW ithTK
or ﬁnd me on LinkedIn. I am always open to discussions and collaborations,
so feel free to reach out at tulasi@artofux.com .
Introduction
There is so much that goes into a truly exceptional SaaS-product than just
checking off a list of features. Of course, functionality is crucial for sure,
but it is the UX in all its wholesomeness that will make or break your
product. After all, one does not buy a new car because it fulﬁlls a bare
minimum of requirements - not even hardly - but for its seamle ss, intuitive
experience and pure joy of ownership. Hopefully , users will not want "it
works" - they want "it feels good" unless your product is in huge demand.
This is the ultim ate guide to make sure that your SaaS product is the one-of-
a-kind experience that every user begs to experience repeatedly . I will help
you highlight the weak area, give you an insight and give you a strategy to
help you bring it right back on course or stay right on course with an
extremely satisﬁed user base.
This book is helpful for anyone working in Product or UI/UX Design, and
speciﬁcally for you as a SaaS product owner . Here is what you get by
reading this book.
A 360-Degr ee Perspective : This book will help you take the much-needed
step back and look at your SaaS product from its user's 360-degree
perspective. We will discuss the 11 most important areas that cumulatively
contribute to its user experie nce, right from creating the best ﬁrst
impression throu gh effective onboarding to creating a great visual design
and performance.
Self-Assessment Tools: Every chapter is accompanied by a comprehensive
checklist, enabling you to objectively review your product's strengths and
weaknesses. It is like taking lessons from your personal trainer for
improving your product offering: it zooms in on areas for improvement that
need special attention and consolidates what you are already doing well.
Real-W orld Insights:  As one reads through the book, there are plentiful
examples of real-life scenarios of excellent and poor implementation of the
various principles of design and user experience strategies. The relatable
cases act as a guide on what to do and what not to do.
Actionable Recommendations:  Every section is ﬁlled with actionable
ideas and strategies to bring your product's user experience into truly
exceptional territory. It is like a personal guidebook for tackling  and solving
any issues or weaknesses holding your product back.
Return on Investment Analy sis: We will discu ss how to count and
whether it is worth investing in some improvements, and how to depend on
sound business principles every decision concerning investment on design
by having a clear explanation of returns as the consequence of that.
Continuous Improvement:  This book is intended to be a living, breathing
resource that one should revisit time and again. Go through these checklists
from time to time to re-assess your product- update your knowledge and
learnings on a constant basis.
User -Centric Focus: Only one thing - a user-centric focus - tops this list.
The product should evolve based on what they need, what they like or
dislike, or what they want-to do, rather than just on what you want to do for
them.
Collaborative Approach:  Involve your cross-functional team members
while assessing your product using this book. Try to bring fresh views from
different members which could give out genius results.
I hope you ﬁnd this guide helpful: your secret weapon to developing a
second to none user experience; driving growth in business and therefore
keeping you ahead of the competition in an ever-changing mark et - time to
take your SaaS product to the next level and make a mark in your industry .
Chapter  1: User  Onboarding
There is only one boss. The customer . And he can ﬁr e everybody in the
company fr om the chairman on down, simply by spending his money
somewher e else. - Sam W alton
Imagine a guest coming over to your house for the ﬁrst time; you give them
a little bit of a tour, show them where everything is, make them feel
comfortable. Precisely the same thing applies to the onboarding for your
SaaS product - it is setting the tone and making that great ﬁrst impression.
A smooth onboarding experie nce means that the user feels welcome,
knows how they will gain value from the product, and understands
exactly how to use it.
Think of onboarding as if it were a job interview . Users are interviewing
your product to see if it is what they need, and the quality of the onboarding
displays to them the service they can expect overall. When onboarding is
clear , helpful, and engaging, users believe there really must be value and
reliability in what you offer. An initial phase to shine and show them that
they have made the right choice.
From my experience, I see that users are getting lazier and convenience-
seeking. They skip as many steps as they could to get right into the product.
At the same time, quite often, product owners make a mistake either by not
knowing what to include or demanding too much information from the very
beginning. I have had clients  who want to make every ﬁeld on the
onboarding form required to get maximum information. Sometimes, it takes
actual examples of excellent and disastrous onboardings to change their
minds.
Now I have broken this section up, for reading and clarity sake, into two
areas: common problems users create while onboarding; and, common
mistakes product owners make.   
Problems that users would cr eate during onboarding
Let us take a detailed look at common problems users innoc ently cause
during onboarding, the issues these problems cause, and how to ﬁx them.
1.       Skipping Tutorials or Guides
2.       Ignoring Product Documentation
3.       Not Customizing Settings
4.       Ignoring Notiﬁcations or Emails
5.       Not Completing Proﬁle Setup
6.       Not Providing Feedback or Reporting Issues
7.       Not Exploring Integrations or Add-ons
Let us dive in.
Skipping Tutorials or  Guides
Whenever new users join a product, the most frequent mistak e that they
make is skipping the tutorials or guides. Why does this happen?
Imagine you just signed up for some new app. you are excited to get
involved and start using it at that very moment. Chances are, as soon as a
tutorial pops up, you think "I do not need this. I will ﬁgure it out as I go."
So, you skip it.
Now why is this a problem for us?
Skipping tutorials might seem pretty harmless. Or at least that is the case
until a few signiﬁcant issues arise.
Ignorance and Over conﬁdence : It is easy to assume that you can work it
out yourself. You may be unaware of the complexity of the product. This
overconﬁdent attitude can easily make us blind to the value of the guidance
given. I have done this so many times myself, sooner or later ending up
desperately searching for help when dumbfounded.
Missing Key Featur es: The tutorial is there to introduce you to the most
important features of the app. Thus, by skipping it, you might just miss out
on some tools that are going to make the experience way better for you.
Contrarian View: If your product's UX is extremely smooth and intuitive,
you might argue that a tutorial is not necessary .
However , imagine jumping right into this without any guidance:
Underutilization:  If you skip onbo arding being unaware of the core feature
of the app, you may end up underutilizing it. The product might actually
have features that would save your time or get your goals in a more
effective way .
Consider Microsoft Excel, for example-almost everyone has used it at some
point. Everyone  is familiar with the most obvious features, like creating
tables and entering data and even performing basic formul as. A small
section of users is aware that Excel could also be used for advanced features
such as pivot tables and VLOOKUP  and even connecting to an API, which
can signiﬁcantly enhance productivity and insight.
Frustration : Withou t understanding how to use all the features, users might
get frustrated trying to ﬁgure things out on their own. That may deliver poor
user experience. Ultimately , it may kill your product.
Users may not remember something they have seen in onboarding when
they do not know when they need it
This frustration can escalate quickly , especially if you are under time
pressure to get something done. Take an example of a photo editing
software that is really dense on its own and you are trying to ﬁnd the Zoom
tool. You start clicking random ly and begin to get ﬂustered because you
skipped tutorials and the software is clearly too complex, eventually you
may abandon the software for something simple.
How do Duolingo do onboarding?
Duolingo is a very popular language learning application with several
gamiﬁcation techniques implemented to engage the user and make the
learning process as painless as it is addictive.
This app keeps track of your development in a skill tree, with every node
opening up with a new lesson or idea. With new nodes unlocked; after
ﬁnishing lessons , a sense of progression and achievement comes in. Users
earn XP on completion of lessons as well as practicing the skills. There is a
leveling up as users accumu late XP, giving a sense of growth and
accomplishment. This is linked to catering for our desire for mastery and
progression.
The system encourages users to practice daily like a streak. Users develop
their streak by practicing every day uninterruptedly . Then, in line with
certain levels of practice, the app celebrates milestone streaks - a 7-day
streak, a 30-day streak, etc. by rewards and badges. This gives people a
sense of commitment and reinfo rces a sense of habit. The user also can earn
virtual rewards like lingots and badges for doing certain practices or for
reaching some milestones. Rewards give a feeling of accomplishment and
serve as good positive motivation.
But the very lessons themselves are made gamiﬁed and interactive. The
words are matched, sentences are translated, mini-games are played,
making the process of learning less of a hard memorization and more
engaging. Thus,  the app breaks the lessons down into very, very bitesize
chunks, enablin g users to squee ze the process of learning into their daily
routine and feel progress much more often.


Duoligo onboar ding scr eens
Use below self-assessment to see where your product stands.
Self-Assessment
★      Do I understand why users might skip the tutorial or guide?
★      Have I considered the excitement and eagerness of new users to jump
straight into the product?
★      Are users aware of the complexity of the product, or might they
underestimate it?
★      How might skipping the tutorial impact users' understanding of key
features?
★      Does my tutorial clearly introduce the most important features of the
app?
★      Have I highlighted tools and features that signiﬁcantly enhance the
user experience?
★      Is the product's UX so intuitive that it minimizes the need for a
tutorial?
★      Have I considered providing contextual help signals within the
product?
★      Does the onboarding process explain the core features ef fectively?
★      Could users be missing out on features that save time or help achieve
their goals more efﬁciently?
★      Have I considered how advanced users might miss complex features?
★      Is there a way to introduce advanced features gradually?
★      How do I ensure users understand all features to prevent frustration?
★      Do users remember the information from onboarding when they need
it?
★      Are there ways to reinforce key information later in the user journey?
★      Have I implemented any gamiﬁcation techniques to make onboarding
engaging?
★      Do I track user progress and provide a sense of achievement and
progression?
★      Do I encourage daily use or practice through rewards or streaks?
★      Are there milestone celebrations to reinforce commitment and habit
formation?
★      Are lessons or tutorials broken down into manageable chunks?
★      Can users easily ﬁt learning or onboarding into their daily routine?
★      Do I gather user feedback on the onboarding process?
★      How often do I iterate on and improve the tutorial based on user
feedback?
Ignoring Pr oduct Documentation
You have just launched a new app, eager to get started. You run into a snag
but you think "I do not have time to read all that documentation. I will
ﬁgure it out myself. " So, you bypass the help resources.
This happened to me once. I got a loan from a Fintech compa ny, but later
realized I was charged high fees for late payments that I was not aware of.
Yes, I did not take time to read or was not keen on terms of service when it
popped up for the ﬁrst-time during signup.
Poor documentation impacts your ROI and Support Costs
When the users cannot ﬁnd the information, they seek themselves, they
contact your support staff to get assistance. This puts more workload on
your support staff and increases the operational costs also. That is, poor or
inadequate documentation means you may end up having to spend more on
customer support to resolve the issues, which could have been resolved
easily and quickly with proper and well-directed documentation.
JIRA ’s QuickStart Guide
JIRA, the popular issue tracking, and project management tool by Atlassian,
offers a "QuickS tart" guide to help new users get up and running with the
platform quickly and efﬁciently . It is more of an out-of-box  feature for
QuickStart, which automatically pops up when a new user logs in to JIRA
for the very ﬁrst time. It is actually a guided tour towards what is important
and thus makes the onboarding process much easier .
It leads the user along a series of steps. Each of these steps corresponds to
one feature of JIRA  or another . All these steps are sensibly arranged in a
line, underlined by basic notions, including project creation, issue creation,
and inviting team members. Instead of pure text-based instructions, there
are interactive elements in the QuickStart guide. The user can click parts of
the JIRA interface, and such elements will be highlighted by this guide and
described in terms of their functionality .
The guide provides contextual assistance. It will automatically jump to the
next action and provide instruct ions on what to do to a user who clicked on
the "Create Issue" button and ﬁll in the issue details.
This is how the role-based QuickStart will illuminate most of the relevant
features and workﬂows for a project manager; similarly , it will be useful for
developers or administrators. This facility tracks the progress of the users
and will allow them to continue the place where they left in case of any
pause experiencing their QuickStart interaction with the application. They
can skip parts from the guide once they have used those features. They can
go back if they need a review about something.
Within QuickStart guide itself, additional links to documentation, tutorial
videos and other support resources are provided by JIRA  for those who may
want to deep dive into speciﬁc topics.
Self-Assessment
★      Do I understand why users might ignore the product documentation?
★      Have I considered how skipping documentation can lead to
misunderstandings or issues with the product?
★      How might poor documentation impact the user experience and
satisfaction?
★      Have I assessed the potential increase in support costs due to
inadequate documentation?
★      Is the documentation easily accessible and visible to users?
★      Does the documentation clearly explain important terms and
conditions that users need to know?
★      Have I considered using interactive elements in the documentation to
engage users better?
★      Does the onboarding process include a guided tour of the product’ s
key features?
★      Are the steps in the guide logically arranged and easy to follow?
★      Does the guide provide contextual assistance based on user actions?
★      Is the documentation role-based, of fering speciﬁc guidance for
different types of users (e.g., project managers, developers,
administrators)?
★      Does the guide track user progress and allow them to pick up where
they left of f?
★      Can users skip parts of the guide once they are familiar with certain
features?
★      Are there links to additional documentation, tutorial videos, and other
support resources within the guide?
★      How often do I review and update the documentation to ensure it
remains current and useful?
★      Do I gather user feedback on the documentation and incorporate their
suggestions?
★      Are there mechanisms in place to measure the ef fectiveness of the
documentation in reducing support requests?
★      Do users feel more conﬁdent and self-sufﬁcient after using the
documentation?
★      Is there a balance between providing enough information and
overwhelming users with too much detail?
Not Customizing Settings
Another frequen t mistake that most users may commit as soon as they start
using a new SaaS product is Not Customizing the setup to their liking or
business requirement. Why?
This might happen due to time constraints, overwhelmed with choices,
making an assumption of default suitability , fear of breaking something or
simply due to lack of awareness.
Missed Optimization Chances
You are not making the product optimal to your workﬂow by keeping all the
settings exactly as is, for your utmost best interests.
It is like using Tinder without a display picture; you are not representing
yourself to full potential, nor do others see it in you.
Inefﬁciencies
You may use the product in its most generalized way without
customization.
For exam ple, if you are using a calendar application and you have not set
your time zone, then that may cause missing appointments and scheduled
meetings. Similarly , if the default setting of notiﬁcations results in too much
frequency , then unnecessary alerts may become a distraction.
Ignor ed Featur es
There are some useful featur es, which become available through the
settings.
Multiple times, I have signed up for new products and ended up getting
spammed by marketing emails.  This happened because I ignored the pre-
selected email option during the signup and had to go into the settings to
update my preferences manually .
Also, sometimes  I manually opt in for new alerts because I missed out on
some important update or notiﬁcation. Must have happened to you too.
PRO TIPS:
1. Provide an interactive walkthrough that guides users through key
settings
2. Offer default settings that are optimized for most users, but
always emphasize the option to customize.
3. Ensure that the settings menu is easily accessible
4. Regularly provide tips and tricks through newsletters or in-app
messages on how to use dif ferent settings ef fectively .
The Gmail Inbox
As Gma il is such a powerful email service, there are numerous ways to
customize settings for the betterment of your inbox and your productivity .
Defaults, no doubt, work ﬁne by themselves, but a user who takes the pain
to personalize some of the Gma il features can unlock much more organized
and efﬁcient ways of managing emails.
Filters are the major customization tools of Gmail. A ﬁlter allows the setting
of rules  for sorting, labeling, archiving, or doing a speciﬁc action in an
automatic manner while receiving emails from a predetermined criterion
like sender , subject, or keywords.
Labels are another extremely powerful feature. Rather than the standard
folder approach, Google's Gmail labeled the e-mails in such a way that they
can be associated together by context or project and found like this as
opposed to being found in folders.
The Priority Inbox automatically separates relevant emails from irrelevant
emails so that the user can focus and view his or her most important emails
only. Multiple Inboxes refer to when different categories of emails, unread,
starred, or any particular label, are displayed in their separate sections for a
better view of the various types of emails at one glance.
This is achievable through the combination of ﬁlters, labels, and custom
inbox types which can all work together in creating an extremely
personalized email experience matching the workﬂow and priorities of
users. While Gmail's default setup alone serves most purposes, spending a
little time and effort to explore and organize these extremely useful
customization options can make all the dif ference.
Self-Assessment
★      Do I understand why users might not customize the settings of the
product?
★      Have I considered time constraints that might prevent users from
customizing their setup?
★      Are users potentially overwhelmed by too many customization
choices?
★      Do users assume that default settings will be suitable for their needs?
★      Is there a fear among users that changing settings might break
something?
★      Are users aware of the customization options available to them?
★      How might not customizing settings impact the user ’s workﬂow and
productivity?
★      Have I highlighted the beneﬁts of customizing settings to users?
★      Are there inefﬁciencies users face by using default settings instead of
customizing them?
★      How can incorrect default settings (like time zones or notiﬁcations)
negatively af fect the user experience?
★      Are there useful features available through settings that users might
miss if they do not customize?
★      Do users understand how to change settings to avoid issues like
unwanted marketing emails or missed notiﬁcations?
★      Have I provided an interactive walkthrough to guide users through
key settings?
★      Are the default settings optimized for most users while still
emphasizing the option to customize?
★      Is the settings menu easily accessible to users?
★      Do I regularly provide tips and tricks through newsletters or in-app
messages on how to use dif ferent settings ef fectively?
★      How often do I review and update the settings options to ensure they
remain relevant and useful?
★      Do I gather user feedback on the settings and incorporate their
suggestions?
★      Are there mechanisms in place to measure how customization
improves user satisfaction and productivity?
★      Do users feel more conﬁdent and efﬁcient after customizing their
settings?
Ignoring Notiﬁcations or  Emails
Today we ﬁnd ourselves under continuous attack by notiﬁcations and emails
from all sorts of apps, services, and devices chosen to notify and inform us.
Though well intended to keep us informed and up to date, the barrage of
such alerts and emails the avera ge person receives in a typical day sums up
to what has come to be known as "notiﬁcation fatigue" or "alert
desensitization. "
It is common for users to ignore such notiﬁcations or alerts, thinking
perhaps that they are not import ant or that it is just another distraction. This
is because, for most people, they get several notiﬁcations per day that can
range anywhere  from social media updates to promotional offers, and so
desensitization becomes very easy. This may cause the user to miss
important inform ation or key updates that are meant to give them the best
experience possible or even rectify pending issues.
One important effect of ignoring notiﬁcations is that new features,
important updates, or special offers that may greatly enhance the user's
experience with that product or service are not taken advantage of.
Companies very often release updates of new functionality , ﬁxing a bug, or
improvements, and in case a user constantly ignores these notiﬁcations,
he/she may never beneﬁt from any of these features.
Security is also one other area where missing an alert can be highly
critical.
Most services and platforms alert users over suspected activities or actions
required to secure their account. In missing such alerts, it risks unsuspected
breaches of the account, unauthorized access, or security issues that would
otherwise have been avoided by taking appropriate action propelled by the
alert.
Also, missing notiﬁcations means missing the opportunity or missing the
deadline. They might miss the notiﬁcation of events which are time
bounded, specia lly offered, or any reminders of tasks or appoin tments and
miss their chance of carrying them out or be late.
PROTIPS:
1. Instead of sendin g lengthy notiﬁcations, be concise and clear . For
instance, "Your password was changed successfully" is clear and
concise.
2. Customize the notiﬁcations to keep those relevant like sending a
reminder "We have added new features since your last visit!" if
the user has not logged in for a while
3. Provide clear actions that users can take directly from the
notiﬁcation. For instance, "Update your proﬁle now to improve
security" with a direct link to the proﬁle update page.
4. Let users choose which notiﬁc ations they want to receive and
how often. Nobody like to be bombarded with alerts
5. Occasionally send an alert to review and update their notiﬁcatio n
preferences
6. Allow users to manage their notiﬁcations more effectively , such
as adjusting notiﬁcation settings to ﬁlter out less important alerts
7. Use catchy email subject lines that spark curiosity or offer a clear
beneﬁt. Instead of “Welcome to Our Service,” try “Unlock the
Secrets to Getting the Most Out of [Product]!”
8. Ensure each email contains useful tips, resources, or quick guides
that help users solve problem s or learn something new. For
instance, include  a link to a short video tutorial on how to use a
key feature of your product.
9. If a user has started using a particular feature, send them tips on
how to get even more out of it. “We noticed you started using
[Feature]. Here are some pro tips to master it!”
10. Relate the content of your emails to the speciﬁc actions users
have taken within the app. If a user has just completed their ﬁrst
project, send an email saying “Great job on ﬁnishing your ﬁrst
project! Here are some advanced tips to take it to the next level.”
11. People are busy and do not have time for long emails. Keep your
messages concise and to the point.
12. Guide users to take the next step. “Click here to watch our 2-
minute video on setting up your proﬁle” or “Start your ﬁrst
project now with these easy steps.”
13. Incorporate images, GIFs, or short videos if possible. A quick GIF
showing a cool feature in actio n can be more effective than a
lengthy explanation.
14. do not send all onboarding emai ls at once. Space them out to give
users time to digest the information.
15. Include interactive elements like buttons or quick surveys. “Tell
us what you are most excited to use!
Dropbox Emails
Dropbox developed a sequence of onboarding emails that smoothly guide
the user through  the basics. Then, following up on how to upload and share
ﬁles, the tool sends further tips on the use of more advanced features. Each
email is short, visual, and about one topic so that the user may easily follow
along and learn at his/her pace. It also references some of the user's recent
acts within the app. For example, when a user uploads their ﬁrst ﬁle, an
automatically triggered email to them can be congratulato ry in tone,
suggesting tips related to the organization of uploaded ﬁles and folders for
increased productivity .
Example Periodic Email Series About New Featur es
It is extremely effective to keep sending a series of emails to the users from
time to time regarding new features that keeps them engaged and informed
but not burdened with information all at once. Following is a sample on
how you can do it:
Initial Introduction Email:  "Welcome to [Product Name]! Here is What You
Need to Know" – This email introduces users to the basic features of the
product and encourages them to explore further .
Weekly Feature Highlights:  "Discover Our Top Features: Week 1" – Focus
on one or two key features each week, explaining their beneﬁts and
providing tips on how to use them ef fectively .
User Tips and Best Practices:  "Boost Your Productivity with These Tips" –
Share best practices on how to use the product more efﬁciently , tailored to
different user needs or industries.
Success Stories  and Case Studies:  "See How [Product Name] Helped
[Company Name] Succeed" – Showcase real-world examples of how other
users or companies have beneﬁted from using your product.
Interactive Tutorials and Webinars:  "Join Our Live Webinar to Learn More"
– Invite users to participate in live tutorials or webinars where they can ask
questions and see the product in action.
Feedback and Customization:  "Help Us Impro ve – Share Your Feedback" –
Encourage users to provide feedback on the features they have explored so
far and suggest what they’d like to see next.
Periodic Summary Emails:  "Here is What You Might Have Missed" – Send
monthly summary emails highl ighting the new features, updates, and any
major announcements from the past month.
Self-Assessment
★      Do I understand why users might ignore notiﬁcations or emails from
the product?
★      Have I considered how notiﬁcation fatigue and alert desensitization
might impact user engagement?

ignored notiﬁcations?
★      How might missed notiﬁcations af fect users' experience with new
features, important updates, or special of fers?
★      Have I assessed the potential security risks if users miss critical alerts
about account activities?
★      Could ignoring notiﬁcations lead to users missing time-sensitive
events, special of fers, or reminders?
★      Do I ensure notiﬁcations are concise and clear , providing only
essential information?
★      Are notiﬁcations customized to be relevant to the user , such as
reminders about new features?
★      Do notiﬁcations provide clear actions that users can take directly from
the alert?
★      Can users choose which notiﬁcations they want to receive and how
often?
★      Do I periodically remind users to review and update their notiﬁcation
preferences?
★      Do email subject lines spark curiosity or of fer a clear beneﬁt to
encourage opening?
★      Does each email contain useful tips, resources, or quick guides to help
users?
★      Are emails personalized based on speciﬁc actions users have taken
within the app?
★      Do I keep email messages concise and to the point to respect users'
time?
★      Do emails guide users to take the next step with clear calls to action?
★      Are there visual elements like images, GIFs, or short videos in emails
to enhance engagement?
★      Do I space out onboarding emails to give users time to digest the
information?
★      Are there interactive elements like buttons or quick surveys included
in emails to engage users?
★      How often do I send periodic emails about new features to keep users
informed without overwhelming them?
★      Do emails include success stories and case studies to demonstrate the
product’ s value?
★      Are users invited to participate in interactive tutorials or webinars for
deeper engagement?
★      Do I encourage users to provide feedback on features and suggest
improvements?
★      Are monthly summary emails sent highlighting new features, updates,
and major announcements?
Not Completing Pr oﬁle Setup
Whenever users  join some new platform or download an app, they are given
a chanc e to ﬁll in their proﬁle information. That could be simple
information such as their name, location of operation, interest, preference,
or any other relevant information. What users commonly do is bypass the
step or keep it pending because they feel that they can do it later, or it is not
necessary at all.
Although this is not a major inconvenience at this time, skipping this step
could have unfavorable consequences that signiﬁcantly diminish the value
of of ferings and personalization.
Moreover , an incomplete proﬁ le may also inﬂuence the functionality of
some of the features or integrations of the product.
For example, some platforms utilize location data for the localization of
services - which include event recommendations about the user's area,
recommending nearby business facilities, or activating location-based social
networking features. On the other hand, if there is no such information, a
user may not be of fered all these potentially valuable aspects of the product.
To overc ome this, most platforms and apps have initiated several ways that
aim at ensuring users ﬁll up their information during initial onboarding; it
may be through gamiﬁcation, use of rewards, or pointing out the beneﬁts
that come along with ﬁlling up one's proﬁle. Additionally , some platforms
may periodically remind users to update their proﬁle information, ensuring
that the personalization algorithm s have access to the most accurate and up-
to-date data.
The Quora Pr eferences
One good example is the quest ion-answer platform, Quora. While signing
up, Quora asks its users to pick up interests of theirs. If someone  misses that
part, they do not see relevant content on his feed once they log in but rather
sees random stuff that does not really fall in their area of interest and
expertise and hence turns out to be a bad experience because the platform is
not able to successfully mine the value gold out for that particular user .
This is more than just a content curation problem. An incomplete user
proﬁle means the platform does not have the much-needed information on
the user's preferences, behaviors, and characteristics. That information is
needed to make personalization algorithms as well as recommendation
engines work effectively . If it does not have such information, then it cannot
provide suggest ions, recomme ndations, and overall experien ces highly
speciﬁc to the user's needs and interests-that make the product not valuable
which may lead to a decrease of engagement or lost users.
Quora topics of inter est scr een
PROTIPS:
1. Instead of asking for all the information at once, ask for a little bit
at a time.
2. Divide proﬁle information into sections and ask for the most
critical details ﬁrst.
3. Clearly explain why completing their proﬁle is beneﬁcial, like
receiving personalized recommendations or quicker support.
4. Reward users for completing their proﬁles. Offer something like a
proﬁle completion badge, acces s to exclusive features, or a small
discount. Everyone loves a little extra motivation!
5. Use conditional  logic to show only relevant ﬁelds based on
previous answers. This reduces the number of unnecessary ﬁelds
users need to ﬁll out.
6. Send periodic email or in-app reminders to users who have not
completed their proﬁles.
7. Provide options to import data from other platforms (like
LinkedIn for professional proﬁles). This can signiﬁcantly speed
up the process
8. When a user completes their proﬁle, celebrate it with a
congratulatory message or animation. A small celebration can
make the user feel accomplished
Consider a ﬁtness application that asks you for your weight, height, and
workout goals. Unless you ﬁll this setup form, the application cannot offer
you customized  workout plans nor will it show the proper progress of
yourself. But if you invest some minutes in ﬁlling up your proﬁle, you get a
plan custom-made for you so that it is easier to reach your goals.
So, by breaking  the proﬁle setup, clearly reﬂecting the beneﬁts  of offering
something heterogeneous in the reward section, let them visualize/realize
the beneﬁts of proﬁle completion so that it leads to a much better
experience and personalized experience of the product.
Self-Assessment
★      Do I understand why users might skip or delay completing their
proﬁle setup?
★      Have I considered the impact of incomplete proﬁles on the user
experience and product functionality?
★      How might incomplete proﬁle information af fect the personalization
and relevance of the content and features of fered to users?
★      Are there location-based services or features that users might miss
out on due to incomplete proﬁle information?
★      Have I highlighted the beneﬁts of completing the proﬁle to users
during the onboarding process?
★      Are there incentives or rewards of fered to users for completing their
proﬁles?
★      Do I use gamiﬁcation techniques to encourage users to ﬁll out their
proﬁles?
★      Is the proﬁle setup process broken down into manageable sections to
avoid overwhelming users?
★      Do I ask for the most critical information ﬁrst, gradually collecting
additional details over time?
★      Are the reasons for providing proﬁle information clearly explained to
users?
★      Are periodic reminders sent to users to update or complete their
proﬁles?
★      Is there an option for users to import proﬁle data from other platforms
to speed up the process?
★      Do I celebrate proﬁle completion with a congratulatory message or
animation to make users feel accomplished?
★      Are there conditional logic features in place to show only relevant
ﬁelds based on users' previous answers?
★      How often do I review and update the proﬁle setup process to ensure
it remains user -friendly and relevant?
★      Do I gather user feedback on the proﬁle setup process and incorporate
their suggestions for improvement?
★      Are there mechanisms in place to measure the impact of completed
proﬁles on user engagement and satisfaction?
★      Do users feel more personalized and valuable experiences with the
product after completing their proﬁles?
★      Are there clear examples provided to users on how proﬁle completion
beneﬁts their experience, such as personalized recommendations or
quicker support?
★      Have I considered sending periodic email or in-app reminders to
users who have not completed their proﬁles?
Not Pr oviding Feedback or  Reporting Issues
When users access an application or software for the very ﬁrst time, they
usually face problems or have ideas for improvements but often say
nothing. This is one massive missed opportunity for any company behind
such an app.
Think about it: if you went to a restaurant and your food was too salty or
really slow service, you would probably tell them, would not you? So that
they know there  is something wrong and they can do somethin g to correct
it. Same idea with apps and software.
A lot of times, if users are not reporting or providing feedback, that is
similar to visitin g that restaurant, having a bad time, and walking out the
door without saying a thing. The restaurant never knows there was an issue,
so they cannot reﬁne anything.".
Feedback is critical for people building apps.
Without knowing what is confusing, frustrating, or ﬂat-out broken during
the ﬁrst-time setup process, the makers of the app may just keep chugging
along thinking everything is working perfectly . Meanwhile, more and more
new users could be getting stuck and giving up on the app entirely .
There are several reasons people often do not speak up:
●         They believe their feedback may not really matter or make a
difference.
●         The process for reporting an issue sounds too troublesome.
●         They simply for get about reporting it upon moving on.
No one wants to go through some long, complicated process just to leave a
suggestion. A feedback, however short, is invaluable to the people working
hard to improve  the app every day. A few sentences can help them to iron
out a confusing step or remove something that continually trips users up.
The easier it is to report issues or give feedback throughout the setup
process, the more insight companies will have, which leads to making
things smoother for new users. This leads to more people sticking with the
app and getting value from it, rather than giving up in frustration.
PROTIPS:
●         Integrate a feedback button directly within the app interface that users
can access at any time. “Encountered an issue? Click here to let us
know .”
●         Include brief, contextual surveys within the app experience that are
triggered based on speciﬁc user actions or milestones.
●         Make use of in-app prompts, email surveys, or even a dedicated
feedback section on your website. “W e value your input! Share your
thoughts via our in-app feedback form or email us directly .”
●         Allow users to react with emojis or simple sentiments (e.g., thumbs
up, thumbs down, or a rating) at various points within the app
experience. This can provide quick, lightweight feedback without
requiring extensive input from the user .
●         Invite a subset of users to participate in moderated usability testing
sessions, where they can provide real-time feedback as they navigate
the app.
●         Let users know that their feedback is being heard and acted upon. Tell
them when their feedback is being implemented.
●         Keep an eye on comments, mentions, and discussions about your app
on social media platforms to identify common issues or requests.
●         Instead of static tooltips, create interactive tooltips that allow users to
provide feedback or report issues directly from that context.
●         Build an online community forum or slack channel where users can
engage with each other , share their experiences, and provide feedback
to the company .
●         Ask a group of users to maintain a diary or journal, documenting
their experiences, pain points, and suggestions over a period of time.
●         Set up a dedicated phone number or voice messaging system where
users can leave audio feedback or report issues verbally . This can be a
more natural way for some users to provide input, especially for those
who prefer not to type.
●         Encourage users to submit screenshots or screen recordings when
reporting issues or providing feedback. This visual context can be
invaluable for the product team to understand the user's perspective
and experience.
●         Incorporate gamiﬁcation elements, such as badges or rewards, for
users who actively provide feedback or report issues.
●         Offer incentives or rewards (e.g., discounts, premium features, or
recognition) for users who provide particularly insightful or actionable
feedback.
●         Use natural language processing and machine learning to
automatically analyze user feedback from various channels (e.g.,
support tickets, social media, app reviews) and identify common
themes or areas for improvement.
Airbnb Feedback Email
The Airbnb feedback email does a great job of subtly encouraging users to
provide feedbac k in a thoughtful and user-friendly manner . The email starts
with a personalized greeting, addressing the recipient by name. This small
touch makes the message feel more human and inviting. They express
gratitude for the user choosing Airbnb, which sets a positive tone and makes
the user feel valued.
The email clearly states the purpose of requesting feedback  - to help
improve the Airbnb experience for the user and others. This motivates users
to provide feedback, as they can see the direct beneﬁt. By specifying that
the survey will only take 3 minutes, they remove the perceived barrier of a
lengthy or time-consuming process, making users more inclined to
participate.
The email reinforces the beneﬁt of providing feedback, stating that the
user's answers will help make Airbnb "even better" for them and other
guests. This appeal to self-interest and the broader community can
encourage partic ipation. The prominent "Take the Survey" button serves as
a clear and visua lly appealing call-to-action, making it easy for users to take
the desired action.
While the subject line "We'd Love Your Feedback! It will Only Take 3
Minutes" is clear and concise; it could be more enticing or intriguing to
increase open rates. Offering a small incentive, such as a discount or
reward, for completing the survey could further motivate users to provide
feedback.
Medium’ s Feedback r esponse with Incentive
I used to be a paid member of Medium.com for some time just to get the
taste of high-quality content. After a few months of usage, I had cancelled
my paid subscription because it seemed a lot of the conte nt was AI-
generated and not real stories, hence killing its value for me. They must
have heard this from many other users too. However , Medium directly
addressed this concern in their re-engagement email.
They explicitly pointed out that they were working on "cracking down on
AI-generated stories" and reafﬁrmed their commitment to "human
storytelling, not AI-generated writing." It goes on to prove that they actually
respect user feedback and do their best to enforce the same.
By highlighting how their
incentives have changed to "focus on high-quality human writing," and the
improvements that have been made, it became very clear that Medium had
ﬁxed the problem which had caused me to originally cancel. The offer of
20% off then gave me a compel ling reason to give their improv ed platform
another go.
All in all, such an approach by Medium—directly addressing exactly what
the issue was in my feedback, real improvements relevant to that feedback,
and incentivizing to rejoin—epitomized their customer -centric mindset for
me. I'm quite sure this type of personalization with respect to feedback-
driven strategy is what has gone to enhance the probability of re-
engagement for others who lapsed similarly over similar sentiments.
This pleasant email response by Medium is an excellent way to win back its
lapsed subscribers through a detailed address of the feedback it got,
highlighting the key recent improvements, and of fering an incentive to
rejoin.
Self-Assessment
