launches) with special promotions or events to strengthen the bond
with the user community?
Lack of Clear  Progress Indicators
We, as humans, take pride in the sense of fulﬁllment that we get from a
sense of progres s. Little triumphs can even make the least of us tick and
continue. This statement is not only valid for people but also for using
software. The researchers point out that when we can track our progress, we
are more motiva ted and committed. Such tracking can be a key factor in
marketing for some companies.  Imagine you are in a busy Ikea furniture
shop which can be a real challen ge. There are no staff around, and you have
to ﬁgure out how the parts should be assembled on your own. With no
directions, you would be clueles s about what to do next or even know if you
are halfway through. The syste m with software onboarding is the same.
Without being able to see their progress, users feel misdirected and doubtful
about how to proceed, which ultimately may cause them to feel frustration
and become disengaged.
Strava's Pr ogress Indicators
Strava, a leading app for track ing athletic activities such as running and
cycling, faced signiﬁcant challenges in user engagement and retention,
particularly among new users. Strava was founded in 2009 by Mark Gainey
and Michael Horvath, who envisioned a platform that combined social
networking with GPS tracking for athletes.
As of 2023, Strava reported annual revenues exceeding $200 million,
driven largely by its subscription model offering advanced features such as
personalized coaching and analysis tools. The platform has grown to serve
millions of active users globally , solidifying its position as a leader in the
ﬁtness and activity tracking market.
A key issue identiﬁed was the lack of clarity in how users could track their
progress and achievements within the app. Without clear indicators of their
athletic perform ance and goals, users often felt frustrated and disengaged
from using the platform effectively . To address these challenges and
enhance user experience, Strava implemented several strategic
improvements focused on enhancing progress indicators:
Strava introduce d intuitive visual indicators to help users monitor their
athletic perform ance over time. For instance, they redesigned the activity
feeds to prominently display recent activities and achievemen ts, such as
completed runs or cycling routes. This allowed users to easily visualize
their progress and compare their performance against previous activities.
Recognizing the importance of goal-oriented motivation, Strava enabled
users to set speciﬁc goals within the app. Whether it was achieving a
distance target or improving their pace, users could track their progress
towards these goals through clear , visual charts and notiﬁcations. This
feature not only provided users  with a sense of accomplishm ent but also
encouraged continued engagement with the app.
To have good outcomes from our product, we need to have visual indicators
that let our users  see their progress, such as progress bars, or checklists, etc.
Ensure that the guidelines are straightforward enough for the users to
subsequently ﬁnish the onboarding process. First, we can begin to develop
the visual progress indicators:
Progress Bars
Progress bars are a very practic al yet simple way to let customers see how
their product is performing. These are the types of progress bars that one
could choose:
Linear  Progress Bars:  An easy progress bar, which the user can see to
sections, is moving ahead, step by step. It is simple and clear to
communicate. Example: A horizontal bar at the top of the scree n that goes
from left to right as the user completes onboarding steps.
Step Progress Bars: The ﬂow of each process that is separated for the steps
and notes the steps that have been done, as well as the one that is being
done, is displayed. Example: A series of circles or checkmarks that
illuminate accordingly as the user moves through each step of the setup
process.
Circular  Progress Indicators:  It is a circular bar which progr esses in the
direction of the clock as time is passing, which is common in mobile
applications. Example: A circular meter around an item that ﬁlls in once a
task is accomplished.
Task Lists:  A list of tasks that contain a task or step written in a line. They
get to know what is done and what is left in a moment. Example: A sidebar
or a popup wind ow with a check list that users can complete by checking the
tasks they have done.
Percentage Completion: The precise percentage of the onboarding process
that has been ﬁnished is represented. Example: A percentage number beside
a linear progress bar or in a circular indicator .
Interactive Progress Maps:  An interactive progress list displaying the
user's route and the achieved goals. Example: A map or timeline that
illustrates the various stages of onboarding and each milestone has a mark
on the head.
Checklists
Checklists represent yet another  approach through which to steer new users
across the onboarding journey . They outline the speciﬁc steps that a user
has to take to get onboarded, which makes it easier for them to comprehend,
what they have to do and, at the same time, to monitor their own progress.
The onboarding process can be divided into easy-to-handle tasks thanks to
checklists. They  can see and plan for the next thing to be done by tracking
their completed tasks. Marking the tasks that have been done gives users a
feeling of accomplishment, thus motivation to continue. They are a step-by-
step guide that brings about less confusion and a smaller process.
This is a simple process that you should follow when you want to
implement checklists in your onboarding:
Task Breakdow n: Enumerate all the vital tasks that the new members must
accomplish. For example, tasks to be done to a project management tool are
setting up a proﬁ le, creating a project, adding team members and deadlines
setup.
Visible Placement: The checklist should be located in a visible and
reachable locatio n on the app. For example, a scrollbar or a pop-up window
with the option to be minimized but remains accessible.
Interactive Elem ents:  Develop the checklist that can be used interactively .
Users are going to check off tasks and see that they are done with them. For
instance, let users tick checkboxes situated next to the task they are
accomplishing, so they would be able to have a sense of accomplishment.
Detailed Descri ptions:  Give brief explanations or a tooltip for each task for
the users to be able to refer to and get help in case they need it. For
instance, a checklist item as “Create Your First Project” could have a tooltip
with a short tutorial or a link to a help article.
Progress Tracking:  Indicate how many tasks have  been completed as the
average progress . Example: A progress bar or a percentage bar at the top of
the list.
Celebrate Completion:  Congra tulate the users who ﬁnish all the tasks in
the checklist. Example: A congr atulatory message or a small reward like a
badge or discount.
Pretend you are looking at a ﬁtness app with a list of things to do, such as:
1. Setting ﬁtness goals
2. Adding personal details
3. Choosing a workout plan
4. Completing the ﬁrst workout
5. Connecting with a ﬁtness community
Each time an item is ticked off, positive achievement in the form of
progress is portrayed to the user. This aspect actively involve s them and
pushes them to go through the onboarding process.
Milestone Markers
It is normal for product owners to miss the kickstart of clien ts with the
onboarding process. If users do not recognize the milestones, they might
declare that their progress is not that good, which obviously is not a good
thing, there will be no motivation and they will feel aimless.
Add milestone markers, to highlight important achievements, and remind
your users of their incredible progress you need them to stay motivated in
their onboarding journey . The following ideas can be a good basis for a
company for the achievement of milestones through the interaction between
product and user .
How to insert milestone markers in your product:
These are the main steps to implement Milestone Markers in your product:
Identify Key Milestones: Specify the activities that users have to ﬁnish
during the onboarding process. For example, a project management tool's
key milestones are ﬁrst project, adding new team members, and setting up
some fundamental tasks.
Visual and Interactive Marke rs: To encourage users, take advantage of
the imag e cues like these characters, signs, or signs and banners for the
achievements that have a great impact on the student's academ ic progress.
When a user creates their ﬁrst, project setup, a badge or icon might appear
on their dashboard to recognize the achievement of the task.
Celebratory Messages: Display the congratulatory messages when you
team up with a new achievement. It is an in-app pop-up, and email)
"Broken record alert! No longer having a blank canvas, or users will be able
to explore with dif ferent settings and get to know more about the software!"
Progress Summaries:  Upon the completion of big milestones, besides the
Ha-Ha telling the user what they have accomplished a report would
summarize his progress too. "You have done the ﬁrst step! Here is what You
have done: Created a project, added team members, set up tasks. As your
next step: Advanced features will follow ."
Next Steps Guidance:  After a milestone, give the users the manifesto on
what they must do over the road in the onboarding journey . "Great job on
setting up your project! Next, let us dig the secrets of how you can manage
your task-scheduling better .
Gamiﬁcation Elements:  Present elements with the aim of acquiring points,
obtaining badges, and the like. "You have earned the 'Project Initiator'
badge for setting up your ﬁrst project. Keep going to unlock more
achievements!"
Feedback and Encouragemen t: Besides congrat ulating them, the user
should be provi ded with little shock and on-the-spot aid in case he falls
through. "you are doing great! Completing the project setup is a big step.
Keep up the good work!"
Social Sharing:  Enable users to share their achievements on platforms they
are using for fun or within the app's community . "Share your 'Project
Initiator' badge with your team and celebrate your progress together!"
Take an example of a cooking app where users start with the ﬁrst recipe
selection, then they create a shopping list, and ﬁnally , they go for a cooking
session. Each of these is the named milestone. When a user is done with his
ﬁrst cooking session, they get a popup that congratulates them, they get a
badge, and an email is sent to them that tells them about their progress and
what is next. This appreciation does not only increase their motives but it
also gives them a clear sense of fulﬁllment and direction, thus, they are
motivated to keep looking for new formulas and, consequently , they
upgrade the skill.
Clear  Instructions
If you want to avoid user confusion and make the onboarding process more
efﬁcient, it is very important to provide comfortable step-by-step
instructions, even though they would be slightly more prominent.
Try these out:
Tooltip:  It is a little box with a piece of information that appears over or
near an application's interface on the screen. Tooltips can also be a means to
quickly give a short explanation of some of the less visible features on the
icon.
For example, a user may not have an idea always of all the buttons
available. When a user points a mouse over a "Create Project" the pointer
could change appearance, the tooltip could be "Click here to start a new
project and invite your team members."
On-Scr een Prompts:  As soon as a new user is logged in the screen, a
gentle pop-up appears instructing the user to take the next step. They can be
either simple instructions or active hints that adjust as the user works
through the application.
Upon startup, a pop-up screen may appear to help the user ﬁll out their
proﬁle details under a visual hint like, “Begin your proﬁle setup from this
point.”
Short Instructi onal Video:  Videos can be utilized to display users how to
do a speciﬁc task in the most effective way and these videos can be very
helpful in return. They should just be brief and get to the point in order to
keep the user ’s focus.
A 60-second clip of the dashbo ard navigation, project creation , and team
member addition will assist users to comprehend the app shortly .
Just be Concise : The main thing  for good instructions is their simplicity .
do not be afraid of technical jargon and instead break down complex tasks
into smaller , easier steps.
“Customizing the user interface” can be instead of “Selecting the layout and
colors you like in order to create your own dashboard”.
Summary Scr eens
Create summary screens to deepen user understanding of their progress and
guide them on what is going  to follow . After clients accomplish an
important step in their onboarding, a summary screen can both promote
such a feeling of success and incentivize them to move to other areas. Here
is how to implement ef fective summary screens:
Recap Accomplishments:  Draw attention to the successfu l tasks they have
already done. This is an excellent way to display their achievements as well
as motivating them by providing pleasant experiences.
“Good! You have a perfect proﬁ le and you apply the introductory projects
perfectly .”
Preview Next Steps:  Let the users know the next moves they should make.
By doing this they will see the big picture and be prepared for future tasks.
“After that, you will be taught how to add team members and assign tasks
to keep your project on the right path of the schedule.”
Visual Elements:  Add green checkmarks, icons, or images to improve the
summary screen’ s visual appeal and make it more engaging.
Link checkmarks of completed tasks and icons for the upcoming steps
together with the page.
Motivating Messages:  Incorporate the usage of motivational talk to ensure
users are eager to keep going.
“Great job! Keep up the pace so that you can unleash the full might of our
app.”
Progress Indicators:  Depict the user ’s advancement through visual
graphics, for example, a status bar or a percentage, which is already done.
Check the previous progress chapter of the book for the rest.
“You are halfway through the onboarding!”
Call to Action:  Give a direct call to action for the subsequent work.
“To begin adding team members, click ‘Next’”
For instance, one can think of an app for Personal Finance Planning. After
the user accomplishes the initial setup, for example by adding ﬁnancial
goals and linking bank accounts, the summary screen may show something
like this:
Well done!
√ You have completed the initial setup. Here is what You have done so far:
✓         Added your ﬁnancial goals.
✓         Linked your bank accounts
✓         Set your monthly budget.
Next Step:
➢         Include regular costs
➢         It starts saving schemes
➢         Look at the ways to invest
You are in the middle of signing up. That is very nice of you to say ,
although you still need to continue to make the full use of this ﬁnancial
planning session.!

Under estimating User  Resistance to Change
Most product owners think that users learn to use new products rapidly
without thinking  of their resistance to change. Users are remarkably slow to
switch to a new product that is convenient, rather they are more into the
same old task. Anyone whose hypothetical forecasts are incorrect here
might be looking at very small adoption rates.
Let us clarify this with a scenario. Think about a group of accountants
reliant on particular bookkeeping software. They have come to know all of
the software's ins and outs, establishing helpful routines that utilize it. Now
imagine bringing in a new, presumably better , software. Your accountants
may not be thrilled. They could react with resistance, choosing to stick with
their tried-and-true systems, in spite of any existing issues.
Often, the roots of resistance lie in fear of redundancy , fear of
incompetence, or simply fear of the unknown.
People are more likely to accept change if they feel they have had a say in
it.
Involving users in decisions, or at least in the feedback process, fosters a
sense of ownership. For example, the "build in public" community involves
potential users right from the building stage and creates a community
around the product. By seeking continuous feedback and making changes
based on user input, these projects often see higher adoption rates and more
enthusiastic users.
Windows Vista’s Challenges in User  Adoption
Microsoft Windows Vista, released in January 2007, was intended as a
signiﬁcant upgrade from its widely adopted predecessor , Windows XP. It
promised enhanced security features, a redesigned user interface known as
Aero, and improved system performance.
However , Windows Vista encountered substantial challenges, primarily
stemming from user resistance to change. One major issue was its
demanding hardware requirements. Many existing computers did not meet
these speciﬁcati ons, forcing users to either upgrade their hardware or face
performance issues, which deterred immediate adoption.
The new Aero interface, with its redesigned visuals, window animations,
and revamped Start menu, also posed a hurdle. Users familiar with the
straightforward interface of Windows XP found it difﬁcult to navigate and
adapt to these changes, leading to frustration and resistance.
Compatibility problems further hindered Vista's adoption. Many existing
software applications and hardware drivers were not compatib le with the
new operating system upon its release. This lack of compatibility  resulted in
crashes, compatibility errors, and usability issues, which discouraged early
adopters from making the switch.
Moreover , Windows Vista initially suffered from performance and stability
issues. Users experienced slower system performance compared to
Windows XP, alongside occasional crashes, which signiﬁcantly damaged its
reputation. The negative publicity surrounding these issues further
exacerbated user reluctance to upgrade.
Consequently , adoption of Windows Vista was slower than Microsoft had
anticipated. Many businesses and individual users opted to stick with
Windows XP, waiting until Microsoft addressed the initial problems
through service packs and updates.
Microsoft's response included releasing updates to impr ove Vista's
performance and compatibility . These efforts aimed to mitigate user
concerns and enhance the overall user experience over time. Eventually ,
with the release of Windows 7 in 2009, Microsoft built upon Vista's
foundation but addressed many user concerns, which helped regain user
trust and accelerate adoption.
The experience with Windows Vista provided valuable lessons for
Microsoft and the technology industry as a whole. Clear communication
about product beneﬁts, thorough compatibility testing, and incremental
changes rather than drastic overhauls are crucial for managing user
resistance and ensuring successful product adoption.
Here are some steps you can take to handle the resistance to change:
When coming out with a new product, it is very important to note that users
will struggle with the change. Expect the pushback from them so that you
can develop effective solutions. Before you launch it, ﬁnd some potential
customers to give you feedback  on their concerns and objection s. This will
make it a more  efﬁcient onboarding process by tackling the particular
issues. You can use the results of the survey to ﬁnd out what are the main
things that the users currently like about their tools and what are the hard
parts for them with the present system.
Emphasize The Advantages
Represent clearly how your product is better than existing solutions.
Identify the beneﬁts that the clients are looking for in particular . For
example, "With our tool, you can interact with all your project management
stuff in one place, and save time as well as avoid the botherso me need to
log in-and-out with dif ferent apps".
The establishme nt of comparison graphs that outline the merits of your
software over today's products is a must. The contrast in a visual sense can
make these beneﬁts more real. Perform reportedly a side-to-side
comparison between the time kept or the functions being availa ble on your
product here than the competitor s. This comparison will likely be enough to
sell your product by itself.
Present Training Pr ogram
Provide extensiv e training materials not only for users to learn how to use
the new product but also for them to learn about all its features and
functionalities. Moreover , live webinars and elaborate materials should also
be prepared where users can learn directly and with a little bit of time.
Organize live online, interactive  training events where the questions of the
participants are answered instantaneously .
Progressive Disclosur e
Introduce use rs to a new product bit by bit. Progress may happen during
the  usage phase or by providing the choice  to switch slowly . How ever, users
can still use  both the old and new tools togethe r. Concede to allow users to
transfe r data and details from their old tools to the ne w product. This helps
them adapt easily .
Create an environment whereby  people want to adopt the product that is
different and good by offering direct and indirect rewards. These can be
done through appreciation, rewa rd or presenting the product's capability in a
tangible way (like adding quick wins that can be used as examples) . Provide
a discou nt or a month free of service to those users who come  aboard the
rapidly-emer ging process within a limited time.
Suppose you are a salesperson introducing a new CRM system to a sales
team that has been using spreadsheets for years. Here are ways you could
potentially handle their resistance:
1. Familiarize Themselves with the Situation: The sales agents, on
the other hand, are familiar with spreadsheets and may have
reservations about the rigidity of a possible new system.
2. Explain the Advantages: Demonstrate how the CRM system may
be a remedy for error -prone data entry , can offer them an insig ht
into their custom ers’ needs, and allow the CRM to sync with their
email that will save them time and money .
3. Provide Training: Offer games to exhibit the new CRM to them
as a fun way to learn the software or conduct a competitio n.
Teach them through a set of video guides and ongoing weekly
webinars showing them some tips and the right way of employing
the CRM tool.
4. Transposing: Permit the use of the CRM to begin with their
spreadsheets and gradually juggling the rest of the functions into
the CRM.
Self-Assessment
★      Have I accurately assessed potential user resistance to the new
product or feature?
★      What are the primary reasons users might resist switching from their
current solution?
★      How can I empathize with users who are reluctant to change?
★      Have I ef fectively communicated the beneﬁts of the new product
compared to existing solutions?
★      What speciﬁc advantages does the new product of fer that address pain
points of current users?
★      How can I create clear and compelling comparison materials (e.g.,
graphs, testimonials) to illustrate these beneﬁts?
★      Have I developed comprehensive training materials and resources to
help users learn how to use the new product?
★      What additional support mechanisms (e.g., webinars, tutorials, help
articles) can I provide to ease the transition for users?
★      How can I make training sessions interactive and engaging to increase
user conﬁdence and adoption?
★      Are there progressive adoption strategies I can implement to allow
users to transition gradually?
★      How can I facilitate data transfer or integration from existing tools to
the new product to make the transition smoother?
★      What incentives or rewards can I of fer to encourage early adopters
and mitigate resistance?
★      Am I actively seeking and listening to user feedback throughout the
onboarding process?
★      How can I incorporate user feedback into iterative improvements to
enhance user experience and address concerns?
★      What mechanisms can I establish to ensure continuous feedback loops
beyond the initial launch phase?
★      How can I manage user expectations regarding initial challenges or
learning curves associated with the new product?
★      What proactive measures can I take to address potential performance
or compatibility issues before they impact user perception?
★      How can I leverage success stories or early adopter testimonials to
build conﬁdence and positive perception among potential users?
★      How can I foster a sense of community around the new product to
encourage peer support and shared learning?
★      What channels (e.g., forums, social media groups) can I establish for
users to share experiences, ask questions, and provide support to each
other?
★      Are there opportunities to showcase user success stories or
achievements to inspire others and build momentum?
Ignoring Mobile Onboarding
Even though it seems like our main focus has been on web apps, nearly all
the mistakes mentioned above could be made with mobile apps as well.
Forgetting onboa rding for mobile could be as fatal as neglecting the users of
laptops. In both cases, insufﬁcient onboarding leads to user anger and a
higher rate of turn over .
Product owners give preference to desktop or web-based onboarding
experiences and show indifference to mobile users. These days, there are
many people who use their mobile devices in almost every area of their
daily life, including communication to productivity to actual entertainment.
Avoiding mobil e onboarding will cause a signiﬁcant negative impact on
your user base and thus result in increased churn and potentially lower
engagement. You can implem ent user-friendly interfaces, simplify the
process, and use as little text as possible to allow easy user onboarding on
small screens.
Milehighcomics.com’ s Mobile V iew
So how can you ensure that your onboarding adapts to these very mobile
devices? Below are some ways to do so:
Responsive Design
Responsive design is a crucial aspect of creating a seamless onboarding
experience for users across different devices. It ensures  that your
onboarding process looks and functions well on all screen sizes, from
desktops to smartphones. let us dive deeper into how you can achieve
effective responsive design in your onboarding process:
Use Grid Systems:  Use a grid system that scales proportionally according
to the screen size. This system is also known as Fluid Grid System.
Frameworks that are popular like Bootstrap, Material CSS, tailwind, or CSS
Grid gives you the ability to develop dynamic layouts, which alter as per
the viewport size.
Flexible Media:  To guarantee that images and other media features resize
appropriately without blurring or changing their aspect ratio. For instance,
max-width: 100% as a CSS style property is used in making images
responsive.
Deﬁne Breakpoints: You shall deﬁne the points within your CSS that will
have various styles for a device at a certain screen size. The usual points of
the break are the following:
1. Small devices (e.g., smartphones) at 576px and below
2. Medium devices (e.g., tablets) at 768px and below
3. Large devices (e.g., laptops) at 992px and below
4. Extra-lar ge devices (e.g., desktops) at 1200px and above
Screen adaptation strategies should be practiced in order to project
progressively altered designs. This is particularly elementary to any front-
end dev with a decent amount of experience, I'd say .
Relative Units:  Employ relative units such as em, rem, or percentages in
place of the ﬁxed ones such as px in typography . That will ensure that text
sizes are signiﬁcantly decreased  or increased according to whatever device
they are viewed on.
Viewport Units:  You may also opt to use units of viewport that are the size
of a virtual screen (vw and vh) for this font size that can be scaled down or
up according to the user's screen.
Large Clickable Areas: Buttons  and interactive elements should be large
enough to be operated by a ﬁnger on small screens. Compliance with the
minimum touch target sizes is sought on touch-based devices (e.g., 44x44
pixels).
Gestur es: Integrate common mobile gestures like swiping, pinching, and
tapping to the UX onboarding process.
Use Browser  Developer  Tools:  The majority of modern browsers
incorporate deve loping tools enabling to have a display of various devices
and screen sizes. The use of developer tools for check ing on the
responsiveness of your design is highly recommended.
When possible, empirically verify the onboarding process through practical
devices. This lets you detect any problems that may not be evident in
simulations. The techniques listed here are often used but not just here.
We shall deal with an overview  of mobile issues here but I am going to
produce another  issue of the book, and it will be totally devoted to the
design of mobile  applications. Please wait for more details coming soon and
strategies that will be completely tailored to mobile applications.
Self-Assessment
★      Have I optimized the onboarding process for mobile devices,
considering screen size, touch interaction, and device orientation?
★      Are interactive elements like buttons and forms designed to be easily
accessible and usable on small screens?
★      Have I integrated common mobile gestures (swiping, tapping,
pinching) to enhance the UX during onboarding?
★      Does the onboarding experience maintain consistency and usability
across dif ferent screen sizes and resolutions?
★      Have I used responsive design principles like ﬂuid grids, ﬂexible
media, and viewport units to adapt to various device dimensions?
★      Are fonts and other elements using relative units (em, rem) rather
than ﬁxed pixels (px) for better scalability?
★      Are touch tar gets for buttons and interactive elements lar ge enough
(at least 44x44 pixels) for easy tapping on touch-based devices?
★      Have I considered er gonomic factors to ensure comfortable
navigation through onboarding steps on mobile devices?
★      Have I optimized the onboarding process to minimize loading times
and ensure smooth performance, especially on mobile networks?
★      Do I conduct performance testing on real mobile devices to identify
and address any latency issues or delays?
★      Have I thoroughly tested the mobile onboarding process across a
range of devices, including smartphones and tablets?
★      Am I gathering feedback from users who onboard via mobile to
understand their experience and areas for improvement?
★      Is the onboarding process accessible to users with disabilities who
may use assistive technologies on mobile devices?
★      Have I ensured that content is presented clearly and succinctly ,
avoiding unnecessary text that could clutter smaller screens?
★      Have I reviewed and compared the onboarding experiences of similar
mobile apps to learn best practices and identify areas for
differentiation?
★      Am I using analytics and user behavior data to reﬁne and enhance the
mobile onboarding process over time?
★      Do I have a strategy for ongoing iteration and enhancement of the
mobile onboarding experience based on user feedback and
technological advancements?
★      How can I maintain engagement and keep the onboarding process
dynamic to encourage users to explore more features of the app?
Failing to Addr ess Security Concerns
In a world where data breaches and cyber threats are now an everyday
occurrence, we are experiencin g a situation where people need to have
more interest and greater security of their personal information. Security is
a critica l issue that is not just about user conﬁdence but is about product
sustainability as well. You should not overlook these matters during users
onboarding as it will lead to users being insecure, lacking trust and over
time, they will no longer be customers. Identiﬁcation of access to their
personal data and account details is safe is the wish of your users when they
use your product.
During the regis tration or onboarding process, reveal everythin g about the
security features  of the product and data handling policies. Make people
feel better by offering legal compliance certiﬁcates, the use of secret codes,
and the conﬁde ntiality of their personal data. Add in the choice of two-
factor authentic ation and other safety elements to result in trust in
customers who apply them.
Coinbase Security Practices
Coinbase, a prominent cryptocurrency exchange platform, faces  substantial
challenges in safeguarding user information and digital assets due to the
inherent risks associated with ﬁnancial transactions and the high value of
cryptocurrencies.
Coinbase, found ed in 2012 by Brian Armstrong and Fred Ehrsam, has
established itself as a leading cryptocurrency exchange platform  known for
its robust security practices. As of the twelve months ending  March 31,
2024, Coinbase Global reported substantial revenue of $3.973 billion USD,
underscoring its signiﬁcant growth and market position in the
cryptocurrency exchange industry .
One of their primary challenges involves cybersecurity threats, including
phishing attacks, hacking attempts, and malware designed to compromise
user accounts. Given the sensitive nature of ﬁnancial data involved,
maintaining robust security measures is paramount to protecting user trust
and ensuring the platform's integrity .
Coinbase Account Setup Pr ocess
To address these challenges, Coinbase has invested heavily in a
comprehensive security infrastru cture. This includes employing  encryption
protocols to secure user data, implementing secure storage mechanisms
such as cold storage for digital assets, and continuously monitoring
transactions for suspicious activities. Additionally , Coinbase emphasizes the
importance of two-factor authentication (2FA) during the user onboarding
process, encouraging users to enable this additional layer of security to
mitigate the risk of unauthorized access.
Educational initiatives play a crucial role in Coinbase's strategy as well.
They provide users with comprehensive resources and guides  to educate
them about security best practic es. These efforts aim to empow er users to
recognize and respond to potential threats like phishing attempts effectively .
Coinbase also adheres strictly to regulatory compliance requirements,
including anti-money laundering (AML) and Know Your Customer (KYC)
regulations. This commitment not only ensures legal complian ce but also
reinforces Coin base's credibili ty and trustworthiness among users and
regulatory authorities.
In preparation for potential security incidents, Coinbase has developed a
detailed incident response plan. This plan enables them to promptly detect,
respond to, and mitigate secur ity breaches or threats. In the event of a
security inciden t, Coinbase prioritizes timely communication with affected
users, providing transparent updates and guidance to mitigate any potential
impact on their accounts or assets.
Overall, Coinbase's proactive approach to addressing securi ty concerns
during user onboarding and throughout its operations underscores its
commitment to protecting user interests and maintaining a secure trading
environment. By integrating robust security measures, prom oting user
education, ensuring regulatory adherence, and maintaining effective
incident response capabilities, Coinbase not only enhances user trust but
also strengthens its position as a leading cryptocurrency exchange platform
in an increasingly competitive market.
Take the case of Slack, the communication tool is very attentive to security
right from the beginning, when it comes to onboarding. New users are the
ﬁrst to register on Slack, and Slack provides them with an in-depth
description of the security measures including data encryption  and GDPR
challenged by data. Moreover , there are also two-factor authentication titles
jogged with the account's description. Further , Slack gives instructions on
how to secure their data, and they even introduce tools for the users to use
in case of suspe cting a data breach. Through suggesting the security issues
directly , Slack builds up conﬁdence and thus, they possess their clients
assured about the correctness of their service.
Here are some practical steps that you can implement to address security
concerns of your users. You may not need to meet all the steps but do look
out for steps that are necessary for you based on your functioning and
industry:
Transpar ency
Transparency is key in establishing trust with your clients, and it is
especially impo rtant when it comes to security . Users of your product
expect to know that their data is in good hands and that you are doing
everything necessary to protect their privacy . Through transparency that is
done in an authentic and sincere manner , you can take away fear from the
users and alternatively make them satisﬁed with the ideas of telling you
their personal information.
Users are more comfortable with your product and are more likely to buy
when you make  it clear what security measures you have in place. This
secure link is the basis for customer loyalty and satisfaction which is a
major concern of very sensitive industries, such as ﬁnance, healthcare, and
e-commerce.
Data Encryption:  State clearly that your product is using encryption to
secure the user data. For instance, you can articulate that all the data sent
from the user’s hardware to your servers is encrypted using recognized
protocols which are industry standards.
Data Storage Safety:  A message to the clients on how the data is kept safe
could go like this, "Your data is stored in big machines with protection
against hackers.  We use a data center that comes under the protection of
secure technology and is realized through ﬁrewalls and security systems."
Credential and Certiﬁcate:  When your product is onboarding, it would
help establish trust if you can add the compliance certiﬁca tes such as
GDPR, HIPAA, and ISO 27001. This is a clear sign that the products have
closely been safeguarded and data has only been accessed the right way .
Audit Reports:  Usually, in the ﬁrst place, make sure that your product has
been audited regularly by some  independent specialists. Make a reference
like the following, "To ensure that improve the compliant level of our
software we constantly audited by the independent specialists in the ﬁeld."
on the website it says that we under go regular security audits by third-party
experts.
Concise Summ ary: Give a short and precisely formulated summary of
your privacy policy . Omit the use of jargon and legal code language; thus,
reach for simple terms that all users can grasp. An example, "Respecting
privacy of your information is our ﬁrst priority , and that is why we use no
third party besides us to get it."
Detailed Policy Access:  Lead users to the entire privacy policy who want
to see speciﬁc details. Create a link that says "Read our full privacy policy
here."
Explain Permis sions:  Defend  the necessity of some permissions to users
and specify how the data will be utilized in the process. As an instance, "We
would like to ﬁnd your location to be able to provide precisely  accurate
weather details that are based on your vicinity ."
Opt-In Choices: Opt for the setting where users can decide to share data or
not. This way users can have the authority over their data and also it shows
you do comply with their privacy preferences.
Two-Factor  Authentication (2FA): Present a 2FA during sign-up and
explain it by using simple terms. You could express, "Two-Factor
Authentication functions as an extra layer of security when a preliminary
step of authentication "password" is followed up by another step such as a
code sent to the mobile or generated by the authentication app."
Security Updates:  Our customers are aware of the regularity of our
security updates  and improvements. For instance, "We update our security
policies continuously to ﬁght of f the latest cyber threats."
Transpar ent Incident Reporting: When a security incident happens,
speciﬁcally describe what occurred, what measures were taken, and what
steps are being taken to avoid  the recurrence of similar incidents. For
instance, "Just recently , we detected and patched up a security ﬂaw. We
guarantee the safety of our trafﬁc and have, in addition, erected the very
best protection to avert something similar in the future."
Suppose you are creating a health tracking app. Here is the procedure to
execute the transparent communication during onboarding:
1. Highlight Secur ity Measures: "When transmitting your health
data, it is ﬁrst encrypted and then stored securely on our servers."
2. Showcase Compliance: "Our software developer screened the app
to make HIPAA rules. That means your data is tested and secured
on the highest standards."
3. Privacy Policy: "We respect your privacy and, moreover , will
never invade the personal space of a user who opts out to disclose
their health information. Check  out our privacy policy to learn
more."
4. Explain Permissions: "We need to have access to your ﬁtness
information in order to generate  custom health tips for you. You
can independently manage your data transfer ."
5. Regular Updates: "We regularl y update our security measures.
Keep track of the latest safety features to keep your data secure."
Teach The User
Teaching the security practices to the users should be a primary  focus area
during the onboarding stage. When the user is being trained in account
protection and the danger signs  to notice, they will feel more secure and
become more efﬁcient in using your product. Here is how that can be done
in a correct manner: Instant Remainder .
Make sure to utilize clear and easy instructions on the subject of the prime
security measure s. For instance, 'Make sure your passwor ds are as complex
as possible by using lower and upper -case alphabet character s, numbers,
and special symbols. ' Birthda ys or words commonly used by people would
be some examples of information that can be easily guessed by others."
Make sure your employees are aware of common attributes of phishing e-
mails. This will not only help them detect phishing e-mails but also, users
will no longer be a victim of such scams. For instance, "Do not fall for
emails or messages that ask you to share personal information or contain
dubious links. Whenever the sender is wanting to click on any links, never
forget to verify the sender's identity .
Ensure that these resources are engaging by making them interactive, such
as, quizzes, and short videos. For instance, "Watch this short video to learn
how to identify phishing emails and take our quiz to test your knowledge. "
Create multiple support channe ls where users can ask question s and solve
problems that are related to security . If for instance, "What security issues
do you want to be solved help you. You have urgent problems that need
immediate resolution. So communicate with us on chat, mail, or call us. We
are eager to help you.  "
Display a resource center by composing articles, FAQs, and tutorials to
answer users questions. Once  people get onboarded, they should be
promoted so that they go to the Resource Center . They will be informed of
the details, for example by saying, "Go to our Secur ity Resour ce Center and
read the detailed guides in order to protect your account and be safe
online. "
Make sure that users are involv ed in community forums, where they can
share their own tips and experiences. For example, "Begin by getting
engaged with our community forum to share with other users the best
practices for security and to ask for help. ” From other past experiences, you
will learn a lot.

27001 available for users to see?

preferences?

accessible to users?
★      Is your incident response plan detailed and capable of quickly
addressing security breaches?
Not Monitoring User  Engagement Metrics
In SaaS, one of the key success factors is the knowledge of how users
interact with your product during onboarding. On the other hand, many
product owners lack the habit of tracking customer engagement metrics.
Thus, opportunities to identify bottlenecks and improve the user onboarding
process might be missed, which, in effect, can lead to less satisfaction and
retention of the customers.
What is Customer  Engagement?
Customer engagement is the relatively interdependent connection between a
product and its users. Simply put, it stands for what we do to keep our
shoppers engage d with the products we offer. Engaged users will go the
extra mile of delving into their role with the software and know the product
well, whereas non-users are more likely to give up on it soon.
Although engag ement appears clear , it is actually not easy to measure,
especially in SaaS. Engaging and monitoring engagement metri cs can be an
interesting way to better understand user behavior which can lead to better
optimization of the onboarding process.
Why User  Engagement Matters in SaaS
User engagement in the SaaS domain is vital because it indicate s the beneﬁt
that users are getting from your product. Low engagement signa ls that users
do not perceive  your offering as a valuable one which increases the
probability of churning. High engagement means that people are gaining
value, which might lead them to recommend, upgrade, and bring the overall
company growth.
For SaaS companies, product managers and customer success managers, the
activities of tracking user engagement, and making improvement decisions
are the most crucial in their development. Engagement scores above and
beyond very many other beneﬁ ts might also help to check the usage of
speciﬁc functionalities, prioritize the product roadmap and improve the user
experience through focusing on the most likely attraction point.
How to Get to That Data?
When you look at the behavioral patterns of the users, you will come across
friction points that deter users and, hence do not outweigh the utility of the
onboarding process over the period of time.
On the earning front, let us consider product owners' attempt to evaluate
what user engagement mistakes  they have made in the analytic process. In
case you implement any of these you are obviously going the wrong way .:
Trying Random Trial and Err or Engagement Tactics
Product owners of SaaS tend to over-engineer the concept of engaging with
their products through the practice of just adding points of engagement that
is only different from the plan. New suggestions are given by the last agent,
as an example, they present the live chat tool or inform the custo mers about
the price  discou nts but these are only quick ﬁxes, often use it may result in
low engagement and unworthy personal experiences.
There is the possibility of getting different results with regard to users due
to the fact that such tactics are not directly targeting the speciﬁc  interests of
the users. In addition, the management of features is not strategic and does
not guarantee either customer satisfa ction or user retention in the long run if
the company does not have a clear plan of action.
For building sustainable success , do not be tempted by a fad or seek quick
remedies. Instea d of a scatter gun approach, create a sustainable model that
is connected with the growth of your product and that offers the customer
value. Doing so will ensure strategic actions are the center of your
engagement projects; they will, therefore, lead to increased product use and
customer satisfa ction. Also, the approach is right in the sense that the user
gets the maximum value of the product and thus the company generates
more income through thoughtful engagements and functionalities.
Attracting the Wrong Customers
In a product-oriented company , the customer's journey consists of the stages
of Acquisition, Engagement, Monetization, and Expansion. On the other
hand, in a sales-led model, they are Acquire, Monetize, Engage, and
Expand. Although it might seem quite logical to address problems of
engagement at the middle stage, there is also a critical need to analyze the
acquisition stage as one of the possible culprits for the problem.
Attracting clients who do not ﬁt your product may reduce the achievement
of your acquisition goals. These clients will not ﬁnd your product important
and beneﬁcial which will cause low activation rates and high drop-of f rates.
Also, the customers who under go the "Aha" moment initially may never
come back if your product is not catering to their pain points.
Thus, it is funda mental to the right customers right from the start. It is only
when you are ensuring that your acquisition initiatives are focused on
people who need and can take the maximum advantage from your product
that will result in increased engagement while maintaining consumer
satisfaction. This proactive method eliminates engagement problems and
provides a solid base for customer success.
Over emphasis on New Featur es Over  Modiﬁcations
Product teams often prefer creating new features as compared to perfecting
current ones. New ideas are thrilling and more attractive to the respective
teams as well as the poten tial customers as compared to brand
improvement. In any case, this style may still affect the engagement
strategy .
So, this is the case:
1. Extended Development Time: Creating the new features usually
takes a couple of months. You keep on engaging your existin g
problems during this period, which may become more acute as
time goes by .
2. Initial Imperfect ions: The ﬁrst version of the new features is often
not perfect and it is necessary to reﬁne them further . The result
will be longer before these developments will bring about actual
beneﬁts to your already presented ones.
The wise move here is to put your tech talents into developing something
new alongside making the current products more efﬁcient. Rather than
devoting your entire budget to new releases, you can give 20%-30%, for
example, to upgrading and updating the current products, thus opening a
window for better quality .
This will in turn guarantee the consistent improvement of the user
engagement. This will lead to the improvement of the current product and
the introduction of the new features. Your current product, which is also
innovating throu gh the introduction of new features, will thereby remain
valuable and will consequently , have the right level of user engagement.
Over  Reliance on Messaging and Incentives
A lot of companies mistakenly think that by ﬁring timely emails and giving
rewards they will escalate the user engagement. Speciﬁcally , they are under
the illusion that they can get clients into the habit of using the product if
they catch users' attention at the right moments.
However , though these methods can be used to achieve some happiness
with the product, they cannot be the only solution to bad user engagement.
If we ask for the main issue, we will be told that the one and only reason
why your product failed—the value it gives the user. Your product should
directly address a problem or help users reach their objectives. The
marketing efforts, as well as the communication via rewards, are a
standalone to attract and keep the customers.
The building of engagement is a battle across the organization that has
many fronts. The key point of focusing is the exploration of the product's
ability to actually deliver the promised beneﬁts. The marketing team and the
customer suppor t ones should interact with the clients so that they can see
the product's value with their own eyes, and also get feedbac k from the
users.
Focusing on Vanity Metrics
One of the examples of vanity metrics like page views, downloads, and the
number of sign-ups is very ﬂashy to the eyes of an enthusiast. Nevertheless,
it does not truly inform you of the user commitment to your product or
service. Here, point out the fact that since these criteria focus on the
quantity instead of the quality . Some of them do not cover the real value
that the app provides to users. Thus, such a mistake will limit the
developments and the best possible opportunities that the real users may
bring.
The key to measuring user engagement in depth comes from the relevant
metrics which can be examined from different angles. The assessment
should be based on your business goals and meanwhile, you will get
concrete insights that can help in improving the user experience.
Ignoring Cohort Analysis
Cohort analysis is a way to segment users into groups based on a common
trait- their start date of the product or the time they ﬁrst interac ted with the
product. By assessing these groups separately , you gain new insights about
user behavior and engagement  trends that are difﬁcult to observe when
looking at the whole user base.
Each of the user cohorts may have various engagement patterns, and
discussing all users as an integ rated group might be just one convenient
way to cover these differences. The latter might result in misinterpreted
engagement of users and onboarding strategies as it does not include
variables like changes over time or the distinction of users.
One of the best ways to use the cohort analysis is to do a trafﬁc comparison
on different users' start dates. This plotting approach helps you to observe
different groups  of users and see if users from one cohort perform
differently than the others. As a result, this helps you not only to notice the
commonality of the data but also to spot any new trends that might occur .
Steps to Implement Cohort Analysis:
1. Deﬁne Cohorts: The ﬁrst step is to identify the basis for the
cohorts. On the one hand, you can separate the cohort data into
two sets, i.e., the data that was collected earlier and the data that
was collected later. But you can as well deﬁne other
characteristics of your cohorts such as their origin or the
advertisements they came from etc.
2. Track Key Metrics: The ﬁrst step is to track the main indicators
of user engagement at the quarterly frequency for each cohort.
You might also track retention rates, feature usage, or the time to
ﬁrst value for each cohort.
3. Compare Cohor ts: The globally performed customer analysis is
the key tool we use. A list of the differentials between the cohor ts
will indicate, for example, how the rate of changes in retention
rates over time or how differences/changes in them/are adopted
by the dif ferent cohorts through such devices.
4. Identify Insights: The ﬁrst thing is to take from these ﬁndings and
answer two important questions: "Which of the projects does?"
and "Which of the projects does not?" To clarify: a speciﬁc group
might be the difference between a higher and a lower engageme nt
level according to the scenario. Investigate which project points
might have led to better user engagement among these particul ar
users.
5. Implement: It can be thought the obtaining of purposeful data
from the cohort analysis can be intensely useful for modifyin g
features and the onboarding process to the visitors' liking. The
feedback from the latest cohorts he vessel assessing humankind
has revealed show that most of them end up being more fruitful.
Who of you might be involved in the busy times of the project? You might
decide to run a project management SaaS product and use the cohort
analysis to ﬁnd out how different user groups interact with your product in
due time.
Step 1: Deﬁne Cohorts You group cohorts by the month when the user
registration took place. For example, by adding January , February , and
March, you thus create a separate cohort for each of the users joining you.
Step 2: Track Key Metrics For each cohort, you track:
1. Retention rates at 1 week, 1 month, and 3 months.
2. Frequency of key feature usage (e.g., task creation, project
sharing).
3. Average time spent in the app.
Step 3: Compare  Cohorts You compare the retention rates and feature usage
of the January , February , and March cohorts. You notice that the January
cohort has signiﬁcantly higher retention and feature usage as opposed to the
February and March cohorts.
Step 4: Identify Insights The inquiry pushes you to get to the core of the
issue and you discovered that during January , you brough t in a new
onboarding tutorial that was eventually stranded due to technical issues.
This implies the tutorial had a positive ef fect on the user engagement.
Step 5: Implement Through these insights you reintroduce and enhance the
onboarding tutorial. You also choose to monitor subsequent cohorts to make
sure the tutorial is signiﬁcantly impacting engagement.
Ignoring Dr op-off Points
When users drop off or stop using your product at a certain stage, it is
crucial to understand why this happens. Not taking notice of these drop-of f
points would mean that you are losing out on important chances  to ensure a
customer's satisfaction and user retention.
Imagine you run an e-learning platform where users sign up for courses,
complete lessons, and earn certiﬁcations. You have noticed that a signiﬁcant
number of users drop off after signing up but before completing their ﬁrst
lesson.
Analyzing User  Flow:
To deal with this, one should start by visualizing the typical user journey:
1. Sign-Up: Users make their account.
2. Course Selection: Users view and pick a course.
3. Lesson Start: Users start the ﬁrst session.
4. Lesson Completion: Users do the task and pass from stage to
stage.
By the help of the analytics tools, you will be able to track where all users
are falling out. To continue the example, assume you see a high drop-of f
rate between "Course Selection" and "Lesson Start".
Identifying the Cause:  Subsequently , the cause of begin with the one that
is godown by users at this point by researching it. Probable reasons might
be:
1. Complex Onboarding: Users can be the kind to waste the entire
lesson after subject picking as they may fail to commence the
assigned course.
2. Lack of Motivation: Users may be very down and out about the
course contents and that is the reason for their being inactive.
3. Technical Issues : There might be some bugs or loading issues that
can make them non-starters.
Assuming All Engagement is Positive:
When scrutinizing the level of user engagement, it tends to be the easiest
thing to think that all activities in your product are good indicato rs. Yet, it is
not alwa ys the case because some activities might indicate user frustration,
confusion, or dissatisfaction. Given the fact that the sampling of the data of
the page visits prefers the project management tool where users are in the
privilege of creating projects, assign tasks, and get to know the progress.
You are impressed with how often people are visiting this section of your
app.
One option for learning more about this concept is to tell the difference
between positive and negative engagement:
Positive Engag ement:  Positive  are activities that your target audience or
users do which can be interp reted as showing they ﬁnd your product
beneﬁcial and can reach their goals. For example, a user always does what
he/she is supposed to do, and the more challenging part such as code
creation, is left to team members. they bury themselves in an advanced list
of functions and so on.
Negative Engag ement:  In other words , the actions that customers do the
most are the cause and the symptoms of the problem type of engagement
that need to be solved. For instance, a user who obviously does not know
exactly what he wants, keeps going over the material in the "help" section
more than any other . The user on their third print run is gettin g the same
paper jam each time. or the abandoners are guiding the task halfway .
There should be awareness of the fact that interactions are not always
positive, and therefore you need  to do something to separate the ones that
are right from the ones that are wrong.
Self-Evaluation
