experience?
When will the onboarding end?
In fact, onboard ing will never end. Let us get into the meat of this article:
continuous onboarding. Onboarding for the ﬁrst time is merely a minor
portion of the process. Onboarding is a long-term project. At different levels
of product usage, onboarding has varied objectives. AHA  moment,
activation, adop tion, retention , and expansion are the stages of an
onboarding mission.
AHA  moment is the ﬁrst aim to surprise and gratify users.
Activation represents that the user has used the most basic product features.
For the ﬁrst onboarding, the AHA  moment and activation are the objectives.
Onboarding, we often say, is about allowing people to have an AHA
moment with our product and get activated. Adoption refers to users
gradually integrating SaaS with their existing workﬂows over time.
Adoption is best expressed when  SaaS becomes a part of the job. Retention
is the continuit y of users through the next payment cycle. Onboarding
should aim to eliminate all issues that affect the renewal at this time and
complete the renewal. Expansio n is when customers buy more advanced
plans or other cross products. Onboarding is the process of assisting
consumers in perceiving (or experiencing) the features of advanced plans
that can help them be more productive at work.
For continuous onboarding, the goals of adoption, retention, and expansion
must be met. To achieve these, we must continue to guide users to use non-
basic functions  of the product after initial onboarding. The new
functionality is another initial onboarding for our existing customers.
Measuring the Success of Your Onboarding Pr ocess
Just relying on your intuition that your customer onboarding  is actually
functioning is not enough. To genuinely understand and measure the
success of your onboarding process, what you need is measurable data
which is solid and made with proper techniques.
The four critical metrics are the ones to discuss it in more detail; these are
the key metrics on which you need to focus to make your onboarding look
good:
1. Activation Rate
Activation rate is the segment of new users who have been direc ted towards
the product's primary functions and core value for the ﬁrst time. It is
applicable in that they (users) take on the product.
A high-quality rate of activati on, that your onboarding design properly
steers new users to the main scope, is obviously the best scenario you can
have. For most products, a good example of a high, healthy activation rate
is within the range of 25-30%  but it can be different with the type of
product you have.
For a marketing automation platform, activation could be about users'
connection with their website and getting info from the two data streams
and another stream that is the result of the interplay among the two, which
would be leads scoring. On the other hand, people are showing the
product’ s compe titive start by treating the above completion steps o the
‘activation’  level of the product.
Some people consider the concept of activation as being similar with the
‘Aha’  moment, but there is a slight dif ference.
The users’  realization of the product’ s possible value is the ‘Aha’  moment.
Activation, which takes the form of user’s ﬁrst-hand exposure to that value,
is the exact opposite.
Imagine Sarah, a freelance graphic designer , who seeks more efﬁcient
means to her client project management, She comes across a project
management app that promises to facilitate her tasks, set deadlines, and
collaborate with customers step by step. The description of these features
causes her an 'Aha!' moment, where she realizes that this tool could really
improve her workﬂow . She signs up for a trial for free and kicks off
everything through the app be-creating a project, inputting her tasks, and
communicating with her clients. When Sarah successfully uses these
features, she moves from realizing the potential value (Aha moment) to
experiencing it ﬁrsthand (activation).
Also, activation of a user can be different not just from one product to
another but even within the same product depending on the personas and
use cases. To clarify , a marketing manager might activate differently than a
data analyst even if they are using the same software.
E-commerce companies often consider “activation” the ﬁrst transaction a
person makes.
2. Retention Rate
We will now address customer retention. Consider it as the tally of clients
that remain devoted to your product past a speciﬁc initial date. See this as a
reﬂection of your product's impact, and consequently , your user
contentment over time. Traditionally this calculation happens monthly , but
you are free to adjust the tool as it ﬁts your requirements.
For example, assuming you acquired 100 clients on January 1 and by
February 1, 90 of them were still active, the retention rate comes to 90%.
This is a good sign as it seems that the majority of them are enjoying the
product. The relatively strong customer retention points out the recurring
and stable income, which is the main target for subscription-based services
as for this they 'll get steady income.
In order to comp rehend retentio n, you have to also observe your churn rate,
the percentage of customers who stop using your product. People can leave
due to many reasons. Some may leave on their own decision, after they
have gotten all, they need from your product, or it does not ﬁt their needs
anymore. Possi bly, some other people will be involuntarily forced out
because of problems like payment failures, server issues, or even unhelpful
customer suppor t. So, when both sides of the coin (retention and churn) are
kept under control, you have the full picture.
There are severa l ways to keep customers, but the ﬁrst step is understanding
who is using your product. By ﬁnding out that users have different needs
and actions, you can create strategies that work for each group. This
personalized approach can make users happy . It also helps the company to
have better customer keeping rates.
Various Categories of Users
In order to understand what your users need you have to analyze your users
under three dissimilar conditions:
New Users: These are indiv iduals who have recently joined up and are just
getting acquainted with your product. They typically use simple functions
and are mostly in the trial-and-error stage of learning the system. Imagine
them as newbie s in a class moving around and asking for guidance while
also being a bit awkward.
Inactive Users:  This group comprises individua ls who either do not interact
with your product or only enga ge occasionally . They are in the "promix"
phase and require a bit more motivation to remain interested. A ﬁtting
analogy would be friends you sometimes lose contact with, but desire to
reignite that old bond, reminding about 'the good old times'  is necessary .
Power  Users:  These are speci alists that have a profound knowledge of your
product and its features. They  are always testing it to the extreme and
getting the maximum value from it. They are like old-timers who are
familiar with all the smart ways and complex features of your products and
are always searching for more ways to obtain better results.
Customer  Retention Strategies for  New Users
New users need to ﬁnd immediate value in your product so they stick
around. Here is how to help them:
Offer  In-App Guided Onboard ing: Instead of bomb arding new users with
long demos or endless documents, use interactive, step-by-step tutorials
right inside your app. This makes learning hands-on and practical, helping
users see value quickly .
Use Gamiﬁcation:  Make onboarding fun by adding game-like elements.
For exam ple, reward users with badges when they complete certain tasks,
show progress bars for milestones, or offer perks for ﬁnishing steps. It
keeps users motivated and engaged.
Provide On-Demand Self-Help Options: Many users prefer solving
problems on their own. Equip your app with self-help widgets, a detailed
knowledge base, chatbots for quick queries, and an FAQ page. This
empowers users to ﬁnd solutions without waiting for support.
Ask for Feedback:  Regularly seek feedback from new users at key points,
like after they complete their proﬁle or a signiﬁcant task. Use in-app
surveys to gather insights when  the experience is fresh in their minds. This
helps you improve the onboarding process and address issues promptly .
Reach Out When Issues Arise : If some thing disrupts the new user ﬂow,
immediately reach out to help. Quick assistance makes users feel welcomed
and accommodated, reinforcing their decision to choose your product.
Whether it is a technical glitch or confusion about a feature, timely support
can turn a potential frustration into a positive experience.
Customer  Retention Strategies for  Inactive or  Occasional Users
Inactive or occasional users need  regular nudges to return to your app. Here
is how to better engage and retain them:
Send Regular  Progress Reports:  Use email to remind users of their goals
and achievements. For example, Grammarly sends weekly writing updates,
and Tettra sends  daily digest emails. These reports keep your product top of
mind and show users their progress.
Use Content to Keep Customers Engaged:  Share helpful content
regularly to remind users of the value your product offers. Tutorials,
product guides, and research can re-engage users by showing them new
ways to beneﬁt from your product. Zapier , for example, publishes guides on
automating work and productivity tips.
Identify Red Flags for Churn: Track product usage patterns to spot early
signs of disengagement. Look at how often users log in, the key actions
they complete, and their overall activity . This data helps you identify at-risk
users and reach out before they churn.
Reach Out for Feedback:  Try to learn why users are inactive by reaching
out for feedback. You will never know the problem until they spill the
beans. Send surveys or personal messages asking about their experience and
how you can help them get more value from your product. Understanding
their issues can help you address pain points and improve retention.
Offer  Personalized Re-Engagement Campaigns:  Create  campaigns
tailored to the needs and interests of inactive users. This could be special
offers, reminders of features they have not tried, or personalize d tips to get
more out of the product.
Customer  Retention Strategies for  Power  Users
Power users know your product inside and out and use it to its fullest
potential. Here is how to keep them engaged and loyal:
Offer  Add-Ons and Upsell Discounts:  Show appreciat ion for power users
by offering them add-ons and discounts. Deﬁne what makes a power user
for your product, such as frequen t use of features or high referral rates. Give
them free trials of advanced features or discounts when they reach
milestones. For example, Trello offers a free month of Trello Gold for users
who invite the most people.
Give Early Access to New Offerings:  Let power users try new features
before anyone else. This not only provides you with valuable feedbac k but
also makes power users feel valued and involved in the development
process. Ask them to beta test new features and share their opinions.
Cross-Sell and Upsell:  Encoura ge power users to try additional products or
features. Track their activity to identify upsell opportunities, and offer them
relevant upgrades. For example, Dropbox prompts users to upgrade when
they run out of space, turning a necessity into an upsell opportunity .
Build Communities Around Your Product:  Create a community where
power users can share their experiences, learn from each other, and help
troubleshoot problems. Host virtual and in-person events, start a community
newsletter , and promote user-generated content. Notion, for instance, builds
a strong community by encoura ging users to share creative ways to use its
product.
Invest in Customer  Success Management:  Offer a hands-on, detailed
customer success experience that helps power users meet their goals.
Proactively address their needs and help them achieve their outcomes to
improve satisfaction and reduce churn.
Offer  Referral Programs:  Implement referral programs to incentivize
power users to bring in new customers. For example, Dropbox’ s referral
program rewards users with extra storage for every new user they refer,
encouraging existing users to spread the word.
Eliminate Frict ion in the Customer  Journey:  Make sure power users can
complete key tasks without any hassle. Provide step-by-step cues, offer help
and tips based on their role and usage, and create self-help widgets for
quick access to tutorials and guides.
Offer  Integration Opportunities First:  Provide power users with early
access to integration opportuniti es. Enable them to build plugins and third-
party connectors, and allow them to make money from their creations. This
not only enhances your product ’s ecosystem but also provides power users
with additional value and investment in your platform.
3. Time to Value
Time to Value (TTV) is a crucial metric that gauges how long it takes for a
new user to experience the core beneﬁts of your product after they sign up.
The quicker users realize this value, the more effective your onboarding
process is.
Consider the experience of buying something at a physical store . When you
purchase an item and exchange money for it, you get the product
immediately . The Time to Value in this scenario is zero because you
instantly receive  and start beneﬁting from the product you bought. This
immediate gratiﬁcation boosts satisfaction and reinforces the value of the
purchase.
Now let us say you offer a project management tool. If new users can
understand and start using key features like task creation, team
collaboration, and progress tracking within 10 minutes of signing up, you
are on the right track. This fast-onboarding experience helps users quickly
grasp how your product can enhance their productivity .
Users who experience value quickly are more likely to continue using
your product and explore its other features.
If users do not see the value of your product soon after signing up, they are
more likely to abandon it and look for alternatives.
Here, the value  is what you have promised the customer . This could be
increased revenue, saved time, reduced costs, enhanced experience, better
efﬁciency , or improved quality . Different customer segments will likely
have dif ferent tar gets and ideas about what they consider valuable.
Once the value has been determined for each customer segment, you can
work out on reducing the time customers should take to achieve this value
or reaching the ‘Aha’  moment. Driving early value is helping the customers
win early , this is the key .
Now let us quickly do a short dive into the types of TTV  that are usually
considered as measurements and see what ﬁts your product.
Immediate Time to Value (ITTV): When you visit a restaurant and look at
a restaurant menu, you immediately understand the options and can start
making a choice. Here you experience an instant beneﬁt. This is Immediate
Time to Value, similar to our physical store example earlier .
If your product has this ITTV  you have a greatest advantage and your
product could be easy to advertise
Time to Exceed Value (TTE V): Take the example of Netﬂix, you
subscribe to it primarily for access to a few favorite shows. Initially , you
enjoy watching those shows, which meets your immediate value
expectations. However , over time, you discover personalized
recommendations, exclusive content, and streaming across devices. These
additional features and beneﬁts exceed your initial expectations,  making the
service itself more valuable and compelling. This is Time to Exceed Value.
If your product  has this you may have to keep a close watch on your
customer behavior and keep ﬁnetuning the product. Also, produce
enormous value that customers would not expect.
Long Time to Value (LTTV ): People who run a custom software
development company may relate to this. The client needs to wait for a
considerably long time to see the value. This is due to complexity in the
process and custom software has speciﬁc needs. So LTTV  occurs when it
takes a while for customers to see the beneﬁts of a product
Usually for LTTV  products, the implementation process is time-consuming,
requiring extensive customization, data migration, and employ ee training.
In some cases where Advanced analytics and automated reporting are
involved, it may take a while to see the actual value.
Time to Basic Value (TTBV): TTBV  focuses on the duration it takes for a
customer to receive the speciﬁc  value they anticipated from a product or
service. Unlike overall Time to Value (TTV), which considers the complete
beneﬁts of the product, TTBV  assesses how quickly customer s attain the
speciﬁc value they were expecting.
let us imagine a language learning app like Duolingo that promises users
the ability to handle basic conversations in a new language within a week of
consistent usage. Upon signing up, users expect to start learning and
practicing basic vocabulary and phrases immediately .
Here the TTBV  is determined by how quickly users gain the ability to
engage in simple conversations after beginning their language learning
journey .
If your product has TTBV  it is easy manage customer expectations as there
could be a deﬁnitive time to see the value
Short Time to Value (STTV): STTV  refers to the brief duration between
customer engag ement and experiencing the value proposition of a product
or service. It represents the "sweet spot" in Time to Value (TTV), where
customers swiftly recognize the beneﬁts of a business offering shortly after
initial interaction.
Consider a food delivery service that promises customers to have their
favorite meal delivered within 30 minutes of placing an order . When a
customer installs the app and selects a meal, the STTV  begins. As soon as
the order is conﬁrmed, the delivery service initiates the proces s, aiming to
deliver the meal within the speciﬁed time frame. If the food arrives
promptly and meets the customer's expectations regarding taste and quality ,
the business achieves a short time to value.
If your product involves deliver ing a service, prioritize efﬁciency in service
delivery . Minimize any unnecessary steps or complications that might delay
users from experiencing value.
4. Featur e Usage
Feature usage tracks how many customers use speciﬁc features of your
product and how this usage changes over time. This metric is essential for
understanding which features are popular , which ones are underutilized, and
how effectively your onboarding process introduces these features to new
users.
High feature usage indicates that users are engaging with the product
deeply , which often correlates with higher satisfaction and retention rates.
One key goal of onboarding is to teach customers how to use your
product’ s features ef fectively .
Think about a social media management tool like Hootsuite. After
introducing a new feature that allows users to schedule Instagram Stories,
the team  monito rs how many users start using this feature. If there is a
noticeable uptick in usage after the feature is highlighted during
onboarding, it shows that the onboarding process is successfully driving
adoption of the new feature.
How to measure Feature Usage:
1. Measure how many users engag e with a feature immediately after
it is introduced.
2. Check if users continue to use the feature regularly over time.
3. Look for patterns where users stop using a feature, which could
indicate issues with usability or value.
Remember , if a feature is too complicated, users might try it once and then
abandon it. Users might not know the feature exists, especially if it is buried
within the app. Users may not see the beneﬁt of using the feature, or it
might not align with their needs.
If your product has multiple feature releases planned in the road map, you
can use some of below methods to drive better feature usage:
1. Send out email newsletters or in-app notiﬁcations highlightin g
new features and their beneﬁts.
2. Host live webinars or on-demand videos that demonstrate new
features in action.
3. Use interactive, in-app tutorials that guide users through new
features step-by-step.
4. Provide tooltips  and hints within the app that appear when users
ﬁrst encounter a new feature.
5. Encourage users to provide feedback on new features. 
Use analytics tools that have Heatmaps, session recordings, and user ﬂow
analysis to track feature usage and detailed insights into how users interact
with your product. This process can reveal where users drop off or which
features they frequently use. This data can inform improvements in the
onboarding process and highligh t which features need better explanation or
promotion.
Self-Evaluation

Chapter  2: Navigation and Information Architectur e
It takes 20 years to build a r eputation and ﬁve minutes to ruin it. If you
think about that, you will do things differ ently . - Warren Buffett
Navigation and information design are key in crafting people's e xperiences.
Picture entering a library with books tossed around randomly , and signs are
puzzling. Movin g around that area would be irritating and will waste a lot
of time.  Likewi se, if users cannot simply locate what they need in your
SaaS product, they will feel annoyed in no time and might even stop using
the product entirely .
What is navigation?
Navigation is how users get around your item. It is similar to the roadmap
and guideposts that lead them to their end goal. Effective navigation makes
sure users locate functionalities and details quickly and intuitively , without
being confused.
An efﬁcient navigation is easy to understand, direct, features explanatory
labels meaningful to the user , and maintains consistent navigation patterns
on all pages.
What is Information Architectur e?
Information Architecture (IA) works to arrange and order content so it
aligns with user understanding. It is similar to a librarian who meticulously
places books in a sensible order , making it simple for readers to locate the
book they want. In a SaaS product, IA includes classifying features and
data, identifying how various sections of the product connect, and creating a
logical progression.
An effective Information Architecture takes into account the thought
processes and goals of users.
It organizes functions and data into sensible groups. It constructs an
understandable layout that exhibits the connection between various sections
of the product.
Though similar , navigation and IA differ. Navigation regards the user's
journey within your product, whereas IA addresses the layout of the
product's conten t. Navigation looks into routes and user ﬂow, while IA
concentrates on the arrangement and order of the content.
Imagine IA  like a building's blueprint. Consider navigation like the
hallways and doors helping users travel within this building.
Why ar e Navigation and IA  important in SaaS?
In SaaS products, the importanc e of effective navigation and IA cannot be
overstated. Here is why:
1. User Satisfaction: When users can easily ﬁnd what they need,
they are happier and more likely to continue using the product.
2. Efﬁciency: Good navigation and IA  reduce the time it takes for
users to complete tasks, making them more productive.
3. Retention: If users ﬁnd your product easy to use, they are less
likely to churn and more likely to become long-term customers.
4. Onboarding: A clear and intuitive navigation helps new users get
up to speed quickly , improving their initial experience and
increasing the likelihood of retention.
Having glanced at the foundatio ns of Navigation and IA, it is time to delve
into standard issues linked with navigation and information architecture in
SaaS goods. We will also suggest strategies to ﬁx each problem:
Complex Navigation Structur es
Using a SaaS product should  be easy and direct. Yet, if menus are
overﬂowing with choices or set up in a puzzling format, users can feel
swamped. Think about rummaging through a kitchen drawer for a spoon,
but it is cramm ed with various tools in no order . Finding what you need
would take ages, and you would likely feel annoyed. This is similar to
navigating your product.
When people struggle to locate their needs swiftly , it gets on their nerves.
This annoyance also augments their cognitive burden - the mental energy
needed to operate the product. Consequently , they spend more time
ﬁnishing tasks, and may ﬁgure the product's trouble outweighs its beneﬁts,
contributing to diminished enjoyment and possible churn.
Wayfair ’s Long dr opdown menu
How to simplify complex navigation structur es?
Organize Functions Se nsibly:  Put similar functions in one bunch.
Imagine, you are creating a project handling tool, bunch features such as
'Tasks,' 'Calendar ,' and 'Notes' under a 'Project Management' section.
Instead of scattering 'T asks,' 'Subtasks,' and 'T ask Templates' throughout
various menus, gather them all under a 'T asks' section
Pick Straightforward Names: Names should be direct and easy to grasp.
Steer clear of technical terms or short forms that may puzzle users. Rather
than naming a menu option as 'PRJ MGMT ,' name it as 'Project
Management' so users instantly comprehend what it entails.
Use Organized Layers:  Start organizin g info using a structure that goes
from broad to speciﬁc. For exam ple, an online selling platform might have
a key category titled 'Products.' This could then grow to include
subcategories such as 'Electronics,' 'Apparel,' and 'Household Items.' Each
of these can then break down into even more particular goods.
Simplify Menu Layout:  Make sure users do not need to go through a lot of
submenus to get to what they want. Strive for an easy-to-navigate layout
where functions  are only a few clicks away . Avoid tucking away 'Order
History' under lots of tabs such as 'Account > Purchases > Orders >
History'. Instead, put it straight under 'Account' for fast retrieval.
Use Mega Menus:  When dealing with intricate products with many
features, think about using mega menus. They present all choices in one
view , eliminatin g the need for lots of clicks. This lets users look and locate
their wanted option quicker . The 'Content' category in a content
management system (CMS) can adopt a mega menu. It will show choices,
for instance, 'Add New Post,' 'Manage Categories,' 'Tags,' and 'Archives' in
one complete view .
Add a Sear ch Option:  Include  a solid search tool that enables users to
promptly locate features or details without exploring various menus. The
search tool should provide autocomplete ideas to assist users in improving
their searches. In a CRM system, a search box situated at the interface's top
could permit users to input "contact" and instantly receive suggestions like
"Add New Contact," "Contact List," or "Contact Settings.
Adaptive Navi gation:  Offer adaptive navigation choices that show
depending on the user's current job or display . This ﬂexible method lowers
mess and gives users with suitable choices when they need them and where.
For instance, in a design applica tion, when someone is modifying a picture,
the toolset could adaptively switch to display picture modiﬁcation tools like
'Cut,' 'Scale,' and 'Effects,' rather than ordinary tools that are not related to
the current job.
Gradual Unveiling: Use gradual unveiling tactics to initially present only
the most crucia l navigation choices, making more available as necessary .
This simpliﬁes the starting point and lets users focus on main jobs. In a
project organization tool, the chief dashboard might display regular project
tasks and deadlines, while complex options such as "Project Settings,"
"Team Management," and "Reporting" come into view through enlar ging
sections or extra clicks.
If at all there is a possibility , create a focus group of your interested
customers and perform a user testing.
1. Create a prototype of your navigation structure and have real
users test it. Observe where they struggle and what seem s
intuitive.
2. Collect feedback directly from users through surveys or
interviews. Ask them what they found confusing or easy to use.
3. Use the feedback to make iterative improvements. Small,
continuous adjustments based on user feedback can signiﬁcantly
enhance navigation over time.
Self-Evaluation
★      Is my SaaS product's navigation currently simple and intuitive, or do
users struggle to ﬁnd what they need?
★      Have I or ganized similar functions together to streamline navigation,
or are functionalities scattered across multiple menus?
★      Are the menu names and options straightforward and easily
understandable, or do they use technical terms that might confuse
users?
★      Does my menu structure follow a logical hierarchy from broad
categories to speciﬁc options, or is it disor ganized and difﬁcult to
navigate?
★      Have I minimized the number of clicks required for users to access
essential features, or are important functions buried deep within
submenus?
★      Have I considered using mega menus for complex products with
many features to provide a comprehensive view of choices at once?
★      Is there a robust search tool in place that allows users to quickly ﬁnd
features or information without extensive navigation?
★      Have I implemented adaptive navigation options that adjust based on
the user's current task or display , providing relevant choices when
needed?
★      Have I employed gradual unveiling tactics to initially present only
essential navigation choices, revealing more options as users progress?
★      Have I conducted user testing or gathered feedback from real users to
assess the ef fectiveness of my navigation structure?
★      Am I open to making iterative improvements to navigation based on
user feedback, ensuring continuous enhancement over time?
★      Have I considered creating a focus group of interested customers to
perform in-depth user testing and gather speciﬁc feedback on
navigation?
★      How do I plan to incorporate user feedback into the reﬁnement and
optimization of my product's navigation and information architecture?
Inconsistent Navigation Patterns
Inconsistencies in navigation styles, such as different menus on different
pages, can confuse users and hinder their ability to predict where to ﬁnd
information. For instance, if the main menu is on the top of the page on
some screens and on the side on others, users might struggle to locate
familiar options.
If the navigati on is not consistent, users could start doubting how
dependable the  product is. Their irritation might grow , spending more time
searching for features. The experience then becomes less enjoyable.
Because of these, users might not want to keep using the product. Chances
are, they might not suggest it to friends either .
Picture a project organization software. The main screen, task section, and
schedule area all have unique ways you navigate them. The main screen has
a toolbar at the top. The task section has a menu on the side. The schedule
area lets you navigate from within its own layout. A person who wants to
swap between these sections might ﬁnd it tricky and perplexin g. This can
lead to a feeling of disconnection.
Setting up regular navigation methods across the product is vital for a
smooth user journey . Follow these speciﬁc steps to accomplish this:
Design System  Documentatio n: Create a comprehensive design system
that includes guidelines for navigation elements. This documentation should
outline the placement, appearance, and behavior of menus, buttons, and
links. A design system might specify that the main navigation bar should
always be at the top of the page with speciﬁc sections like "Dashboard,"
"Tasks," "Calendar ," and "Settings."
Consistent Placement:  Ensure that key navigation elements are placed
consistently across all pages. Users should not have to hunt for the menu or
main options. If your application has a sidebar menu, it should appear in the
same position on every page, with the same hierarchy and options.
Predictable Behavior: Menus, buttons, and links should behave
predictably . Clicking a menu item should always lead to a page or section
related to that item, without unexpected detours. If clicking "Tasks" always
takes the user to a list of tasks, this should be the case regardless of where
the user is in the application.
Bigbasket’ s Footer Links that ar e not actually links
Visual Consistency: Use consistent colors, fonts, and styles for navigation
elements to reinforce their purpose and make them easily recognizable. All
primary navigat ion items might use a speciﬁc color and font size, while
secondary options use a dif ferent, but consistent, style.
Dynamic Breadcrumbs:  Implem ent dyna mic breadcrumbs that show users
their current location within the product. This helps users understand their
path and navigate back to previous sections easily . In an e-commerce
platform, bread crumbs like "Home > Electronics > Mobi le Phones >
Smartphones" help users trace their steps and return to higher -level
categories ef fortlessly .
User -Centric Navigation Customization:  Allow users to customize their
navigation expe rience according  to their preferences. Users should be able
to pin frequent ly used features or reorganize menu items to suit their
workﬂow . A software developm ent tool might let users drag and drop menu
items to create a personalized navigation bar, making it easie r to access
their most-used features quickly .
Think about a SaaS item such as a CRM (Customer Relationship
Management) software employed by sales groups. In the beginning, the
software utilized varied navigation models for the dashboard, handling
contacts, and sections for analyz ing trends. The dashboard featured a menu
at the top, the contact management system had a menu on the side, and
analysis sections contained tabs within the webpage.
Issues Identiﬁed:
●         Sales representatives struggled to switch between contacts and
analytics quickly .
●         New users found it challenging to locate the necessary features.
●         The inconsistent layout led to longer training times and reduc ed
efﬁciency .
Solutions Implemented:
●         Uniﬁed Top Navigation: The CRM team standardized the navigation
with a uniﬁed top navigation bar across all sections. This bar included
dropdown menus for "Dashboard," "Contacts," "Analytics,"
"Settings," and "Reports."

navigation bar across all sections.
●         This bar included dropdown menus for "Dashboard," "Contacts,"
"Analytics," "Settings," and "Reports."
●         Design System Creation: A design system was developed, detailing
navigation element styles, interactions, and placement rules. This
ensured that any new feature or section added in the future would
follow the same navigation principles.

interactions, and placement rules.
●         This ensured that any new feature or section added in the future
would follow the same navigation principles.
●         Consistent Icons and Labels: Icons and labels were standardized,
making them easily recognizable. For example, a user icon always
represented user settings, and a graph icon always led to analytics.
●         Icons and labels were standardized, making them easily recognizable.
●         For example, a user icon always represented user settings, and a
graph icon always led to analytics.
●         User Feedback Integration: After implementing these changes, the
team conducted user testing sessions and collected feedback.
Adjustments were made based on this feedback to further enhance
navigation clarity .
●         After implementing these changes, the team conducted user testing
sessions and collected feedback.
●         Adjustments were made based on this feedback to further enhance
navigation clarity .

Ambiguous or  Vague Labels
If button s have unclear or confusing labels, people may hold back from
clicking them. They are not sure about where they will end up. This doubt
can mean they miss out on impo rtant aspects or facts, leading to annoyance
and poor user experiences. As time goes on, these experiences can cut down
happiness levels and up the chances of people leaving the product.
For example, consider an educa tional platform where navigation labels like
"Modules," "Activities," and "Resources" are used. If "Modules" contains
lesson plans, "Activities" includes interactive exercises, and "Resources"
holds supplemen tary materials, students might not know where  to go ﬁrst.
This confusion can hinder their learning process and lead to unnecessary
frustration.
Vague or unclear labels for navigation features might cause
misunderstanding about the purpose or end of links. If users ﬁnd labels that
are not detailed or are tricky  to decode, they might have difﬁculty
maneuvering smoothly .
Dealing with unclear or confus ing labels involves using precise, easy to
understand language that truly represents each corresponding page's content
or purpose. The following are comprehensive directions to make that
happen:
Descriptive Labels:  Use labels that clearly describe the content or function
of the destination page. Avoid using jargon or overly broad terms that might
confuse users. For example, instead of "Resources," use more speciﬁc
labels like "Help Center ," "Downloadable Guides," or "T utorial Videos."
Intuitive Langu age: Ensure that the language used in labels is intuitive and
matches user expectations. Use terminology that is familiar to your target
audience. For an e-commerce site, use labels like "Shopping Cart" instead
of "Basket" if that is the more commonly understood term in your region.
Consistency Across the Product:  Ensure that labels are used consistently
throughout the product. This helps users learn and predict where to ﬁnd
information. If "Settings" is used to denote conﬁguration options on one
page, use "Settings" consistent ly in all other relevant areas, rather than
mixing in terms like "Preferences" or "Options."
Contextual Help: Provide contextual help or tooltips that explain the
function of navigation element s. This can assist users in understanding
labels without cluttering the interface. Include a small question mark icon
next to labels like "Dashboard"  that, when hovered over, provides a brief
description such as "V iew your activity summary and key metrics here."
Visual Cues:  Use visual cues such as icons alongside text labels to provide
additional conte xt and improve understanding. For example, pair a gear
icon with the label "Settings" to reinforce that this section involves
conﬁguration and options.

"Resources"?

★      Am I using terminology familiar to my tar get audience?
★      On an e-commerce site, am I using common terms like "Shopping
Cart" instead of less familiar ones like "Basket"?

using "Settings" consistently in all other relevant areas?

navigation elements?

descriptions when hovered over?
★      Am I using visual cues such as icons alongside text labels to provide
additional context and improve understanding?
★      Have I paired a gear icon with the label "Settings" to reinforce that
this section involves conﬁguration and options?
★      Are my labels speciﬁc and clearly indicating their purpose to avoid
ambiguity?
★      Am I using terminology that is familiar and expected by my tar get
audience?
★      Is there consistency in labeling across my product to help users
navigate smoothly?
Hidden or  Buried Featur es
If ﬁndin g key features becomes tough for users, their interest might dwindle
due to frustration. Users frequen tly miss vital features hidden in multi-level
menus. This greatly affects their capacity to use the product to its fullest. As
a result, their involvement decreases, they fail to recognize its beneﬁcial
aspects, and in the end, they could potentially stop using the product.
Adobe Photoshop's Hidden Treasur e Hunt
Adobe Photosho p is a well-known software used for editing images. It has
been around for a long time and has many features that can be used by both
beginners and experts. Howeve r, in older versions of Photosho p, like CS5
and earlier , some of the important features were hard to ﬁnd.
The problem was that Photoshop had so many powerful tools, but they were
hidden deep inside menus or needed special keyboard shortcuts to use. This
made it difﬁcult  for people, especially those who were new to the software,
to ﬁnd and use all the advanced features.
Because of this, users had a tough time discovering and learning about
everything Photo shop could do. They spent a lot of time searchi ng for tools
instead of being able to work quickly . Some users even got frustrated or lost
interest when they could not easily use the advanced features, making them
enjoy Photoshop less.
To ﬁx this, Adobe made some changes. In newer versions of Photoshop,
they redesigned the menus and layout to make features easier  to ﬁnd and
use. They also added helpful pop-up menus and tooltips that guided users to
commonly used tools. Additionally , they created better tutorials, help pages,
and community spaces to teach people how to use the hidden features.
As a result of these changes, Adobe made users happier  and more
productive with Photoshop. It helped Adobe stay competitive against other
image editing programs by making Photoshop suitable for users of all skill
levels. Adobe continues to improve Photoshop's design based on user
feedback to make it even better .
The main lesson s from this case study are that designers shou ld focus on
making features easy to ﬁnd and use if they want people to enjoy their
product. Listenin g to user feedba ck is crucial for knowing how to enhance a
product's design. Providing users with help and resources to better
understand the product is also very important.
In conclusion, Adobe's efforts to address the hidden featur es issue in
Photoshop demonstrate the signiﬁcance of making products user-friendly .
By doing so, companies can increase customer satisfaction and attract more
people to use their product.
To tackle the problem of concealed or overlooked aspect s, we need
thoughtful design and a focus on the user. We can boost user activity and
pack more punch into your product by making crucial features easier to ﬁnd
and use. Check out these practical answers:
Prioritize Key Featur es: Identify and prioritize key features that are
crucial for users to achieve their goals. These features should be
prominently placed in the main  navigation or the dashboard. For a project
management tool, features like "Create New Project," "Task Management,"
and "Team Collaboration" should be easily accessible from the main
navigation bar. This ensures that users can quickly ﬁnd and use these core
functions without having to dig through multiple submenus.
User -Driven Customization:  Allow users to customize their navigation
experience by pinning or highlighting frequently used features . Provide a
customizable dashboard where users can add shortcuts to their most-used
features. This way, users can tailor the interface to their needs, ensuring that
the tools they use most often are always within easy reach.
Contextual Menus: Use contextual menus that appe ar only when relevant.
This helps keep the interface clean while ensuring that users have access to
the features they need when they need them. In a photo editing app,
advanced editing  tools could be hidden in a context menu that appears when
a user selects an image to edit. This keeps the interface uncluttered while
still providing access to powerful features.
Featur e Spotlighting:  Implement a feature spotlighting system that
highlights new or important features to users as they navigate through the
product. When a new feature is added, use a pop-up tutorial or a highlighted
icon to draw attention to it. For instance, a new "Analytics Dashboard"
feature in a marketing platform could be spotlighted with a brief tutorial
that shows users how to access and use it.
Sear ch Functionality:  Implement a robust search function that allows
users to quickly ﬁnd features and tools by typing in keywords. A search bar
in an enterprise software applic ation could allow users to type in "create
report" and directly navigate to the report creation tool, bypassing complex
menus.
Progressive Disclosur e: Use progressive disclosure to reveal features as
users become more familiar with the product. For new users of a graphic
design tool, initially display only basic editing tools. As users become more
proﬁcient, gradually introduce more advanced features through guided
tutorials or notiﬁcations.
Interactive Help and Tooltips:  Provide interactive help and tooltips that
guide users to discover and use hidden features. When a user hovers over a
particular sectio n of a dashboard, tooltips can pop up to highlight and
explain hidden features. An interactive help guide can also walk users
through ﬁnding these features step-by-step.
User  Behavior  Analytics:  Use analytics to track user beha vior and identify
which features are underutilized. Based on this data, adjust the navigation to
make these features more prominent. If analytics reveal that users rarely use
a valuab le reporting feature because it is buried in submenus, consider
moving it to the main navigation bar or adding a shortcut on the dashboard.

users become more familiar with the product?

features?

Lack of Sear ch Functionality
When there is no good search feature, it gets tough for users to quickly ﬁnd
the right info or features in a product. This can make people frustrated,
lower how well it works, and give a bad impression of how easy the product
is to use. When  users struggle to simply ﬁnd what they need, they might
feel overwhelm ed and not happy . This might make them interact less or
even completely reject the product.
Here are some search-based solutions to make the user experience less
challenging:
Implement a Powerful Sear ch Bar:  A searc h bar should be promin ently
placed and easy to access on every page of the application. It should be
capable of handling complex queries and returning relevant results.
Think about a web-based learn ing platform. In it, people have to locate
particular classes, study module s, or materials. Without any search feature,
they would have to move throug h several pages and types. This could take a
lot of time and cause annoyance.
Put a search box on the dashboard's top. It lets users enter keywords about
courses, lessons, or subjects. This search feature should also understand
parts of keywords and usual typos to make the user's experience better .
Autocomplete Suggestions: Improve your site's search functionality by
providing instant suggestions while users type. This feature aids users in
rapidly locating what they want by presenting pertinent results based on
their typed phrase. Imagine a shopper on an online store starts entering
"sneakers" in the search ﬁeld. As they type, the search box gives instant
suggestions like "sneakers for men", "sneakers for women", "sneakers on
discount", and so on. This allow s users to reﬁne their search faster and ﬁnd
the items they want more swiftly .
Advanced Filters and Sortin g Options: Put in complex ﬁlters and
arrangement choices to aid users in honing their search outcome s. They can
use dif ferent standards like date, importance, cost, or group.
Within a project management tool, users often look for tasks or projects.
However , they get drowned in an excess of results. Include sorting options
so users  can organize tasks based on deadline, importance, project type, or
allocated team. Thus, users can promptly locate the particular tasks they
want without sorting through unrelated results.
Integrated Analytics:  With integrated analytics, monitor and scrutinize
typical search inquiries and user engagement with the search feature. This
information can aid in enhan cing and bettering the search experience
progressively .
Within a software product's knowledge base, users usually seek out guides
to ﬁx issues, but ﬁnding the most pertinent articles can be difﬁcult. Check
search queries to spot frequent ly searched words. This ensures the most
useful articles are ﬁne-tuned to show up ﬁrst in search results. Consistently
look over analytics to tweak and enhance the rankings of search results and
relevance of content.
Context-A ware Sear ch: Implement context-aware search functionality that
adapts results based on the user’s current context or location within the
application.
In a customer relationship management (CRM) system, people often look
for information like contact info, messages, or reminders. Suppose a person
is looking at a particular client's details. In that case, the search tool may
give priority to displaying data tied to that client, like recent
communications, emails, or reminders. This method, context-aw are search,
helps users locate pertinent data more quickly .
Natural Language Processing (NLP):  Use Natural Language Processing
(NLP) so people can use more everyday words in their search queries. This
can make their searching better .
Take the example of a HR system for managing human resources, people
may want to locate rules, reports, or staff records. Let people type in
questions such as "Display the vacation rule" or "Look up John Doe's most
recent job assessment." The search, boosted by natural language
understanding, can make sense of these questions and give accurate
answers. It makes looking for particular papers or data simpler for its users.
Sear ch Result Previews:  Show previews of search ﬁndings to aid users in
swiftly ﬁguring out if a result matters without the need to browse numerous
pages.
In content management syste ms, people search for speciﬁc items or
documents. When presenting search results, give a brief preview or a small
glimpse of what is inside - maybe the initial lines of an article or crucial
points from a document. This helps people quickly decide whether each
result is key .
Faceted Sear ch: Faceted search is handy . It lets users tweak their search
results. They can use several categories or dimensions at once for this.
When shopping  online, people sometimes feel swamped by too many
product choices.  Incorporating a faceted search helps. This allows shoppers
to ﬁlter results by type, cost bracket, brand, rating, and more . For example,
if someo ne wants a "laptop", they could reﬁne the search to show only
options from a particular price band and brand. This can greatly improve
the shopping experience.
Personalized Sear ch Results:  Customize search results based on
individual user preferences and past behavior to provide a more
personalized experience.
On a streaming platform, people  search for movies or TV shows. Adjust the
results according to what the person has seen before, their reviews, and
what they have watched. let us say a viewer often watches science ﬁction
ﬁlms. Then, put forward new and popular science ﬁction ﬁlms in what you
show them in their search results.
Voice Sear ch Integration:  Add a feature for voice search. This will let
people search using vocal commands. it is particularly handy for those
using mobile devices.
In a navigation app, users need to search for directions or locations while
driving. Integra te voice search so users can speak their queries, such as
"Find the nearest gas station" or "Navigate to 123 Main Street," without
needing to type. This feature enhances safety and convenience for users on
the go.

★      Do you provide visual cues or highlights for new or important search
results to draw user attention?
★      Are there mechanisms in place to prevent spam or irrelevant content
from appearing in search results?
★      Do you support multiple languages in your search functionality to
cater to a diverse user base?
Ignoring Mobile Responsiveness
Nowadays, being mobile-friendl y is not just nice to have; it is a must-have.
More and more, people are using their smartphones and tablets to use
software-as-a-service  (SaaS) products. If these are not optimized for
mobile, it can badly affect how users experience and engage  with them.
This section dives into a frequent misstep: overlooking mobile-friendliness.
It also of fers practical ways to not make that error .
If SaaS tools do not focus on mobile-friendliness, problems can crop up.
Menus that function ﬂawlessly  on a computer can become messy and
impractical on tinier screens. Buttons made for mouse usage might be too
tiny for touch use, and the total designs may not adjust well to different
mobile gadgets.
Think about our example projec t management tool, TaskMaster . It was ﬁrst
made for desktop users. TaskM aster stood out because of its solid features
and easy-to-use interface. But, as more people started using mobiles, the
team saw that many users were using the platform on their phones and
iPads. Despite this change, TaskMaster's mobile performance was weak.
This led to many serious problems.
When mobile responsiveness is overlooked, it makes simple tasks tough for
users. This can lead to notable annoyance. People using mobile spend less
time on the platform. They also do not use its features much. Many users,
upset with the mobile experience, leave the product. They prefer
competitors who are more tuned-in to mobile needs.
For resolving such problems, teams handling SaaS products should utilize
numerous vital techniques. This to make their navigation tools amiable for
mobile users and easily reachable:
Design Touch-Friendly Navigation
Ensuring navigation elements are touch-friendly is crucial. This means
designing interactive pieces such as buttons, links, and menus, to be
effortlessly tapped on touchscree n devices. Now , let us explore the methods
to create navigation that is easy on touchscreens ef fectively:
Appr opriate Size for Touch Targets: Small buttons and links can be hard
to hit correctly , causing users to become annoyed and make mistakes. Make
sure all touch spots are big enough to tap comfortably with a ﬁnger . A
common guideli ne is to have touch spots be no smaller than 44x44 pixels.
This size ﬁts an average ﬁnger and reduces accidental taps.
In our example, TaskMaster ’s buttons were 20x20 pixels, making them hard
to tap. Later buttons were resized to 44x44 pixels, improving tap accuracy
and user satisfaction.
Adequate Spacing Between Elements: When elements are too close
together , accidental taps can happen, making users upset. Make sure there is
enough room between things you can touch to stop wrong clicks. This room
helps the site be easier to use and makes it look better overall.
If TaskMaster ’s navigation links were densely packed, causing frequent
mistaps, increased spacing betw een links ensures each tap was intentional
and precise.
Intuitive Gestur es: People might be confused about using the interface if
the gestures are not straightfor ward. Include simple gestures  that match
usual behaviors of mobile users, like swiping, pinching, and holding down
for a while. These actions should seem like second nature and align with
other mobile apps.
Consistent Navigation Patterns:  Users can get confused if navigation
patterns are inconsistent, makin g things more complex. Make sure the app
has the same navigation pattern all through. Icons and placemen ts should be
easy to recognize, and navigation should behave as expected.
Let us say TaskMaster had different navigation styles in different sections,
confusing users. The solutions could be Standardized navigation patterns
across the app, with consistent iconography and placements.
Interaction Feedback:  When there is no feedback for interactions, an app
can seem inactive, and users might doubt if their inputs were even noticed.
Provide immediate visual feedback for touch interactions. This can include
highlighting buttons when tapped, showing loading indicators, and offering
haptic feedback where possible.
If your buttons did not change when tapped, making it unclear if the action
was recognized. Implementing visual feedback (e.g., color change) and
haptic feedback for touch interactions, would enhance user conﬁdence.
Optimize Layout for  Smaller  Screens
Designing layou ts for small screens is more than just an extra; it is a
necessity . Users  frequently interact with SaaS products using phones and
tablets. If layouts do not adjust for these devices, it can badly impact user
interaction and involvement. This part explores the intricacies of creating
layouts for small screens. It also provides useful solutions to avoid common
pitfalls.
Accessing SaaS products meant for desktops on mobile devices can lead to
a messy and bewildering display. Things that ﬁt well on a big screen
monitor look crowded on a phon e screen. This makes it tough to locate and
use critical functions.
To addre ss these issues, SaaS product teams must implement key strategies
to optimize layouts for smaller screens ef fectively:
1. Responsive Design Principle s: Static layouts do not work well on every
screen, leading to a not-so-gre at user experience. Use responsive design
techniques that let your designs change smoothly for different screen sizes.
You must use changing grids, adjustable images, and media queries. This
will create a lively , adaptive design.
Let us say TaskMaster used a ﬁxed-width layout that did not adapt to
smaller screens . Then they should adopt a responsive grid system that
adjusted elements based on screen size, providing a consistent experience
across devices.
2. Simpliﬁed Navigation Menus: Menus made for desktop can be
confusing on mobile devices. Simplify navigation by using mobile-friendly
formats like hambur ger menus or tab interfaces. These methods help
condense complex menus into accessible and user -friendly choices.
For exam ple, if your navigation bar displayed all options in a horizontal
line, causing clutter on mobile screens. You can implement a hambur ger
menu that neatly tucked away options while still being easily accessible.
3. Content Prioritization: Equally displaying all content elements can
overwhelm users on smaller screens. Prioritize essential content and
features that users need most frequently . Place critical information and
primary actions in prominent positions, making them easily accessible
without excessive scrolling.
4. Adaptive and Fluid Layouts: Fixed elements do not reﬂow
appropriately on different screen sizes, leading to a disjointed experience.
Use adaptive and ﬂuid layouts that automatically adjust eleme nt sizes and
positions based on screen dimensions. This approach ensures a seamless
and cohesive user experience across all devices.
Conduct Thor ough Mobile Testing
Conducting thorough mobile testing is essential to ensure that your SaaS
product delivers a seamless and satisfying user experience across a wide
range of devices. This process involves identifying and addressing issues
speciﬁc to mobile interfaces,  ensuring consistency , and continuously
reﬁning the user experience based on real-world usage and feedback. Here
is a deeper dive into the steps and strategies involved in effective mobile
testing:
1. Diverse Device Coverage: Different mobile devices have varying screen
sizes, resolutions, and operating systems, which can lead to inconsistencies
in how your product is displayed and used.
Test your product across a wide range of smartphones and tablets to ensure
compatibility and a consistent user experience. This includes devices with
different screen sizes, resolutions, and operating systems (iOS, Android,
etc.).
2. Func tional Testing: Mobile interfaces often have unique features, such
as touch  gesture s and sensors, that need to be tested to ensure they work
correctly .
Conduct functio nal testing to verify that all interactive elements and
features work as intended on mobile devices. This includes testing touch
interactions, form inputs, navigation menus, and any device-speciﬁc
functionalities like GPS or camera access.
3. Usability Testing: Even if the app functions correctly , it may still be
difﬁcult to use due to poor design or layout issues speciﬁc to mobile
devices.
Perform usability testing to observe real users interacting with the product
on mobile devices. This helps identify areas where users struggle, allowing
you to make design adjustments to improve the overall user experience.
4. Perfo rmance Testing: Mobile devices often have less processing power
and memory compared to deskt ops, which can impact the performance of
your app.
Conduct performance testing to ensure your app runs smoothly on various
mobile devices. This includes testing load times, responsiveness, and
overall performance under dif ferent network conditions.

Inconsistent Terminology and Language
When making SaaS products, it is vital to use steady terms and language.
This helps users  use and get the product with no troubles. This part talks
about the problems from not using steady language. It shares what can go
wrong for the user , and how to make language that makes sense together .
When distinct sections of a product use different names for the same actions
or ideas , it can puzzle users. Take for instance, a part of an app labels a
feature as "Proje cts", but somewhere else, it is referred to as "Tasks". Users
could ﬁnd it hard to realize they mean the same. Inconsistency like this may
also reach tooltips, error notes, and guide manuals, making the puzzlement
worse.
Think about a software used for managing projects. In this software, the
primary navigat ion menu has a segment labelled as "Projects." However ,
the guide documentation uses the term "Workstreams" for the same section.
Further , the pointers or tooltips label individual elements in this section as
"Tasks." This lack of consisten cy confuses users. They wonder if these
terms mean the same thing or represent distinct ideas.
Micr osoft Teams Inconsistent Naming
Microsoft Teams is a popular collaboration platform that integrates various
communication tools such as chat, video conferencing, and ﬁle sharing.
Initially , the platform faced several challenges related to inconsistent
terminology and language, which impacted user experience and adoption
rates.
One major challenge was inconsistent naming within the platform. For
example, early versions of Micr osoft Teams used the terms "Channels" and
"Conversations" interchangeably in different parts of the interface. This
inconsistency made it difﬁcult for users to understand the relationship
between the two and how to effectively use them. Similarly , the platform
referred to collaborative spaces as "Teams" in some instances while other
parts of the interface and documentation called them "Groups".  This led to
confusion, especially for users transitioning from other Microsoft products
like Outlook, where "Groups" had a dif ferent connotation.
Another issue arose from varying terminology across integrations with
other Microsoft products such as OneDrive, SharePoint, and Planner . Each
product had its own set of terminologies, and the lack of a uniﬁed language
strategy across these integrations caused further confusion. For example,
ﬁle storage locations were refer red to differently in Teams, OneDrive, and
SharePoint, leading to misunderstandings about where ﬁles were actually
stored and how to access them.
Additionally , tooltips and error messages often used different terms for the
same actions or features. For instance, a tooltip might refer to "starting a
meeting," while an error message might mention "initiating a call," even
though both actions were the same.
These inconsistencies led to signiﬁcant user frustration. Users reported
feeling overwhelmed due to the conﬂicting terminology , which often
resulted in decreased producti vity as they spent more time trying to
understand the interface rather than completing their tasks.
To addre ss these issues, Microsoft undertook a comprehensive review of the
language used across Teams and its integrations. They standardized terms to
ensure consistency , such as consistently referring to "Channels" as
"Channels" across all sections of the platform, and uniformly using the term
"Teams" instead of "Groups".
A uniﬁe d language strategy was developed in collaboration with the teams
responsible for other Microsoft products like OneDrive and SharePoint.
This strategy ensured that all products used the same terminology for shared
features and actions, providing a seamless user experience.
Microsoft also updated its user guides, help articles, and training materials
to reﬂec t the standardized terminology . Tooltips and error messages were
revised to ensure they used consistent language.
To address these issues, it is essential to implement strategies that ensure
consistent terminology and language throughout the product:
1. Establish a Uniﬁed Vocabulary
Many teams or docume nts often  give different names to the same tasks or
positions. Create  a shared langu age that explains all parts, duties, and ideas
related to the subject. Everyone involved should agree to this language. It
needs to be clearly documented for use in the future.
2. Cr eate a Style Guide
When writing is not consistent, it can lead to different ways of expressing
things in a product. Create a full style guide. This will explain the best
words, tone, and way of writing for everything within the product. It should
detail things like menus, helpful hints, problem notices, help guides,
promoting items, and more.
3. Consistency Across All Channels
Different terms across the product and support resources can be confusing.
it is important to always use the same words and style guide in all parts of
the product. This includes menus, tips, error pop-ups, and guides. Regular
checks ensure everything stays uniform.
4. Solicit User  Feedback
Actively seek feedback from users to discover areas where words may be
unclear or bafﬂing. Use these reactions to reﬁne the word selection and
style guide, assuring simplicity and easy understanding.
5. Regular  Reviews and Updates
Words and terms can change as the product develops. Plan routine checks
of the language and style guide to conﬁrm they are still current and
complete. Adju st them as the product changes to keep evenness and
clearness.

Inefﬁcient Cr oss-Linking Between Featur es
The ability for users to seamlessly switch between related tasks and sections
can enhance work efﬁciency and productivity . Many SaaS products do not
meet this standard, resulting in frustrated users and bad interactions. Picture
using a project management tool requiring you to continuously navigate
unnecessary complexities to modify a chore, overview the project
progression, or interact with colleagues. This absence of smooth transitions
can drastically impede your work process. Let us delve into widespread
issues tied to inefﬁcient interconnections between features, and discover
applicable solutions to create a more integrated and instinctive user
interaction. Facilitating navigation and uniformly blending features is
imperative in ensuring a ﬂuid user experience in SaaS based products.
JIRA  by Atlassian
JIRA  by Atlassian is a widely-u sed project management and issue tracking
tool, popular among software development teams for tracking bugs, tasks,
and project progress. Despite its widespread adoption, JIRA  has faced
criticism for inefﬁcient cross-linking between features, leading to a
suboptimal user experience.
One of the signiﬁcant challenges JIRA  users encountered was complex
navigation. Users had to navigate through a dense and intricate hierarchy of
menus to switch between tasks, backlogs, sprints, and other project
management elements. This complexity increased the time and effort
required to perform simple actions, resulting in frustration and decreased
productivity . For instance, transitioning from a task detail view to the sprint
backlog often required several clicks and navigation through multiple
pages.
Another challenge was the lack of contextual integration. Key features
within JIRA  were not contextually integrated, forcing users to manually
search for related elements. For example, while viewing a task, users could
not direc tly access related sprint or project data without manually searching
for these elements. This repetitive context-switching interrupted the ﬂow of
work and led to inefﬁciencies. The absence of direct links betw een related
features meant users had to remember where relevant information was
located and navigate there independently .
Additionally , JIRA  suffered from inconsistent user interfaces across
different sections. The interface for task management was different from the
sprint planning interface, which  made it difﬁcult for users to maintain a
consistent workﬂow . This inconsistency confused users and increased the
time required to become proﬁci ent with the tool. New users, in particular ,
found it challenging to navigate between dif ferent features efﬁciently .
To address these issues, Atlassian introduced several key improvements.
First, they streamlined navigation by implementing a more intuitive system
with a uniﬁed menu that allowed users to switch between key features more
easily . They introduced a sideba r providing quick access to frequently used
sections like projects, boards, and issues. This change reduced the number
of clicks and time required to move between related tasks, signiﬁcantly
enhancing user productivity .
Next, Atlassian worked on better contextual linking and integration of
features. When viewing a task, users could now see and navigate to related
sprints, backlog s, and team communications directly from the task view .
This integration provided a smoother workﬂow , as users could access all
relevant information and tools without leaving the current context. This
minimized disruptions and improved overall efﬁciency .
Finally , Atlassian undertook a UI overhaul to ensure consistency across
different features. They standardized the design language and interaction
patterns throughout the platform. This consistent interface reduced the
learning curve and made it easier for users to transition between different
parts of the application. The uniformity helped in maintainin g a steady
workﬂow and improved user satisfaction.
JIRA  Issue T ypes Conﬁguration
Here are several solutions with detailed explanations and examples:
Implement Contextual Links:
Integrate contextual links within each feature or module, allowing users to
quickly access related functionality without having to navigate through
multiple menus.
For exam ple, users working on a task need to update project timelines but
ﬁnd it cumberso me to switch between the task view and the project timeline
view . To solve this, add contextual links within the task view that allow
users to directly access and update the project timeline. For instance, a link
labeled "Update Timeline" can take users from the task details page to the
project timeline view , pre-ﬁltered to the relevant task.
Cross-Refer ence Related Featur es:
Cross-reference related features and modules to ensure users can easily
navigate between interconnected functionalities. Users might ﬁnd it difﬁcult
to switch betwee n parts like project tasks and team chat rooms. This could
cause uncoordin ated teamwork. You can connect project tasks and team
chat rooms more closely with each other .
For exam ple, include a "Discuss Task" button within the task details page
that opens the relevant discussion thread in the team chat module. Similarly ,
allow users to link speciﬁc chat messages to tasks, creating a seamless
navigation experience.
Design Inter connected Workﬂows:
Design workﬂows with interconnected pathways that intuitively guide users
through related tasks and features. let us say users managin g a project
timeline need to assign tasks to team members but ﬁnd it difﬁcult to
navigate between the timeline and team management views. In order to
solve this, you can create interconnected workﬂows that guide users
through the process.
For example, when users create a new task in the project timeline, provide
an option to assign the task to a team member directly within the timeline
view . Additionally , include links within the team management view that
allow users to quickly access and update tasks assigned to team members.
Uniﬁed Dashboards:
Develop uniﬁed dashboards that consolidate related features and provide an
overview of interconnected functionalities. Sometimes users ﬁnd it time-
consuming to switch between different modules for example, project
progress, task statuses, and team performance.
For that you can design a uniﬁed dashboard that provides an overview of
project progress, task statuses, and team performance in a single view .
Include interactive elements that allow users to drill down into speciﬁc
tasks, update project timelines, and communicate with team members
directly from the dashboard.
Tooltips and Inline Help:
Use tooltips and inline help to provide guidance on navigating between
related features . When Users are unsure how to access related
functionalities when working within a speciﬁc module. Implement tooltips
and inline help that guide users on how to navigate betw een related
features.
For insta nce, when users hover over a task in the project timeli ne, a tooltip
can display options for assigning the task, updating the timeline , or opening
the task discussion thread. This provides users with clear navigation options
without requiring them to leave their current view .

in one view?

between related features?

leaving their current context?

features?
★      Are workﬂows designed to reduce the need for excessive context-
switching?

of your product?
★      Is there a consistent design language and interaction pattern
throughout your platform?

your product?
