★      Have I received any feedback from users about difﬁculties in
navigating my interface?
★      Are there real-world examples of ef fective visual hierarchy that I can
draw inspiration from?
Using Stock Images Inappr opriately
Stock images are a convenient  resource for many designers due to their
availability and variety . Howeve r, over-reliance on these images can result
in a generic and unoriginal appearance. This happens when stock images
are chosen without considerat ion for brand identity or user relevance,
leading to a disjointed visual experience that fails to engage users.
Why ar e stock images commonly used in web design?
Cost-effective: Cheaper than custom photography or illustration
Stock images are signiﬁcantly less expensive than commissioning custom
photography or illustrations. Here is why: You do not need to hire
photographers, models, or rent equipment and studio space. Many stock
image sites offer subscriptions or bulk purchase options, furth er reducing
per-image costs.  Stock images are typically ready to use, elimina ting editing
and retouching costs.
For example, a custom photo shoot for a website might cost several
thousand dollars, while a monthly subscription to a stock imag e site could
be as low as $29-$249, providing access to millions of images.
Time-saving: Readily available for quick implementation
Stock images offer immediate  solutions, which is crucial in fast-paced
digital environments: Images can be downloaded and used immediately
after purchase. Unlike custom  shoots, tHere is no need to coordinate
schedules of photographers, models, and locations. Many stock images are
designed to be easily customizable, allowing for rapid modiﬁcations to ﬁt
speciﬁc needs. Using stock images can signiﬁcantly reduce the time from
concept to ﬁnal product launch. For instance, ﬁnding and implementing a
suitable stock image might take an hour, compared to days or weeks for
organizing and executing a custom photoshoot.
Variety:  Offer a wide range of options for dif ferent needs
Stock image libraries provide an extensive selection of visuals: From
business to nature, technology to lifestyle, stock libraries cover virtually
every topic. Options include photos, illustrations, vectors, and even video
footage. Many libraries now focus on providing images representing
various ethnicit ies, ages, and cultures. Stock sites often update their
collections to include images related to current events and trends. Even
specialized or unusual subjects often have multiple options available.
For example, Shutterstock, a popular stock image site, boasts over 390
million images in its library as of 2023, with thousands of new images
added daily .
Consider a small startup creatin g its ﬁrst website. With a limited budget of
$5,000 for the entire site, allocating $2,000-$3,000 for custom photography
would be a signiﬁcant portion of their budget. Instead, they might opt for a
$49/month stock image subscrip tion, allowing them to access thousands of
professional images for their site, saving both money and time.
However , it is important to note that while stock images offer these beneﬁts,
their overuse or inappropriate use can lead to the problems as follows.
Generic Appearance: Stock images often lack uniqueness and can make a
product look similar to countless  other products that use the same or similar
images. The overuse of common stock images can dilute the visual identity
of a brand, making it harder for users to distinguish it from competitors.
Lack of Authenticity: Stock images might not accura tely represent the
brand’ s values, culture, or target audience. Users can usually spot stock
images, and this can come across as insincere or lazy, reducing the brand’ s
authenticity .
Inconsistency: Different stock images can vary signiﬁcantly in style, tone,
and quality , leading to a visually inconsistent experience. This
inconsistency can confuse users and make the product feel disjointed.
Decr eased Trust: Users may perceive the produ ct as unprofessional or
hastily put together , which can undermine trust. Lack of original imagery
can make users question the ef fort and care put into the product.
Reduced Engagement: Generic images fail to capture users' interest or
resonate with their experiences, leading to lower engagement. Users are less
likely to form an emotional connection with a product that feels impersonal
and unoriginal.
Brand Dilution: The unique attributes and personality of the brand can be
overshadowed by the generic nature of stock images. This dilution makes it
challenging for the brand to stand out in a crowded market.
Allocate Budget for Professional Photography: Hire professional
photographers to create custom images that reﬂect the brand’ s unique
identity and values. For exampl e, a ﬁtness app could use custom photos of
real users in action, showcasing diverse body types and ﬁtness levels.
Ensure that the style, tone, and quality of the images are consistent with the
brand’ s overall aesthetic. This consistency helps in creating a cohesive and
recognizable visual identity .
Use Authentic and Relevant Images:  Use images that reﬂect real users
and their experiences. Authent ic images of actual customers using the
product can build trust and relatability . For instance, a local bakery could
feature photos of its customers enjoying freshly baked goods. Select images
that are directly relevant to the content and context. Avoid generic images
that do not add value or context to the content they accompany .
Create a Custom Image Libra ry: Develop a library of custom images that
can be reused across differen t touchpoints. This helps maintain visual
consistency and ensures that images are always on-brand. Regularly update
the image library to keep the visuals fresh and relevant. Seasonal updates or
new product launches can be great opportunities to refresh the imagery .
Airbnb excels in using custom imagery . The platform features high-quality
photos of actual properties listed by hosts, providing an authentic glimpse
into what users can expect. The images are taken with a consistent style,
ensuring that the platform maintains a cohesive look and feel.
Airbnb’ s image guidelines for hosts ensure that photos are clear , well-lit,
and representative of the space, contributing to a professional and
trustworthy user experience.
A corporate website that uses cliché stock photos of smiling business people
shaking hands can feel impersonal and uninspired. These images often fail
to communicate the company’ s unique culture or value proposition.
Users may perceive the company as lacking originality and authenticity ,
leading to decreased trust and engagement. The website’ s visual identity
does not stand out, making it forgettable among countless similar -looking
sites.
Here are some tips to overcome these pitfalls:
Brand Identity:  Deeply understand the brand’ s identity , values, and
personality . This understanding will guide the creation of custom images
that authentically represent the brand.
User  Personas:  Develo p detailed user personas to understand the target
audience’ s preferences, needs, and experiences. Use these insig hts to create
imagery that resonates with users.
Creative Direction:  Work with photographers to plan photo shoots that
capture the essence of the brand . Provide clear creative direction to ensure
the images align with the brand’ s visual and emotional tone.
Diverse Scenar ios: Capture a varie ty of scenarios and contexts that reﬂect
how users interact with the product in real life. This variety adds depth and
relatability to the imagery .
Authentic Contributions:  Encourage users to share their own photos of
using the product. User-generat ed content can add authenticity and show
real-life applications of the product.
Incorporate Feedback:  Use feedback from users to reﬁne and improve the
imagery . Unders tanding what resonates with users helps in creating visuals
that better connect with the audience.
Self-Evaluation
★      Do you prioritize establishing a clear visual hierarchy in your product
design?
★      Are you aware of how a lack of visual hierarchy can lead to user
confusion and inefﬁciency?
★      Have you experienced challenges with users struggling to ﬁnd key
information or complete tasks efﬁciently due to a cluttered interface?
★      Do you recognize the importance of using size to indicate the
importance of elements in your design?
★      Are you utilizing proportional sizing to guide users' attention to key
actions or calls to action (CT As)?
★      Do you understand how high-contrast colors and consistent color
schemes can help create a clear visual hierarchy?
★      Are you using color ef fectively to make important elements stand out
and indicate their importance?
★      Have you considered the impact of whitespace on reducing visual
clutter and highlighting key elements?
★      Are you grouping related elements together and using spacing to
separate dif ferent sections in your design?
★      Do you understand the role of typography in creating a typographic
hierarchy and guiding users through content?
★      Are you using dif ferent font sizes, weights, and styles to dif ferentiate
between headings, subheadings, and body text?
★      Have you evaluated the readability of your chosen fonts and their
appropriateness for the context?
★      Do you recognize the beneﬁts of a clear visual hierarchy in improving
user experience and engagement?
★      Are you monitoring user feedback and behavior to identify areas
where visual hierarchy improvements are needed?
★      Have you conducted usability testing to assess the ef fectiveness of
your visual hierarchy in guiding users through the interface?
★      Do you have a strategy in place for continuously reﬁning and
optimizing the visual hierarchy of your product?
★      Are you aware of industry best practices and design principles related
to visual hierarchy , and are you implementing them in your designs?
Overdesigning
When you are designing your product, it is tempting to add all sorts of
ﬂashy elements and fancy featu res to make it look super impressive. But
Here is the thing : just like with overpacking, overdesigning can actually do
more harm than good. When you stuff your product full of visual elements
that do not really serve a clear purpose, you end up with a cluttered,
confusing mess. It is like trying to navigate through a maze of ﬂashing neon
signs – you do not know where to look or what to focus on!
All that visual clutter can be really overwhelming for your users. Instead of
being able to focus on the impo rtant stuff, like actually using your product,
they get distracted by all the unnecessary bells and whistles. it is like trying
to read a book while someone's waving a sparkly banner in your face – not
exactly easy to concentrate, right?
And when users  get overwhelmed and distracted, they are more likely to get
frustrated and give up on your product altogether . That is deﬁnitely not
what you want!
Myspace's Decline (2008-201 1)
Myspace, once the dominant social networking platform, provides a
compelling example of how overdesigning and visual clutter can contribute
to a product's downfall. Myspac e was founded in 2003 and quickly became
the most popula r social networking site in the world. By 2006, it was the
most visited website in the United States, surpassing Google.
As Myspace grew , it allowed users increasingly more control over their
proﬁle designs. This led to several issues: Users could modify their proﬁle's
HTML  and CSS, leading to wildly different designs across the platform. As
a result, many proﬁles became cluttere d with ﬂashing graphics, auto-
playing music, and garish color schemes. The lack of design standards
meant that navigating from one proﬁle to another could be jarring and
confusing. Heavily customized pages are often loaded slowly , especially on
slower internet connections. Additionally , in an attempt to monetize,
Myspace added numerous advertisements, further contributing to the visual
noise.
Impact on User Experience: Users often felt overwhelmed by the visual
assault of cluttered, overly customized proﬁles. The lack of consistent
design made it hard for users to ﬁnd common features across different
proﬁles. The chaotic designs were particularly challenging for users with
visual impairments or cognitive  disabilities. The vast differences in proﬁle
designs diluted Myspace's brand identity .
A decent pr oﬁle fr om Myspace, it does not stop her e.
Myspace's active user base dropped from 75.9 million in Decem ber 2008 to
34.9 million by August 2011, a 54% decrease. Average time spent on
Myspace decreased from 2 hours and 6 minutes per month in December
2008 to 59 minutes in May 2010. News Corp bought Myspace for $580
million in 2005. By 2011, it was sold for just $35 million, a 94% drop in
value.
During this period, Facebook  was gaining popularity with its clean,
consistent design and limited customization options. Facebook's active user
base grew from 100 million in 2008 to 845 million by the end of 201 1.
In 2010  and 2011, Myspace attempted several redesigns to simplify its
interface:
1. They introduced new proﬁle templates with less customization.
2. They pivoted towards becoming a music and entertainme nt
platform.
3. They implemented a more consistent navigation structure across
the site.
However , these changes came too late to reverse the platform's decline.
So how to know if your product is overdesigned?
It can be tricky to ﬁgure out if your product is overdesigned, but there are a
few telltale signs to watch out for. Here are some key indicators that your
product might be suf fering from a case of overdesign:
Visual Clutter  and Confusion:  If your product's interface is so jam-packed
with elements that users do not know where to look ﬁrst, that is a big red
ﬂag. it is like trying to ﬁnd your keys in a room that is overﬂ owing with
junk – it is just too much visual noise! When users feel confused or
overwhelmed by the sheer number of things going on, that is a sign that
your design has crossed the line into "too much" territory .
Lack of Clear  Hierar chy and Focus: In a well-designed product, there  is a
clear visual hierarchy that guides users' attention to the most important
elements. If your design lacks this clear focus, with everything competing
for attention, it might be overdesigned. It is like a movie with too many
plotlines - when there is no clear main story , it is hard to know what to care
about!
Unnecessary Decorative Elements: Decorative elements can be great for
adding visual interest and personality to your design, but when they start to
take over and distract from the core functionality , that is a problem. If your
product is full of fancy ﬂourishes, gradients, and animations that do not
really serve a purpose, it is like putting a bunch of sprinkles on a cupcake -
sure, it looks cute, but it does not make the cupcake taste any better!
Inconsistent or Clashing Styles: When your product's visual style is all
over the place, with clashing colors, fonts, and design elements, it can be a
sign of overdesi gn. it is like wearing polka dots with stripes - it is just too
much going on! Consistency is key in good design, so if your product feels
like a mishmash of different styles, it might be time to simplify and
streamline.
The Litmus Test: Does It Serve the User?  At the end of the day, the best
way to tell if your product is overdesigned is to ask yoursel f: does this
element truly serve the user and enhance their experience? If the answer is
no, it is probably just adding unnecessary complexity .
So, you have realized your product has fallen victim to overdesign – do not
worry , it happens to the best of us! Now it is time to roll up your sleeves
and start simplifying. Here is a step-by-step guide to help you streamline
your overdesigned product:
Conduct a Design Audit: Imagine you are going through your closet and
pulling out every single item of clothing you own. That sparkly sequin top
you wore once to a New Year's Eve party three years ago? Probably not
essential. The same goes for your product's design.
Let us say you have a ﬁtness tracking app with a ton of different graphs,
charts, and widgets on the dashboard. During your design audit,  you realize
that most users only really care about tracking their daily steps and calories.
All those extra visualizations are just taking up space and confusing people!
Prioritize and Simplify:  Once You have  ﬁgured out which elements are
just clutter , it is time to start pruning. Think of it like gardening  – you want
to trim away the dead leaves and overgrown branches to let the healthy
plants shine.
Going back to our ﬁtness app example, you might decide to simplify the
dashboard by removing all the extraneous graphs and focusing  on just the
key metrics that users care about. Suddenly , the app feels much more
intuitive and streamlined!
Embrace Whit e Space:  White space is like the breath ing room in your
design. it is the equivalent of having a clean, uncluttered countertop in your
kitchen – it just makes everything feel more or ganized and inviting.
Imagine an e-commerce product  page that is crammed with product photos,
descriptions, and reviews all jammed together . By adding some white space
around each element, you can create a much more pleasant browsing
experience that does not overwhelm the user .
Streamline Your Color  Palette : Just like how you would not wear neon
green pants with a hot pink shirt (unless you are going to an '80s themed
party), your product's color palette should be cohesive and purposeful.
Let us say you have a product ivity app that currently uses a rainbow of
different colors for each feature . By streamlining the color palette to just a
few key hues that match your brand, you can create a much more polished
and professional look.
Simplify Your Typography:  You know how it is hard to read a book that
uses a bunch of different fancy fonts? The same goes for your product.
Stick to a couple of clear , easy-to-read fonts and your users will thank you.
Imagine a news app that uses a different font for each article headline. It
might look creative, but it is also pretty confusing for readers. By
simplifying the typography to just one or two fonts, you can create a much
more cohesive and readable experience.
Test and Repea t: Simplifying your product's design is like trying out a new
recipe – you might not nail it on the ﬁrst try, but with some tweaking and
tasting, you will get there eventually!
After streamlining your ﬁtness app's dashboard, you push the update live
and wait for user feedback. Some people love the new simpliﬁed look, but
others miss having more detailed data visualizations. Based on this
feedback, you might decide to add a few key graphs back in, but keep the
overall design much cleaner than before.
Remember , the key is to be ruthless in cutting out the clutter and focusing
on what really matters to your users.
Self-Evaluation
★      Do you often ﬁnd yourself tempted to add ﬂashy elements or fancy
features to your product design?
★      Have you experienced challenges with a cluttered and confusing
interface in your product?
★      Are you aware of how overdesigning can lead to user distraction and
frustration?
★      Have you noticed a lack of clear visual hierarchy or focus in your
product's design?
★      Do you recognize the importance of prioritizing elements that truly
serve the user's needs?
★      Are you familiar with the concept of unnecessary decorative elements
in design?
★      Have you encountered inconsistencies or clashes in your product's
visual style?
★      Do you understand the impact of overdesign on user experience and
engagement?
★      Have you conducted a design audit to identify cluttered or
unnecessary elements in your product?
★      Are you actively working on prioritizing and simplifying your
product's design based on user feedback and usability testing?
★      Do you embrace whitespace in your design to create a more or ganized
and inviting experience?
★      Have you streamlined your color palette to create a more cohesive
and purposeful design?
★      Are you simplifying your typography to improve readability and
coherence in your product?
★      Do you regularly test and iterate on your design changes to ensure
they enhance the user experience?
★      Are you willing to be ruthless in cutting out clutter and focusing on
what truly matters to your users?
Ignoring Gestalt Principles
It all started with this guy name d Max Wertheimer back in the early 1900s.
He was part of a crew of psychologists who were totally over the whole
"break everything down into tiny pieces" approach that was popular at the
time (thanks to another psychologist named Wilhelm Wundt and his
structuralism theory).
Instead, Werthe imer and his buddies were all about looking at the big
picture – the total mind and behavior experience. They called this new way
of thinking "gestalt psychology " (gestalts just means "uniﬁed wholes" in
German).
From this holist ic philosophy , the gestalt principles of visual organization
were born. Rather than focusing on the nitty-gritty details, these principles
describe how our minds autom atically perceive and make sense of entire
visual scenes.
But ﬁrst, let us quickly cover what these Gestalt principles actually are. do
not worry , I will keep it simple – no dense psychological jar gon here.
What ar e Gestalt principles?
Gestalt principles are basically a set of rules that describe how our brains
naturally perceiv e and organize visual elements. it is all about how we make
sense of the world around us by grouping things together based on certain
patterns and relationships.
For example, if you see a bunch of dots scattered on a page,  your brain
automatically tries to make sense of them by grouping the ones that are
close together (proximity) or look similar (similarity). it is like your brain is
playing a giant connect-the-dots game.
And when you see an incomplete shape, like a circle with a tiny gap, your
brain automatically ﬁlls in the missing piece to create a complete, closed
form (closure). It is your brain's  way of tidying things up and making sense
of the world.
These principle s might seem intuitive, but they are incredibly powerful
when it comes to design. By applying them correctly , you can guide your
users' perception  and make your visuals feel organized, cohesive, and just
plain make sense.
So, in a nutshell , Gestalt principles are like a secret language that your brain
speaks ﬂuently – and as design ers, it is our job to learn that language and
use it to create visuals that really click with our users.
Now that You have got the backstory , let us go over the main Gestalt
principles that designers live by. Think of them as the building blocks for
creating visuals that just make sense to our brains:
Proximity  - This one's all about giving your brain a hint that things close
together are related. it is like seeing a couple cuddled up on a park bench -
you automatically assume they are an item, right?
Similarity  - Our minds tend to group together elements that look alike .
Whether it is shapes, colors, sizes, you name it - similarities help our brains
categorize and or ganize visuals.
Closur e - Our brains crave completenes s. Even if something's incomplete,
We will automatically ﬁll in the blanks to make a whole shape or form. it is
why we see a circle even if tHere is a tiny gap!
Continuity  - We visually hunt for patterns and lines that seem to continue
through space. Think of a road disappearing over the horizon - your mind
just connects the dots.
Figur e-Gr ound  - This is about  separating objects from their surrounding
environment or background. It is how we instantly know the text on this
page from the blank space around it.
Common Fate  - Eleme nts that seem to be moving or changing in the same
direction are naturally grouped together as one unit. Like a ﬂock of birds or
a marching band.
Emergence  - This is where the whole is given more importance than the
individual parts.  Like how a beautiful mosaic "emer ges" from a bunch of
tiny tiles.
Multistability  - Some visuals can actually ﬂip between multiple
interpretations based on perspective. Those weird optical illusion pictures
are a perfect example.
Invariance  - Despi te changes in perspecti ve, lighting, or orientation, our
brains still perce ive the same object or pattern. We recognize a cup whether
it is upright or sideways.
Prägnanz (Pray-nah-ntz)  - Our minds naturally look for the simplest, most
stable interpretation of sensory patterns. The "law of good ﬁgure " or "law of
pregnance."
Symmetry and Order  - We are drawn  to visual patte rns that are orderly ,
regular , and symmetrical over random, discordant arrangements.
Common Region  - Elements located within the same closed region or
boundary are grouped together . Like clustering text and graph ics inside a
box.
See, not too bad, right? Just some simple ideas for how to arrange and
present visuals in a way that aligns with how our brains natur ally process
things.
How ar e these principles important in Pr oduct design?
Neglecting Gestalt principles in your visual design can quickly lead to
chaos, confusion , and a poor user experience. These principles exist to align
with how our brains naturally perceive and organize visual information.
Ignoring them is like intentionally making your product  harder to
comprehend.
Imagine an e-commerce product page where the "Add to Cart" button
constantly shifts positions, changes colors, and uses inconsisten t icon styles
on each reload (violating invariance). Your eye has to hunt for the familiar
pattern instead of focusing on the content itself.
What about a dashboard interface that lacks any sense of visua l hierarchy ,
symmetry , or stable ﬁgures? Important data modules are randomly sized
and positioned with no sensible order or alignment cues. It feels chaotic and
makes extracting insights an exercise in frustration.
These are all examples of visua ls that defy Gestalt principles of perceptual
organization. There are no cohesive patterns, sensible groupings, or
predictable arrangements for the brain to latch onto. It requires a good
cognitive effort to understand the information - the opposite of an intuitive
user experience.
On the other hand, adhering to Gestalt principles creates design
environments that feel harm onious and naturally understandable.
Appropriate use of proximity , similarity , continuance, closure and ﬁgure-
ground guide the user's eye intentionally . Symmetry and consistent visual
regions promote a sense of order and logic.
Let us dive deep er into some concrete examples and solutions for applying
these principles in UI:
Proximity
This principle is all about lever aging spatial relationships and distances to
indicate groupings. There are several ways to implement this:
Whitespace - Provid ing ample padding between separate elements/sections
helps distinguish them as distinct groups. Conversely , minimizing
whitespace between related items (card layouts, navigation links, etc)
implies they are one cohesive unit.
Containers - Using boundar ies like rules, boxes, or background colors to
encapsulate related elements within a contained region. This physical
closeness reinforces their conceptual proximity .
Layouts - The strategic positioning and spacing of elements on a page can
provide strong proximity cues. For example, distinctly separati ng areas for
the header , content well, sidebar through liberal use of whitespace gaps.
Similarity
Our brains innately group things that share similar visual properties. You
can apply this principle through consistency:
Styling - Ensuring elements intended as a single concept share the same
typography styling, colors, textures, shapes etc. Uniform button styles,
identically formatted cards, patterned backgrounds, matching  icon styles
etc.
Sizing - Not just uniform dimensions between components, but maintaining
consistent sizing  for related elements like headers, paragraphs, inputs across
sections.
Alignment - Proper alignment goes a long way in tying elements together
visually . Lining  up items horizontally or vertically based on baselines
automatically groups them for the brain.
Closur e
This gestalt law describes how our minds automatically follow the principle
of closure, mentally completing shapes, patterns, and totalities.
Borders - Using borders, lines, or contained shapes to encapsulate sections
provides a complete perceivable  region or boundary that the brain registers
as a group.
Connectedness - Things like solid/consistent line separators, ﬂowed curved
paths, or repeate d design motifs promote a sense of conceptual closure and
connection between elements.
Backgr ounds - Different colored/textured background sections allow
components to be perceived as complete, enclosed, segregated wholes
rather than boundless pieces.
The key with all these principles is providing ample visual cues for how
elements should be grouped, separated, prioritized, and understood as
uniﬁed constructs.
For example, on an ecommerce product page you might have:
●         Product images and details contained in one distinct bordered region
(closure)
●         Call-to-action buttons sharing exactly matched sizing and styles
(similarity)
●         Related products distinctly separated by generous margins and
background colors (proximity)
you are essentially saying "Hey brain, here are the explicit groupings and
relationships between these pieces of information." It optimizes for
understandability .
Continuance and Continuity
Ever noticed how your eye naturally follows lines, patterns, and curves as
they ﬂow across a design? You can leverage principles of continuance and
continuity to guide users through workﬂows and page layouts.
For example, on a signup form, you might use arrows or faded line
separators to clearly indicate the continuous "path" a user should follow
when ﬁlling out the form ﬁelds. Their eye will instinctively want to follow
that visual direction and movement.
Or for a multi-step purchase process, having a linear progress bar or tab-
style navigation  provides a sense of continuity from one step to the next. It
allows users to orient themselves and predict where they are headed.
Figur e-Gr ound
We have touched on this, but techniques to emphasize ﬁgure-ground
relationships deserve their own spotlight. Exaggerating the distinction
between objects and their surrounding environment is key .
On a landing page, you may want the hero content (ﬁgure) to appear
elevated with subtle drop shado ws or an offset outline to separate it from
the background.
For data visualization, using contrasting colors and saturation levels makes
charts and graphs pop out from supporting info (ground). Maybe the chart
areas have a soft background shading to enhance the ﬁgure perception.
Common Fate
This principle suggests elements  with the same dynamics or movements are
perceived as a cohesive unit. You can use animation, transitions , and micro-
interactions to demonstrate common fate.
let us say you have a product tour that cycles through different UI callouts
and tooltips. Having those elem ents animate onto the screen in a uniform
way (e.g. the same entrancing transition) tells the brain to group  them into a
singular guided experience.
Similarly , on loading or refresh  states, you might use a rotating progress
spinner animation where each dot moves in tandem. The common rotational
"fate" allows the brain to process it as a uniﬁed loader concept.
Emergence
Emer gence is all about perceivi ng the whole as greater than the sum of its
parts. Our brains naturally look for overarching patterns, uniﬁed concepts,
and holistic experiences rather than just disconnected pieces.
One of the best ways to leverage emer gence in design is through cohesive
systems and experiences that feel interconnected at a high level.
Design Systems:  Having a comprehensive design system with reusable
components, shared typography , color palettes etc. allows individual UI
elements to be recognized as part of a consistent, holistic system when
experienced together .
For exam ple, Apple's suite of built-in apps all adhere to their proprietary
San Francisco font and design patterns. While each app is a distinct piece,
the mind processes them as integrated parts of iOS's uniﬁed design
language.
Thematic Visuals:  You can also unite seemingly disparate elements under a
cohesive metaphor or visual theme. This helps the mind perceive
conceptual emer gence beyond individual components.
A classic example is the use of a virtual desktop metaphor for operating
systems. Individ ual windows, icons, and chrome UI around them emer ge as
part of a workspace rather than segregated pieces.
Games frequen tly employ cohesive visual themes as well. The HUD,
characters, environments, and gameplay mechanics - while distinct
elements - emer ge as parts of that game's overall world and experience.
Motion and Animation:  Animation and transitions can be key for implying
emer gence between states and screen ﬂows. Subtle micro-interactions
suggest interconnectivity .
For instance, introducing new UI layers or modal windows with a ﬂuid
animated transition. Or having screen navigation updates reinforced with
animated eleme nt movements helps the mind process it as one continuous
experience emer ging.
You see this frequently in mobile app design, where views have
standardized push/slide transitional animations to reinforce the holistic app
navigation.
Essentially , the goal with emergence is implying: "All these individual
pieces - despite their dif ferences - are part of a singular , cohesive whole."
Each distinct element exists as part of a higher -level uniﬁed construct,
rather than being segregated silos.
Multistability
This tendency for visuals to represent multiple interpretations is harder to
"solve" for , but you can leverage it intentionally .
Logo design often employs elements of multistability - like the famous
FedEx logo where the negative space seems to depict an arrow (suggesting
movement).
In product design, you could explore parallax scrolling effects where layers
in the backgroun d move at varying speeds. This multiplies the perspective
and creates an illusion of depth.
Invariance
Remember , this principle means an object's appearance transcends changes
in orient ation, angle, size, etc. Sticking to consistent design patterns helps
uphold invariance.
Using a design system with reusable components like buttons, inputs, icons
ensures they render reliably in different contexts. Your login button looks
identical whether on the homepage or within the app's guts.
It also means maintaining uniform spacing, padding, typography sizes
across all layouts. This predictable consistency allows components to
remain recognizable despite variations in layout.
Prägnanz (Law of Prägnanz)
This principle refers to our tendency to perceive simple, stable  forms over
complex or ambiguous ones. Designs should strive for visual simplicity
where possible.
Leverage techniques like negati ve/empty space, sharp/distinct shapes over
organic blobs, and minimal color palettes. Each element should have a
singularly clear shape or form.
For example, many compact modern icon designs honor prägnanz with
geometric shapes suggesting functionality (square for a "box", circle for a
timer , etc). Our brains gravitate to these clear , uncomplicated forms.
Symmetry and Order
Our brains are hardwired to prefer symmetrical, ordered arrangements over
chaos. You can capitalize on this by using grids, repetitive patterns, and
balanced visual weight in your layouts.
For exam ple, Pinterest populari zed the uniform, tiledGrid layout where all
the image thumbnails are the same size and aligned pristinely . This
predictable, symmetrical structu re allows your eye to metabo lize tons of
visual information rapidly .
On dashboards or product admin areas, utilizing a consistent modular grid
system with proper margins creates a sense of logical order . Related data
cards are aligned, whitespace is balanced - it simply looks "correct."
You can apply similar grid-based structure on ecommerce catego ry pages or
galleries. The human brain will subconsciously thank you for this aligned,
ordered presentation.
Common Region
The Common Region principle states that elements located within the same
distinct boundar y or region are perceived as being grouped together . Our
brains intuitively associate things sharing a common area or container .
This is a powerful technique for visually implying relationships and
hierarchies within your UI. Here are some ways to leverag e Common
Region:
Layout Containers:  One of the most obvious applications is using
containment areas like bordere d sections, cards, or boxed off regions to
explicitly group related elements together .
For example, on a dashboard you may have separate boxed sections for
high-level metrics, visualizations, data tables etc. The common region
reinforces that those componen ts are meant to be consumed  as uniﬁed
sections of information.
Modal Windows:  Modal UI elements like popup s, lightboxes or dialogs
create a distinct region separate from the main UI. This screams "temporary
contained experi ence!" which the brain registers as a uniﬁed grouping from
the back-end ﬂow .
Accordion boxe s or expand/collapse regions apply a similar principle - the
opened area becomes the active "common region" to process.
Backgr ounds: Using different colored or textured background sections is
another way to deﬁne a common region grouping on the page.
For instance, you may use an alternating gray background to unite all the
form inputs into one distinct region from the surrounding UI, implying they
are a single cohesive unit.
Drop Shadows & Layers
You can create a common region with perceived depth and separation via
container layers or drop shadow ef fects. This visually elevates components,
segregating them into their own region distinct from the "background" UI.
This is frequently seen with "sticky" UI elements like hover cards - their
drop shadow imparts a sense of independent regionality and grouping.
These are just a few examples, but I hope you can see how intentionally
applying each Gestalt principle creates experiences optimized for how our
visual processin g systems work. We are essentially designing with built-in
intuition!
Self-Evaluation
★      Are you familiar with Gestalt principles and their signiﬁcance in

★      Do you understand how Gestalt principles describe how our brains
naturally perceive and or ganize visual elements?
★      Have you applied Gestalt principles in your product design to create
cohesive and understandable visuals?
★      Do you recognize the importance of proximity in indicating
relationships between elements in your design?
★      Have you leveraged similarity to group together visually similar
elements in your UI?
★      Are you mindful of closure and how it helps users perceive complete
shapes or forms in your design?
★      Do you use continuity and continuance to guide users through
workﬂows and page layouts seamlessly?
★      Have you considered ﬁgure-ground relationships to emphasize objects
and separate them from their background?
★      Are you familiar with common fate and how it groups elements based
on shared dynamics or movements?
★      Have you explored emer gence in design to create holistic and uniﬁed
experiences for users?
★      Do you understand multistability and its impact on visual
interpretation in design?
★      Are you conscious of invariance and the importance of maintaining
consistent design patterns across dif ferent contexts?
★      Have you applied Prägnanz in your design to prioritize simplicity and
clarity over complexity?
★      Do you leverage symmetry , order , and common region principles to
create balanced and or ganized layouts?
★      Are you intentional about using grids, containers, and background
distinctions to group related elements together visually?
Misusing Animation
It is easy to get carried away with animation when you are designing a
product. After all, it is so much  fun to make things move and groove! But
just like with any good party , there is a ﬁne line between a fun time and a
chaotic mess.
When you overu se or misapply animation in your interface, it is like having
a disco ball in every room of your house – it is just too much! Suddenly ,
your users are bombarded with ﬂashing text, spinning icons, and bouncing
buttons at every turn. it is enough to make their heads spin!
When animation is used excessively or inappropriately , it can quickly
become a distraction and a hindrance to your users. Instead of enhancing
the experience, it starts to detract from it.
Imagine trying to read a book while someone's waving sparklers in your
face – it is not going to be a very pleasant or productive experience!
Similarly , when your interface is ﬁlled with gratuitous animations, your
users are going to have a hard time focusing on the task at hand.
Excessive anima tion can also be overwhelming and annoying for users. If
your animations  are too ﬂashy , too frequent, or too prolonged , your users
are going to start looking for the nearest exit.
So, how do you avoid the pitfalls of animation overload and create an
interface that is both engaging and usable? The key is to use animation with
purpose and subtlety .
Ling's Cars Website (2000s - Pr esent)
Ling's Cars is a UK-based car leasing company owned by Ling Valentine.
The website, lingscars.com, has become infamous in the web design
community for its unconventional and overwhelming use of animations and
visual elements . Ling's Cars website is a prime example of animation
misuse, taking the concept to an extreme. Here is how the site misuses
animation:
●         Excessive Move ment: Almost every element on the page is animated,
from blinking text to rotating images and ﬂying objects.
●         Lack of Purpose: Many animations serve no functional purpose and
do not enhance user understanding or navigation.
●         Distracting Elements: Animated GIFs, auto-playing videos, and
ﬂashing text compete for user attention.
●         Performance Issues: The abundance of animations causes slow load
times and can crash browsers on less powerful devices.
●         Accessibility Problems: The constant movement makes the site nearly
unusable for people with visual impairments or cognitive disabilities.
●         Inconsistent Styling: Animations vary widely in style and execution,
creating a chaotic visual experience.
A nuclea r missil e silo animation in the background, rotating 3D car models,
ﬂashing text and prices, an animated version of the owner's head ﬂoating
across the screen , auto-playing music and videos and many more things you
can notice.
Users report feeling overwhelme d and unable to focus on the actual content
or purpose of the site. The exces sive animations make it challenging to ﬁnd
important inform ation or navigate the site effectively . The site is virtually
unusable for people with epilepsy , motion sickness, or certain cognitive
disabilities. The animations cause signiﬁcant lag, especially on mobile
devices or slower internet connections.
While exact numbers are not publicly available, industry analyses suggest:
Estimates sugge st a bounce rate of over 70%, with many users leaving
within seconds due to the overw helming design. Despite high trafﬁc (partly
due to its notor iety), the conversion rate is estimated to be well below
industry standar ds for car leasing sites. Nearly 90% of mobile users are
estimated to abandon the site immediately due to poor performance and
usability . Interestingly , Ling's Cars has turned this unconventional design
into a unique selling point. The website's notoriety has generated signiﬁcant
publicity , and the owner claims  that the unusual design helps the business
stand out in a crowded market.
Keep it Subtle: A Gentle Tap, Not a Loud Bang
When it comes to using animation in your product, it is important to
remember that less is often more. You want your animations to be like a
gentle tap on the shoulder , not a loud bang in the ear! Think of it like
seasoning your food. A little bit of salt and pepper can enhance the ﬂavors
and make the dish more enjoya ble. But if you dump a whole container of
spices into the pot, it is going to be overwhelming and hard to stomach!
The same principle applies to animation. A subtle, well-placed animation
can add a touch of delight and engagement to your interface. But if you go
overboard with ﬂashy , excessive animations, it is going to be distracting and
annoying for your users.
Finding the Right Balance
So, how do you ﬁnd the right balance of subtlety in your anima tions? Here
are a few simple tips:
Keep it short and sweet:  Animations should be brief and to the point. They
should not linger on the screen or take too long to complete. A good rule of
thumb is to keep most animations under 300 milliseconds (that is just a
fancy way of saying "pretty darn quick!").
Avoid big, ﬂashy movements:  Animat ions should be relatively small and
contained. Avoid large, sweeping movements or dramatic transitions that
take up the whole screen. Instead, opt for subtle effects like gentle fades,
slight slides, or soft pulsing.
Use it sparingly:  Animation should be used strategically and sparingly
throughout your interface. do not feel like you need to animate every single
element or interaction. Choose a few key places where animation can really
enhance the experience, and use it thoughtfully .
Make it meaningful:  Every animation should serve a clear purpose and
communicate something to the user. Whether it is providing feedback,
guiding attentio n, or adding a moment of delight, make sure your
animations are meaningful and not just decorative.
To help illustrate the concept of subtle animation, here are a few examples
you might encounter in everyday products:
●         When you hove r over a button on a website, it might gently change
color or slightly grow in size to indicate that it is interactive.
●         When you send a message in a chat app, a small checkmark might
appear next to the message to conﬁrm that it has been sent
successfully .
●         When you complete a task in a to-do list app, the task might brieﬂy
ﬂash or fade out to indicate that it has been marked as done.
In each of these cases, the animation is subtle, brief, and purposeful. It
communicates something to the user without being overly  ﬂashy or
distracting.
Animation as Feedback: A Friendly Nod fr om Your Product
When you are using a product, it is always nice to know that it is listening
to you and respo nding to your actions. That is where animation comes in as
a handy tool for providing feedback! Think of animation feedback like a
friendly nod or a thumbs-up from your product. it is a way for the interface
to say, "Hey , I saw what you did there, and everything's working just ﬁne!"
or "Gotcha, I'm processing your request right now , hang tight!"
By using little animated cues and responses, your product can keep your
users informed and reassured throughout their experience. It is like having a
helpful guide by your side, letting you know that you are on the right track!
So, what does animation feedba ck actually look like in a product? Here are
a few common examples:
Loading Indicators:  When your product is processing a request or loading
some data, you can use an animated loading indicator (like a spinning circle
or a progress bar) to let your users know that something's happening behind
the scenes. it is like the produc t saying, "I'm work ing on it, be right with
you!"
Success Conﬁrmations:  When a user completes an action, like submitting
a form or making a purchase , you can use a brief animation (like a
checkmark or a thumbs-up) to conﬁrm that the action was successful. it is
like the product giving your user a virtual high-ﬁve!
Error Alerts:  If something goes wrong or the user makes a mistake (like
typing in the wrong password ), you can use an animated alert (like a
shaking form ﬁeld or a red cross) to draw attention to the issue and guide
them towards a solution. It is like the product gently pointing out the
problem and helping your user get back on track.
Interactive Responses:  When a user interacts with an element in your
interface (like hovering over a button or dragging an item), you can use
subtle animations to acknowle dge their action and provide a sense of
responsiveness. it is like the product nodding and saying, "I feel you, I'm
ready when you are!"
Here the animation serves as a clear and friendly form of communication
between your product and your users. It keeps them informed, reassured,
and engaged throughout their experience.
Tips for  Using Animation Feedback
To make sure your animation feedback is effective and helpful , keep these
tips in mind:
Keep it clear  and consistent:  Use the same animation cues consistently
throughout your product, so users can easily recognize and understand the
feedback they are receiving.
Make it timely:  Provide feedback immediately  after the user's action, so
they can see the connection between what they did and how the product
responded.
do not overdo it:  Too much animation feedback can be overwhelming and
distracting. Use it strategically and sparingly , only when it truly adds value
to the user experience.
Test it out: As with any aspect of your product, it is important to test your
animation feedb ack with real users to make sure it is clear , helpful, and not
confusing or annoying.
Animation as a Spotlight: Pointing the Way
Imagine you are in a big, busy store, and you are trying to ﬁnd a speciﬁc
product. It can be overwhelming with so many shelves and items to look at!
But then, sudden ly, you see a bright, ﬂashing arrow pointing directly to the
item you need. That is kind of like how animation can guide attention in
your product! When you use animation strategically , it is like shining a
spotlight on the most important  parts of your interface. It helps your users
cut through the clutter and focus on what really matters, without getting lost
or confused.
Here are a few common examples to show how use animation to guide your
users' attention:
Highlighting New Elements:  When you add a new feature or piece of
content to your product, you can use a subtle animation (like a brief pulse or
a slide-in effect) to draw your users' eyes to it. it is like saying, "Hey , check
this out, it is new and important!"
Indicating Next  Steps:  If your product has a speciﬁc ﬂow or sequence of
actions, you can use animation to guide your users through it step by step.
For example, you might use a bouncing arrow or a pulsing button to show
them where to click next, like a trail of breadcrumbs leading them to their
destination.
Emphasizing Key Information:  If there  is a critical piece of information
or a important message you want your users to see, you can use animation
to make  it stand out. For instance, you might use a gentle shake or a color
change to draw attention to an error message or a time-sensitive
notiﬁcation.
Providing Visual Feedback:  Animation can also be used to provide visual
feedback and reinforce important actions. For example, when a user adds an
item to their cart, you might use a brief animation (like a ﬂying icon or a
checkmark) to conﬁrm the action and guide their attention to the cart icon.
In each of these cases, the animation acts like a helpful guide, pointing your
users in the right direction and making sure they do not miss anything
important.
Tips for  Guiding Attention with Animation
To make sure your attention- guiding animations are effective and not
distracting, keep these tips in mind:
Be selective:  do not try to guide attention to everything at once. Choose the
most important elements or actions, and use animation sparingly to avoid
overwhelming your users.
Keep it subtle:  Attention-guiding animations should be noticeable but not
overpowering. Use gentle, unde rstated effects that do not take away from
the overall experience.
Make it meaningful:  Only use anima tion to guide attention when it truly
adds value and helps your users navigate your product more easily . Avoid
using it for purely decorative purposes.
Test and iterate:  As always, test your attention-guiding animations with
real users to make sure they are helpful and not confusing or annoying.
Iterate and reﬁne based on their feedback.
Animation as a Smile: Adding a Sprinkle of Joy
Using your product should be like hanging out with a good friend – it
should be enjoyable, engaging, and maybe even a little bit fun! That is
where animation comes in as a way to add some delight to the experience.
Think of delight ful animations like a friendly smile or a playful wink from
your product. They are little moments of joy and personality that make your
users feel more connected and engaged, even if they are just using an app or
a website.
So, what does a delightful animation actually look like? Here are a few
examples:
Celebratory Moments:  When a user completes a big task or reaches a
milestone (like ﬁnishing a projec t or achieving a goal), you can use a festive
animation (like a burst of confetti or a shower of stars) to celebrate their
success. it is like the product is giving them a big virtual high-ﬁve!
Playful Interactions:  You can use animation to add a sense of playfulness
and interactivity to your interface. For example, when a user hovers over a
button, it might do a little dance  or change colors in a fun way. it is like the
product is saying, "Ooh, you found me! let us play!"
Engaging Transitions:  When a user moves between different sections or
screens in your product, you can use animated transitions (like a slide, a
fade, or a ﬂip) to make the experience feel more dynamic and engaging. It
is like the product is putting on a little show for them as they navigate!
Surprising Delights:  Sometimes, you can add a touch of delight just by
surprising your users with an unexpected animation. For example, if they
complete a form, the submit button might transform into a paper airplane
and ﬂy off the screen. It is like the product is saying, "Ta-da! Was not that
fun?"
The key with delightful animations is to use them sparingly and
purposefully . They should add to the overall experience, not distract from it.
A little bit of joy goes a long way!
Tips for  Adding Delightful Animations
To make  sure your delightful animations are effective and not annoying,
keep these tips in mind:
Keep it subtle:  Delightful animations should be small, subtle, and not too
frequent. You do not want to overwhelm your users with constant visual
noise.
Make it optional:  Some users might not apprec iate or want the extra
animations, so consider making them optional or allowing users to turn
them of f if they prefer .
Match your  brand:  Make sure your delightful animations match your
product's overall brand and tone. A playful animation might not be
appropriate for a serious or professional app.
Test with users:  As with any feature, test your delightful animations with
real users to see how they react. Do they ﬁnd them charming, or do they
think they are cheesy? Iterate based on their feedback.
The key is to use animation thoughtfully and purposefully , always
considering how it will impact the user experience. Animation should
complement and enhance your interface, not compete with it for attention.

distracting?
Inconsistent Visual Feedback
Imagine you are using an app and you tap on a button, but nothi ng seems to
happen. You tap again, and still no response. Suddenly , you are not sure if
the button actually worked or if the app is just ignoring you. Frustrating,
right? That is what happens when your product provides inconsistent visual
feedback for user interactions. It is like having a conversation with someone
who keeps chan ging their tone and facial expressions – you are never quite
sure if they are happy , sad, or just messing with you!
When your product's visual feedback is all over the place, your users are
left to guess what is going on. They might wonder:
●         Did that button press actually do anything?
●         Is the system processing my request?
●         Am I supposed to wait, or should I try again?
It is like playing  a guessing game with your product, and that is not a fun
experience for anyone. When users are unsure about the outcome of their
actions, they lose conﬁdence in using your product. They migh t hesitate to
explore new features or even give up altogether! So, how do you keep your
users in the loop and avoid the confusion of inconsistent visual feedback? it
is all about providing clear and consistent cues that guide them through
your product. Think of it like being a good host at a party – you want to
make sure your guests always know where to go and what to do!
Here are some key ways to provide clear visual feedback:
Hover  States: A Closer  Look
Hover states are like the secret handshake of user interface design – they let
your users know that they are in the right place and ready to interact. It is
like when you are at a party and you see someone you know across the
room. When your eyes meet and they give you a little nod or smile, you
know it is okay to go over and say hello!
In the same way, hover states give your users a subtle but clear signal that
an element is interactive. it is a way of saying, "Hey, I'm clickable! Come on
over and give me a try!" Without hover states, your users might not even
realize that certain elements are interactive, leading to confusion  and missed
opportunities.
Designing Effective Hover  States
So, how do you create hover states that are both eye-catching and intuitive?
Here are a few tips:
Change the color:  One of the simp lest and most effective ways to indicate
a hover state is to change the color of the element. It could be a subtle shift
in hue or a more dramatic contrast – just make sure it stands out from the
regular state! Imagine a set of navigation buttons in a soft blue color . When
the user hovers over each button, it turns a deeper shade of blue, signaling
that it is ready to be clicked.
Add an underl ine or border:  Anothe r way to draw attention to a hover
state is to add an underline or border to the element. This is particularly
useful for text links, as it helps them stand out from the surrounding
content. Picture  a blog post with several inline text links. When the user
hovers over each link, a dashed underline appears below the text, indicating
that it is a clickable link.
Introduce a subtle animation:  A touch  of animation can go a long way in
making your hover states feel more engaging and responsive. It could be a
slight scaling effect, a gentle pulse, or even a fun little wiggle! Imagine a set
of social media icons on a webs ite. When the user hovers over each icon, it
does a little bounce or spin, adding a playful touch to the interaction.
Use clear labels  or tooltips:  Sometimes, a hover state alone is not enough
to conve y the full meaning of an interactive element. In these cases, you can
use clear labels or tooltips to provide additional context. Picture  a complex
data visualization with several interactive elements. When the user hovers
over each element, a small toolti p appears with a brief descriptio n of what it
represents and how to interact with it.
The key is to choose hover states that ﬁt the overall style and tone of your
product. If you are designing a serious business app, you might want to
keep things subtle and professional. But if you are creating a fun social
media platform, you can get a bit more playful with your hover effects! The
most important thing is to be consistent with your hover states throughout
your product. Once You have established a particular style or behavior for
your hover effects, make sure to apply it consistently to all interactive
elements.
Button Animations: Adding Some Sizzle
Button animations are like the cherry on top of your product's sundae – they
are not strictly necessary , but they sure do make things feel a lot more
delightful and engaging! it is like when you hit a game-winning shot in
basketball and do a little victory  dance – it is not required, but it sure feels
good!
In the same way, button animations give your users a little burst of feedback
and satisfaction when they interact with your product. it is a way of saying,
"Hey, you did it! Nice work! " Every time a user clicks a button and sees a
fun little animation, they will feel more connected to your product and more
motivated to keep exploring.
Crafting Engaging Button Animations
So, how do you create button animations that are both fun and functional?
Here are a few ideas to get you started:
Color  changes:  One of the simp lest but most effective button animations is
a color change. When the user clicks the button, you can have it change to a
different color to indicate that the action has been registered . Imagine a
"Submit" button on a form. Whe n the user clicks it, the button turns from a
bright green to a darker shade, signaling that the form has been successfully
submitted.
Scale or bounce effects:  Another fun way to animate your buttons is to add
a slight scaling or bouncing effect when they are clicked. This can add a
playful and dynamic feel to your interface. Picture a set of emoji reaction
buttons on a social media post. When the user clicks on each emoji, it does
a little bounce or pulse, bringing some liveliness to the interaction.
Loading spinne rs: If your button triggers an action that takes a moment to
process, you can use a loading spinner animation to keep the user informed
and enga ged. Imagine a "Search" button on a website. When the user clicks
it, the button transforms into a spinning loader icon, indicat ing that the
search is in progress.
Creative ﬂourishes:  do not be afraid to get a little creative with your button
animations! You can experiment with unique effects like spark les, ripples,
or even little bursts of confetti. Picture a "Purchase" butto n on an e-
commerce site. When the user clicks it, a shower of color ful confetti bursts
out from the button, adding a festive touch to the purchase experience.
The key is to choose animations  that ﬁt the tone and style of your product.
If you are design ing a banking app, you might want to keep things sleek and
subtle. But if you are creating a kid-friendly game, you can go wild with the
whimsy!
When it comes to button animations, timing is crucial. You want your
animations to be snappy and responsive, but not so fast that they are jarring
or puzzl ing. A good rule of thumb is to keep your animations under 300
milliseconds – anything longer can feel sluggish and unresponsive.
Sure thing! let us take a deeper dive into status indicators and how they can
help keep your users informed and engaged throughout their journey with
your product.
Status Indicators: Keeping Users in the Loop
Picture this: you are waiting in line at a coffee shop, and you have no idea
how long it is going to take to get your drink. You crane your neck, trying to
see if your order  is being made, but you cannot tell what is going on behind
the counter . Frustrating, right?
Now imagine if the coffee shop  had a big screen that showed the status of
each order – "In Progress," "Ready for Pickup," etc. Suddenly , you would
feel a lot more informed and at ease, knowing exactly where your drink is
in the process.
That is exactly what status indicators do for your product – they keep your
users informed and reassured at every step of the way. Whether they are
waiting for a ﬁle to upload, a page to load, or a complex action to complete,
status indicators let them know what is happening behind the scenes.
Designing Clear  and Informative Status Indicators
So, how do you create status indicators that are both clear and informative?
Here are a few tips:
Loading spinne rs: One of the most  common status indicators is the loading
spinner – that little circular icon that spins while something is loading or
processing. it is a simple but effective way to let users know that something
is happening, even if it is not immediately visible. Imagine a user uploading
a large ﬁle to a cloud storage service. While the ﬁle is being processed, a
loading spinner appears next to the ﬁle name, indicating that the upload is
in progress.
Progress bars:  For longer processes or multi-step actions, a progress bar
can be a great way to keep users  informed and motivated. By showing how
far along the process is, you give users a sense of accompli shment and
reassure them that things are moving forward. Picture a user going through
a multi-page registration process for a new service. At the top of each page,
there is a progress bar showing how many steps are left, giving the user a
clear sense of where they are in the process.
Textual updates:  Someti mes, a simple text message can be the most
effective way to keep users informed. By providing brief, clear  updates on
what is happenin g, you can help users feel more in control and aware of the
process. Imagine  a user placing an order on an e-commerce site. After they
submit their payment, a message appears: "Thank you for your order! We
are processing it now and will send you a conﬁrmation email shortly." This
lets the user know that their order has been received and gives them a clear
idea of what to expect next.
Visual cues:  In addition to text and icons, you can use visual cues like
color , animation, and layout to convey status information. For example, you
might use a green checkmark to indicate a successful action or a red
exclamation point to signal an error . Picture a user ﬁlling out a form on a
website. As they type, the form ﬁelds change color – green for valid entries,
red for errors. This gives the user immediate visual feedback on the status
of their input.
The key is to choose status  indicators that are clear , concise, and
appropriate for the context. You do not want to overwhelm users with too
much information or distract them with ﬂashy animations – just give them
the essential updates they need to feel informed and in control. Status
indicators are only effective if they are responsive and up-to-date. There is
nothing more frustrating than a loading spinner that spins forever or a
progress bar that gets stuck at 99%!
