Ignoring Cr oss-platform Consistency
Users expect to be able to access your product on a variety of devices and
platforms – from desktop computers to smartphones to tablets and beyond.
But if you are not careful, this can lead to a disjointed and inconsistent user
experience.
Imagine if every time you visited a store, the layout was completely
different – the checkout counter might be in the back one day and the front
the next, the products might be organized by color one week and by size the
next. It would be maddening!
The same principle applies to your SaaS product. If the design,
functionality , and overall experience vary wildly from one platform to
another , your users are going to feel like they are constantly playing catch-
up, trying to relearn how to use your product every time they switch
devices.
Inconsistency across platforms can lead to a whole host of user experience
issues. Here are just a few examples:
Difﬁculty Navig ating:  If the navigation menu is in a different place or uses
different labels on each platform , users might struggle to ﬁnd what they are
looking for or complete basic tasks.
Inconsistent Functionality:  If certain featu res work differently (or do not
work at all) on some platforms , users might get frustrated or lose trust in
your product.
Branding Conf usion:  If the visual design and branding are inconsistent
across platform s, users might not even realize they are using the same
product, leading to a diluted brand identity .
Learning Curve:  Every time a user switches to a new platform, they
essentially have to relearn how to use your product, which can be time-
consuming and frustrating.
All these issues can add up to a disjointed, confusing, and ultimately
frustrating user experience – the opposite of what you want your product to
provide!
So, how can you avoid the pitfalls of cross-platform incons istency and
create a seamles s, cohesive user experience no matter where your users are
accessing your product? Here are a few key strategies:
Develop a Consistent Design Language
A design language is essentially a set of rules, guidelines, and components
that deﬁne how your product should look and feel across all platforms. it is
like a visual grammar that ensures consistency and coherence in your
product's design.
Imagine you are traveling to a foreign country where you do not speak the
language. You might be able to get by with some basic gestures and
pointing, but it would be much easier if everyone just spoke the same
language, right?
Evernote's Platform Fragmentation
Evernote, a popular note-taking and organizational app, faced signiﬁcant
challenges with cross-platform consistency during its rapid growth phase
from 2013 to 2016. Evernote's user interface and feature set varied
dramatically across its many platforms, including web, desktop (Windows
and Mac), and mobile (iOS and Android) versions. This inconsistency led to
user confusion and hindered the app's usability .
Certain features  were available on some platforms but not others. For
example, the Web Clipper was more robust on desktop brows ers than on
mobile. The app's look and feel varied signiﬁcantly across platforms. The
iOS app had a distinctly different design compared to the Android app and
the desktop versions. Core functions like note editing, tagging, and search
worked differently or had different capabilities depending on the platform.
Due to platform differences, some users experienced syncing problems
when using Evernote across multiple devices.
Users had to relearn how to use Evernote when switching betw een devices.
The lack of consistency meant users could not seamlessly transition their
workﬂows across devices. Users were often unsure which features were
available on which platforms. Inconsistent sync and feature availability led
some users to question the reliability of the service.
While Evernote does not share detailed metrics publicly , industry analyses
and public statements indicated : Evernote's user growth rate declined from
40% year-over -year in 2013 to about 20% in 2015. The rate of free users
converting to paid subscriptions dropped by approximately 16% between
2014 and 2016. User churn rate increased by 15% during this period,
particularly among users who utilized multiple platforms.
The same princi ple applies to the visual language of your product. If each
platform version of your product uses a different set of colors, fonts,
buttons, and icons, it is like they are all speaking different languages. Your
users might be able to ﬁgure out how to use each version, but it would be
much easier (and more delightful) if they all spoke the same visual
language.
That is where a consistent design language comes in.
The Beneﬁts of a Consistent Design Language
By developing and adhering to a consistent design language, you can:
Strengthen Your Brand Identity:  A consistent design language helps
reinforce your brand identity and makes your product more recognizable
and mem orable to users. When your product looks and feels the same
across all platforms, it creates a stronger sense of brand cohesion and trust.
Impr ove Usability:  When users encounter familiar visual elements and
patterns across different version s of your product, they can navigate and use
them more easily and intuitivel y. They do not have to relearn how to use
your product every time they switch devices.
Streamline Development:  A consistent design language also streamlines
the developmen t process by providing a clear set of comp onents and
guidelines for designers and developers to follow . This can help reduce
inconsistencies, bugs, and wasted time in the design and development
process.
Enable Scalabi lity: As your produ ct grows and evolves over time, a
consistent desig n language provides a foundation for creating new features
and component s that feel cohesive and integrated with the rest of the
product. It enables you to scale your product more efﬁciently and
effectively .
Creating a Consistent Design Language
So, how do you go about creating a consistent design language for your
product? Here are a few key steps:
Deﬁne Your Brand Identity:  Start by deﬁnin g the key elements of your
brand identity , such as your logo, color palette, typography , and tone of
voice. These elements should be consistent across all versions of your
product.
Establish Design Principles:  Develop  a set of design principles that guide
the overall look and feel of your product, such as "simple and intuitive,"
"clean and modern," or "friendly and approachable." These principles
should inform all of your design decisions.
Create a UI Kit: Create a comprehensive UI kit that includes all of the key
components and elements used in your product, such as buttons, icons,
forms, and navigation. These components should be designed to work
consistently across all platforms.
Document Guidelines:  Document clear guidelines for how to use and
implement your design language, including specs for colors, typography ,
spacing, and more. This documentation should be easily accessible to all
designers and developers working on your product.
Regularly Review and Update:  As your product evolves over time,
regularly review , and update your design language to ensure that it stays
consistent and relevant. Make sure any new features or components are
designed to ﬁt seamlessly into your existing language.
Developing a consistent design  language is like giving your product a
universal translator – it ensures that all versions of your product are
speaking the same visual language, no matter what platform they are on.
Use Responsive Design:
With responsive design, you create one ﬂexible layout that can scale and
rearrange itself based on the size and resolution of the user's screen. Instead
of creati ng separate designs for desktop, tablet, and mobile, you create a
single design that works seamlessly across all platforms.
Imagine you had a magic t-shirt that could automatically adjust  its size and
shape to ﬁt anyone who wore it, whether they were tall or short, big, or
small. That is kind of like what responsive design does for your product – it
ensures that your user interface automatically adapts and adjusts  to different
screen sizes and devices.
The Beneﬁts of Responsive Design
Using responsive design has a ton of beneﬁts for both your users and your
development team:
Consistency Across Devices:  With responsive design, users get a consistent
experience no matter what device they are using. The layout, functionality ,
and overall look and feel of your product remain the same, even as the
screen size changes.
Impr oved Usab ility:  Responsive design ensures that your product is easy
to use and navigate on any device. Users do not have to pinch, zoom, or
scroll excessive ly to access content or features, which makes for a more
intuitive and user -friendly experience.
Reduced Develo pment Time:  By creating one responsive layout instead of
multiple platform-speciﬁc designs, you can save a lot of time and effort in
the development process. You do not have to create and maintain separate
codebases for each platform.
Better  SEO: Respon sive design can also improve your product's search
engine optimization (SEO) by making it easier for search engin es to crawl
and index your content. With one consistent URL  and layout across all
devices, search engines can more easily understand and rank your content.
Implementing Responsive Design
So, how do you actually implement responsive design in your product?
Here are a few key techniques:
Flexible Grids:  Use a ﬂexible grid system that can adapt to different screen
sizes. Instead of using ﬁxed pixel widths, use relative units like percentages
or ems to deﬁne the size and spacing of your layout elements.
Media Queries : Use CSS media queries to apply different styles and
layouts based on the size and resolution of the user's screen. For example,
you might use a single-column layout on mobile devices and a multi-
column layout on lar ger screens.
Responsive Images:  Make sure your images can scale and adapt to
different screen sizes without losing quality or becoming distorted. Use
techniques like responsive imag e sizing and resolution switching to serve
the appropriate image for each device.
Mobile-First Design:  Start by desig ning for the smallest screen size
(usually mobile) and then progressively enhance the layout and
functionality for larger screens. This ensures that your product is optimized
for mobile users ﬁrst and foremost.
Testing and Optimization:  Regularly test your responsiv e design on a
variety of devices and screen sizes to ensure that it works as intended. Use
tools like browser dev tools and emulators to simulate different devices and
resolutions, and optimize your design based on real-world usage and
feedback. Responsive design is a powerful tool for creating a consistent,
intuitive, and user -friendly experience across all platforms.
Designing for  Multiple Operating Systems:
Each operating system has its own unique quirks, requirements, and design
guidelines, whic h means you cannot just create a one-size-ﬁts-all solution
and call it a day. You need to carefully consider the speciﬁc needs and
constraints of each platform, while still maintaining a consistent and
cohesive user experience across the board.
These days users expect to be able to access your product on a variety of
devices and operating systems – from Windows to macOS, Android to iOS,
and beyo nd. But developing for multiple operating systems can be a bit like
juggling while riding a unicycle – it requires a lot of skill, balance, and
coordination!
So, how can you ensure that your product operates smoothly and
consistently across multiple operating systems? Here are a few strategies to
consider:
Use Cross-Platform Frameworks:  There are several cross-platform
development frameworks and tools (such as React Native, Flutter , or
Xamarin) that allow you to write code once and deploy it across multiple
operating systems. These frameworks handle a lot of the platfo rm-speciﬁc
quirks and optimizations for you, making it easier to create a consistent
experience across devices.
Prioritize Web-Based Solutions:  If your produc t primarily lives on the
web, you can use responsive web design and progressive enhancement
techniques to ensure that it works well across a variety of operating systems
and brow sers. By designing for the web ﬁrst and foremost, you can create a
more ﬂexible and adaptable solution that is not tied to any one platform.
Create Platform-Speciﬁc Versions:  In some cases, it may make sense to
create distinct versions of your product for each major operating system,
tailored to the speciﬁc needs and conventions of that platform. This
approach requires more resources and maintenance, but can allow for a
more native and optimized experience on each device.
Continuous Testing and Optimization:  Regardless of which approach you
choose, it is important to continuously test your product on a variety of
devices and operating systems, and optimize it based on real-world usage
and feedback. By regularly monitoring performance, identifying issues, and
iterating on your solution, you can ensure that your product continues to
work well across all platforms over time.
Best Practices for  Cross-Platform Consistency
In addition to these high-level strategies, there are a few key best practices
to keep in mind when developing for multiple operating systems:
Maintain Visual Consistency:  Even if the underlying code is different on
each platform, strive to mainta in a consistent visual design and branding
across all versions of your product. Use similar color schemes, typography ,
and layout patterns to create a cohesive and recognizable experience.
Adher e to Platform Guidelines:  While maintaining overall consistency , be
sure to also adhere to the speciﬁc design guidelines and conventions of each
platform. Users expect apps on iOS to look and feel different than apps on
Android, for example, so it is important to strike a balance between
consistency and platform-speciﬁc best practices.
Provide Consistent Core Functionality:  Even if certain features or
interactions may vary slightly between platforms, ensure that the core
functionality and value proposition of your product remains consistent
across all versions. Users should be able to accomplish their key goals and
tasks regardless of which device they are using.
Communicate Platform Differ ences:  If there are signiﬁcant differences or
limitations betw een platform versions of your product, be sure to
communicate these clearly to your users. Provide platform-speciﬁc help
documentation or in-app guidance to help users understand and navigate
any variations.
Developing a product that works seamlessly across multiple operating
systems is no easy feat – but it is essential for creating a truly inclusive and
accessible user experience.
By prioritizing cross-platform consistency and creating a uniﬁed, seamless
user experience across all devices and platforms, you will make your users'
lives easier and more delightful  – no matter where they are accessing your
product from!

compares to that of my competitors?
Overlooking Emotional Design
When you are designing a product, it is easy to get caugh t up in the
functional aspec ts – the features , the layouts, the workﬂows. But if you are
only focusing on how your product works and not how it makes your users
feel, you are missing out on a huge opportunity to create a deeper , more
meaningful connection with your audience.
Think of it like a restaurant that only cares about the nutritional value of its
food, but does not bother to make it look or taste good. Sure, the food might
be healthy , but if it does not engage your senses and emotions,  you are not
going to enjoy eating it – and you are probably not going to come back for
more. The same goes for your product. If it does not evoke the right
emotions and create a positive, memorable experience for your users, they
are not going to feel invested in it – no matter how well it functions.
Why Lack of Emotional Connection is Important?
When you neglect emotional design, you risk creating a product that feels
ﬂat, generic, and forgettable. Without a strong emotional connection, users
may struggle to engage with your product on a deeper level, and may be
less likely to develop a sense of loyalty or advocacy for your brand.
Imagine using a ﬁtness app that helps you track your workouts and
progress, but does not offer any sense of encouragement, celebration, or
inspiration along the way. Sure, the app might be functionally  useful, but
without that emotional layer , it is just another tool – not a meaningful part
of your ﬁtness journey . Or think of a banking app that feels cold, corporate,
and impersonal. Even if it offers all the features you need, you are not going
to feel a sense  of trust, security , or partnership with your ﬁnancial
institution if the app does not emotionally resonate with you.
When your product fails to connect with users on an emotiona l level, you
miss out on the opportunity to create a deeper , more lasting relationship
with them. And in today's crow ded and competitive market, that emotional
connection can be the key differentiator that sets your product apart and
keeps users coming back.
How to Design for  Emotion?
So, how can you avoid the pitfalls of overlooking emotional  design and
create a product that truly resonates with your users? The key is to
intentionally consider the emotional impact of every visual element and
design choice you make.
Here are a few tips for designing with emotion in mind:
Know Your Audience
Let us break down what it means to know your audience and understand
their emotional  needs, desires, and pain points when designing your
product.
Getting to Know Your Users: Designing for your users' emotions starts
with getting to know them on a deeper level. It is kind of like making a new
friend – you cannot just jump in and start cracking jokes or sharing secrets
right away . You need to take the time to understand who they are, what they
care about, and what makes them tick.
The same goes for your users. If you want to create a product that resonates
with them emotionally , you need to start by understanding their needs,
desires, and challenges. What are they hoping to achieve or experience
when using your product? What emotions do they want to feel along the
way?
Understanding Emotional Needs and Desir es: Let us say you are
designing a meditation app. Your users might come to your app with a
desire to feel calm, centered, and focused. They might be looking for a
sense of peace and clarity in their busy lives. Or maybe they are struggling
with anxiety or stress, and they want to feel more grounded and resilient.
As a designer , your job is to understand these emotional needs and desires,
and to create an experience that helps users achieve those feelings. That
might mean using calming colors and imagery , crafting soothing and
encouraging copy , and creating a user experience that feels simple,
intuitive, and distraction-free.
Or let us say you are designing an e-learning platform. Your users might
come to your product with a desire to feel accomplished, empowered, and
conﬁdent in their skills. They might be looking for a sense of progress and
mastery , or a feeling of connection and support from other learners.
In this case, you might design your product to celebrate users' achievements
and milestones, to provide encouraging feedback and guidance, and to
foster a sense of community and collaboration among learners.
Identifying Pain Points and Challenges: In addition to understanding
your users' positive emotional needs and desires, it is also important to
identify the pain points and challenges they might be facing. What emotions
might be holding them back or making it harder for them to achieve their
goals? For exam ple, let us say you are designing a budgeting  app. Your
users might come to your app feeling stressed, overwhelmed,  or anxious
about their ﬁnances. They might  be struggling with feelings of shame, guilt,
or helplessness around money .
As a designer , your job is to understand these pain points and create an
experience that helps users overcome those negative emotions.  That might
mean using positive and encouraging language, breaking down complex
ﬁnancial tasks into simple, manageable steps, and providing tools and
resources to help users feel more in control and empowered.
Choose Colors Wisely
Just like you choose clothes to ﬁt a certain mood or occasio n, you can
choose colors in your design to evoke speciﬁc emotions and set the right
tone for your brand. Colors are like the "outﬁt" of your product – they send
a powerful message about who you are and what you are all about.
The Emotional Impact of Colors
Different colors can have different emotional associations and impacts on
your users. Here are a few examples:
●         Blue is often associated with feelings of trust, stability , and security .
That is why you often see it used in the logos and branding of banks,
healthcare comp anies, and other organizations that want to convey a
sense of reliability and professionalism.
●         Green is often associated with nature, growth, and harmony . It can
evoke feelings of calm, balance, and renewal. That is why you might
see it used in the branding of eco-friendly products, wellness apps, or
other brands that want to convey a sense of health and vitality .
●         Red is a bold, energetic color that can grab attention and evoke
feelings of excitement, passion,  and urgency . That is why you often
see it used in the branding of fast-food chains, sports teams, and other
brands that want to create a sense of enthusiasm and action.
●         Yellow is a bright, cheerful color that can evoke feelings of
happiness, optim ism, and creativity . It is often used in the brand ing of
children's produ cts, educationa l apps, or other brands that want to
create a sense of fun and positivity .
Of cours e, these  are just a few examples – every color has its own unique
emotional assoc iations and can be used in different ways depending on the
context and the brand.
Choosing Colors for  Your Brand
So, how do you choose the right colors for your brand and product? Here
are a few tips:
Consider  Your Brand Personality:  What emotions and values do you
want your brand to convey? Are you fun and playful, or serious and
professional? Choose colors that align with your brand personality and help
reinforce those key messages.
Think About Your Audience:  Who are your users, and what emotions do
they want to feel when using your product? Choose colors that resonate
with their needs  and desires, and that help create the kind of experience
they are looking for .
Use Color  Psychology:  There is a whole science behind the emotional
impact of colors – it is called color psychology . Do some research into the
typical associations and meanings of different colors, and use that
knowledge to inform your choices.
Create a Cohesive Palette:  do not just choose one color and call it a day –
create a cohesive color palette that includes a range of complementary hues.
This will help create a more balanced and visually appealing design, and
give you ﬂexibility to use dif ferent colors for dif ferent purposes.
Test and Iterate:  As with any aspect of design, it is important to test your
color choices with real users and get feedback. See how people  respond to
different colors and combination s, and be willing to iterate and adjust based
on what you learn.
Choosing colors  wisely is a crucial part of creating an emotionally resonant
and impactful user experience. By understanding the emotional associations
of different colors and using them intentionally in your desig n, you can
create a product that not only looks great, but also feels great to your users.
Use Imagery Strategically
Images and illustrations are incredibly powerful tools for evoking emotion
and creating a sense of connection with your users. Think about the last
time you saw a really great movie or read a really great book – chances are,
the imagery played a big role in making you feel something, whether it was
joy, sadness, excitement, or any other emotion.
The same goes for your produc t design. The images and illustrations you
choose can have a huge impact  on how your users feel when they interact
with your produ ct. They can help create a sense of authenticity , relatability ,
and emotional resonance that words alone just cannot match.
Choosing the Right Imagery
So, how do you choose the right imagery for your product? Here are a few
tips:
Be Auth entic:  Choose  images that feel real and authentic, rather than
staged or generic. Users can spot a fake stock photo from a mile away , and
it can actually undermine your emotional connection with them. Instead,
use images that feel genuine and relatable, like photos of real people or
places.
Evoke Emotion:  Choose  images that evoke the speciﬁc emotions you want
your users to feel. If you want  your users to feel inspired and motivated,
choose images that are uplifting and empowering. If you want them to feel
calm and relaxe d, choose images that are serene and peaceful. The imagery
should reinforce the emotional tone you are trying to set.
Tell a Story:  Use imagery to tell a story and create a narrative around your
product. Choose images that help illustrate the problem your product
solves, the beneﬁts it provides, and the experience it creates. The right
imagery can help your users see themselves in your product and understand
how it ﬁts into their lives.
Be Consistent:  Make sure the imagery you choose is consistent with your
brand identity and overall design aesthetic. The images should feel like a
natural extension of your brand, not a jarring departure from it. Consistency
helps create a cohesive and memorable emotional experience.
Test and Iterate:  As with any design choice, it is important to test your
imagery with real users and get feedback. See how people respond to
different images  and illustrations, and be willing to iterate and adjust based
on what you learn. Sometimes a small change in imagery can make a big
difference in emotional impact.
Using Custom Illustrations
In additi on to using photos and other pre-existing images, creating custom
illustrations can be a powerful way to capture the unique spirit and
personality of your brand.
Custom illustrat ions allow you to create imagery that is perfectl y tailored to
your product and your users. You can use them to create characters,
mascots, or other visual elements that help bring your brand to life and
create a stronger emotional connection with your users.
For example, the meditation app Headspace uses custom illustrations to
create a friendly , approachable, and emotionally resonant experience for its
users. The illustrations feature cute, colorful characters that guide users
through the app and help make the practice of meditation  feel more
accessible and enjoyable.
Using imagery strategically is a powerful way to create an emotionally
engaging and memorable user experience. By choosing images and
illustrations that are authentic, relatable, and emotionally resonant, you can
help your users see themselves in your product and feel a stronger
connection to your brand.
Craft Engaging Copy:
When it comes to emotional design, the words you use in your product are
just as importan t as the visual elements. The right words can make your
users feel welcomed, understoo d, and supported – like they are getting a
warm hug from a friend.
Think about the last time you received a really great email or text message
from someone you care about. Chances are, the words they used made you
feel good – maybe they made you laugh, or feel appreciated, or just feel like
someone was thinking about you.
Asana’ s Crash Pr ompt
The same princ iple applies to the copy in your product. The words you
choose can have a big impact on how your users feel when they interact
with your product. They can help create a sense of warmth, friendliness,
and emotional connection that keeps users coming back for more.
So, how do you craft engaging copy that creates an emotional connection
with your users? Here are a few tips:
Use Warm and Friendly Language:  Choose  words that feel warm,
friendly , and approachable. Use contractions (like "you are" instead of "you
are"), and write in a conversational tone that feels like you are talking to a
friend. Avoid jargon or overly formal language that can feel cold or
impersonal.
Be Enco uraging and Supportive:  Use language that is encouraging and
supportive, and that helps users feel like they are making progress and
achieving their goals. Celebrate their successes, and offer encouragement
when they face challenges. Make them feel like you are on their side and
cheering them on.
Show Empathy and Understanding:  Use language that shows empathy
and understanding for your user needs, challenges, and emotions.
Acknowledge their pain points and frustrations, and offer solutions and
support. Make them feel like you "get" them and are there to help.
Be Clear  and Concise:  While it is important to use warm and friendly
language, it is also important to be clear and concise. Avoid lengthy or
convoluted sentences that can be hard to follow . Use simple,
straightforward language that gets to the point and helps users take action.
Infuse Personality:  do not be afraid to infuse your copy with personality
and humor (when appropriate). Use language that reﬂects your brand voice
and helps create a unique and memorable experience for your users. Just be
sure to strike the right balance and avoid overusing jokes or puns that can
feel forced or detract from the user experience.
Examples of Engaging Copy
Here are a few examples of engaging copy that creates an emotional
connection with users:
●         "You have got this! " (Encouragement and support)
●         "We know how frustrating it can be when... " (Empathy and
understanding)
●         "Congrats on hitting your goal! " (Celebration and positive
reinforcement)
●         "We are here to help make your life a little easier ." (Helpfulness and
support)
●         "Let us tackle this together ." (Collaboration and partnership)
Notice how these examples use warm, friendly , and supportive language
that helps users feel understood and valued. They create a sense of
connection and partnership between the user and the product.
Crafting engagin g copy is a crucial part of creating an emotionally resonant
user experience. By using warm, friendly , and supportive language, you can
help your users feel understood,  encouraged, and valued – like they have a
true partner in your product.
So, the next time you are writin g a copy for your product, think about the
emotional impac t you want to create. Choose words that feel like a warm
hug, a pat on the back, or a friendly smile. Infuse your copy with
personality and empathy , and make your users feel like they are part of
something special.
By intentionally  designing for emotion, you can create a product that not
only functions well, but also feels meaningful, memorable , and truly
valuable to your users.

★      Has my product been designed to create shareable moments of

Disregarding Fitts's Law
Fitts's Law is a fundamental principle of human-computer interaction that
describes the relationship between the size and distance of a target and the
time it takes for a user to interact with it. In simple terms, it means that the
larger and closer a tar get is, the faster and easier it is for users to hit it.
At its core, Fitts's Law is all about making things easy for your users. It is a
scientiﬁc principle that explains how the size and distance of a target affects
the time it takes for a user to move their cursor or ﬁnger to that target. In
other words, Fitts's Law helps us understand how to design interfaces that
are easy and efﬁcient to interact with. It is like the secret sauce of UX
design – the key to creating buttons, links, and controls that feel intuitive
and satisfying to use.
How Fitts's Law Works
Fitts's Law is based on a simple equation that looks like this:
do not worry if that looks like gibberish to you - Here is what it means in
plain English:
●         MT stands for "movement time" - the time it takes for a user to move
their cursor or ﬁnger to a tar get.
●         a and b are constants that depend on the speciﬁc device and interface
being used.
●         D stands for "distance" - the distance between the user's starting
position and the center of the tar get.
●         W stands for "width" - the size of the tar get along the axis of motion.
In other words, Fitts's Law tells us that the time it takes for a user to hit a
target depends on two things: the distance to the target, and the size of the
target. The farth er away a target is, the longer it will take to reach it. And
the smaller a tar get is, the more precisely the user will have to aim to hit it.
But when designers neglect Fitts's Law, they create interfaces that feel
clunky , frustrating, and difﬁcult  to use. They scatter tiny buttons and links
across the screen, or place important controls in hard-to-reach corners –
leaving users struggling to navigate the interface and complete their tasks.
Imagine trying to thread a needle while wearing oven mitts – that is how it
feels when you are trying to tap a tiny button on a poorly designed
interface. It is frustrating, time- consuming, and can lead to a lot of missed
shots and wasted ef fort.
Apple's iT unes 1 1 Sidebar  Removal (2012)
In late 2012, Apple released iTunes 11, a major redesign of its popular
media player and management software. One of the most  signiﬁcant
changes was the removal of the sidebar , a longstanding navigation feature
that had been present since the software's early versions.
The sidebar had provided quick access to different media types (Music,
Movies, TV Shows, etc.) and playlists. Its removal meant users had to
navigate through a series of dropdown menus instead. Without the sidebar ,
users had to move their cursors much farther across the screen to access
different section s of their library . The sidebar had made various options
immediately visible. Its removal meant many features were now hidden
behind menus, increasing the cognitive load on users. The new design
scattered controls across the interface, often placing them far from where
users expected to ﬁnd them.
As a result, users reported taking signiﬁcantly longer to perform previously
simple tasks, like switching between different media types or accessing
playlists. The dramatic chang e in navigation led to widespread user
complaints and negative reviews. Even long-time iTunes users had to
relearn how to use the software, leading to temporary drops in productivity .
The increased mouse travel and smaller click targets would have made the
software little more difﬁcult to use for people with motor impairments.
In response to user feedback, Apple made several changes in subsequent
updates. In iTunes 12 (2014), Apple brought back an optional sidebar ,
allowing users to choose their preferred navigation method. Controls and
features were reorganized to reduce unnecessary mouse travel. Apple
increased the size of buttons and other clickable elements in later versions.
Users were given more control over the interface layout, allow ing them to
optimize for their own usage patterns.
Drastic changes to established navigation patterns can signiﬁcantly impact
user experience, even in well-known software. Fitts's Law applies not just
to indiv idual elements, but to the overall layout and ﬂow of an interface.
Gradual evolution of design might be preferable to radical overhauls,
especially for software with a lar ge, established user base.
Make Targets Large Enough:  Ensure that clickable targets like buttons,
links, and controls are large enough for users to easily tap or click. A good
rule of thumb is to make targets at least 44x44 pixels in size, or about the
size of a ﬁngertip on a mobile device.
As a general rule of thumb, it is recommended to make your targets at least
44x44 pixels in size. This is based on the average size of a human ﬁngertip,
which is about 10mm wide.
By making your targets at least 44x44 pixels, you ensure that they are large
enough to be easily tapped by most users, even if their ﬁngers are slightly
off-center . This helps reduce errors and frustration, and makes your
interface feel more responsive and easier to use.
Buttons are one of the most common interactive elements in UI design. To
make them easy to tap, they should be at least 44x44 pixels in size, with
clear labels and plenty of space around them. For example, the "Add to
Cart" button on an e-commerce site should be large and prominent, so users
can easily tap it to make a purchase.
Place Targets Strategically:
Place important targets in easy- to-reach locations, such as the center of the
screen or near the user's natural ﬁnger or mouse position. Avoid placing tiny
targets in corners or edges where they are hard to hit accurately . When it
comes to UI design, it is not just about making your targets big enough – it
is also about putting them in the right place. Just like in real estate, location
is everything!
Think about it: if you are trying to ﬁnd a speciﬁc button or link on a website
or app, you do not want to have  to hunt all over the screen for it. You want
it to be right where you expect it to be, so you can ﬁnd it and tap it easily .
That is where strategic target placement comes in. By putting your targets
in the right locations, you can make your UI feel intuitive and easy to
navigate – like everything is right where it should be.
So, how can you place your targets strategically in your UI desig n? Here are
a few tips:
Follow convent ions: Whenever possible, follow existing conventions for
where certain types of targets should be located. For example, users expect
to ﬁnd the "back" button in the top-left corner of a mobile app, and the
"search" button in the top-right corner of a website.
Group related targets together : If you have multiple targets that are
related to each other (like a set of navigation links or a group of form
controls), try to group them together in a logical way. This makes it easier
for users to ﬁnd and use them in context.
Put important targets in easy-to-r each places : Make sure your most
important or frequently-used targets are in easy-to-reach locatio ns, like the
center of the screen or near the user's thumb on a mobile device. Avoid
putting critical tar gets in hard-to-reach places like the corners of the screen.
Use visual hierar chy: Use visual hierarchy to guide users' attention to the
most important targets ﬁrst. You can do this by making key targets larger,
bolder , or more colorful than less important ones.
Imagine you are designing a mobile app for a food delivery service. One of
the most impor tant targets in the app is the "Order Now" button, which
allows users to place an order for their favorite meals.
To make this tar get easy to ﬁnd and tap, you might:
●         Place it in the center of the screen, where it is easy to reach with a
thumb
●         Make it large and colorful, so it stands out from the other eleme nts on
the screen
●         Surround it with plenty of whitespace, so it is easy to tap without
accidentally hitting something else
By placing this key target strategically , you make it easy for users to ﬁnd
and use the most important feature of your app – ordering food!
When it comes to UI design, it is not just about what you put on the screen
– it is also about what you leave  out. And one of the most important things
to leave out is clutter!
That is where padding and spacing come in. Padding is the space inside a
target (like a button or a form ﬁeld), while spacing is the space between
targets. By using padding and spacing strategically , you can give your
targets some breathing room and make your UI feel more open, organized,
and easy to use.
The Importance of Padding and Spacing
Using padding and spacing ef fectively is important for a few key reasons:
●         Clarity : When your targets have plenty of space around them, they
are easier to see and distinguish from each other . This makes your UI
feel clearer and more organized, and helps users focus on one thing at
a time.
●         Readability : When your text has plenty of padding and spacing, it is
easier to read and scan. This is especially important for long-form
content like articles or instructions, where users need to be able to
quickly ﬁnd and process information.
●         Touchability : When your targets have enough padding and spacing
around them, they are easier to tap or click without accidentally hitting
something else. This is especially important on mobile devices, where
users are interacting with your UI using their ﬁngers.
Tips for  Using Padding and Spacing
So, how can you use padding and spacing effectively in your UI design?
Here are a few tips:
●         Use consistent values : Try to use consistent values for padding and
spacing throughout your UI. This helps create a sense of visual
consistency and makes your UI feel more cohesive.
●         Give targets enough space : Make sure your targets have enough
padding and spacing around them to be easily tappable or clickable. A
good rule of thumb is to have at least 8px of padding inside a target,
and at least 16px of spacing between tar gets.
●         Group related elements : Use spacing to group related elements
together and separate them from unrelated elements. This helps create
visual hierarchy and makes your UI feel more organized and easier to
navigate.
●         Use whitespace strategically : do not be afraid to use whites pace
(empty space) strategically in your UI. Whitespace can help create a
sense of openness and clarity , and guide users' attention to the most
important elements on the screen.
●         Test with real content : When designing your UI, test it with real
content (text, images, etc.) to make sure the padding and spacin g still
work well. Sometimes, what looks good with placeholder content
might feel cramped or cluttered with real content.
An Example of Effective Padding and Spacing
let us look at an example of how padding and spacing can be used
effectively in a real-world UI design:
Imagine you are designing a form for a sign-up page. The form has several
ﬁelds (like name, email, and password) and a submit button.
To make this form easy to use and understand, you might:
●         Give each form ﬁeld plenty of padding (say, 12px) so the text inside
is easy to read and the ﬁeld is easy to tap or click
●         Add spacing (say, 24px) betwee n each form ﬁeld to visually separate
them and make the form feel less cramped
●         Put extra spacing (say, 48px) between the form ﬁelds and the submit
button to make it clear that the button is a separate action
By using padding and spacing strategically , you create a form  that feels
open, or ganized, and easy to use – even if it has a lot of ﬁelds!
Use Padding and Spacing:  Use padding and spacing around targets to
create clear visual and physical separation, and to make it easier for users to
hit the right tar get without accidentally hitting the wrong one.
Optimize for Differ ent Device s: Conside r the different ways users will be
interacting with your interface across different devices and screen sizes.
Optimize your targets and layouts for touch, mouse, and keyboard
interactions as needed. We discussed this more in other sections
By desig ning interfaces that comply with Fitts's Law and prioritize easy,
efﬁcient interac tion, you can create a more satisfying and effective user
experience – one that feels like a natural extension of your users' abilities
and intentions.

my product?

interaction?

screen?

(touch, mouse, keyboard)?

interface?

accidental taps?

movement?

reach?

reach zones?

or tappable?

key UI elements?

interaction patterns?

through my product?

frequently used controls?
★      Have I used consistent sizing for similar types of interactive elements
throughout my product?
Neglecting Visual Consistency in Err or States
Treating error states as an afterthought rather than an intentionally designed
experience. This results in error messages, screens, and UI elements that
look jarring and out-of-place compared to the normal product interface.
Imagine you are trying out a hot new recipe app for delicious home-cooked
meals. The app's design is sleek and modern, with a minim alist color
scheme of muted grays and elegant typography . As you are browsing
recipes, you accidentally tap the wrong button. Suddenly , an error message
window pops up that looks like it was yanked straight out of an ancient
computer progra m from the 90s. Bright red text screaming "ERROR!" in an
intense, harsh font. Chunky blue title bars. Harsh square corners
everywhere.
Not every err or message needs a popup, it is like blaming the user
It is a total visual slap in the face compared to the reﬁned, calmi ng aesthetic
you were just immersed in seconds ago. The error screen sticks out like a
sore thumb, feeling completel y alien and disconnected from the app's
established look and feel. In that moment of surprise and confusion, the
jarring inconsistent design makes it seriously difﬁcult to understand and
process what went wrong. you are struck by how drastically different this
error experience looks from the app's normal state. it is so unfamiliar that
you need to freeze up, uncertain of what to do next.
This is the core mistake - failing to maintain visual consistency and
language contin uity when transitioning to error states. Rather than gently
guiding you through the resolut ion, this inconsistent approach puzzles and
frustrates you.
A well-designed error experience should have looked and felt like a
seamless extensi on of the app's interface, just optimized for communicating
the erroneous situation. The same clean aesthetic, typograp hy, and UI
patterns - simply repurposed to provide clarity around the issue in a way
that puts you at ease.
Design error experiences that maintain visual consistency with the
established UI patterns and styles used throughout your product's core
experiences.
This provides a reassuring familiar environment for users to interpret and
resolve errors from.
Error states should adhere to the same design system principles:
●         Styles like typography , colors, icons
●         UI element construction like buttons, input ﬁelds
●         Layout, spacing and grid behaviors
●         Accessibility and responsiveness standards
The goal is for error moments to still feel like a natural integrated part of
your product experience rather than a completely separate realm. The key is
treating error state design with the same level of care and attention as your
product's core user interfaces. Error messages, screens, and patterns should
feel like natural, integrated extensions of your overall visual language and
brand expression.
Do not go rogue with aggressive, jarring treatments just because it is an
error scenario. For example, if your product has a clean, minimal aesthetic
with ample whitespace and soft accent colors, maintain that look and feel.
An error modal should integrate seamlessly , not stick out like a red, ﬂashing
eyesore.
For communicating the error itself, lean on micro-copy best practices with
your brand's familiar voice and terminology . Instead of cold technical
jargon, provide friendly , easily understood error descriptions and resolution
steps aligned with how your product normally communicates. You can still
differentiate errors with subtle visual cues and affordances that are not out
of context. Think temporary icon badging, pulsing UI element states, or
composed color  highlights within your existing palette. But avoid extreme
deviations from  your core styles. The overall page structure, components,
and layouts should adhere to your design system's reusable patterns. An
inline form error should not completely reﬂow the UI into an unfamiliar
stack - simply highlight the problematic ﬁelds.
For particularly  destructive or unrecoverable scenarios, you may need a
dedicated static  error screen or page state. But even here, its aesthetic
compositions should still align with your product's foundational visual
architecture and brand attributes.

Under estimating the Impact of Micr ocopy
Microcopy refers to those little bits of text you ﬁnd sprinkled throughout
user interfaces - things like button labels, input ﬁeld instructions, error
messages, tool tips, and menu item descriptions. While tiny, this "micro"
content plays an oversized role in shaping the user experience.
These little text blurbs are not just space ﬁllers - they are crucial bits of
functional writing providing context and guidance for users as they navigate
your product's layout and features. When utilized thoughtfully , microcopy
bridges the gap between visuals and functionality .
Typeform's Conversational Micr ocopy
Typeform, a company that specializes in online form building and surveys,
provides an excellent examp le of how microcopy can transform a
potentially dull user experience into an engaging one.
In 2015, Typeform recognized that ﬁlling out forms was often seen as a
tedious task by users. They decided to revolutionize this experience by
using conversational microcopy throughout their platform, both for form
creators and respondents.
The company's approach was to make form-ﬁlling feel more like a natural
conversation rather than a series of questions. Here is how they
implemented this strategy: Instead of traditional form labels like "Name:" or
"Email Address:", Typeform used conversational prompts. For example, a
form might start with "Hi there!  What is your name?" This immediately set
a friendlier tone. For multiple-choice questions, rather than just listing
options, Typeform would phrase it conversationally: "Which of these
sounds most like you?" This made the process feel more personal and
engaging.
When users were creating forms, Typeform used encouraging microcopy .
Instead of "Add Question," the button read "What would you like to ask?"
This subtle change made the form creation process feel more intuitive and
less technical. Error messages were written to sound like a helpful friend
rather than a computer . If a required ﬁeld was left empty , inste ad of "This
ﬁeld is required, " users would see something like "Oops! We need this info
to continue. Mind ﬁlling it in?"
Progress indicators were also given a conversational twist. Instead of "50%
complete," users  might see "Halfway there! You are doing great. " At the end
of a form, instead of a plain "Submit" button, Typeform might use "All
done! Send it my way." This maintained the conversational  tone right
through to the end of the interaction.
The results of this microcopy strategy were impressive:
Typeform saw a 57% increas e in form completion rates across their
platform. The number of busine sses using Typeform grew by 120% in the
two years following the implementation of conversational microcopy . In
surveys, 89% of respondents described Typeform as "enjoya ble to use,"
compared to 62% for traditional form tools. Form creators spent  on average
20% more time on the platform , indicating increased engagement with the
tool. Typeform saw a 35% increase in organic referrals, as users shared
forms they found engaging. Typeform became known for its conversational
approach, setting it apart in the crowded form-building market.
Effective microcopy ampliﬁes the impact of your visual design by:
Reducing Confusion
It explains the meaning and purpose behind UI elements when it is not
obvious from visuals alone. At its core, effective user interface design is
about clear communication and ensuring users understand how to properly
interact with each element. While visuals like icons, colors, and layouts
provide important context clues, microcopy is often the MVP  in eliminating
ambiguity .
Take a look at search input ﬁelds for instance. An icon of a magnifying
glass provides a visual hint that this UI element relates to search
functionality . However , the microcopy label "Search" or "Search recipes..."
takes it a step further by explicitly clarifying this input's purpose and
reducing any remaining uncertainties.
Similarly , for submission buttons, the microcopy label is crucial. Using
"Submit" alone leaves room for confusion over what is actually being
submitted. More descriptive options like "Submit Payment" or "Create
Account" provide reassuring speciﬁcity . Even common icons and UI
patterns are not always obviou s, especially for less tech-savvy users. A
microcopy tooltip hovering over the triple-line "hambur ger" menu icon
could explain "Tap here to explor e more sections. " Simple, but extremely
clarifying.
Format example s and ﬁeld instructions are another way microcopy prevents
misunderstandings. For email inputs, ' name@example.com' as greyed
placeholder text sets clear expectations. And for sensitive  ﬁelds like
passwords, legends like "Must be at least 8 characters" eliminate
guesswork.
Microcopy also becomes pivotal for differentiating similar -looking
components with distinct actions. Two identically styled buttons could be
clariﬁed with labels like "Save as Draft" versus "Publish Live" to ensure the
right intent is conveyed. And sometimes you need microcopy because there
simply are not common visual shorthand symbols. Terms like "Subscribe"
or "Unfollow" require a straig htforward text label for users to properly
comprehend.
In each case, microcopy bridges the gap between visual design and
functionality by providing simple, no-nonsense speciﬁcs. The labels,
instructions, and descriptions give users the added clarity they need to feel
conﬁdent and in control - rather  than second-guessing based on ambiguous
visuals alone. So, while icons and visual treatments are incredibly important
for brand cohesion and comprehension, strategic microcopy is what allows
UIs to be utterly  unambiguous. With the right text in the right contexts, you
transform your visual elements from being interpretable to being intuitive.
Providing Instruction
Whether describing input requirements, ﬂow sequences or feature usage,
good microcopy  prevents users from feeling lost or stuck within your
layouts through guiding cues. Providing clear instructional microcopy is
critical for ensuring users feel guided and empowered as they navigate your
product's UI and features. Even the most thoughtful visual design cannot
compensate for a lack of instru ctional text support. Let me illustrate with
some examples:
Input Fields Single-line input ﬁelds often need instruction on formats,
requirements, and examples:
Poor: [ ___________________ ]
Better: Email [ _____________________ ]
Best: Work Email [name@company .com]
Multi-Step Flows Numbering, progress indicators and brief sequential
instructions prevent losing context in stepped processes:
Poor: " Next Step "
Better: " 2/4 - Payment Details "
Best: "2/4 - Enter your credit card or PayPal details to complete this
purchase. "
Feature Tooltips For new or power features, tooltip microcopy provides
easily scannable use case instructions:
Poor: "?" Icon
Better: " Schedule an email to send later "
Best: "Schedule Draft - This allows you to write emails now and choose a
speciﬁc send date/time. "
Dynamic Updates - When UIs update based on user interactions, microcopy
alleviates confusion over changes:
Poor: screen elements shift unexpectedly
Better: " Showing r esults for 'summer dr esses' "
Best: "Neat! Based on your location, we have updated with desig ners
popular in Chicago. "
Error Prevention - Instructive microcopy before destructive actions advises
users and prevents mistakes:
Poor: "Delete"
Better: " Delete this draft for ever? "
Best: "Think this one's a dud? No worries - you can delete this draft
permanently at any time. "
By providing this guiding, instructional microcopy content throughout your
UI, you ensure users never feel stuck, lost, or accidentally perform
unintended actions. Each bit of copy provides the context and
straightforward advice they need to conﬁdently navigate and properly
utilize features.
Without this instructional guidance, you risk users becoming frustrated and
puzzled. They will abandon processes and miss out on key functionality -
all because there was no frien dly voice offering simple directions when
visual cues proved insufﬁcient. But when your designs are supported by a
thoughtful instructional language framework, you create an assisted
experience wher e every step and possibility is clearly articulated. Users stay
oriented, empowered, and able to properly leverage your product's
potential.
The combination of visual design and instructional microcopy simply
makes for radically more intuitive and frictionless user experiences. do not
let usability fall apart due to lack of helpful instructions!
Injecting Personality
Microcopy is your chance to reinforce brand voice and tone within the UI.
Done well, it humanizes the experience and builds rapport with delightful
writing. Injecting personality through microcopy is a hugely impactful way
to humanize your product's user experience and forge real conn ections with
users. These tiny text snippets provide the opportunity to showcase your
brand's unique voice and build  delightful rapport. Let me illustrate with
some examples:
Error Message s: Error messages are typically seen as frustrating negative
experiences. But with a personal brand voice, you can defuse frustration:
Generic: "An error occurr ed. Please try again later." Brand Voice: "Uh-oh,
looks like we hit a small hiccup there! Give it another go and I'm sure We
will get you sorted. "
Empty States: When interfaces are blank due to no data, microcopy gives
you a chance to inject deligh t and personality: Generic: "No data to
display ." Brand Voice: "This place is looking a little...empty . That just will
not do! Why do not you upload some snazzy pics to spruce it up? "
Conﬁrmations: Even basic user conﬁrmations allow you to celebrate with
a spot of branded positivity:
Generic: "Upload complete. " Brand Voice: "Boom! That new cover pic is
looking absolutely  ��. Portfolio updated just like that! "
Progress Updates:  Microcopying progress states provides space to
entertain users during wait times: Generic: "Loading..."
Brand Voice: "Just putting the ﬁnishing touches on your tappity-tap
dashboar d. One sec! "
Instructional Guides:  Setting up new users? Use this chance to bake your
brand's friendly voice right into onboarding: Generic: "Click the button to
get started." Brand  Voice: "Feeling a little lost? Kick off your
Wanderlusting adventur es by smashing that 'Start Exploring' button! "
See how each of these injects  doses of delightful personality that build
emotional resonance? By imbuing your UI's microcopy with your brand's
unique tone and manner of speaking, you essentially make your product
feel like it has a fun persona.
It huma nizes the experience - transforming it from a cold technical
implementation into a friendly , guided interaction with emotional rapport.
Rather than feeling like another piece of faceless software, your product's
voice and writing style start to feel like an affable conversation with
someone you are building a real connection with.
Ultimately , nailing your brand voice's conversational personality in
microcopy facilitates experiences that are perpetually engaging and
charming. Every  little user touchpoint has the possibility to surprise and
delight with clever writing that reinforces your style and values.
So have fun with it! do not be afraid to get playful, empathetic, or even a bit
cheeky through your microcopy . Leveraging your brand's unique voice is a
huge opportunity to create resonant, human-centered experiences rather
than just dry user interfaces.
Enhancing Usability
Clear microcopy improves scannability and makes interfaces feel more
intuitive overal l. Descriptive labels facilitate faster recog nition and
streamlined workﬂows. Microcopy plays a pivotal role in enhancing the
overall usability and intuitive feel of user interfaces. Descriptive labels and
clear language facilitate faster recognizability and comprehension, guiding
users seamlessly through ﬂows and interactions. let us look at some
examples:
Navigation Menus: Ambiguous menu titles can leave users unsure where
to go to complete tasks. Descr iptive microcopy makes destinations clear:
Poor: "Manage" Better: "Account Settings " Best: "Update Your Proﬁle &
Preferences "
Button Labels:  Well-written button microcopy reinforces which action will
occur , preventing surprises: Poor: "Submit" Better: "Place Order" Best:
"Complete Pur chase - $49.99 "
Section Titles:  Descrip tive section titles improve scannability so users can
quickly ﬁnd relevant areas: Poor: "Content" Better: "My Videos " Best:
"Your Practice Session Recor dings "
Tooltip Instruction:  Tooltips use microcopy to explain UI elements,
reducing guesswork: Poor: "???" Better: "Click to reorder items " Best:
"Drag and dr op to r eorder your top podcast picks "
Pagination Labels:  Clear pagination microcopy signiﬁes where the user is
in an iterative process:
Poor: "Next" Better: "2 of 12" Best: " Subscription Details: Step 2 of 3 "
In each case, the thoughtful microcopy reinforces functionality , purpose,
and sequential steps - reducing uncertainties. Users can rapidly interpret
interface elements without excess cognitive load. This clear labeling
enhances scanna bility and efﬁcient navigation. Rather than getting hung up
deciphering vague UI elements, users can instinctively interpret purpose
and take action.
Microcopy facilitates comprehen sion, preventing frustrating guesswork that
diminishes usability . UIs transform from ambiguous visual puzzles into
clearly articulated, intuitive experiences.
The interface effortlessly guides users through desired workﬂows with
helpful, descriptive language at every turn. Clean information architecture
combined with supportive, clear microcopy results in smooth, obstruction-
free UX. So, in addition to personality and guidance, well-crafted
microcopy acts as essential foundational UX "glue" - empowering your
users with the simple comprehension they need to leverage your product's
capabilities with conﬁdence and ease.
Let us look at some more common UI elements and how thoughtful
microcopy enhances their usability and impact:
Buttons: Buttons are a core UI element for initiating actions, but their label
microcopy is crucial for communicating purpose. Consider: Poor
microcopy: "Click Me" Better: "Add to Cart" Best: "Move Party Llama to
Cart "
Forms: Forms require tons of microcopy guidance on input requirements,
format examples, error states, and success conﬁrmations. Poor: "Entry
Invalid" Better: "Please enter a valid email address" Best: "Oops! Looks
like 'emailaddr es@oops.com' is not quite right. Mind trying  that email
again for me? "
Menu Navigation:  Menu items need clear language indicating their
purpose, especially for new or ambiguous functions users may not
recognize. Poor: "V iew List" Better: " View Subscriptions " Best: " Manage
your Snacky Boxes subscription "
Tooltips: Tooltips allow you to fully explain UI elements and provide
additional context through temporary microcopy popups. Poor: " ? " Better:
"Search pr oducts, brands, categories " Best: " What snacky tr eats ar e you
craving today? Sear ch by name or br owse categories! "
Progress Loaders:  Loading states are a perfect opportunity to inject brand
personality into normally for gettable microcopy . Poor: "Loading..." Better:
"Fetching your data... " Best: " Crunching the numbers behind that bod of
yours... "
Error Handling:  Errors are famously unfriendly , but with clever
microcopy you can maintain rapport even when stuff goes wrong. Poor:
"Error 404" Better: "Oops, page not found! " Best: "Uh oh, looks like this
page took a wrong turn somewher e! Why do not you check out our brand-
new Shenanigans section instead? "
See how focusi ng on clear , conversational yet nicely branded  microcopy
elevates each of these UI elements? The text provides the context and
guiderails to make visual components intuitive rather than ambiguous.
Without thoughtful microcopy customization, even the most aesthetically
pleasing UI runs the risk of being incomprehensible - just pretty pictures
without any real substance or clarity behind interactions.
But when you pair compelling visual design with best-in-class  microcopy
that informs and delights? That is when you create cohesive, resonant
product experiences that users cannot get enough of.

Ignoring Visual Design for  Differ ent User  States
So many products fall into this trap - designing an awesome experience for
one type of user, but completely dropping the ball when it comes to other
states and scenarios. it is the design version of only planning for sunshine
and getting caught in a torrent ial downpour . Neglecting to consider how
your visuals and UI will need to adapt for different contexts like new users
versus returning  users, or logged-in versus logged-out states. it is having a
one-size-ﬁts-all visual design mentality .
The solution boils down to creating dynamic, adaptable visual designs that
can shape-shift seamlessly based on each user's immediate context and
journey state. Instead of a rigid , one-size-ﬁts-all layout, you need ﬂuid UI
templates that can reconﬁgure their layout, emphasis, and even components
on the ﬂy to ﬁt the situation.
For example, let us say you are designing an online bank's web app. For
brand new users landing on the homepage while logged out, the ideal
experience would be a clean, minimal layout laser-focused on explaining
the bank's value proposition and guiding them through opening a new
account.
The visuals would emphasize simple calls-to-action like "Open Account"
front-and-center , supported by animated explainer videos, testimonial
highlights, or other persuasive content optimized for driving new signups.
But then once that same user creates their account and logs in, boom! The
UI seamlessly transitions to adapt for their new "logged-in" state as an
authenticated customer . This view now prioritizes different core functions
like checking balances, making transfers, paying bills, etc.
The visual emphasis shifts from convincing and educating to providing a
personalized overview of their speciﬁc ﬁnancial activities and needs as an
active customer . More account management options are surfaced while
those initial sign up prompts take a backseat. And then let us say this user
comes back in a week. The UI could re-adapt again, complete ly changing
the home layout to highlight their recent transactions, new billing
notiﬁcations, or recommended actions tailored to their latest use patterns.
The key is that the UI visuals and offered experiences do not remain static
based on a solo perceived default state. They continuously nix and adapt to
stay hyper -relevant for each user's up-to-the-minute context and journey
stage. This level of dynamic visual orchestration provides a coherent,
guided experience tailored to an individual's needs at every turn rather than
fragmented separate silos that disrupt the ﬂow .
The solutions could involve techniques like:
Template Layouts That Show/Hide Sections
This involves having multiple layout templates prepared for different
authentication states (logged in vs out) or user types (new vs returning).
Certain sections like login/sign up prompts can be visually prioritized and
expanded for anonymous visitors. But once authenticated, those guest
sections get minimized or hidden while secured areas are unlocked.
For example, an e-commerce  site could have one layou t template
highlighting the shopping cart and checkout ﬂows for logged out users. But
for recognized members, that same template switches to prioritize their
order history , subscription details and personalized recom mendations
instead.
Smart Content Sections
These are dynamic UI component sections that can intelligently populate
with hyper -relevant content based on the current user's context and data.
For new users, these sections could display tips, tours, and featured
highlights. But for existing customers, they would populate with details like
recent activity summaries or tailored upsell of fers based on their proﬁle.
A smart  section  on a ﬁtness app's home screen could show workout class
schedules for regular subscribed  users. But for churned or new members, it
would showcase promotional videos extolling the class beneﬁts instead.
Visual Flourishes & Transition States
Using animated  transitions, microinteractions and visual ﬂourishes helps
reinforce changes between layout states in a smooth, cohesive way .
For example, modal lightboxe s could animate in with a slick entrance
transition for logged out contexts to grab attention and focus. But in logged-
in modes where familiarity is preferred, they could open with a simpler no-
frills transition state.
These subtle animated delight s help maintain UI continuity and draw
emphasis to what is most important in each unique scenario.
Evolving Layouts
Similar to having different layout templates, evolving layouts can
progressively expose new sections or shifts in UI element
positioning/emphasis over time.
Perhaps for a new user the navigation menu starts extremely stripped down
for simplicity . But as their engagement and authorization levels rise, that
same nav menu progressively "opens up" with new levels of options
unfolding.
Or an onboarding tour could kick off with a fullscreen layout funneling
users through a speciﬁc introduction ﬂow. But as that tour completes across
sessions, the layout shifts to prioritize the core app experience in the front.
The key is being thoughtful about how your UI visuals need to
progressively or regressively evolve and reshape themselves to provide the
most intuitive experience throug hout the entire user journey across all their
ﬂuctuating contexts.
Self-Evaluation

and data?

the product?

experience?

context?
