Heavy Client-Side Rendering
In SaaS develop ment, it is easy to get caught up in the latest and greatest
trends and techn ologies. One such trend that has gained a lot of popularity
in recen t years is client-side rendering (CSR), where the bulk of the
rendering work is done in the user's browser using JavaScript.
Even though CSR gives a livelier and engaging experience, it has a notable
downside too: heavy client-side  rendering might cause longer wait times.
This happens because the browser needs to handle and display a lot of
JavaScript code before the user can view and interact with the page.
The Consequences of Heavy Client-Side Rendering
On the surface,  relying heavily on client-side rendering may not appear
problematic. After all, are not up-to-date browsers quick and robust? They
should manage additional JavaScript without any struggle.
However , the truth is a bit more complex. Let us discuss some possible
outcomes of leaning too much on client-side rendering:
1. Slow Initial Load Times
If a person visits a webpage that depends a lot on rendering on the client's
side, their intern et browser needs to get, comprehend, and run a big chunk
of JavaS cript code. Only then can it begin to display the webpage. This
process can prolong the initial loading duration, even more so on slower
device or network connections.
Let us picture a person using a mobile with a 3G network, attempting to
open a SaaS application loaded with intensive client-side rendering. It could
take them a few seconds, maybe minutes, to see something on their display .
This may cause annoyance and could even push them to give up
completely .
2. Poor  Sear ch Engine Optimization (SEO)
Google and other search engines need to scan and understand a webpage's
content to ﬁgure out how releva nt it is to speciﬁc keywords. But, if a page
uses a lot of client-side renderin g, the ﬁrst chunk of HTML  that gets to the
browser might have hardly any valuable content. The real conte nt is mostly
loaded and shown later by JavaScript.
Search engines might struggle to correctly scan and list the page. It can
result in lower search ratings and decreased exposure for the SaaS
application.
3. Reduced Accessibility
Users with disabilities, who depend on assistive tools like screen readers,
can face issues if a website relies many on client-side rendering. When a
webpage's inform ation is mostly created by JavaScript, these assistive tools
might not gain proper access. This could make it challenging, or even not
feasible, for these individuals to use the site.
4. Incr eased Complexity and Development Overhead
Creating a SaaS application that heavily depends on client-side rendering
might bring extra complications  and development tasks for the team. Rather
than concentrating on developin g the main features and business rules of
the application, developers may notice they are using much of their time
and energy on the ﬁne details of client-side rendering. For example,
handling state, boosting perform ance, and conﬁrming compatib ility across
different browsers.
Extra complexity might slow the rate of development. It could also heighten
the chance of glitches and backsliding. Moreover , it could make introducing
new members to the team more challenging.
The Solution: Server -Side Rendering and Static Site Generation
Thus, if loads of client-side rendering leads to many issues, what is our
other option? Two pivotal methods provide the solution: server -side
rendering (SSR) and static site generation (SSG).
Server -Side Rendering (SSR)
The method in which a web page's ﬁrst display is handled on the server , not
in the user's web browser , is referred to as server -side rendering. When
someone asks for a webpage, the server builds all the necessary HTML
details. It forms everything, including the page's material and design. Then,
it gets sent back to the web browser . The good thing is, the browser can
show this HTML  straight away . There is no need for any JavaS cript to load
or be put into action.
Here is a simpliﬁed example of how SSR works:
A user navigates to a page in the SaaS application.
The browser sends a request to the server for that page.
The server receives the request  and fetches any necessary data
from databases or APIs.
The server uses this data to render the full HTML  for the page,
including the content and layout.
The server sends this HTML  back to the browser as the response.
The browser receives the HTML  and can immediately display it
to the user, without having to wait for any JavaScript to load or
execute.
If the page includes any interactive elements or dynamic
functionality , the necessary JavaScript can be loaded and
executed after the initial HTML  has been displayed.
By shifting the rendering work from the client to the server , SSR can
provide several beneﬁts:
The page loads quicker at ﬁrst because the browser does not have to wait
for JavaScript to load and execu te, it can show the content right away . SEO
gets better since search engines ﬁnd it easier to crawl and index  the content
when it is part of the initial HTML  load. Also, accessibilit y improves
because assistive devices can understand and use the content from the initial
HTML  without difﬁculty .
Yet, SSR does not come witho ut its hurdles and compromises. Creating
pages on the server might take up more resources than doing it on the user's
end. The server  has to handle fetching data and making HTM L for each
command. This could increase server expenses and could possibly slow
down response speed when it gets overly busy .
Moreover , using  SSR can be harder than doing rendering on the client side.
The reason? It takes a lot of planning. The server and the clien t must work
together in a well scheduled way. After the ﬁrst HTML  comes in, the client-
side JavaScript must be able to hydrate right. It should also take charge of
making the page interactive smoothly .
Static Site Generation (SSG)
A method to lessen the load and SEO problems linked with intense client-
side rendering is static site gene ration. With this, the whole website or app
is pre-made as grouped static HTML, CSS, and JavaScript ﬁles. These can
be sent straight from a content delivery network (CDN) or another kind of
static hosting service to the users.
Here is a simpliﬁed example of how SSG works:
The SaaS application is developed as a collection of template s,
components, and content ﬁles (e.g., Markdown, JSON).
A static  site generator (e.g., Next.js, Gatsby , Hugo) is used to
process these ﬁles and generate  a complete set of static HTML,
CSS, and JavaScript ﬁles that represent the entire application.
These static ﬁles are then deployed to a CDN or static hostin g
service.
When a user navigates to a page in the application, the browser
requests the corresponding static HTML  ﬁle from the CDN.
The CDN returns the static HTML  ﬁle to the browser , which can
immediately display it to the user without any further processin g
or rendering.
If the page includes any interactive elements or dynamic
functionality , the necessary JavaScript can be loaded and
executed after the initial HTML  has been displayed.
By pre-building  the entire application as a set of static ﬁles, SSG can
provide several beneﬁts:
Page loads are super quick because the browser only needs to fetch and
show the static HTML  ﬁles. There is no extra processing needed. SEO gets
a boost because all the details of each page are in these static HTML  ﬁles.
That makes it easier for search engines to ﬁnd and list them. Server
expenses and complexities lowe r, since you can serve the app straight from
a CDN. You do not need any dynamic server or backend. Your site's also
more secure and reliable; no server-side processing or logic to manipulate
or mess up.
Yet, SSG comes  with its own set of drawbacks and compromis es that need
attention. Since the whole  app is pre-prepared as ﬁxed ﬁles, any ﬂexible
data or subject matter which must be gathered from APIs or databases
during running will necessitate more client-side JavaScript for management.
This can bring extra sophistication to the building phase and possibly affect
efﬁciency if not executed properly .
Furthermore, using SSG migh t not work well with apps needing often
updates or instant data. This is because each time there is a change in the
content, the static ﬁles must be made again and put back into service.
When choosing between client-side rendering, server -side rendering, or
static site genera tion for your SaaS application, it is key to fully understand
your project's unique demands. Consider these top tips for guidance:
1. Use the right tool for  the job
There is no universal solution  for enhancing render and performance.
Certain applicat ions thrive on the dynamic and adaptable nature of client-
side rendering. Yet some necessitate the swiftness and ease of static site
creation. Opt for the method that syncs best with your application's
objectives, user base, and tech boundaries.
2. Optimize for  performance
It does not matter the method you select for display; you have to enhance
your app's speed at each stage of creation. You need to decreas e the count
and size of JavaScript and CSS ﬁles. You should also compact and enrich
images and other resources. Lastly , use methods like delayed loading and
dividing code to minimize the ﬁrst load size.
3. Test and measur e
Checking your application's efﬁciency regularly is crucial for its running
smoothly . Tools like Google  Lighthouse, WebPageT est, and browser
developer tools are great for this. They can help ﬁnd spots in your code that
slow things down, improve your program and resources, and see how things
change over time.
4. Consider  the user  experience
Finally , the success of your SaaS software hinges on providin g a superb
user experience. This experienc e needs to match what your target audience
is on the hunt for. When you are pondering over display adjustments and
performance tweaks, always keep the value of user engagement  at the fore -
front. Choose gains and improvements that will greatly elevate user
satisfaction and participation.
Picking an appropriate way to depict your work is just part of the struggle.
To genuinely improve your SaaS application's speed, you must adopt a
comprehensive method. This must involve thoughtful planning,
examination, and tracking at each phase of the creation process.

Neglecting to Monitor  Performance Metrics
Many SaaS companies make the mistake of assuming that if there are no
obvious signs of trouble, then everything must be running smoo thly. But the
reality is that performance issue s can often go unnoticed until it is too late -
when users start complaining about slow load times, unresponsive pages, or
frequent errors.
The Consequences of Neglecting Performance Monitoring
Neglecting to monitor performance metrics can have serious consequences
for your SaaS business. Here are just a few of the potential impacts:
1. Poor  User  Experience
The clearest result of ignoring speed tracking can be a bad user experience.
If your product is sluggish, unresponsive, or full of mistakes, users tend to
lose patience. They might even stop using your product entirely .
In today's highly competitive SaaS market, consumers have big
expectations for both reliability and speed. They anticipate your application
to start-up swiftly , respond smoothly to their inputs, and operate perfectly
across different devices and network environments. If your application fails
to meet these expectations, customers are ready to ﬁnd alternatives that
provide a better experience.
2. Reduced User  Engagement and Retention
Bad user experiences can directly hit user involvement and loyalty . When
performance hitches or mistakes  crop up, users are not so eager  to use your
software and more prone to bail out.
Think about a person using your product to ﬁnish an important job. They
encounter slow loading and pages that do not respond. The user attempts to
ﬁnish the job but without success. Eventually , they become upset and exit
your program, never to come back.
Gradually , such experiences can lead to considerable reductions in user
interest and loyalty . This could  also fuel negative talk about your brand.
Eventually , it can harm your brand's image and make obtainin g new users
more challenging.
3. Incr eased Support Costs
Unnoticed and unresolved performance problems can cause an uptick in
help requests from annoyed users. This could stress your help team and
raise your assist costs, as representatives grapple to handle the ﬂood of
tickets and quarrels.
Beyond the straightforward expenses of managing customer queries, hidden
costs from subpar operation play a role too. These include a slump in
productivity and lower customer happiness. If people continually face
problems and need to contact customer support, their work effectiveness
tends to drop. This leads to lower satisfaction with your product.
Consequently , it can af fect how much they use it and stick with it.
4. Missed Opportunities for  Optimization
If you ignore tracking performance stats, you miss chances to make your
app better . You can learn from performance information. It show s how your
app is used, where it slows down and where you need to make things better .
For instance, keeping track of how fast pages load helps highlight those
pages that take longer , pointing out parts of your application that might
need a tune-up. This could include making images smaller or cutting down
on HTTP requests. Just like that, by looking for common errors and keeping
tabs on the frequency of these mistakes, you can ﬁgure out which tweaks
and bug repairs will make users happier and more satisﬁed.
Without such data-guided know ledge, your work regarding performance
enhancement is blind. You depend on guesses and assumptions, not
concrete facts.
The Solution: Compr ehensive Performance Monitoring
Ignoring perform ance tracking can lead to problems, so it is vital to use a
thorough monitoring plan. This plan should keep tabs on crucial
performance numbers throughout your entire program. Let us go over some
important rules to stick to:
1. Identify Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)
When starting a system to watc h performance, the initial move is to ﬁgure
out the critical performance factors (KPIs). These are vital for your
software. Examples might include:
●         Page load times: How long does it take for each page of your
application to load, from the initial request to the ﬁnal render?
●         Server response  times: How long does it take for your server to
respond to requests from the client, and how does this vary by
endpoint or resource?
●         Error rates: How frequently are users encountering errors, and what
types of errors are most common (e.g., 404 errors, 500 errors,
JavaScript errors)?
●         Throughput: How many requests can your server handle per second,
and how does this vary by endpoint or resource?
●         Resource utilization: How much CPU, memory , and network
bandwidth is your application consuming, and how does this vary by
server or instance?
By identifying the KPIs that are most relevant to your application and
business goals, you can focus your monitoring efforts on the metrics that
matter most.
2. Choose the Right Monitoring Tools
After spotting your crucial performance metrics, the following  move is to
pick suitable tracking tools for them. Plenty of monitoring tools exist,
varying from basic open-source resources to extensive paid platforms.
Some common types of monitoring tools include:
Software that monitors how well applications are working (APM tools):
These tools help you keep an eye on your software from start to ﬁnish. They
tell you how it is doing from the user's end to the server's end. These tools
track different things. These include the speed of loading a webpage, how
quickly the server responds, and how many mistakes happen. The tools also
give a thorough breakdown of every action made within the software, as
well as going deep into code to identify problems.
Infrastructure Monitoring tools: They keep an eye on how your basic
systems are doing. Think about your servers, databases, and networks.
These tools watch things like how much computer power you are using,
how much memory is being used, and how much data is moving through
your network. They also let you know if something is going wrong and
when it is time to step in.
Real User Monitoring (RUM) tools: These items follow the exact user
interaction of your program. They gather information straight from real
users when they use your application in reality . These tools offer
understanding into numbers such as how long a page takes to load, error
percentages, and user paths. Also, they lend a hand in spotting performance
issues that might only occur on certain browsers, equipment, or places.
Picking out the right monitoring tools requires thought. Points like user -
friendliness, adjustability , matching with your current tech tools, and price
are crucial. Consider mixing various tools for a wider view of how your
application is doing.
3. Set Up Alerts and Notiﬁcations
After setting up your observing instruments, it becomes crucial  to establish
alerts and messages to activel y ﬁnd and react to operationa l problems.
Warnings can get activated due to speciﬁed limits or oddities, such as an
unexpected rise in mistake percentages or a reduction in output.
Creating alerts requires a careful balance. They need to be sensitive enough
to quickly ﬂag real problems, yet not create a ruckus with pointless
warnings or tiny changes.
Some best practices for setting up alerts include:
●         Deﬁning clear thresholds and baselines for each metric, based on
historical data and business requirements.
●         Using dynamic thresholds that adjust based on patterns and trends,
rather than static thresholds that may become outdated or irrelevant
over time.
●         Prioritizing alerts based on severity and impact, so that the most
critical issues are addressed ﬁrst.
●         Routing alerts to the appropriate  teams or individuals, based on their
roles and responsibilities.
●         Providing context and actionabl e insights with each alert, rather than
just raw data or generic messages.
By setting up ef fective alerts and notiﬁcations, you can ensure that
performance issues are detected and addressed in a timely manner , before
they have a signiﬁcant impact on user experience or business outcomes.
4. Conduct Regular  Performance Reviews
Along with immediate tracking and notiﬁcations, carrying out consistent
check-ups on how things are moving is key. These check-ups help point out
ongoing patterns, themes, and opportunities to make things better . These
assessments should be done often like every week, month, or three months.
It is also a good idea to includ e a team of designers, manage r, and those
who have a stake in the business.
During a performance review , you should:
●         Review key performance metric s and trends over time, looking for
patterns and anomalies that may indicate underlying issues or
opportunities for improvement.
●         Analyze the root causes of any performance issues that were detected
and resolved during the period, and identify any systemic issues or
areas for improvement.
●         Discuss any planned changes or updates to the application or
infrastructure, and assess their potential impact on performance.
●         Identify any new or emer ging performance risks or requireme nts,
based on changes in the business or technology landscape.
●         Prioritize performance optimization efforts based on their potential
impact and feasibility , and assign ownership and timelines for each
initiative.
Regular performance reviews are critical. They keep performan ce front and
center for your SaaS business, and they help you constantly better and ﬁne-
tune your application over time. do not delay until your users voice
performance problems - begin tracking your numbers now. Take steps ahead
of time to reﬁne and enhance your application over the course.

Not Using Asynchr onous Loading
User comfort is king. Customers want speedy , re active platforms that load
in a ﬂash and give a smooth journey . Still, lots of SaaS ﬁrms  slip up by
selecting synchr onous loading for all their tools. This can drasti cally reduce
the speed at which a webpag e displays and deﬁnitely hurt the user's
experience.
The term 'synchronous loading' explains how resources like scripts,
stylesheets, or pictures are loaded in a speciﬁc order . One after the other ,
where each must completely load before the next starts. If one item takes
too long to load, it might pause the whole webpage from showing up. This
can make the webpage slow and could annoy users.
The Consequences of Synchr onous Loading
Failing to use asynchronous loading can have serious consequences for
your SaaS application. Here are just a few of the potential impacts:
1. Poor  User  Experience
Synchronous loading's main drawback is it disrupts the user experience. If
people are made to wait for a webpage to load for a too long, it can lead to
irritation. Worse, they might stop using your application.
In our modern, quick-moving era, people demand impressive performance
and swift responses. They want pages to pop up without delay . If your
product does not match up to these standards, they will not think twice
about searching elsewhere for better user experience.
2. Incr eased Bounce Rates
When pages load all at once, you might see more people leave your website
because it takes too long. This is a big problem for main pages or places
people ﬁrst see when they visit your website. The ﬁrst impressions they get
are super important.
Let us say, for instance, a person taps your SaaS app's ad. But instead of
expected results, they see a plain white screen. It takes a few moments to
fully load. The webpage eventually pops up. However , the user might have
become disinterested by then. They may have navigated to a rival's website.
When people leave your website quickly , it is called a high bounce rate.
This can lower your ranking on search engines. This means search engines
think users do not ﬁnd your content useful. It becomes difﬁcult for possible
customers to ﬁnd your app naturally . It puts a limit on how much your
business can grow by getting new users.
3. Reduced Conversion Rates
Directly , synchro nous loading can affect your conversion rates. When pages
take time to load, users are less inclined to continue. They might not
complete impor tant steps like signing up for a no-cost trial or buying
something.
Think about a person who wants to use your SaaS program. They get
annoyed by the slowness and quit before they ﬁnish subscribing. You lose a
possible customer . And, their bad experience might stop them from trying
again later .
Lower conversion rates might affect your earnings and proﬁt in a big way.
Even minor enhancements in conversion rates can result in considerable
revenue growth over time.
4. Incr eased Infrastructur e Costs
Ultimately , loading everything at once can result in higher operational
expenses. This is because your servers must exert more effort and time to
provide all the necessities for a webpage to load fully .
If a webpage loads in order , the server pauses for each resource to complete
loading before going to the next. This makes the server get stuck at each
resource during loading, increas ing both CPU and memory demands. It also
lengthens the reply times.
As time goes by, using more resources can become expensive. It might lead
to hefty infrastructure fees becau se you could have to get more servers. You
may also need to improve your current setup to deal with the growing
demand. This can pose a big challenge for SaaS applications that have a lot
of users or require intricate resources.
The Solution: Asynchr onous Loading Best Practices
It is vital to apply asynchronous loading for non-critical resourc es to dodge
the impacts of simultaneous loading and enhance user interaction. This type
of loadi ng lets the necessary components of a webpage, like the HTML,
CSS, and key scripts, be loaded before everything else. Meanwhile, the less
important elements, like images , videos, and scripts from other parties, get
loaded in the backdrop.
Here are some best practices for implementing asynchronous  loading in
your SaaS application:
1. Prioritize Critical Resour ces
The ﬁrst step in implementing asynchronous loading is to prioritize the
critical resource s that are essential for rendering the basic structure and
content of a webpage. These typically include:
- The HTML  document itself
- The main CSS stylesheet(s)
- Any essential JavaScript ﬁles required for basic functionality  (such as a
framework or library)
It is crucial that these key resources load in sync, as they make the webpage
work properly . Yet, keeping these elements as lean and streamlined as we
can is vital. It helps to lessen the load times ef fect.
2. Defer  Non-Essential Resour ces
After the key resources are loaded, we can defer other non-essential
resources. They can load quietly in the background. Here are few examples:
●         Images and videos
●         Third-party scripts (such as analytics or social media widgets)
●         Non-essential CSS and JavaScript ﬁles
By putting off these tools and loading them bit by bit, the vital parts of the
website can load ﬁrst. This provides a quicker , better user experience.
Many methods  can be used for non-critical resources to load
asynchronously . It is inﬂuenced by the resource kind and your software's
unique needs. A few usual methods are:
●         Using the "async" and "defer" attributes for script tags, to allow
scripts to be loaded asynchronously and executed after the HTML
document has been parsed.
●         Using the "preload" and "prefetch" link tags, to give hints to the
browser about which resources should be loaded in the background.
●         Using lazy loading for images and videos, to only load them when
they are visible in the viewport.
●         Using code splitting and dynamic imports for JavaScript modules, to
only load the code that is required for a speciﬁc page or feature.
3. Optimize Resour ce Size and Delivery
Besides decidin g on and delaying resources, reducing the size and
improving the delivery of these resources to lessen their effect on loading
times is also crucial. Some suitable methods for enhancing resource
optimization are:
●         Minifying and compressing CSS and JavaScript ﬁles, to reduce their
size and improve load times.
●         Using image compression and optimization techniques (such as
responsive imag es and WebP format) to reduce the size of image ﬁles
without sacriﬁcing quality .
●         Using a content  delivery network (CDN) to distribute resources
across multiple  servers and reduce latency for users in different
geographic locations.
●         Implementing caching and versioning strategies, to allow resources to
be cache d by the browser and reduce the number of requests required
on subsequent page loads.
Making the size and delivery of resources better helps cut down the effect
of asynchronous loading on loading times. This imparts a quick er and more
receptive user experience.
4. Monitor  and Measur e Performance
At the end of the day, keep on checking and assessing how your SaaS
application is performing. Find any problem areas or complications linked
to resource loading and make necessary improvements. There are some
critical performance ﬁgures you need to keep an eye on. These include:
●         Page load times (both initial load and subsequent loads)
●         Time to ﬁrst byte (TTFB)
●         Time to interactive (TTI)
●         First contentful paint (FCP)
●         Largest contentful paint (LCP)
●         Cumulative layout shift (CLS)
When you monitor these meas ures consistently and contrast them with
standard industr y metrics and what users anticipate, you can spot the need
for enhancements. This data-oriented approach aids in making informed
choices regarding loading and optimizing resources.
There are many tools and techniques available for monitoring and
measuring web performance, including:
●         Browser developer tools (such as Chrome DevT ools or Firefox
Developer Tools)
●         Synthetic monitoring tools (such as Lighthouse, WebPageT est, or
Pingdom)
●         Real user monitoring (RUM) tools (such as Google Analytics, New
Relic, or LogRocket)
●         A/B testing and experimentatio n platforms (such as Optimizely or
VWO)
Utilizing these strategies and methods helps you constantly check and
enhance your SaaS application's performance. This guarantees the ﬁnest
experience for your users, boosting your business outcomes.
Performance is key for your app's success and user happiness. If you do not
use async loading for resources that are not crucial, you might offer a bad
experience for users. This could lead to higher bounce rates and lower
conversion rates. It might also raise your infrastructure expenses.
Self-Evaluation

asynchronously?

perceived performance?

Not Utilizing Br owser  Caching
Web browser storing, or caching, means regularly used items (like pictures,
programs, and design sheets) get saved on some one's device instead of
always fetching  them from the server . Using this storing trick, SaaS
applications are quicker to load because they need to send less data over the
network. This faster speed improves the experience for the users.
The Consequences of Not Utilizing Br owser  Caching
Not using browser caching could really impact how your SaaS  application
works and feels to users. Let us check out some possible ef fects:
1. Incr eased Load Times
Not using browser caching leads to one main effect: longer load times.
When someone uses your application for the ﬁrst time, their browser has to
get all the neede d elements (like pictures, scripts, and style sheets) from the
server . Without browser caching , the user's browser must get these elements
each time they open your appli cation, even if there are no changes to the
elements.
Your app's load times can go up a lot, especially for those with slow or not
so steady internet. People today want pages to load right away . Even a small
delay might make them annoyed and leave. Research has found that if a
page takes one more second to load, conversions go down by 7%, page
views drop 1 1%, and customer satisfaction falls by 16%.
2. Incr eased Server  Load
Not using browser caching has a side effect - it raises the server burden.
When your server gets a request for a ﬁle from a user's browser , several
tasks kick in. It has to acknowledge the ask, fetch the document, and return
it. No browser caching makes the server repeat these tasks whenever a ﬁle
is asked for , even if the ﬁle remains the same.
Your server can really feel the pressure, especially when there is a lot of
trafﬁc. Besides affecting performance, more server load can also raise
infrastructure expenses. When  your server resources are overworked,
scaling up your infrastructure may become necessary . This means you
might have to add more servers  or switch to stronger hardware  to meet the
demand. This can put a lot of ﬁnancial pressure, especially on early-stage
SaaS companies that do not have many resources.
3. Poor  User  Experience
The biggest trouble of ignoring browser caching could be a disappointing
user experience. When users come across sluggish loading instances,
interfaces that do not respond or regular errors, they hastily grow annoyed
and might decide to leave your app for good.
The SaaS market today is extrem ely competitive. The experienc e of the user
determines everything. Many alternatives are available to users. They will
not think twice about changing to a rival if your software fails to live up to
performance and reliability standards. According to research, 88% of
internet shopper s do not revisit a website if the experience was negative.
Even worse, 39% mention the unpleasant experience to their loved ones.
If your user experience is subpar , it can harm your brand image and
customer loyalty . When consumers link your brand with weak, inconsistent
performance, they might not suggest your application to their friends or
think about your other of ferings later on.
4. Reduced Scalability
Lastly , your SaaS application might not scale properly if you do not use
browser caching. When more users join and trafﬁc builds up, your server
resources migh t be overworked. Without suitable optimization, your
application could fail to meet the increasing demand. This could result in
longer loading hours, more mistakes, and even total system failure.
For SaaS companies growing fast or entering new areas, this could be
especially tricky . If your app is not built to scale well, the rise in user trafﬁc
and demand may not be handle able. This can limit your business growth
and expansion possibilities.
Alongside technical roadblock s, inadequate scalability can hurt your
business's image and client faith. If your program often has problems or
shuts down when it is busy, customers might doubt your capacit y to provide
a steady , superior service.
The Solution: Implementing Br owser  Caching Best Practices
For better performance and user experience of your SaaS applic ation, using
browser caching  effectively is critical. This can help you dodge potential
issues from not using it correctly . Look at these important tactics below:
1. Set Appr opriate Cache-Contr ol Headers
Initiating brows er caching begins by correctly adjusting your app's Cache-
Control headers . These headers, utilized by HTTP , give instruc tions about
how to store a resource. This process includes the usage of the browser and
middle-man caches, such as CDNs.
There are severa l Cache-Contro l directives that you can use to control how
your resources are cached, including:
●         max-age: Speciﬁes the maximum amount of time (in seconds) that a
resource should be cached before it is considered stale.
●         no-cache: Speciﬁes that a resource should not be cached without ﬁrst
checking with the server for a new version.
●         no-store: Speciﬁes that a resource should not be cached at all, and
should be retrieved from the server every time it is requested.
●         public: Speciﬁes that a resource can be cached by any cache,
including intermediate caches and the browser cache.
●         private: Speciﬁes that a resource should only be cached by the
browser , and not by intermediate caches.
Think about each resource's unique needs and traits while determining
Cache-Control headers. For instance, you could keep static resources, like
pictures and style formatting, stored for a longer span (perhaps days or
weeks) because they rarely alter. On the other hand, dynamic resources like
API returns or personalized info might require a shorter cache time (maybe
minutes or hours) or even not caching them at all.
2. Use Versioning for  Static Resour ces
One more top tip for using browser caching involves adding versions to
static resources. The process of versioning means attaching a unique label
(like a timestamp or hash) to the name of a static resource. This enables the
browser to tell the dif ference between various versions of the resource.
For example, instead of serving an image ﬁle as "logo.png", you might
serve it as "logo.png?v=1234567890", where "1234567890" is a unique
identiﬁer that changes whenever the image ﬁle is updated.
With versioning , your browser will always fetch the newest version of a
static resource. This is even if it had stored the resource earlier . This feature
is cruci al for resources that often change. For example, stylesheets or
scripts. It makes  sure that users always get the latest resource version. This
eliminates the need to manually empty their browser cache.
3. Implement Cache Busting for  Dynamic Resour ces
Static resources like images or stylesheets can stay cached for quite a while,
but it is a different case for dyna mic resources. Things like API responses or
personalized content might need faster cache clearings, or sometimes no
caching whatsoe ver. do not count out dynamic resources though, they can
still gain from browser caching if used correctly .
One method for storing dynamic resources involves cache busting. This
technique requires attaching an exclusive identiﬁer (like a time marker or
random digit) to the URL  of a dynamic resource. That way, the browser can
tell apart dif ferent versions of the same resource.
For example, instead of serving an API response at "/api/data", you might
serve it at "/api/data?timestamp=1234567890", where "1234567890" is a
unique identiﬁer that changes every time the API response is updated.
Cache busting helps your browser to always get the most recent version of
dynamic resources. Even if these resources were previously cached, it is
still effective. This process cuts down on server load, boosting the
performance of your app. Plus, it ensures users only see the newest data.
4. Leverage Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)
One more useful method to apply browser caching is by using Content
Delivery Networks (CDNs). CDNs are extended structures of servers
created to provide content (like pictures, footage, and scripts) to consumers
in relation to their geographical area.
With a CDN, your app's static resources can be stored on servers nearer to
your users. This cuts down network transfer time. It can really boost your
application’ s load times, especially for users far from your main server .
CDNs do not just boost performance, they can also cut down on server
burden and infrastructure expe nses. Giving the duty of delivering static
resources to the CDN can drop the trafﬁc your main server has to deal with.
This makes your app scale more smoothly .
Setting up a CDN involves picking a dependable, efﬁcient provider that
caters to your app's mandatory specs (like SSL/TLS encryption and
personalized domain names). Renowned CDN providers are Cloudﬂare,
Amazon CloudFront, and Akamai.
5. Monitor  and Optimize Performance
In conclusion, keeping your internet browser's caching setup working well
requires constant attention. This includes regularly checking how well your
software performs. you would need certain tools to gauge things like how
fast a page loads, the server's reaction speed, and the cache success rate.
Finding spots where it is slow and where you can improve is also necessary .
There are several tools and techniques that you can use to monitor and
optimize the performance of your SaaS application, including:
●         Browser Developer Tools: Most modern web browsers (such as
Chrome, Firefox, and Safari) include built-in developer tools that
allow you to inspect the network activity and performance of your
application. These tools can help you identify which resources are
being cached, how long they are being cached for, and where
performance bottlenecks may be occurring.
●         Web Performanc e Monitoring Tools: There are also a variety of third-
party web performance monitoring tools (such as New Relic,
Pingdom, and GTmetrix) that can provide more detailed insights into
the perfo rmance of your application, including page load times, server
response times, and user experience metrics.
●         A/B Testing: A/B testing involves comparing the performance of two
different version s of your applic ation (e.g., with and without browser
caching) to determine which version performs better . By using A/B
testing, you can identify the speciﬁc impact of browser caching on the
performance and user experience of your application, and make data-
driven decisions about how to optimize your implementation.
Keeping a keen eye on your application's performance and enhancing it
regularly can help. This way, you make sure the way you are storing data on
web browsers is working well and smoothly . Your users? They always get
an excellent experience.
Self-Evaluation

Chapter  7: User  Personalization and Customization
The str ongest principle of gr owth lies in the human choice. - Geor ge Eliot
Everyone is special. We all have different personalities and likes. Yet, many
apps treat us like robots. They give us a boring experience, the same for all.
It is like a restaurant giving everyone a plain chicken dish. No matter if you
want veggies, spice, or meat. They just say, "Here's your food. Eat it or
leave. "
But great products know making users happy is about more  than basic
features. Customizing each expe rience to the person can change  it from just
working to feeling made just for you. It is like dressing yourself instead of
wearing bland clothes from a bin. One is boring. The other lets you express
your personality through your style.
This chapter explores personali zing and customizing product experiences.
We will discuss feature ﬂags, changing content based on the user, deep
preference customization, and optimizing user journeys based on behaviors.
In the end, people do not want to feel like nameless users.  They want
experiences tailored to them that change as their needs change over time.
Your product can change for each person. It shows only what matters to
them.
It happens the way they want. And it happens when they want. This is not
about simple help. It is about letting people make your product part of their
lives.
Overloading Users with Options
Having several customization options in a product might appear to be a
great idea in the beginning. However , providing users with an abundance of
choices can easily result in them being lost and disappointed from the start.
It is like being at a restaurant which has a menu with hundreds of dishes.
You can easily spend the whole  day, in fact, in some cases, you need more
time, but you have to get to the point quickly anyway , by choosi ng from the
swathe of option s available. This can make your head swirl even before you
take a bite!
The correct way could rather be to make things easier for the beginners.
You should never overwhelm them with every possible switch, setting, and
customization option. They will not appreciate that.
Start with a bare-bones, leaner solution to start out. Be sure to identify those
easy-to-choose options that would be recognized as fundamental to anyone
who is starting out and put them  in the forefront. The rest of the options can
just sit in a corner for now .
Gradually intro duce more sophisticated options to users as they gain
proﬁciency with your product. Let them get used to it step by step.
When we talk about applications such as Spotify or Slack , those are
designed to be very free and clear from the very beginning, basically you
are not overwhelmed with a million customizations. The dashboard is
pristine and core features only are those which are obvious. However , once
you utilize the apps regularly , some more settings and options would be
showcased, most frequently along with guides that impart the know-how on
handling them.
Even a difﬁcult app like Salesforce does not have a lot of customization at
the start. Moreover , they prefer to implement the customization of the user
interface in steps, not all at once, as they are aware of the fact that getting
into everything at the beginning would cause terrible confusion.
Micr osoft Word's Pr ogressive Customization Method
Instead of overwhelming you with a myriad of different things to do and
places to look, the ﬁrst thing you see when you open Microsoft Word is a
very clean, basic page. The ribbo n at the top is simplistic, featuring only the
most necessary options for the tasks like creating a new document, printing,
saving, manipulating text, and so forth.
If you do not get bombarded with everything under the sun such as every
possible setting , advanced style options, macro functionality , and
customization choice that are already present in Word, it would be
overwhelming if you were just typing a simple letter .
Instead, Microsoft has gone for a gradual approach to show the whole
spectrum of Word's abilities. The beginning approach concentrates the
toolset only to the basic ones that most of the users are familiar with. Next
to it, the new options and custom settings that can be set up will be
discovered if you get used to the basic Word.
When you start using styles and themes, the program will guide you to all
its custo mization options to further personalize those. If you add images to
your document, it will surface additional options like image editing to you.
If you make use of a feature such as track changes to review a draft, you
will decide on the symmetrical distribution price.
There are also the so-called "Developer" tabs that are not displayed if you
are not using features like these. These are tabs where users have to reveal
by them selves if the features to be programmed or the coding editor tools
which would cause such complex changes exist or are not present.
Word gradually adds more choices as your experience level grows. It
functions as follows:
1. A simple, friendly interface for newcomers is available right from
the start
2. Start with the fundamental functions
3. As you experience further Mogul options/settings that deman d
them, the more advanced the features become like for instanc e
the appearance of other optional deeper customizations makin g
the customization of the entire browser window possible for
power users.
4. Hit or miss with some faint idea of the relative strengths and
weaknesses of a particular item is primarily for newbies.
This logical step allows Word to service everyone ranging from amateurs to
professionals without scaring off any group by that. you are not forced to
learn or tweak everything all at once.
The desire was to suggest that the individuals understand the basis of the
given problem, and after that, they get more options accord ing to their
competence. Each product's starting point is easiest for the user and then
gradual reﬁning  of its interface according to the user's proﬁcie ncy is done
regularly .
Think of it as getting a new car .
Automobiles are complex machines, and the ideal way is for the users to
absorb the capabilities of the vehicles step by step rather than trying to
download all the features ﬁrst and then expect to understand the vehicle.
Another fantastic method to not let users feel terrorized about excessive
initial settings and tweaks is to suggest the preset conﬁgurations for
common user roles, needs, or experience levels.
What if instead of loading every possible mechanism such as setting or
toggling for the users, the products deﬁne simple setup options that include
only the primary elements for certain use cases. You can think  of them as
opinionated starter kits or templates for speciﬁc situations.
As an illustration, one of the accounting software settings might look like
this:
1. Tax Professionals
2. Small Businesses
3. Personal Finance
For instance, each one would  only take into account the nonessential
aspects of the user interface and make the most important features
available. The small business instance would deal with invoicing, expenses
and payroll-i.e. would hide personal ﬁnance items not related to small
business. The tax pro setup could offer users a simple option to search for a
ﬁle by choosing a date or provi de an advanced search that would let users
ﬁnd ﬁles from a particular year till the last one by default.
Separate presets associated with video editing tools can be offered to
different categories of users - the simplest ones for beginner s, the most
interactive ones for social media personnel, the professional moviemakers
and the buoyant world of virtua l effects creators. Each of one of these will
have their’ own exposure to the appropriate tools – so, the very ﬁrst
impressions they will have is which toolset they would use. Therefore, you
will expose only the tab that is needed for given user’s type of video
experience. This is achieved through the template approach, where the user
is notiﬁed of the one or two preset options that meet the requirements of
his/her proﬁciency level and the objective of his/her idea.
Presets offer a big advantage to users by giving them a helpful head start.
They provide a good starting point with recommended setting s already in
place. These settings are usually based on the user's goals and skill level.
This means users can begin using the product effectively witho ut having to
ﬁgure out all the settings them selves. Presets also avoid confusing visual
elements that might distract new users. By using presets, peop le can jump
right in and start using the produ ct without getting bogged down in complex
setup processes. This makes it much easier for users to get started quickly
and comfortably .
After users get comfortable with the preset starting point, they can slowly
start to customi ze their display . New features are added one at a time to
avoid overwhelm ing users. Most products offer three or four basic setups
that work well for most users. The more complex the technology , the harder
it is to make one setup that ﬁts everyone perfectly . Sometime s, important
features might be overlooked if they are not what users are mainly
interested in.
To help users, it is best to keep things simple at ﬁrst. Start with easy choices
and gradually add more compl ex options as users become more familiar
with the product. This way, users can learn at their own pace without
feeling confused . Remember , even simple decisions should not be made
complicated.

feature presentation?

might be occurring?

processes?

★      Have I implemented a way for users to easily reset to default settings
if they get overwhelmed?
★      Am I using progressive enhancement to add features and options as
users become more proﬁcient?
★      Have I considered using AI or machine learning to suggest relevant
options based on user behavior?

simplify initial setup?
★      Have I implemented a way for power users to access all options
without overwhelming beginners?
★      Am I using visual hierarchy ef fectively to prioritize important options
and de-emphasize less common ones?
★      Have I considered using a "basic" and "advanced" mode toggle for
settings and customizations?

or customizations?
★      Have I implemented undo/redo functionality for customization
changes to reduce user anxiety?
★      Am I using progressive disclosure in my documentation and help
resources as well?
★      Have I considered implementing a "most popular" or "recommended"
ﬁlter for options and customizations?
Not Pr oviding Default Settings
You decided to join the productivity app your coworkers talked  about. You
were excited to start being organized and efﬁcient! But later, you saw an
empty space with no settings or preferences set up. It was like a blank
canvas staring back at you, asking you to build everything from scratch.
And just like that, your motivation started decreasing.
It felt like buying a new coffee maker but ﬁnding disconnected plastic
pieces and a long, confusing manual inside. You wondered, "Why do I have
to do everything ? Is not this supposed to make my life easier? " Asking  new
users to manually conﬁgure many toggles, ﬁelds, and customizations right
away can make them tired before even starting. When you realize you have
to create every setting and ﬂow just to use the main feature, it can be
overwhelming and confusing.
Thankfully , many product makers realized it is better to provide smart,
ready-to-use default settings initially . They understood giving opinionated
defaults helps users get started more easily with their product.
New apps can now start with sample content and settings ready for you.
This can give you a sense of how the app works. You do not have to build
everything from scratch. it is like getting a bookshelf already built instead
of having to assemble it yourself.
For exam ple, when you join Trello, it shows you a sample board with lists
and cards for different uses like marketing and product plans. This helps
you understand how to use Trello. But you can still start fresh or edit the
templates. Apps  like Zapier and IFTTT  also give new users pre-made
automation routines based on their interest s. With one click, you get many
scripts ready to go. No manual setup needed at ﬁrst.
Notion's Template Gallery and Onboarding Revamp (2018-2020)
When Notion launched in 2016, it was like giving people a blank canvas
and a huge box of art supp lies. You could create anything - notes,
documents, databases, and more . Tech-savvy users loved this freedom, but
many others felt lost.
Imagine walking into a workshop full of tools, but with no instructions.
That is how many new Notion users felt. They would open the app and see
an empty page with countless options. "It looks powerful, but I do not know
wher e to start, " was a common reaction. Some users gave up before they
even began.
Notion's team realized they had a problem. People were signing up but
leaving quickly . Those who stayed were not using many of Notion's cool
features. The support team was swamped with basic questions like "How do
I set this up? "
In 2018, Notion decided to make some big changes. They wanted to keep
the freedom that made Notion special, but also give new users a helping
hand. It was like adding a "beginner mode" to that workshop full of tools.
First, they created a gallery of templates. Now , instead of staring at a blank
page, users could choose from ready-made setups for things like to-do lists,
project plans, and team wikis. It was like giving people a head start.
They also added a guided tour for new users. This tour helped people set up
their workspace based on what they needed. It was like having a friendly
expert show you around the workshop and help you get started.
Notion did not stop there. They made a sample workspace that new users
could explore. This showed people what was possible with Notion. They
also added little tutorials that popped up as you used the app, teaching you
new tricks along the way. One of the coolest things they did was create a
place where Notion users could share their own templates. This turned into
a bustling marketplace of ideas,  where people could ﬁnd new and creative
ways to use Notion.
The results were impressive. More new users stuck around. People started
creating more pages and using  more of Notion's advanced features. The
support team got fewer "How do I start? " questions. By 2021, Notion had
grown to over 20 million users. Notion's story shows how impo rtant it is to
give new users a good starting point. They kept the freedom that made
Notion special, but added guides and examples to help people get going. It
is like giving someone a map along with their blank canvas - you are not
telling them what to create, just helping them ﬁnd their way .
The goal is to give you a basic working setup for common uses right away .
You do not have to conﬁgure  anything at the start. But you can still
customize your setup however you want later. It lets you start using the app
quickly ﬁrst. Then, as you get comfortable, you can personalize it more.
Asking users to build everything  from a blank start is a mistake . Providing
opinionated default options reduces early frustration. It sets up new users
for successful customization from day one.

Lack of Flexibility
Each of us has our personal style, the way we prefer to get along with, and
different ways of doing things. Still, there are many products that employ a
"my way or the highway " approa ch and stick to this static  idea of a one-size-
ﬁts-all model, which is imposed on everyone.
It can be compared with a brand  of clothing, which produces only one size
and style of jeans. Admittedly , these may perfectly ﬁt some people.
However , the others, who have different body shapes or type of attire, feel
dissatisﬁed with it and even become resentful for not being  taken into
account. The case with ﬂexibility and customization options is the most
frustrating in the tools and software that are meant to be our assistants and
productivity boosters. The whole point of apps is not only to offer highly
customized solutions that are perfectly in line with the requirements set by
individuals but also to escape from the straitjacketed state.
Take an example of a very effective writing application such as Scrivener . It
not only includes the crucial standard features but you can also modify
nearly the whole program, so rendering it in the way that fulﬁlls your
personal creativ e writing style and process. You can set up the UI (User
Interface) in a very detailed fashion. For instance, you can customize the
placement of buttons on the toolbar . You can even change the color themes
and background images along with the typography . Folders can be
organized in various ways. The personalized use of compilation makes it
more interesting . It makes Scrivener more of a user's speciﬁc writing
environment rather than the mostly opposite way .
Comparing with endless restrictions on Windows Phone in 2010-2012 is the
other side of that scenario. Microsoft was very focused on a speciﬁc design
concept, and therefore, they denied users the essential ﬂexibility to do such
things as rearranging tiles or alternately choosing which apps they want to
use or even customize the home screen layout at all. It was their way or no
Lumia.
People believed it was very stiﬂing that they were not allowed to alter the
interface that would suit their own personal needs the most. Here, the use of
adaptable ﬂexibility should be a main priority from the very beginning even
in the core product experiences. The concept of a one-size-ﬁts-all product or
system is alway s so ﬂawed that even the use of custom keyboard shortcuts,
saved workspace layouts, chances available for home screen tweaks, or
enabling plugin/integration ecosystems makes your product a malleable and
adaptable one for every single user .
To be clear , you do not have  to sacriﬁce your own point of view or
signature style. Personalized experiences can be awesome ! However ,
include hooks and surfaces, which encourage customization following the
standard rules. Panning for gold where landscapes are the most variable
admits that not every site or every person can allow the same degree of
ﬂexibility , but the decisive solution is to ﬁnd and apply the perfect one
feasible for the users to genuin ely shape their product experience rather
than being kept hostage by it.
Users are the owners and you are the collaborators here
Here are some of the key types of ﬂexibility and customization options
products can provide users:
Interface Customization
●         Ability to rearrange UI elements like toolbars, panels, sidebars
●         Custom themes/skins to adjust colors, typography , icons
●         Options to show/hide speciﬁc UI components
●         Saved workspace/layout presets
Featur e Conﬁguration
●         Toggles to enable/disable certain features
●         Customizable settings to adjust feature behaviors
●         Plugins/extensions to add new capabilities
Personalization
●         Custom proﬁle setups tailored to roles/preferences
●         Saved view states (e.g. ﬁltered views, default sort order)
●         Personal branding/styling options
Workﬂow Adaptation
●         User-deﬁned keyboard shortcuts
●         Customizable toolbars/shelves for frequent actions
●         Macro/scripting support to automate sequences
●         Integration with other apps/services
Content Pr eferences
●         Display density/zoom controls
●         Font family , size, spacing customizations
●         Light/dark mode, reduced motion, accessibility conﬁgs
Flexibility is a top priority across the main layers like UI, Features, Data
views, Inputs, Integrations, Accessibility , and more. Coding of layouts,
settings, and behaviors should not be strictly rigid as this will restrict the
layout of the content and the program setting.
Allow saving preferences, deﬁning presets, toggling views, remapping
inputs, styling elements, and adapting the product to unique environments
and workﬂows. The entire interface and experience should be a malleable
canvas for users to shape usage their way over time.
With the right ﬂexibility built in across all layers, your product can
essentially "lear n" based on how a speciﬁc user customizes and ﬁne-tunes
their ideal experience over time. That is a key ingredient for driving
stickiness and long-term adoption.
Self-Evaluation
★      Does my product of fer sufﬁcient customization options for the user
interface?
★      Can users rearrange UI elements like toolbars, panels, and sidebars?
★      Do I provide custom themes or skins for users to adjust colors,
typography , and icons?
★      Have I implemented options to show/hide speciﬁc UI components?

and workﬂows?

users?

users?
★      Can users export and import their customization settings?
★      Have I considered how customization options might impact product
performance?
Complicated Customization Pr ocess
Having choices to customize things is supposed to be easy. But if changing
things is too complicated, it ruins the purpose. it is like buying furniture that
should be simple  to put together , but the instructions are confusi ng. An easy
task becomes too difﬁcult.
Some apps make changing settings feel like a maze. Options are hidden or
described in a way that is hard to understand. For example, customizing
Visual Studio Code means digging through many coded ﬁles. One tiny
mistake can cause big problems. This approach makes simple changes feel
dangerous.
The solution is to make customizing straightforward from start to ﬁnish. Put
all settin gs in an easy menu with simple words. Group similar options
together . Show visuals so you can see changes as you make them.
Apps like BetterT ouchT ool do this well. Settings are organized  in a clean
layout with basic and advanced  sections. it is easy to ﬁnd what you need
and understand how to change it.
Understanding what users want to change is important. Make it easy for
them to change those settings step-by-step. Do not use complicated words
or hide options in hard-to-ﬁnd places.
Squar espace's Customization Options
In 2016, Squarespace was known for its beautiful website templates. People
loved how professional their sites looked right out of the box. But there was
a proble m brew ing beneath the surface. While Squarespace offered many
ways to customize these templates, actually making changes was becoming
a headache for users.
Imagine trying to repaint your house, but every time you want to change a
color , you have to climb through a maze of ladders and scaffolding. That is
how many Squa respace users felt when they tried to customize their sites.
They had to click through countless menus and submenus just to make
simple changes . Some of the coolest features were hidden away , like
treasure in a hard-to-ﬁnd cave.
This complexity was causing real problems. Many people would start
building their websites but give up halfway through, frustrated by the
customization process. Squarespace's support team was ﬂooded with
questions about how to make basic changes. Users were leaving comments
like, "I cannot ﬁgure out how to change the font color ," or "Why is it so
hard to move things around on the page?"
Squarespace knew they had to do something. In late 2016, they began a
major overhaul of how people customize their websites. They wanted to
keep all the powerful features but make them much easier to use.
The Squarespace team came up with several big changes. First, they created
a single, easy-to-use panel for all customization options. No more hunting
through multiple menus. They also made it possible to drag and drop
elements on the page, so you could see exactly how your changes would
look in real-time.
They simpliﬁed  how users could change fonts and text styles across their
site. It was like giving people a simple set of paint brushes instead of
complex technical tools. For the more advanced features, they added
helpful explanations and video tutorials right where you needed them.
Squarespace did not rush these changes out all at once. They started with a
small group of users, gathered feedback, and made improvem ents before
rolling out the new interface to everyone. They kept a close eye on how
people were using the new tools and listened to what users had to say .
The results were  impressive. By 2018, people were ﬁnding it much easier to
build and customize their webs ites. Fewer users were giving up halfway
through creating  their sites. The support team saw a big drop in questions
about how to make customizations. More importantly , users were starting to
use some of the advanced design features they had previously ignored.
All these improvements helped  Squarespace grow even more. By 2019,
they had over 2 million paid subscriptions and were valued at $1.7 billion.
But the real win was in user satisfaction. People could now easily create the
websites they imagined, without getting lost in a maze of confusing options.
Squarespace's story shows how important it is to make powerfu l tools easy
to use. They kept all the feature s that made their platform great but found
ways to make those features more accessible. It is like giving someone a
high-performance car with an easy-to-use dashboard – all the power is
there, but now anyone can drive it.
Here are some good ways to organize and show customization options in a
user-friendly way:
Use Simple Categories/Sections
Put related settings together into sections with easy labels. For example:
"Display", "Key board Shortcu ts", "Notiﬁcations", etc. This prevents a
messy list of all options.
Allow Sear ching Options
Include a search feature so users can quickly ﬁnd and go to speciﬁc
customization settings they want. This is helpful for more complex
products.
Separate Basic & Advanced
Have a "Basic" view with comm on settings, and an "Advanced" section for
more detailed/te chnical custom izations. This gradual approa ch prevents
overwhelming users at ﬁrst.
Use Visual Examples
Instead of just text description s, provide visual examples that show how
changing a setting will change the user interface/experience. This helps
users understand the ef fect of options.
Allow Live Pr eviews
Even better - let users see changes they make to customizations in real-time
before applying them. This provides an editable preview .
Guide Customization Flows
Customizing things can be hard. So, let us break it into easy steps. Have a
guide that walks you through each part, one action at a time.
Saved Pr esets
Let people save their custom setups as "Presets." They can quickly use
those later , or share them with friends.
The key? Make customizing simple and clear . do not hide options in
complicated menus or use fancy words. Organize everything in a way that
makes sense.

Failing to Educate Users
You worked hard to make your product ﬂexible. Users can change a lot to
match their needs. But if you do not tell them about these option s, they will
not use them. It is like going to a new city without a map. You will only see
the popular places. You will miss the cool, lesser -known spots off the main
roads. Without guidance, you will not experience much.
That is why you need to teach users about customization capabi lities. If you
do not, those useful features might as well not exist. Even if the options are
well-designed, people need to know about them ﬁrst.
Gmail does a good job at this. When you start using Gmail on the web or
app, pop-ups and tutorials show  main customization options. For example,
how to change your inbox layout, enable desktop notiﬁcations, adjust reply
settings, and more. It lets you know what you can tweak.
But Gmail does not stop there. As you keep using it over time, it
periodically shows new tours or Tips cards. These highlight additional
settings you have not used yet based on your usage patterns. For instance, if
you send lots of emails but never enabled keyboard shortc uts, it may
suggest customizing those.
The key is conti nually surfacing different customization option s instead of
hiding them in menus. Guide users through what is available via:
1. Guides show how to get started.
2. Tips appear when you need them.
3. Tours highlight new or unvisited settings.
4. Learn more in the product itself.
The more you make it easy to discover customizations and provide upfront
guidance, the more likely users will take advantage of powerful
personalization features. do not let all that hard work go unnoticed!
Exploring a new place is better  with a knowledgeable tour guide. Having
thoughtful onbo arding and education ﬂows can unlock the full depth of
your product's experience. do not let your gems remain hidden.

Chapter  8: Accessibility
We are trying to construct a mor e inclusive society . We are going to make a
country in which no one is left out. Franklin D. Roosevelt
As product creators, it is easy to accidentally design experience s that work
well for only certain types of "typical" users. We imagine our ideal
customers and end up building products just for people similar to ourselves,
without realizing it.
But this narrow approach leaves out a huge number of people whose
abilities, situatio ns, and needs are different from the "norm." We are talking
about the millions of individuals dealing with disabilities like:
●         Vision impairments
●         Hearing difﬁculties
●         Mobility/dexterity challenges
●         Cognitive/neurological conditions
●         And many other types of perm anent, temporary , or situational
constraints
These people do not represent tiny outlier groups. They make up one of the
largest populations that could  really beneﬁt from using products and
services accessible.
Ignoring accessibility means alienating a massive number of potential
users. it is like leaving out a huge segment of people who could be your
customers.
Accessibility , in the context of SaaS products, refers to designing and
developing applications that can be used by as many people as possible,
including those with disabilities. Disabilities can be visual, auditory ,
physical, speech-related, cognitive, neurological, or a combination of these.
This chapter covers ways to make your SaaS offerings accessible to
absolutely everyone, regardless of their unique abilities and circumstances.
We will look at:
●         Understanding the diverse range of accessibility needs
●         Legal requirements and guidelines to follow , like WCAG standards
●         How to bake accessibility into your design and developmen t
processes
●         Testing to ensure your product experiences are intuitive for all users
Because Here is the thing - making your product accessible is not just the
right thing to do. it is also a massive business opportunity to include an
enormous group of people as users and customers.
No one should be shut out from beneﬁting from the latest technologies,
online services, and product experiences just because of a disability or
impairment. Everyone deserves an equal opportunity to access and use what
you create. So, get ready to rethink how you approach product design. This
chapter will open your eyes to the often-overlooked need for accessibility
and show you the huge advantages of being inclusive to all potential users.
Why is Accessibility Important?
Making things easy for all people is key. When your product is accessible,
anyone can use it. This matters for those with special needs or disabilities.
But it is also required by laws in many places.
Making room for everyone grows your audience. By designing for users
with disabilities, you let millions more enjoy your creation. it is just the
right thing to do. Accessible design makes things better for all. For instance,
video captions aid those in loud areas or who want quiet viewing. High
contrast color modes help with vision issues, but also work great in bright
light.
The core idea is accounting for varied abilities enhances usability overall. It
creates a better experience universally . Quality design must consider
accessibility as vital. Millions globally live with disabilities impacting web
use. This unders erved group awaits products prioritizing acces s. By being
early with a truly accessible offering, you can rapidly gain delighted users
competitors miss. it is a growth opportunity waiting.
Better accessibility also beneﬁts search rankings. Descriptive text, easy
navigation, and responsive design please both users and search engines.
Sites optimized for all see SEO gains. Web pages made for all people to use
easily follow the best ways to make data organized. They use proper HTML
code. Keyboard s can move around the pages well. These thing s also help
search engines ﬁnd them better .
So beyo nd just letting everyone  use it, making stuff that is accessible can
maybe help search engines see your thing more easily too. That is a nice
extra!
Neglecting Keyboard Accessibility
Many people think computers and web apps need a mouse or touchscreen.
But some users only use keyboards. They may have a disability or prefer
keyboard shortc uts. Your website must work with just a keyboard. This
makes it accessible to everyone. it is not just nice - it is necessary .
Keyboard access means all functions and interactive parts can work with
only the keyboard. This is vital for disabled users, screen reader users, and
power users who like keyboard shortcuts. Not having it excludes many
people.
Target Corporation Accessibility Lawsuit of 2006
The Target Corporation Accessibility Lawsuit of 2006 provides a
compelling case study on the consequences of neglecting keyboard
accessibility in web product UI/UX design.
Target, one of the largest retail corporations in the United States, found
itself at the center of a landmark lawsuit ﬁled by the National Federation of
the Blind (NFB) in 2006. The lawsuit alleged that Target.com was
inaccessible to blind users, with keyboard accessibility being a major issue.
The core of the problem lay in Target's website design, which heavily relied
on mouse-based interactions. Many essential functions of the site, including
product selection, cart management, and checkout processes, were not
operable using only a keyboard. This meant that blind users  relying on
screen readers, which typically navigate web pages using keyboard
commands, were ef fectively locked out of using the site.
Speciﬁc issues included:
1. Image-based links and buttons that lacked alternative text,
making them unidentiﬁable to screen readers.
2. Form ﬁelds that were not properly labeled, making it impossible
for keyboard users to understand what information was required.
3. Dynamic content updates that did not alert screen readers, leaving
blind users unaware of changes on the page.
4. Lack of skip navigation links, forcing keyboard users to tab
through every menu item before reaching the main content.
These accessibility failures not only affected blind users but also impacted
other groups who rely on keyb oard navigation, such as users with motor
disabilities or those who simply prefer keyboard shortcuts.
The consequenc es for Target were severe. The lawsuit dragged on for two
years, during which time Target faced signiﬁcant negative publicity and
damage to its brand image. The company was perceived as being  insensitive
to the needs of disabled customers, which contradicted its public image of
being an inclusive retailer .
In 2008, Target settled the lawsuit, agreeing to pay $6 million in damages to
affected users in California. More importantly , the company committed to a
comprehensive overhaul of its website to ensure accessibility , including full
keyboard navigation support.
Here are the common problems with no keyboard access and ﬁxes:
Elements Cann ot Get Focus:  Imagine ﬁlling a form but cann ot tab to the
next ﬁeld. If you cannot use a mouse, the form is impossible. Every
interactive part like buttons, links, and ﬁelds must get focus with the
keyboard. They should show when focused.
Wrong Tab Order:  Pressing the tab makes the cursor jump around
randomly instea d of top to bottom. This confusing navigation wastes time.
Put HTML  elements in a logical order , like the visual layout. Use the
tabindex attribute to control focusable element order .
Hidden or Invisible Focus:  Sometimes, you press the tab key but cannot
see which item is currently chosen. This happens when items do not have a
clear visual indication when they get focus. Make sure that items that can
get focus stand out visually . You can use CSS to add an outline or highlight
to the focused item.
Lack of Skip Navigation Link s: Imagine having to press tab many times
to get past a long list of navigat ion links before reaching the main content.
This can be very frustrating. Add a "Skip to Content" link at the top of
pages. This lets keyboard users quickly jump over the navigation and go
straight to the main content area. it is especially helpful for screen reader
users.
Modal Dialogs Without Focus Management:  Sometimes a pop-up
window opens, but you cannot  interact with it using the keyboard. This
happens when the focus stays behind the pop-up or does not stay within it.
When a pop-up opens, move the keyboard focus into it. Keep the focus
trapped within the pop-up until it closes. When it closes, move focus back
to whatever opened it. This lets keyboard users easily use pop-ups.
Keyboard Traps:  You start using a section of a page but cannot exit it
using the keyboard. it is like getting trapped within that section. Make sure
users can enter and exit all sections using just the keyboard. do not create
sections that trap the keyboard focus. Test custom sections to ensure focus
can move freely .
Self-Evaluation

when opened?

completed using the keyboard?

readers?

using the keyboard?
