addressed?
★      Have I ensured that my site meets the WCAG 2.1 AA standards for
keyboard accessibility?
★      Can users navigate through image galleries and sliders using the
keyboard?
★      Do I provide clear instructions for keyboard users on how to navigate
and use the site?
★      Are all error messages and form validation feedback accessible to
screen readers?
★      Have I ensured that all custom components and widgets are keyboard
accessible?
★      Can users easily access help and support options using the keyboard?
★      Are all interactive advertisements and third-party content accessible
using the keyboard?
★      Have I checked for any hidden elements that can receive keyboard
focus and need to be addressed?
Insufﬁcient Color  Contrast
Making colors stand out from each other is very important for accessibility .
It helps people see words and buttons easily . This matters a lot for those
with vision issues, like trouble  seeing certain colors. Let's look at why
having good color contrast is key, what goes wrong without it, and ways to
ﬁx these problems.
Color contrast means how different two colors look from each other . When
text and background have a big contrast, it is easy to read. But low contrast
makes words hard to see. High contrast lets all users view things clearly .
Why is Color  Contrast important?
Readability:  Adequate contrast makes text readable for everyone, including
people with low vision and color blindness.
Usability:  Good contrast enhances the overall usability of an interface,
making it easier for all users to navigate and interact with content.
Compliance:  Many accessibility standards, such as the Web Content
Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG), require sufﬁcient contrast to ensure
content is accessible to users with disabilities.
Inclusivity:  By considering color contrast, you ensure that your product can
be used by a broader audience, promoting inclusivity .
Common Pr oblems:
Low Contrast Text: Text that blends  into the backg round can be hard to
read, especially for users with visual impairments. Ensure that text has a
high contrast ratio against its background. Follow WCAG guidelines, which
recommend a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for
large text.
Spaceistheplace.ca’ s Home Page
Color -Dependent Information: Using color alone to convey information
(e.g., red for errors, green for success) can be problematic for colorblind
users. Use additional cues like icons, patterns, or text labels to convey
information, ensuring it is accessible to everyone.
Inconsistent Contrast: Inconsistent use of contrast across different parts of
the application can confuse users and make navigation difﬁcult. Maintain
consistent contrast standards across your entire application. Establish and
follow a design system with deﬁned color palettes and contrast ratios.
Domino's Pizza Accessibility Lawsuit of 2016-2019
Guillermo Robles, a blind man, sued Domino's Pizza in 2016  because he
could not use their website and mobile app to order food, even with screen-
reading software. While the lawsuit covered various accessibility issues,
one of the signiﬁcant problems was insufﬁcient color contrast, which
affected users with low vision or color blindness.
Domino's website and app used a color scheme that did not provide enough
contrast between text and background in many areas. This made it difﬁcult
for users  with visual impairments to read menu items, prices, and important
information about their orders. The problem was particularly acute in areas
like the pizza customization interface, where users needed to distinguish
between dif ferent toppings and options.
Speciﬁc issues included:
1. Light gray text on a white background for some menu
descriptions, making them hard to read for users with low vision.
2. Colored buttons with text that did not have sufﬁcient contra st
with the button color .
3. Color -coded information (like indicating whether a topping was
on the left or right side of a pizza) without alternative text
explanations.
These design choices not only affected users with visual impairments but
also made the interface less usable for all customers in certain lighting
conditions or on screens with poor color reproduction.
The consequences for Domino' s were signiﬁcant. The lawsuit dragged on
for years , going all the way to the Supreme Court. Domino's initially won in
a lower court, arguing that the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) did
not apply to online spaces. However , in 2019, the Supreme Court declined
to hear Domino 's appeal, effectively ruling that the ADA  does apply to
websites and apps.
This decision had far-reaching implications for the entire e-commerce and
digital service industry . It reinforced that web accessibility , including proper
color contrast, is not just a best practice but a legal requirement under the
ADA.
Mitigation Strategies:
Follow WCAG Guidelines: WCAG 2.1 provides speciﬁc
recommendations for color contrast:
●         Minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text.
●         Minimum contrast ratio of 3:1 for lar ge text (18pt or 14pt bold).
●         For non-text elements, a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent
colors.
Use Contrast Checking Tools: Tools like WebAIM’s Contrast Checker , the
Colour Contrast  Analyser , and Lighthouse can help evaluate and ensure
sufﬁcient color contrast. For example, use WebAIM’ s Contrast Checker by
inputting the hex values of your text and background colors to check if they
meet the required contrast ratios.
Design with Accessibility in Mind: From the start of the design process,
consider accessibility . Choose color schemes that inherently provide good
contrast. Use tools like Adobe Color or Color Safe to create accessible color
palettes.
Test with Real Users: Conduct usabilit y testing with users who have visual
impairments to gather feedback and make necessary adjustments.
Consider an example of a ﬁnancial dashboard used by banking
professionals to monitor transactions and ﬁnancial metrics. If this
dashboard has poor color contrast, ﬁnancial ﬁgures and metrics could
become hard to read, leading to potential errors in ﬁnancial analysis. Alerts
using only color (e.g., red for negative trends) would be missed by
colorblind users, possibly resulting in unnoticed critical issues.
The Solution: The dashboard should be redesigned to ensure all text had a
minimum contra st ratio of 4.5:1 . Key ﬁgures were displayed in dark text on
a light backgro und. Alerts should be supplemented with icons and text
descriptions, ensuring that critical information was accessible regardless of
color perception. A design system with deﬁned contrast standards should be
established, ensuring consistency across all parts of the application.
Self-Evaluation

background?

Contrast Checker?

Inadequate Alt Text for  Images
Images are a big part of websit e content. They provide enhanced meaning
and help people understand the message. But some people cannot see
images. For these people, text that describes the images is very important.
This section talks about why describing images with text is helpful and how
to do it well.
What is Alt Text?
Alt text gives a short description of an image. Screen readers read this text
aloud for people who cannot see the image. Alt text also shows up if an
image does not load properly . Good alt text tells people what the image is
showing. Alt text helps people with vision issues understand images on a
website. Search  engines also use alt text to ﬁnd images. Alt text makes
websites better for everyone by explaining images clearly .
Common Pr oblems:
Missing Alt Text: Images without  alt text provide no information to screen
reader users, making the content inaccessible. Always include alt text for all
meaningful images. Describe the content and function of the image
concisely .
Generic Alt Text: Alt text like "image1" or "picture" is unhelpful and does
not provide useful informatio n about the image. Use speciﬁc and
descriptive alt text that accurately conveys the image's content and purpose.
Overly Detailed Alt Text: Providing too much detail can overwhelm users
and detract from the main conte nt. Keep alt text concise and focused on the
most important aspects of the image. Generally , aim for 125 characters or
fewer .
Redundant Alt Text: Including alt text that repeats the same information
already provided in nearby text can be redundant and annoyin g for screen
reader users. Ensure that alt text provides unique information that
complements rather than duplicates the surrounding content. For example,
if a caption under an image already describes the image, the alt text can be
more succinct or even omitted if it is purely decorative.
Incorr ect Alt Text: Alt text that inaccurately describes the image can
confuse users and provide misleading information. Verify that the alt text
accurately reﬂec ts the content and context of the image. For instance, if an
image shows a dog playing in the park, the alt text should not describe it as
a cat or a different scene. Regular reviews and updates to the alt text can
help maintain accuracy .
Mitigation Strategies:
Meaningful and Concise Alt Text: Provide alt text that describes the
image’ s content and function. For example, for an image of a person
reading a book, alt text could be "Person reading a book."
Alt T ext for an Image
Empty Alt Attributes for Decorative Images: Use empty alt attributes
(alt="") for decorative images that do not convey essential information.
This allows screen readers to skip these images, reducing unnecessary
distraction. For example, a background image or a purely decorative icon
should have an empty alt attribute.
Contextual Alt Text: Ensure that the alt text ﬁts the context of the
surrounding content. It should provide the information that users need to
understand the image in relation to the text. SaaS products often include
data visualizatio ns, diagrams, and instructional images. Alt text makes these
visual elements accessible to all users. Providing alt text helps  meet legal
accessibility standards and guidelines, such as WCAG. Proper alt text
contributes to a better user experience for all users, including those using
assistive technologies.
Self-Evaluation
★      Does every meaningful image on my website have alt text?
★      Is the alt text speciﬁc and descriptive of the image’ s content?
★      Have I avoided using generic alt text like "image" or "picture"?
★      Is the alt text concise and focused, generally aiming for 125
characters or fewer?

surrounding content?
★      Have I checked for redundant alt text that might repeat nearby
information?

★      Have I included empty alt attributes (alt="") for purely decorative
images?

★      Have I used meaningful alt text for data visualizations and diagrams?
★      Is the alt text helping to meet legal accessibility standards like
WCAG?

★      Have I ensured that images used as links have alt text describing their
purpose?
★      Are images that convey important instructions or information
properly described in the alt text?
★      Have I reviewed and updated alt text regularly to maintain accuracy?
★      Have I ensured that images of text have alt text that conveys the same
information?
★      Do all form image buttons have alt text describing their function?
★      Have I tested my website with screen readers to ensure alt text is
correctly interpreted?
★      Are complex images like infographics accompanied by detailed
descriptions in the main content?
★      Have I avoided overloading users with overly detailed alt text for
simple images?
★      Are my alt text descriptions free from unnecessary words or details?
★      Have I used alt text for images that support understanding of the
content, such as charts or graphs?
★      Have I ensured that decorative icons and background images have
empty alt attributes?
★      Do all marketing images, like product photos, have clear and
descriptive alt text?
★      Have I checked that all testimonial or proﬁle images have appropriate
alt text?

relevant context?
★      Have I used alt text for any branding or logo images to describe the
brand?
★      Have I ensured that all social media icons have alt text describing
their purpose?
★      Have I provided alt text for any images used in instructional guides or
tutorials?
★      Are promotional banners and ads described in the alt text?
Poorly Designed Forms
Consider completing a document at the medical clinic, but there are no
markers for the boxes. you would be unsure about where to input your
personal details like your name and address. Now envision that you have
sight issues and you are attempting to ﬁll in a web-based form  without the
right labels or guidelines. The task turns into a tough puzzle, possibly
resulting in mistakes or even making you quit the form completely .
Badly made forms do not just cause trouble; they exclude people. Those
with disabilities, like trouble seeing, use tech aids like screen readers to
manage and ﬁll in forms. If forms do not consider accessibility , it limits
their interaction with your item, leading to less potential users and a bad
view of your product's inclusiveness.
How to Fix This:
1. Use Descriptive Labels for  Form Fields:
Instead of a blank text box, use labels like “First Name” and “Email
Address” that are clearly visible and descriptive. This helps users
understand what information is required.
Putting clear names on form ﬁelds is basic in creating websites . It makes it
user-friendly and easy for everyone. The right names clarify what info
should be in each ﬁeld. This makes ﬁlling out forms quick and easy. it is
very key for people with disabilities. They use screen readers to look at and
use website contents.
Labels are really important for forms. They help people give the right info
easily . Good labels make users sure that they are ﬁlling out the form
correctly . When it is easy to know what to do, people are more likely to
ﬁnish ﬁlling out the form. Clear labels also mean fewer errors and not
having to try again and again to submit the form.
How to Implement Descriptive Labels:
Be Spec iﬁc and Clear: Use labels that explicitly describe the information
required. Avoid vague terms and opt for speciﬁc descriptions that leave no
room for confusion.
Use Placeholder  Text Wisely:  While placeho lder text can be helpful, it
should not replace labels. Placeholders should provide additional hints or
examples but not serve as the primary identiﬁer for the ﬁeld.
Position Labels Appr opriately:  Place labels close to their corresponding
input ﬁelds to create a clear  visual association. Typically , labels are
positioned above or to the left of the input ﬁeld.
Associate Labels with Inputs Using the for Attribute:  Ensure labels are
programmatically associated with their corresponding input ﬁelds using the
for attribute. This attribute should match the id of the input ﬁeld.
Provide Inline Help and Instructions:  Include inline help text or
instructions within the form to guide users on the expected format or
content for each ﬁeld. This can be especially useful for complex ﬁelds like
addresses or payment information.
Use consistent labeling through out your forms. This helps users quickly
recognize and understand what information is needed. Keep labels simple
and to the point. Avoid unnecessary jargon or overly technical terms.
Ensure labels are always visible, even after users start typing. This prevents
users from for getting what the ﬁeld is for if they get interrupted.
2. Associate Labels with Their  Corr esponding Inputs:
Connecting labels to input ﬁelds  is very important for forms. It helps people
use form s easily . This is espec ially true for people using screen readers.
Here is why linking labels and ﬁelds matters, and how to do it well.
Accessibility:  For users who rely on screen  readers, proper association
between labels and input ﬁelds is essential. Screen readers read out the label
text when the user focuses on the input ﬁeld, providing necessary context.
Impr oved User  Experience:  Visual users also beneﬁt from clear
associations, as it reduces confu sion and errors. Users can quickly identify
what information is required in each ﬁeld.
Error Prevention:  Associating labels correctly helps prevent errors by
making it clear what data should be entered in each ﬁeld, reducing the
chance of input mistakes.
Consistency and Standards Compliance:  Following HTML  standards for
label-input associations ensures consistency across different browsers and
devices, leading to a more reliable user experience.
The most straigh tforward way to associate a label with an input ﬁeld is by
using the for attribute in the <label> tag. The value of the for attribute
should match the id of the corre sponding input ﬁeld. Alternatively , you can
place the input element inside the <label> tag. This automatically associates
the label with the input, although it is less commonly used than the for
attribute method.
3. Pr ovide Clear  Error Messages:
Imagine you are ﬁlling out an important form online. You type in your
information, hit submit, and then - error messages pop up. If these messages
are vague or confusing, you might feel frustrated and unsure about how to
ﬁx the issues. This is why clear error messages are so important. They guide
users through correcting mistakes efﬁciently , ensuring a smooth experience
for everyone, including those using assistive technologies.
Why Clear  Error Messages Matter:
Impr oved User  Experience:  Clear error messages help users understand
exactly what went wrong and how to ﬁx it, reducing frustration and making
the process smoother .
Accessibility:  For users with disabilities, clear error messages are crucial.
Screen readers and other assistive technologies depend on these messages
to convey important information about errors.
Efﬁciency:  When error messages are clear , users can quickly identify and
correct mistakes, saving time and avoiding repeated errors.
User  Trust:  Providing clear guidance through error messages builds trust
with users. They feel more conﬁdent using your product when  they know
they will receive understandable instructions if something goes wrong.
How to Pr ovide Clear  Error Messages:
Be Speciﬁc:  Instead of sayin g, "Error ," a good message should specify what
went wrong, like "Please enter  a valid email address." This helps users
know exactly what needs to be corrected.
Place Error Messages Near  the Input:  Position  error messages close to
the relev ant ﬁelds so users can easily see which part of the form needs
attention. For example, if there is an issue with the email ﬁeld, the error
message should appear right next to or below the email input box.
Use Visual Cues:  Combin e text with visual elements like color changes or
icons to draw attention to errors . For instance, an input box might turn red
with an exclamation mark icon when there is a mistake.
Make Error Messages Accessi ble: Ensure that error messages can be read
by screen readers. This means using techniques that allow assistive
technologies to recognize and announce the errors to users who rely on
them.
Use Plain Language:  Write error messages in simple, easy-to-understand
language. Avoid using technical terms that might confuse users. For
example, instead of "Invalid input," say " Please enter a 5-digit ZIP  code. "
Provide Solutio ns: Offer suggestions on how to ﬁx the error . For example,
if a password is too short, the message could say, "Passwor d must be at
least 8 characters long. "
Real-T ime Validation:  Catch errors as users type, providing immediate
feedback. This way, users can correct mistakes before submitting the form.
For example, if an email address is not in the correct format, the form can
show an error message as soon as the user ﬁnishes typing it.
Consistency:  Keep error messages consistent in tone, style, and placement
throughout your application. This helps users learn where to look for help
and creates a cohesive experience.
4. Use ARIA  Live Re gions to Announce Form Validation Err ors:
If you use a screen reader , it can be hard to know where errors are and what
they say . ARIA  live regions help with this. They make sure error messages
are clearly announced to people using assistive technologies.
ARIA  live regions ensure screen readers and other assistive technologies
announce impor tant updates, like form validation errors, right away . This
makes your forms more accessible for people with disabilities. Users get
immediate audio  feedback about errors, so they know right away what went
wrong and how to ﬁx it, without having to search for the information.
Providing instant feedback helps users correct errors quickly , leading to a
smoother and less frustrating form experience.
How to Use ARIA  Live Regions:
Identify Critica l Areas: Determine which parts of your form are likely to
generate error messages and would beneﬁt from live region anno uncements.
This typically includes ﬁelds like email addresses, passwords, and any
required ﬁelds.
Implement ARIA  Attributes:  Use speciﬁc ARIA  attributes to make live
regions. The main attribute you will use is aria-live, which can be set to
"polite" or "assertive." For error messages, "assertive" is often the best
choice, as it ensures that the message is announced immediately .
Update Live Regions Dynamically:  When an error message appears,
update the content of the live region. This triggers the screen reader to
announce the new information.
Placement:  Place the ARIA  live region near the form ﬁelds it pertains to.
This helps ensure that screen readers can accurately associate the error
messages with the relevant input ﬁelds.
Use Simple, Clear  Language:  Write error messages in straightforward
language to ensure they are easily understood when read aloud by screen
readers. For example, instead of "Input invalid," use "Please enter a valid
email addr ess."
Test with Screen Readers:  Regular ly test your forms with various screen
readers to make sure the ARIA  live regions work as expected. This helps
ensure that all users have a seamless experience.
Imagine you are ﬁlling out a contact form and forget to include  your email
address. With ARIA  live regio ns, as soon as you submit the form, your
screen reader announces, "Error: Please enter your email  address." This
immediate feedback helps you correct the mistake without any confusion.
5. Pr ovide Re al-Time Feedback:
Giving feedback as someone ﬁlls out a form is like having a friend help
you. They point out mistakes and give tips. This makes things easier and
less frustrating than ﬁnishing the form and then seeing a list of errors.
With real-time feedback, peop le can ﬁx mistakes right away instead of
having many errors at the end. This makes for a better experience. Getting
quick prompts helps people enter accurate, complete information -
improving the data collected. Receiving help along the way also keeps
people from getting frustrated and quitting the form.
How to Implement Real-T ime Feedback
Input Validatio n: Check the user's input against the required criteria as
they type. For example, if a user is entering an email address, validate the
format in real-time and provide immediate feedback if it is incorrect.
Visual Cues:  Use color changes, icons, or text messages to indicate
whether the input is valid or needs correction. For instance, a green
checkmark for valid input and a red cross or error message for invalid input.
Tooltips and Pop-Ups:  Provide additional informatio n or suggestions
through tooltips  or small pop-up  messages next to the input ﬁeld. This can
guide users on what is required or how to ﬁx an error .
Be Clea r and Speciﬁc:  Ensure  that feedback messages are clear and
speciﬁc, so users know exactly what they need to correct. For example,
"Passwor d must be at least 8 characters " is more helpful than a generic
"Invalid password."
Non-Intrusive Alerts:  Make sure that the feedback is noticeable but not
intrusive. Users should be able to continue typing and making corrections
without being interrupted by the feedback.
Consistent Feed back:  Keep the feedba ck consistent in terms of placement,
color , and format across all form  ﬁelds. This helps users quickl y recognize
and understand the feedback.
Suppose you are signing up for a service and need to create a password. As
you type, the form provides real-time feedback: “Passwor d must be at least
8 charac ters,” “Passwor d must include a number ,” and “Passwor d strength:
Strong.” This guidance helps you create a secure password that meets all
requirements before you hit submit.
In another example, when entering your email to subscribe to a newsletter ,
the form immediately checks the format. If you miss a “@” or type “.con”
instead of “.com ,” a message pops up saying, “Please enter a valid email
address.” This immediate correction prevents errors that could stop you
from receiving updates.
6. Keep Forms Simple and Logical
Having simple and well-or ganiz ed forms is very important. It helps people
ﬁll them out easily and without getting frustrated. When  forms are
straightforward, users can complete them quickly and correctly . This can
help reduce people from abandoning the form before ﬁnishing it.
Simple forms are easy to unders tand and ﬁll out. This leads to more people
completing them. Arranging the form logically prevents confusion. It assists
users in providing accurate details. A clear and concise form design creates
a good experience for users. This encourages users to engage more with
your product or website.
How to Keep Forms Simple and Logical:
Limit the Number  of Fields:  Only ask for essential information. Each
additional ﬁeld increases the complexity and time required to complete the
form.
Group Related Fields:  Organize the form into sections based on related
information. For example, group personal information (name, email)
separately from payment information.
Use Clear  Labels:  Ensure that each ﬁeld has a clear and descriptive label
so users understand what information is required.
Provide Instructions:  Include brief instructions or placeholders within the
form ﬁelds to guide users on how to ﬁll them out correctly .
Logical Flow:  Arrange ﬁelds in a logical order that aligns with how users
naturally think and process information. For example, start with personal
details, followed by contact information, and then payment details.
Reduce Cognit ive Load:  Simplify complex forms by breaking them into
smaller , more manageable sections. For instance, a multi-step form can
guide users through the process step-by-step.
Visual Hierar chy: Use visual cues like headin gs, spacing, and color
contrast to create a clear visual  hierarchy . This helps users easily navigate
and understand the form.
Single Column  Layout:  Use a single column layout to reduce eye
movement and make the form  easier to scan and ﬁll out. Multi-column
layouts can be confusing and lead to errors.
Progress Indicators:  For longer forms, include a progress indicator to
show users how far along they are and how many steps remain. This can
help reduce abandonment rates by setting clear expectations.
Ask Only What is Necessary:  Think about the essential information you
need from users  and focus on those ﬁelds. Avoid asking for extra details
unless absolutely necessary .
Use Placeholders Wisely:  Placeholders within form ﬁelds can provide
examples or additional instructions without cluttering the interface.
Leverage Auto ﬁll: Enable  autoﬁll  for common ﬁelds like addresses and
payment inform ation. This can save users time and reduce the chances of
errors.
Responsive Design: Ensure that your form is mobile-friendly . A responsive
design adjusts the form layout  based on the user’s device, providing a
seamless experience across dif ferent screen sizes.
Inline Validatio n: Provide real-time validation and feedback next to the
form ﬁelds. This immediate feedback helps users correct mistakes as they
go, rather than after submitting the form.
Look at a simple signup form for an online service. It typically includes just
a few ﬁelds: name, email, and password. This simplicity encourages more
users to complete the signup process quickly .
Online retailers often streamlin e their checkout forms to include only the
necessary ﬁelds for payment and shipping. By minimizing the number of
ﬁelds and grouping them logically (e.g., shipping address, payment
method), they enhance the user experience and reduce cart abandonment.
7. Offer  He lp Text and Examples:
Giving help text and examples in forms is like having a friendly helper . This
helper guides people through the process. It answers questions and makes
things clear. This extra help can make people feel more conﬁd ent. It also
helps them understand better . Then they can ﬁll out forms more accurately
and quickly .
Help text explai ns what information is needed and how to format it. This
reduces confusio n and mistakes. Examples show people what is expected.
Some people prefer to see exam ples. Others like written explanations  better .
Having both helps dif ferent types of learners.
How to Offer  Help Text and Examples:
Tooltip or Placeholder  Text: Place brief instructions or examples directly
within the form  ﬁeld as placeh older text or in tooltips that appear when
users hover over the ﬁeld.
Inline Help Icons:  Use small question mark icons or info buttons next to
form ﬁelds that users can click to reveal additional help text or examples.
Adjacent Help Sections:  Include a dedic ated help section adjacent to the
form that provides detailed explanations, examples, and frequently asked
questions (F AQs).
Interactive Tutorials:  Offer interactiv e tutorials or guided tours that walk
users through the form-ﬁlling process step-by-step, highlighting key points
and providing examples along the way .
Be Concise:  Keep help text and examples concise and to the point to avoid
overwhelming users with too much information.
Use Visuals: Incorpo rate visual examples, such as screenshots or diagrams,
to complement written instructions and appeal to visual learners.
Few examples:
Date Format Help:  In a date ﬁeld, provide help text that explains the
required date format (e.g., MM/DD/YYYY) and offers examples of valid
date formats (e.g., 01/31/2024).
Password Requir ements:  For password ﬁelds, include help text that
outlines the password requir ements (e.g., minimum length, special
characters) and provides examples of strong passwords.
Addr ess Forma t: When collecting address inform ation, offer help text that
clariﬁes how to format the address correctly (e.g., street, city, state, postal
code) and provides examples of valid address formats.
Gov.uk's online forms are a great example of accessibility in action. They
use clear labels, logical groupi ng, and real-time feedback to guide users
through the process. Their error messages are speciﬁc and help users
understand what went wrong. Google Forms offers a user-friendly
experience with clearly labeled ﬁelds and instant validation feedback. They
also provide visual cues and instructions to help users understand what is
required.

the form?
★      Are ﬁelds pre-ﬁlled with user information where possible to reduce
input ef fort?
★      Is the language of the form simple and easy to understand?
★      Are there visual or audio cues for users with dif ferent accessibility
needs?
★      Are error messages tested for clarity with actual users?
★      Are forms checked for compatibility with dif ferent browsers and
devices?
★      Is there a mechanism for users to save progress and resume later on
lengthy forms?
★      Are there clear instructions for ﬁelds that require speciﬁc formatting
(e.g., credit card numbers)?
★      Are forms kept as short and concise as possible while gathering
necessary information?
★      Are forms reviewed regularly to improve user experience based on
feedback?
★      Are visual contrast and font size optimized for readability?
Inaccessible Dynamic Content
Today's websites often update content in real-time. This happens with
things like pop-ups, notiﬁcations and interactive features. But it is important
that these updates are accessible to all users, including those who use
assistive technology . If changing  content is not announced to screen readers
or managed properly , it can cause big usability issues for people with
disabilities.
let us say you are on a social media site. New messages appear in a chat
window . If you are visually impaired and using a screen reader , you might
miss those new messages. This is because they were not announced. ARIA
Live regions can help with situations like this.
ARIA  (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) live regions are special
attributes you can add to HTM L elements. They announce changes to
content dynamic ally. With aria-live, you specify that screen readers should
announce updates. There are different "politeness" levels (polite, assertive,
etc.) to control how updates are announced. This ensures people using
screen readers are told about new content right away . It makes the
application more inclusive and accessible.
Managing Focus:
When a pop-up or dialog opens , it is crucial to move the keybo ard focus to
the new content . You need to make sure assistive technology announces it
too. You can do this by setting the focus to the ﬁrst interactive  part within
the pop-up.
Think of a web store. A shopper taps "More Details" about an item. A new
window shows up with extra info. If focus is not done right , the screen
reader user may not know the new window opened. Proper focus lets all
users, even with assistive tech, use dynamic content with no mix-up.
Using ARIA  Role Attributes:
ARIA  role attrib utes like alert or dialog can help screen readers spot and
announce dyna mic content changes properly . These attributes make
dynamic conten t accessible by ensuring screen readers can recognize and
announce key updates to users.
<div role="alert"> Breaking News: Big earthquake hits the city . </div>
On a news site, breaking news alerts may pop up. Without proper role tags,
these alerts may not get announced to screen reader users.
Using Text Alternatives For  Visual Content:
Giving text alternatives for visual content, like alt text for images or aria-
label for icons, ensures all users get the same information. Text alternatives
ensure updates shown visually are also accessible to users relying on screen
readers.
A weath er app shows updates by changing an icon based on current
conditions. If weather changes from sunny to rainy , shown only by an icon,
a screen reader user may miss this update.
A tool for project management updates task status in real-time. If these live
updates cannot be accessed with a keyboard, users who need keyboard
navigation may miss vital changes. You must make sure that dynamic
content updates work with keyboard controls. This means all interactive
parts like pop-ups, drop-down menus, and notiﬁcations can be reached and
used by just a keyboard. Along with following these tips, frequent testing
helps keep a high level of accessibility . This ensures that dynamic content
updates stay inclusive and easy to use for everyone.
Self-Evaluation

opens?

using a keyboard?

displayed?

a user -friendly manner for all users?
Non-Descriptive Link Text
Links on websit es should be easy to comprehend. They should not just say
"click here" or "read more." These brief phrases do not reveal where the
link goes. This section discusses problems caused by unclear link text. It
also suggests ways to improve how links work for all users, even those
using screen readers.
GOV .UK Website Redesign (2012-2014)
The GOV .UK website redesign,  which took place between 2012 and 2014,
offers a compelling case study on the importance of descriptive link text in
web design. When the UK government launched GOV .UK as a uniﬁed
platform to replace various departmental sites, they faced unexpected
challenges related to link accessibility , particularly for users  relying on
screen readers.
Initially , many pages on GOV .UK contained links with non-descriptive text
such as "click here," "read more," or simply "link." This created a host of
issues for users. Screen reader users struggled to understand the purpose of
links when navigating through a list of page links. Users with cognitive
disabilities found it difﬁcult to predict where links would lead them.
Additionally , the site's searchability suffered, as search engines use link text
to understand page content.
These problems led to several negative consequences. Visually impaired
users experienced reduced accessibility , while all users faced an increased
cognitive load when trying to navigate the site. The non-descriptive links
also resulted in lower search engine rankings for important government
information. Perhaps most concerningly , the issues prompted complaints
from disability advocacy groups , highlighting the real-world impact of this
design oversight.
Recognizing the severity of the problem, the GOV .UK team undertook a
comprehensive review and redesign of their link text strategy . They
established clear guidelines for writing descriptive link text and began
rewriting links to be self-expla natory . For instance, "Click here to apply"
became "Apply for a passport." In cases where longer link text was
necessary , they used ARIA  labels to provide additional context for screen
readers.
To ensure ongoing complian ce with their new standards, the team
implemented a site-wide audit tool to identify and ﬂag non-descriptive link
text. They also provided training for content creators on writing accessible
link text, recognizing that maintaining good practices required ongoing
education and support.
The results of these changes were signiﬁcant and positive. GOV .UK saw
increased satisfaction rates among users with disabilities and improved
navigation efﬁciency for all users. The site's search engine performance for
key government services also improved. Perhaps most gratifyingly , they
received positive feedback from accessibility advocates, indicating that
their ef forts had made a real dif ference.
Use Descriptive Text for  Links
Instead of "click here" for product details, say "View product details."
Describing the link's purpose provides clarity . This helps all users
understand where the link will take them. Screen reader users who hear
links out of context especially beneﬁt.
<a href="product-details.html">V iew product details</a>
With meaningful link text, anyone can easily grasp the link's purpose. This
improves user experience and accessibility .
Add Keywords to Link Text
Adding relevant keywords improves accessibility and search rankings.
Meaningful link text signals what content it leads to. On a blog, instead of
"read more," say "Read more about effective SEO strategies." Users quickly
comprehend the context. Search engines recognize relevant keywords.
Screen readers convey the link's purpose.
Links Within Content
Adding links inside relevant text helps explain the link. User s can grasp
what they'll ﬁnd by clicking without extra details.
Our commitment to integrity and transparency is unwavering. <a
href="company-values.html">Learn more about our company values</a>.
This creates a smooth reading ﬂow, improves access, and ensures users
know what to anticipate from the link. In an article discussing company
principles, instea d of a standalon e "learn more" link, use "Disco ver our core
values."
Use ARIA  Labels for  Mor e Info
ARIA  labels can provide extra details about links, making them clearer for
screen readers. This helps when you need short link text for design but want
more context.
<a href="annual-report.pdf" aria-label="Download the 2023 Annual
Report">Download report</a>
This approach enhances access ibility by giving screen readers more info
about the link, ensuring they grasp its purpose. For a "Download report"
link, add an ARIA  label for extra context.
Avoid Repeating Link Text
Repeating link text can confuse , especially for screen readers encountering
multiple identical links. Using  unique, descriptive text for each link
improves clarity and access.
Links should show where they go. Do not use "Read more" for all links.
<a href="article1.html">Remote work beneﬁts</a>
<a href="article2.html">T echnology changes education</a>
Unique link text helps users understand linked content. It is clearer and
more descriptive.
Non-Descriptive links - ‘Read mor e’ everywher e

Ignoring User  Preferences and System Settings
Today's product  design does not focus only on looks; it is about making
universal, easy-to-use experiences that address user's personal likes and
system conﬁgurations. When products forget these preferences, it can cause
uneasiness and accessibility problems for users. This part will explore the
discomforts caused by neglecting user settings and provide methods to
make conﬁdent your product is ﬂexible and mindful of user needs.
Use Relative Units for  Font Size:
Setting font sizes in relative units guarantees the text adjusts to the user's
settings. People who need bigger text for easy reading can use their browser
or system preferences to help.
body {
font-size: 1rem; /* Relative to the root font size */
}
h1 {
font-size: 2rem; /* Twice the size of the body text */
}
This method makes your item easier to use for people with vision issues,
making sure they can read the text without extra zoom or tweaking. Instead
of deﬁni ng typeface sizes in pixels, opt for relative measureme nts such as
em or rem.
Implement CSS Media Queries for  Color  Schemes:
Media queries such as prefers-color -scheme enable your product to
instantly swap between light and dark modes in line with user choice s. This
lessens eye discomfort and improves user ease, especially in dimly lit
spaces.
Matching the user's color scheme choices improves their interaction with
your product. It aligns with their visual preferences, creating a more
pleasant usage experience.
Respect Reduced Motion Pr eferences:
There are users who can feel uneasy or even queasy from too much
movement on-screen. The prefe rs-reduced-motion media query helps you
detect this preference and adjus t the amount of motion accordi ngly. Apply
the prefers-reduced-motion media query with the aim of lessening the
motion for those  who like it that way. This thoughtful step greatly enhances
the comfort level of users who are sensitive to such aspects, ensuring that
your product stays user -friendly and comfy to operate.
Provide User  Contr ol Options:
System settings  are important but giving users control inside the product
adds more ﬂexibility . Let users change text size, color themes, and motion
settings in your product themselves. By doing this, users can make an
experience that ﬁts them best which improves happiness and ease of use.
Valuing user choices improves the whole user experience. Also, it shows
your dedication to accessibility and designs that prioritize users.
Self-Evaluation

★      Have you implemented CSS media queries for color schemes?

and dark modes?
★      Do you respect users' reduced motion preferences?

and motion settings within your product?

according to their preferences?

Inaccessible Media
Media content is key in many SaaS products. Things like videos, sound
snippets, and similar media boost user interaction. They share details in an
interesting way. But, when media content is hard to access, it can leave out
parts of your audience. This is especially true for those with problems
seeing or hearing. This part looks at the usual issues with hard-to-reach
media. It gives clear steps to make sure your media content is good for
everyone.
The Netﬂix Captioning Lawsuit
In 2011, the National Association of the Deaf (NAD) ﬁled a landmark
lawsuit against Netﬂix, alleging  that the company's lack of closed captions
on most  of its streaming content was a violation of the Ame ricans with
Disabilities Act (ADA). This case highlighted the critical importance of
making media content accessible to all users, including those with hearing
impairments.
At the time, Netﬂix was rapidly growing as a streaming service, offering a
vast library of movies and TV shows. However , only a small portion of this
content was captioned, making it inaccessible to deaf and hard-of-hearing
viewers. The lawsuit argued that this lack of captioning excluded a
signiﬁcant portion of the population from fully participating in and enjoying
Netﬂix's services.
The core issues were multifaceted. Netﬂix argued that the ADA  did not
apply to online-only businesse s and that they were not responsible for
captioning content they did not produce. They also cited technical
challenges in implementing captions across their entire library . However ,
the deaf community argued that access to entertainment and cultural content
was a right, not a privilege, and that Netﬂix's position was discriminatory .
The consequences of Netﬂix's initial stance were signiﬁcant. The company
faced negative publicity and a potential loss of subscribers from the deaf
and hard-of-hearing community . The lawsuit also raised broader questions
about the responsibilities of streaming services in providing accessible
content.
In 2012, Netﬂix settled the lawsuit, agreeing to caption 100% of its
streaming conte nt by 2014. This was a monumental shift in their approach
to acces sibility . The company committed to adding captions to all new
content within 30 days of its appearance on the service, and to captioning
their entire back catalog over time.
Beyond just adding captions, Netﬂix made several other improvements:
●         They implemented user -friendly caption customization options,
allowing viewers to change caption size, color , and font.
●         They improved their interface to make it easier for users to ﬁnd and
enable captions.
●         They began working more closely with content creators to ensure
captions were available from the outset.
●         They invested in technology to improve the quality and accuracy of
their captions.
Provide Closed Captions for  Videos
Websites such as YouTube and Vimeo provide inbuilt features to insert and
change subtitles. For videos hosted on your own site, applications like
Amara or CaptionMaker can be used to form subtitle documents (for
instance, SRT or VTT) and integrate them with your video content. Closed
captions show spoken words and signiﬁcant sound signals in written form
on the screen. This beneﬁts people who are deaf or have heari ng issues to
understand. it is essential to add closed captions to every video content to
ensure audio understanding for users suf fering from hearing loss.
Offer  Transcripts for  Audio and Video Content
Transcripts turn sound into words. This helps users who cann ot hear and
those who like to read more than they like to listen. You can use something
like Rev or Otter .ai to do this with audio and video. Make sure people can
get to these transcripts; put a link under the audio or video player or make it
a ﬁle they can download. Transcripts let people who have trouble hearing
enjoy your content. They can also make your content show up more in
search results, as these written  words can be "read" by search engines,
helping you reach more people.
Provide Audio Descriptions for  Videos
Enhancing multimedia accessibility is essential for creating an inclusive
experience. Audio descriptions provide a narration of visual elements,
enabling users with vision impairments to comprehend videos effectively .
Utilizing tools like YouDescr ibe or video editing software, you can
incorporate supplementary audio tracks or separate ﬁles detailing crucial
actions, scene transitions, and visual cues.
Use Accessible Media Players
Employ user-friendly media players equipped with robust accessibility
features. Customizable captions, intuitive keyboard navigation, and
seamless screen reader integrat ion empower users by catering to diverse
needs. These players offer a tailored multimedia experience, ensuring
content remains accessible and engaging for all.
Regularly solicit user feedback and conduct comprehensive testing to
continually reﬁne and optimize your media content's accessibility . This
iterative process creates an environment of inclusivity , enabling all
individuals to fully engage with and appreciate the rich multimedia
experiences you provide. By prioritizing accessibility , you demonstrate a
genuine commitment to accommodating diverse user needs and promoting
equal access to information and entertainment.

customizable captions and keyboard navigation?

★      Do I solicit user feedback regarding the accessibility of my media
content?

★      Am I aware of and compliant with accessibility standards and
guidelines?
★      How do I handle requests for accessible media content?
★      Do I provide options for users to adjust the font size and color of
captions?
★      Are my captions synchronized accurately with the audio?
★      How do I prioritize accessibility when working with content creators?
★      Have I invested in technology to improve the quality and accuracy of
my captions?
★      Are there any areas where my media content currently lacks
accessibility features?

and vision impairments?

browsers?

content?
★      How do I ensure that my media content remains inclusive and
engaging for all users?
★      Have I conducted an audit to identify and address any accessibility
gaps in my media content?
Over -reliance on Hover  States
Many platforms  and applications make use of hover effects. These get
activated when you place your mouse pointer on certain featur es. This can
show more details, such as tooltips or activate extra functions through
hover -triggered menus and buttons.
Hover states give a smooth extra experience for users using a mouse. But
there is a major issue when key details or main functions are only accessible
via these  hover actions. Those with disabilities that restrict precise mouse
movements or those without a mouse cursor , such as touch device users,
cannot access all hover -activated features and content. They ﬁnd themselves
sidelined, missing out on possibly essential elements of the experience.
Imagine a shop placing all its key item features and cost information on
small tags. To read them, you would need to lean your head 2 inches close.
Those who cannot do this exact action are unlucky .
The Accessible Solution
It is essential that all data and features are mainly accessible through
clicking or tapping actions. This should be true for all users, no matter what
way they input or what physical abilities they have.
Hover states are best employed as added ﬂourishes to enhance the main
experience, not as core necessities. Nevertheless, the basics should stay
reachable with the most common interaction around - a straightforward
click or tap gesture.
Taking this approach means rethinking any UI designs where hovers gates
core content or workﬂows. Things like:
Important instructions or tooltips should also display on click/tap
Navigation menus must be fully accessible and usable without
hovering
Dynamic buttons/controls should have visible click/tap triggers
When hovers lead to more features, developers ought to aim for user-
friendly interaction models driven by click actions, separate buttons, or
different programmable triggers.
The aim is to create product experiences that work ﬂawlessly with the most
basic inputs at the start. Only after setting that interaction method should,
we add extra excitement through cursor enhancements such as hover states.
By prior itizing clicks, you secure a dependable foundation for all users, no
matter their physical strengths or device limitations. Users with hover
capabilities can enhance this core accessibility with extra beneﬁts - unlike
the opposite approach, which fundamentally leaves them out.
Self-Evaluation
★      Are any crucial features or information only accessible through hover
actions?
★      Can users without a mouse easily access all the information and
features on your platform?
★      Do touch device users have full access to all functionalities?
★      Are tooltips and important instructions visible on click or tap?
★      Is the navigation menu fully usable without requiring hovering?
★      Are dynamic buttons and controls easily identiﬁable and usable with
clicks or taps?
★      Have you tested your UI design with users who have disabilities or
use assistive technologies?
★      Do all users, regardless of their physical abilities, have equal access to
core functionalities?
★      Have you ensured that hover ef fects are enhancements rather than
necessities?
★      Are there clear , clickable alternatives to hover -triggered features?
★      Have you considered dif ferent interaction models driven by clicks
instead of hovers?
