or tap gestures?
★      Is there a clear visual indication of click/tap triggers for dynamic
features?
★      Have you evaluated your product to ensure it works well with basic
input methods?
★      Are hover states used to enhance the experience rather than being the
primary method of interaction?
★      Do users with disabilities or without hover capabilities have a
seamless experience?
★      Have you rethought any UI designs where hovers gate core content or
workﬂows?
★      Are you conﬁdent that your product experience does not leave out any
user groups?
★      Have you prioritized click actions to create a dependable foundation
for all users?
★      Do users with hover capabilities get extra beneﬁts without excluding
those without?
Non-standard Interactive Elements
At times, developers build elaborate, personalized user interface elements
from the ground up such as distinct dropdown menus, dynamic  controls, or
singular navigation widgets. While these provide opportunities for artistic,
inventive expe riences, there exist major chances for accessibility
complications.
If these custom UI components are not built properly , assistive technologies
like screen reade rs can really struggle to understand what they are and how
to interact with them.
Common issues include:
●         Using generic HTML  elements like divs instead of proper controls
●         Not communicating changes in the component's state to screen
readers
●         Not specifying proper roles, names, or other semantics for screen
readers
For assistive technology , these customized UI elements might  appear like
bafﬂing "black boxes". They are either totally unseen or declared with
pointless tags that do not describe what they are for .
The Accessible Solution
The better approach is to stick with standard, native HTML  controls and
options whenever possible. Things like <button>, <input>, <select> and
others have built-in roles and behaviors that screen readers readily
understand out-of-the-box.
When you must use custom components, make sure to apply correct ARIA
roles, states, and properties. ARIA  serves as an interpreter , connecting your
unique UI with screen readers. Aim to keep to recognized accessibility
design methods and example code rather than creating everything by
yourself. Resources such as WAI-ARIA  Authoring Practices offer tried-and-
true solutions.
Always make sure to conduct thorough tests with actual screen readers and
other supportive technology while developing. This way, communication
issues can be discovered before  causing confusion for the users. The  main
objective is to utilize already available accessible structures, instead of
creating new controls that may not work with supportive technology .
Construct inven tive user interfaces when required, yet do it over inclusive
bases that are already depen dable with screen readers and assistive
technology . This way, you are merging innovation and ke eping accessibility
a priority .
Self-Evaluation
★      Are you using standard HTML  controls like <button>, <input>, and
<select> wherever possible?
★      Have you avoided using generic HTML  elements like divs for
interactive components?
★      Are you communicating changes in the component's state to screen
readers?
★      Have you speciﬁed proper roles, names, and other semantics for
screen readers?
★      Have you tested your custom UI elements with actual screen readers?
★      Are your custom components recognized by assistive technologies?
★      Have you applied correct ARIA  roles, states, and properties to your
custom components?
★      Are you following recognized accessibility design methods?
★      Have you referred to resources like WAI-ARIA  Authoring Practices?
★      Are your custom UI elements described meaningfully for screen
readers?
★      Do your custom components appear as understandable elements to
assistive technology?
★      Have you conducted thorough tests with supportive technology
during development?
★      Are you using accessible structures that are already dependable?
★      Have you ensured that screen readers can interact with all your UI
elements?
★      Do your custom interactive elements enhance the user experience
without compromising accessibility?
★      Are there any "black box" elements in your UI that are invisible or
confusing to screen readers?
★      Have you balanced innovation with accessibility in your UI design?
★      Are your interactive elements clear and usable for users with
disabilities?
★      Have you ensured that assistive technologies can interpret all custom
UI elements correctly?
★      Are you mer ging inventive design with established accessibility
practices ef fectively?
Complex Language and Jargon
Often, for various goods and services, mainly in unique sectors or technical
ﬁelds, there is a common habit to talk using fancy lingo, tricky wording,
and lots of difﬁcult terms.
Yet, this wide ﬁeld of unusual words can turn into a thorny bush, tough to
understand for people with mental challenges, language obstacles, or
merely insufﬁcient knowledge about the topic. Big words confuse and block
rather than shedding light and boosting these audience groups.
Examples where complex, inscrutable language can impede accessibility:
Medical information using hyper -technical diagnoses/procedural
names
Legal documentation overﬂowing with arcane legalese and
formalities
Software/IT  products liberally employing alphabet soup
terminology
People who have cognitive or neurodiverse conditions often ﬁnd
understanding these complicated sayings as hard as hearing information in a
completely new language - no matter how well they know  their own
language. This is like a confusing puzzle without any background context.
Stripe's Journey to Simpler  Words
Stripe is a techn ology company that provides online payment processing for
businesses. Their services are crucial for many internet-based companies,
but they faced a signiﬁcant challenge in how they communicate d with their
users. Stripe's team noticed that their documentation and user interfaces
were ﬁlled with complex ﬁnancial and technical terms. This made it
difﬁcult for many of their users, especially small business owners and
developers without a ﬁnancial background, to fully understand and use
Stripe's services.
In response to this issue, Stripe set an objective to make their products more
accessible. They  aimed to simplify the language used across their website,
documentation, and user interfac es. This task came with several challenges.
First, the nature of Stripe's business involves complex ﬁnancial
transactions, which often require speciﬁc terminology . Second, Stripe's user
base is diverse, ranging from highly technical developers to non-technical
business owners . Lastly , as a global company , Stripe needed to consider
various levels of English proﬁciency and cultural contexts.
To address these challenges, Stripe took a multi-faceted approach:
They conducted extensive research, gathering feedback from a
wide range of users to understand which terms and phrases were
most confusing.
Stripe collaborated with language experts to rewrite complex
sections in simpler terms without losing essential meaning.
The team created a style guide to ensure consistent use of
simpliﬁed language across all platforms and communications.
They incorporated more visual aids, such as diagrams and
examples, to explain complex concepts more intuitively .
Users reported a better understanding of Stripe's services, especially those
without a technical background. There was increased engagement with
Stripe's docume ntation and interfaces, as users felt more conﬁdent and less
intimidated. The simpliﬁcation also made Stripe's resources more accessible
to non-native English speakers and individuals with cognitive disabilities.
The Simple Language Solution
The answer lies in purposely shaping a speech structure focused on
simplicity , friendliness, and understandability as primary pillars - adding
detailed layers only when completely necessary:
Set Clear Foundations. Speak in simple, everyday words void of technical
terms to explain all key product ideas, attributes, and guides. Keep the
number of characters short. Promote using direct speech and steer clear of
confusion.
Deﬁne Key Terms
When special words speciﬁc to a domain are absolutely required, make sure
they get properly deﬁned and broken down into easy explanations. Place
them in a glossary that all users can actively refer to.
Block & Level Difﬁculty: For complex and technical topics, use rational
divisions of content and gradual  revealing techniques to control information
heaviness. Keep  introductions simple to understand, increase the detail level
only when needed.
Translations & Localization
If users speak different mother tongues, make sure vital instructions and
terms are fully translated. This should be done by expert translators who get
the subtle aspects of accessibility .
Consult Readability Guidelines
Stick to proven rules for readable text, such as those suggested by W3C.
Look into advised target reader grade levels. Make sure to clarif y acronyms.
Keep your sentences simple. Use images to support your points, and so on.
By using basic language as the starting point, not just an option , you make
things clear for users with all types of thinking skills. users already
comfortable with complex words can add that to the basic foundation as
they see ﬁt.
The aim is to simplify intricate, expert information into readable, simple
shapes that act as ramps accessible to all. Keep the complexity there, but
avoid hiding it behind words that bar people out. Everyone should have the
chance to understand from the same baseline.
Self-Evaluation
★      Are you using simple, everyday language to explain key product
ideas?
★      Have you avoided using technical terms unless absolutely necessary?
★      When technical terms are required, have you deﬁned and explained
them clearly?
★      Have you created a glossary for special domain-speciﬁc terms?
★      Is your content divided logically to manage the complexity of
information?
★      Do you start with simple explanations and gradually increase detail as
needed?
★      Have you translated vital instructions and terms for users who speak
different languages?
★      Were expert translators consulted to ensure accurate and accessible
translations?
★      Are you following readability guidelines such as those suggested by
W3C?
★      Have you tar geted a suitable reader grade level for your text?
★      Are acronyms clearly explained in your content?
★      Are your sentences kept simple and direct?
★      Have you used images to support and clarify your points?
★      Is your language accessible to users with various cognitive abilities?
★      Have you ensured that your communication is friendly and easy to
understand?
★      Do you periodically review and update your content to maintain
simplicity and clarity?
★      Have you gathered feedback from a diverse range of users to identify
confusing terms and phrases?
★      Have you worked with language experts to simplify complex sections
without losing essential meaning?
★      Have you created a style guide to ensure consistent use of simpliﬁed
language across all platforms?
★      Have you incorporated visual aids like diagrams and examples to
make complex concepts more intuitive?
Lack of Text Resizing Options
People with visual challenges, such as poor sight or issues that hamper their
ability to read, it is essential to have the ability to modify the size of the
text. This is critical for them to easily navigate websites and use
applications. Still, a lot of online tools are built with set, rigid text scales
that the user cannot readily tweak. This uniform approach does not suit
anyone and leaves many users facing obstacles in accessibility .
Imagine going to a diner where the menus use tiny 6-point text size. To the
ones with ﬂawless 20/20 sight, it might not matter , yet for many , this makes
the content almost unreadable, bypassing its actual intention.
The Flexible Font-Sizing Solution
Products must be totally user-friendly . They should let users effortlessly
make the text larger, even up to twice the normal size. And they should do
this while keeping all content, features, and layouts intact.
The feature to adjust and magni fy text to a personal reading level ought to
be seen as neces sary, not a secondary need. After all, our eyesi ght abilities
range wide on a diverse scale.
From a technical implementation standpoint, this means avoiding the use of
ﬁxed units like pixels (px) when specifying font sizes in code. Instead,
relying on relative units like:
em - Font size relative to parent element
rem - Font size relative to root/HTML  element
% - Percentage of another font size
These adaptable parts let text adjust seamlessly in size based on what users
prefer . This could be through changes in web browser magniﬁcation, the
text size settings on the operating system, or special tools designed for
accessibility .
Thorough checks must conﬁrm. Zooming to 200% or enlar gening root
em/rem sizes should not trim any words. No overﬂows or too wide for
containers. Most importantly , should not ruin the reading pleasure.
UDAI’ s Text Resizing Options
Apart from tech requirements, it is equally essential to integrate easy-to-use
zoom features right into the product's system. This provides users the
chance to quickly change font sizes with several simple clicks or taps,
instead of searching through web menu options or device settings. The
ability to save choices is pretty beneﬁcial too.
When we offer ﬂexible text resizing, the issue of fonts changes. It goes
from being an annoying barrier to accessibility to a powerful tool. This tool
can be adjusted to suit each user's speciﬁc visual capacity and reading likes.
Gone are the days of squinting to read tiny text or battling with content
made only for a limited scope of supposed "average" sight.
Self-Evaluation
★      Can users easily resize text on your platform?
★      Does your product allow text to be increased up to twice the normal
size without breaking the layout?
★      Are all content, features, and layouts intact when text size is
increased?
★      Are you using relative units like em, rem, or % for font sizes instead
of ﬁxed units like pixels?
★      Have you tested your platform to ensure no words are cut of f when
zooming to 200%?
★      Is there no overﬂow or text that becomes too wide for containers
when the text is resized?
★      Have you ensured that increasing text size does not degrade the
reading experience?
★      Do you provide easy-to-use zoom features directly within your
product?
★      Can users quickly change font sizes with just a few clicks or taps?
★      Is there an option for users to save their preferred text size settings?
★      Have you tested your product with users who have visual
impairments?
★      Are you avoiding the use of ﬁxed text sizes in your code?
★      Do you allow text resizing through web browser magniﬁcation and
operating system text size settings?
★      Is your product accessible to users with a wide range of visual
abilities?
★      Are there clear instructions for users on how to resize text?
★      Have you received feedback from users about the text resizing
functionality?
★      Do you regularly update and test your text resizing features?
★      Are you compliant with accessibility standards regarding text
resizing?
★      Have you considered the needs of users with low vision or reading
difﬁculties in your design?
★      Are there any parts of your product where text resizing does not work
properly?
Ignoring Touch Targets and Spacing
With more and more product experiences moving to mobile devices and
touch inputs, it is vital to cater to the special demands of these  interaction
methods for better accessibilit y. More speciﬁcally , it is important that
interactive items have big tap areas along with enough spacing to avoid
problems with precision.
Users who have motor disabilities that affect hand movement, steadiness, or
cause shaking, know how hard it can be to accurately tap on tiny targets
only a few pixel s wide. it is like playing a really tough Twitch shooter game
- super frustratin g. And even if you do not have these issues, we have all
felt that annoyin g struggle of mistakenly hitting the wrong small button on
a phone and ending up doing something we did not mean to do.
Beyond just motor disabilities, improper touch target sizing creates usability
hurdles for a range of other situational factors:
Using devices one-handed or with limited hand mobility
Interacting on the go or in constrained environments
Age-related changes impacting touch precision
Simply having lar ger ﬁngers that struggle with compact UIs
Modern graphic design's trend towards miniaturization has just made this
issue worse. It does this by shrinking interactive parts and reducing space to
improve information tightness. But these improvements often sacriﬁce
essential user -friendliness and accessibility .
Accessible Touch Targets, By Design
Optimizing for touch inputs requires purposeful design across several
overlapping dimensions. Here are some key solutions to implement:
Touch Target Sizing
The general guidance is to have a minimum clickable area of at least 44x44
pixels (CSS pixels) for all interactive parts - buttons, links, form controls,
etc. This translates to about 11x11mm for common screen densities.
However , touch targets that are even larger, between 48x48px to 56x56px,
are better if they can be used. These give much more room for users with
larger ﬁngers, mobility problems , or less than perfect tapping situations. Put
into action changeable sizing. This allows targets to smoothly grow on
bigger screens. Also, make certain that the height/width properties stick to
the min-width/min-height limits. This stops the risk of unwanted shrinkage.
Spacing and Padding
Along with the size of tap targets, ensuring enough space between
interactive parts  is essential. Keep at least 8px padding both horizontally
and vertically to distinguish tap areas.
In UIs with a small focus like data grids or tables, it is best to have
individual rows or cells for tap targets. Try not to have links or buttons
close together as they might be tapped by mistake.
Mixing size and spacing creates generous click "zones" around each
interactive feature, reducing chances of unintentional clicks.
Hover/Focus Affordances
Even though making touch targets bigger mostly helps mobile and touch
interactions, remember to also improve the clear focus states for the cursor
and keyboard. Strong visible outlines and states on :hover and :focus
strengthen tap capture for uses that are not touch-based. This visible help
supports users who use a keyboard or mouse, and aids those using assistive
devices, guiding them through cursor moves and extra tools.
Target Shape Optimization
When it comes to size and spacing, the design and placement of touch
points affect usability . Make touch regions simple and rectangular , and line
up the clickable areas on the same line. Stay away from shapes like
triangles, sharp corners, or randomly arranged tar gets.
People's motor skills naturally excel at mastering rectangular shapes
compared to intricate designs. Setting tar gets at expected crossroads further
aids in ﬂuid tar get mastering instead of struggling with motor swaying.
Compensating Alternatives
For high-density UIs where proper sizing/spacing truly cannot be achieved,
explore compensating alternatives:
Conﬁrmation dialogs after detecting improbable tap sequences
Allow keyboard/voice input overrides for overly dense tap zones
Zoom/magniﬁcation features for temporarily amplifying target
areas
Support larger "Accessibility" layout modes optimized for big-
target views
In essence, tailoring the size of touch targets involves considering human
needs in advance - not forcing interface perks past reasonable exactness
limits. This is an accessibili ty aspect that not only enha nces basic
functionality but also invites users who were previously hindered by
barriers to join in.
Self-Evaluation
★      Are your touch tar gets at least 44x44 pixels in size?
★      Have you considered making touch tar gets lar ger, between 48x48px
to 56x56px?
★      Do your interactive elements maintain their size on lar ger screens?
★      Are you using relative units to ensure touch tar gets do not shrink
below minimum sizes?
★      Is there at least 8px of padding around each touch tar get?
★      Are touch tar gets spaced far enough apart to prevent accidental taps?
★      Have you avoided placing links or buttons too close together in data
grids or tables?
★      Do your touch tar gets have strong visible outlines and states?
★      Are touch tar gets rectangular and aligned on the same line?
★      Have you avoided using shapes like triangles or irregular
arrangements for touch tar gets?
★      Are there conﬁrmation dialogs for improbable tap sequences in dense
UIs?
★      Do you support keyboard or voice input overrides for dense tap
zones?
★      Is there a zoom or magniﬁcation feature to amplify touch tar get areas
temporarily?
★      Do you have an “Accessibility” layout mode optimized for lar ger
touch tar gets?
★      Have you tested your touch tar gets with users who have motor
disabilities?
★      Are your touch tar gets easily usable with one-handed or limited hand
mobility?
★      Have you considered age-related changes in touch precision when
designing your UI?
★      Is your design accommodating for users with lar ger ﬁngers?
★      Are touch tar gets usable in constrained environments or on-the-go?
★      Have you tested the usability of touch tar gets across dif ferent devices
and screen sizes?
No Testing with Scr een Readers
Screen readers are crucial tools for people with blindness or serious visual
issues. These apps transform text, buttons, menus, and different UI
components into voiced speec h or braille output, making websites and
sources usable. However , if you have not thoroughly checked your item to
work perfectly with screen reade rs, the entire experience can become tricky
and unclear for these people. The circumstance is similar to putting on a
blindfold and trying to navigate a been obstacle course.
Without proper screen reader testing, critical accessibility pitfalls frequently
crop up like:
Text alternatives (alt text) missing for images/icons
Illogical heading structures breaking navigation
Interactive elements not properly labeled
ARIA  roles and attributes not implemented
Dynamic content updates not announced
For someone using a screen reader, these problems change what should be a
straightforward, interesting experience. Instead, it becomes a jumbled pile
of confu sing text or just pure silence where there should be user-friendly
controls.
The Accessible Solution: Compr ehensive Testing
The only way to truly validate your product's viability for screen reader
users is to regularly test it with the most popular assistive technology tools
during your standard QA  processes. This includes:
Desktop screen readers like JA WS, NVDA, COBRA
Mobile screen readers like VoiceOver , TalkBack
Testing with both speech output and refreshable braille displays
When you use these tools yourself, just like a regular user, you learn a lot.
You can dual-te st them alongside sighted tests. This way you get a ﬁrst-
hand experience. You will know if the main features of the product make
sense, work well, and satisfy the needs of those who depend on alternative
visual aids.
Testing with screen readers is crucial at every development stage. This
includes early stages like rough drafts and code check-overs, as well as ﬁnal
stages like usability checks and trackings once the project is live. If done
this way , we can keep accessibility debt from growing over time.
Some speciﬁc areas to thoroughly test and validate:
Keyboard navigation and focus order
Perceived heading/landmark structures
Presence and quality of text alternatives
Logical reading order of content
Clarity of ARIA  labels and instructions
Dynamic updates appropriately announced
Moreover , team ing up with actual screen reader users via surveys,
interviews, or speciﬁc tests can reveal local problems that your team with
normal vision might miss.
Without steady checks and repairs for screen reader compatibility , you are
probably provid ing a service full of obstacles for people with sight issues.
The absence of familiarity prompts a lack of understanding, hindering
genuine accessibility .
Self-Evaluation
★      Have you tested your product with popular desktop screen readers
like JA WS, NVDA, and COBRA?
★      Have you tested your product with mobile screen readers like
VoiceOver and TalkBack?
★      Have you tested your product with both speech output and refreshable
braille displays?
★      Are all images and icons provided with descriptive alt text?
★      Does your product have a logical heading structure that aids
navigation?
★      Are all interactive elements properly labeled for screen readers?
★      Have you implemented appropriate ARIA  roles and attributes?
★      Are dynamic content updates announced to screen readers?
★      Is keyboard navigation and focus order logical and intuitive?
★      Are perceived heading and landmark structures clear and helpful?
★      Is the reading order of content logical and coherent?
★      Are ARIA  labels and instructions clear and understandable?
★      Do screen reader users receive appropriate feedback for their actions?
★      Have you tested early drafts and code with screen readers?
★      Have you conducted usability tests with screen readers during the
ﬁnal stages?
★      Have you gathered feedback from actual screen reader users?
★      Are there any parts of your product that are inaccessible to screen
reader users?
★      Have you ensured that all text alternatives are of high quality and
relevance?
★      Are dynamic changes in your product (e.g., pop-ups, notiﬁcations)
accessible to screen readers?
★      Do you continuously monitor and update your product for screen
reader compatibility?
Overlooking Audio Accessibility
Creating access ible content usually centers around catering to visual and
motor impairments. Yet, it is crucial that we also consider users who have
diverse hearing abilities or indiv idual auditory processing demands. If your
audience prefers  hearing to reading, things like podcasts, videos and mixed
media can ironically turn into the biggest roadblocks should they not be
inclusively made. Imagine getting your main dish at a restaurant and
abruptly realizing you lack the right tools to eat it.
Some of the common pitfalls that diminish audio accessibility include:
Missing Transcripts: For those with hearing handicaps or deafness,
multimedia material that does not have paired written text makes the
content completely unapproachable and hard to understand.
Fixed Playback Speeds: Peopl e who have cognitive issues or speciﬁc
auditory processing demands might need to change the speed of audio
content. They can make it slower or quicker to match how they understand
things.
Lack of Visual Cues: For conte nt using various media, not having visual
aids like titles or tracked text can make understanding difﬁcult. This stands
true even for users with regular hearing.
The Inclusive Audio Solution
The answ er lies in providing the same sort of assistance you would offer for
visual impairme nts, but now for hearing issues. This involves making sure
that all materials relying on sound provide:
Transcripts & Captions
Text transcripts for every piece of audio and multimedia are vital. This is
because deaf or hard-of-hearing  people can only get all the information this
way. We need to place these transcripts right next to or under the
audio/video player .
More efﬁcient than unchanging scripts are moving subtitles timed to match
the spok en word . This lets view ers keep up and understand what is being
said more smoo thly. Make sure subtitles are easy to see, using suitable font
styles, colors, and sizes, and can be adjusted by viewers if necessary (like
font size, background transparency , etc). Auto-moving or visual signals
such as underlining words can make the subtitles stand out even more.
Playback Contr ols
it is important for users with cognitive challenges, hearing problems, or
preferences for different speeds to be able to change how fast the audio
plays. Make sure there are simple controls, best placed near or within the
audio player itself.
Allow for both small and large changes in speed (like 0.75x, 1.25x, 1.5x)
and the option to set unique playback speeds based on personal user
requirements. do not forget to tweak pitch compensation to prevent audio
distortion at quicker/slower speeds.
Visual Reinfor cement
Even for users with typical hearing abilities, providing visual reinforcement
for audio content creates a more robust, multi-modal learning experience.
This could include:
Rendering transcripts/captions during playback with text
highlighting to follow along
Using animations, graphics, or visualizations that illustrate key
audio concepts
Surfacing visua l outlines, diagrams, or slides that relate to the
audio narration
For educational or highly contextual audio, smart visual cues and synced
visuals prevent information from getting missed or misunderstood when
experienced through just one modality .
Alternate Input Methods
do not forget that controlling audio playback itself needs to be an accessible
interaction. Support standard keyboard controls, but also accommodate
alternate input methods like:
Voice controls for playback navigation
Gesture tracking for play/pause/seek
Head/eye tracking and gaze interactions
Switch device compatibility
The aim is to make sure that no listener misses out. We want everyone to
thoroughly enjoy , manage, strengthen and truly experience audio resources.
We cater to their individual needs and capabilities. Smaller chan ges can turn
a rousing wall into a pleasing, empowering adventure for everyone.
Self-Evaluation
★      Does all your audio and multimedia content have accompanying text
transcripts?
★      Are the transcripts easily accessible and located near the audio or
video player?
★      Do you provide synchronized captions or subtitles for all video
content?
★      Are the captions clear , readable, and adjustable in terms of font style,
size, and color?
★      Can users change the playback speed of audio content?
★      Are the playback speed controls intuitive and easy to ﬁnd?
★      Do the playback speed options include both small and lar ge
increments?
★      Have you adjusted pitch compensation to prevent distortion at
different playback speeds?
★      Do you provide visual reinforcement for audio content, such as text
highlighting during playback?
★      Are there animations, graphics, or visualizations that illustrate key
audio concepts?
★      Do you surface visual outlines, diagrams, or slides that relate to the
audio narration?
★      Do you support standard keyboard controls for audio playback?
★      Are there voice controls available for playback navigation?
★      Have you implemented gesture tracking for play/pause/seek
functions?
★      Is your content compatible with head/eye tracking and gaze
interactions?
★      Can users control playback using switch devices?
★      Have you tested your multimedia content with users who have
hearing impairments?
★      Do you provide clear instructions on how to access and adjust
captions or transcripts?
★      Are visual cues and synced visuals included to prevent
misunderstandings in educational content?
★      Have you gathered feedback from users with dif ferent auditory
processing needs?
Inadequate Training for  Development Teams
Even when intentions are good, without proper training on web accessibility
for your product development groups, you are inadvertently building in
access hurdles right from the start.
Imagine this - would you employ a translator without conﬁrming their
proﬁciency in both languages? Deﬁnitely not, as their insufﬁcient
knowledge would hamper efﬁcient communication. Similarly , tasking
engineers and designers with creating accessible product experiences
without the right basic training is like paving their way towards failure. This
leads to an ongoing blind spot. Teams stay unaware of the exact rules and
methods they must use to produce SaaS products for everyone.
This lack of accessibility ﬂuency leads to patterns like:
Overlooking semantic HTML  structures and ARIA  roles
Not providing text alternatives for non-text content
Keyboard trap controls and broken navigation paths
Improper heading levels and inefﬁcient focus management
Color contrast, text sizing and zoom pitfalls
What might look like harmless code choices to those without training can
actually greatly reduce usability  for users who depend on support tools like
screen readers.
The Solution: Proactive Accessibility Training
Dedicated Accessibility Training Pr ogram
Create an organized training program that each newcomer completes once
they become part of the team. This includes basic concepts in accessibility ,
standard codes such as WCAG/Section 508, identifying various disabilities,
an introduction to helping technologies, tips for effective development,
testing methods, and so on.
This training program can involve a mix of methods:
In-person workshops with hands-on exercises
Online courses with follow-up assessments
Accessibility experience simulations (using screen reader s,
navigating with just a keyboard etc.)
Accessibility expert guest speakers and panels
Include this training in your company's onboarding procedures. At the same
time, of fer repeated training and ongoing learning opportunities.
Accessibility Champions/Guilds
Create in-house "accessibility groups" or societies. Here, staff from various
ﬁelds can join frequent gatherings. They can gain knowledge from each
other's insights, talk about examples, exchange recent methods and
materials, and generally cultivate an atmosphere center ed around
accessibility .
Enable these groups to steer wide-ranging training efforts, appraise
techniques/tools for easy access alignment, and serve as expert advisors that
teams can consult. Active guild members can be motivated and
acknowledged through measures like recognition schemes, career growth
options, or fun, competitive leaderboards.
Accessibility Library & Resour ces
Keep a core center of lasting, easy-to-reach materials that groups can use on
their own. This involves guide s, coding examples, checking plans, self-
guided lessons, how-to videos for accessibility tools, talking presentations,
online broadcasts, and more.
Encourage all staff members to continuously add to this "active" library
with new knowledge or insights. Highlight positive accessibility methods
through highlighted articles or "expert tips."
Accessible Pr oject Kickoffs
Implement a checklist that every new project kickof f must go through to
align all workstreams with accessibility requirements from the start. This
covers aspects like:
Reviewing project-speciﬁc user personas and disabili ty
considerations
Outlining WCAG/508 criteria that will apply
Establishing test plan touchpoints and acceptance criteria
Identifying potential accessibility risk areas to prioritize
This prevents accessibility from being an afterthought by making it a
foundational part of how projects get framed and scoped from day one.
In simple terms, the aim is to integrate thorough understanding of
accessibility and corresponding attitudes into the heart of your business'
culture and operations. It should never be a singular , brief training that is
left behind immediately .
Working accessi bility into your day-to-day activities is a process. It happens
through ongoin g training, in-house groups, carefully picked support
materials, and project stages. This process is what turns access ibility from
just a task on your list to an always-there, ahead-of-time part of what you
create.
Self-Evaluation
★      Does your development team receive regular training on web
accessibility principles?
★      Are new team members required to complete accessibility training as
part of their onboarding process?
★      Do you conduct in-person workshops or online courses focused on
accessibility for your development teams?
★      Have your developers been trained on coding standards such as
WCAG and Section 508?
★      Are there accessibility experience simulations provided to help
developers understand user perspectives?
★      Do you invite accessibility experts to speak or conduct sessions with
your development teams?
★      Have you established accessibility champions or guilds within your
organization?
★      Do these accessibility groups facilitate knowledge sharing and best
practices across dif ferent teams?
★      Are there resources available, such as guides, coding examples, and
how-to videos, speciﬁcally for accessibility?
★      Does your or ganization encourage continuous learning and updating
of accessibility knowledge?
★      Is there a centralized library or repository for accessibility resources
that team members can access easily?
★      Do you integrate accessibility considerations from the beginning of
each project through a structured checklist?
★      Are project kickof fs accompanied by discussions on user personas
and disability considerations?
★      Do you outline speciﬁc WCAG or Section 508 criteria applicable to
each project?
★      Have you established test plans and acceptance criteria that include
accessibility checkpoints?
★      Do you identify and prioritize potential accessibility risks at the outset
of projects?
★      Is accessibility treated as an integral part of your company's culture
and operations?
★      How do you ensure that accessibility practices are not treated as one-
time tasks but as ongoing commitments?
★      Are there measures in place to recognize and reward individuals who
champion accessibility within your teams?
★      How does your or ganization ensure that accessibility remains a
priority across all development stages and activities?
Chapter  9: Data Security and Privacy
If you spend mor e on coffee than on IT  security , you will be hacked. What is
more, you deserve to be hacked. - Richar d Clarke
When you are building a SaaS product these days, you cannot afford to take
data security and user privacy lightly . I am talking lock-it-up, circle-the-
wagons, and next-level protection.
Why? Because our users are entrusting us with their most sensit ive data and
personal inform ation. Financial  details, health records, you name it. One
little security slip-up, and we are looking at a catastrophic data breach that
erodes consumer trust faster than you can say "Hello, lawsuit!"
Think about it - if your SaaS product got hacked, exposing all your users'
private info to cyber criminals, how would you feel? ...Exactly . you would
be utterl y morti ﬁed and want to crawl into a hole. Your reputation would
take a huge hit too.
But that is not even the worst part! In many countries and regio ns, there are
strict data privacy laws like GDPR and CCPA. Violate those regulations,
and you could be smacked with massive ﬁnes and penalties that will make
your head spin. We are talking millions of dollars AND getting dragged
through the mud publicly . Talk about a PR nightmare!
That is why designing your product with airtight data security and privacy
practices baked into its core from the start is an absolute must. This is just
the start! Throughout this chapter , I will dive deeper into speciﬁc data
security and privacy pitfalls to watch out for when designing SaaS products.
Because at the end of the day, a careless approach to data security and
privacy can really ruin the user's experience with your SaaS product. But do
not worr y, by the time you ﬁnish  this chapter , you will know exactly how to
steer clear of those pitfalls.
Weak Password Policies
We have  all been there - you are signing up for a new account, and the
password requirements are so lax you could almost get away with
"password123" and be done with it. I mean, come on, from the product
owner's side, this is nothing but leaving your front door wide open to
hackers by letting your users create insanely weak passwords such as this.
This is huge when their account gets compromised: their perso nal data can
be revealed or even stolen. If ﬁnancial accounts are linked, this may be a
catastrophe waiting to happen. On top of that you have a hassle  of a whole
kind to get the account secured again. it is a huge violation of privacy and a
total breach of trust.
Striking the Right Balance
On the other hand, we have to meet some ridiculous password
requirements. I mean, those asking for 3 different special characters, 2
Egyptian hieroglyphs, and the blood of a unicorn. Okay , maybe not that
extreme, but you get the point!
Top 25 most common passwor ds by year accor ding to SplashData
While strong password policies are extremely important for security , if you
go too far , you will provide a really terrible user experience. People will just
get frustrated and likely ﬁnd a way to circumvent the rules anyway .
The sweet spot? Implement robust but reasonable password requirements.
For example, a great policy would be:
●         Minimum 10 characters
●         At least 1 uppercase, 1 lowercase, 1 number
●         Allow common special characters (! @ # ? ])
●         Restrict against common dictionary words/phrases
This hits the security sweet spot without being too complex and turning
users away .
But perhaps even better? Utilize passwordless authentication!
RockY ou Data Br each (2009)
RockY ou was a company that developed widgets and applications for social
networks like MySpace and Facebook. In December 2009, they suffered a
massive data breach that exposed 32 million user accounts, making it one of
the lar gest data breaches at the time.
RockY ou allowed users to create passwords with no complexity
requirements whatsoever . Singl e-character passwords were accepted, and
there was no minimum length requirement. Shockingly , RockYou stored all
user passwords in plaintext within their database, rather than using any
form of encrypt ion or hashing. The breach was made possible  by a basic
SQL injection ﬂaw in their datab ase, which allowed hackers to easily access
and download the entire user database.
The company's overall approach  to security was severely lacking, with no
apparent consideration given to protecting user data.
The breach exposed username s, email addresses, and passwords for 32
million account s in their raw, unencrypted form. Given that many users
reuse passwords across multiple sites, this breach potentially compromised
user accounts on other platfor ms as well. The breached password list
became a valuable resource for hackers, providing insights into common
password choices and informing future attacks.
Later RockYou faced several class-action lawsuits as a result of the breach.
The company's reputation was severely damaged, contributing to its
eventual decline and acquisition . The breach served as a wake-up call for
many in the tech industry about the importance of proper password policies
and storage techniques.
Next-Level Alternatives
Modern biometric authentication methods like ﬁngerprint or facial scanning
or as simple as authenticating through Google, Facebook or Apple account
as SSO are raising the security game. And for extra protection, you can
layer multi-factor authentication (MFA) on top, requiring an extra code sent
to a user's device.
The takeaway? Never go for dirt-simple passwords that are going to leave
your users wide open. Avoid those password requirements that are just
absurd, driving people nuts in the process. Find the happy balance that
keeps data secure without too much friction.
Even better , explore passwordless authentication for friction less, hyper -
secure user experiences. Options like biometrics and SSO solutions with
MFA bring Fort Knox-like protection without the headaches of passwords.
That is a win-win for all parties involved!
Self-Evaluation
★      Do my password policies ensure a minimum level of security for all
user accounts?
★      Are my password requirements clearly communicated to users during
account creation?
★      Do I enforce a minimum length for passwords (e.g., at least 10
characters)?
★      Do I require a mix of uppercase and lowercase letters in passwords?
★      Are users required to include numbers in their passwords?
★      Do my password policies allow for the use of special characters?
★      Are common dictionary words or phrases restricted in password
creation?
★      Do I prevent users from setting easily guessable passwords like
"password123"?
★      Are users prompted to change their passwords regularly?
★      Do I have mechanisms in place to detect and prevent repeated
password attempts (brute force attacks)?
★      Are user passwords stored securely using encryption or hashing
techniques?
★      Do I of fer multi-factor authentication (MF A) as an additional layer of
security?
★      Are users educated about the importance of creating strong, unique
passwords?
★      Do I monitor and respond to password-related security breaches
promptly?
★      Are password reset processes secure and user -friendly?
★      Do I have a process for users to report suspicious activity related to
their accounts?
★      Are my password policies reviewed and updated regularly to adapt to
evolving security threats?
★      Do I provide alternative authentication methods such as biometric or
SSO options?
★      Are user accounts protected from unauthorized access through robust
security measures?
★      Do I balance security requirements with user experience to avoid
frustrating users?
★      Are password creation guidelines enforced uniformly across all
platforms (web, mobile, etc.)?
★      Do I implement account lockout mechanisms after a certain number
of failed login attempts?
★      Are users notiﬁed of successful and unsuccessful login attempts on
their accounts?
★      Do I conduct regular security audits to ensure compliance with
password policies?
★      Am I using industry best practices for password storage and
management?
★      Do I have a clear policy for handling compromised accounts?
★      Are users given the option to use password managers to store their
credentials securely?
★      Do I test my password policies against real-world hacking scenarios?
★      Am I prepared to handle lar ge-scale data breaches ef fectively?
Lack of Data Encryption
Imagine moving  into a new place and the movers just leave all your stuff
out in the front yard, for anyone to go through - clothing, furniture, personal
documents, you name it. No locks; no protection—100 percent naked and
out in the open. you would be horriﬁed, right?
Well, if you are a product owner and you do not do much to protect the
sensitive data of your users both while that information is at rest and in
transit, then that is very much what you are doing with their data - it is like
leaving private information exposed for any snooper to access.
Why Is It a Serious Pr oblem?
If user data is not encrypted at any stage, then anyone who is able to
intercept the data can read it like an open book. Think hackers, cyber
criminals - any dishonest employee or vendor with system access.
Exposing personal informa tion, ﬁnancial details, and private
communications, the risks of major data breaches skyrocket.  Your users
could have their identities stolen, bank accounts compromised - name it.
Not quite the way to build trust and loyalty!
Uber  Data Br each (2016)
In 2016, Uber suffered a signiﬁcant data breach that exposed the personal
information of 57 million users and drivers. This breach is a clear example
of the consequences of inadequate data encryption.
Uber stored access keys to their AWS servers in plain text on a private
GitHub repository . The GitHub repository was not properly secured,
allowing unauth orized access. The AWS account did not have multi-factor
authentication enabled, making it easier for attackers to gain access once
they had the keys. Uber did not disclose the breach for over a year,
attempting to cover it up by paying the hackers $100,000 to delete the data.
The personal information of 57 million Uber users and drivers was
compromised, including names, email addresses, and phone numbers. For
drivers, license numbers were also exposed. Uber faced signiﬁcant ﬁnes
from multiple regulators. For example, they paid $148 million in a
settlement with US state attorneys general. Reputational damage: The
breach and subsequent cover -up severely damaged Uber's reputation,
eroding trust among users, drivers, and regulators. The handling of the
breach led to the ﬁring of two top executives, including the Chief Security
Ofﬁcer .
How to Over come Data Encryption Pr oblems?
Encrypting data is like sending a secret message locked tight in an
unbreakable safe. Only the intended recipient with the proper decryption
key can open it up. To everyone else, it is gibberish.
The ﬁx is implementing strong, industry-trusted encryption protocols for
data both "at rest" (stored) and "in transit" (being sent from A to B). A few
battle-tested methods include:
AES Encryption:  The Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) is the gold
standard, using rock-solid 128-bit, 192-bit or 256-bit encryption keys to
scramble data. Implement this for encrypting data at rest.
TLS/SSL:  Transport Layer Security (TLS) or its predecessor Secure
Sockets Layer (SSL) should be used to encrypt all data being transmitted
over networks. This secures data in transit.
Hashing & Salting:  For storing passwords, use secure hashing algorithms
like SHA-256 along with salting to make them incredibly difﬁcult to
decrypt.
The key is using a layered approach, encrypting data at every state using
multiple proven  methods. Think of it as putting your users' personal info in
a locked vault, then putting that vault in a safe, then buryin g that safe
under ground! No sneaky hands getting in there.
Now , I will be the ﬁrst to admit that proper data encryption for a SaaS
product is just scratching the surface of comprehensive security . Truthfully ,
it is a vast and complex domain that even seasoned developers can struggle
with.
If you really want to lock down your data with industry-best practices, your
best bet is to hire an experienced data security consultant or ﬁrm. The
investment will be well worth avoiding a catastrophic breach down the
road.
With guidance from the pros and the right encryption fundamentals in
place, you will be giving your SaaS users the privacy and security they
absolutely deserve. Their sensitive details will be as sealed up as Fort Knox,
not exposed like last week's laundry!
Self-Evaluation
★      Do I encrypt all sensitive user data both at rest and in transit?
★      Am I using industry-standard encryption protocols such as AES for
data at rest?
★      Do I use TLS/SSL  to encrypt data transmitted over networks?
★      Have I implemented secure hashing algorithms like SHA-256 along
with salting for storing passwords?
★      Do I regularly audit my encryption practices to ensure they meet
current security standards?
★      Are encryption keys stored and managed securely?
★      Have I implemented multi-factor authentication (MF A) for accessing
sensitive systems and data?
★      Do I encrypt backups of user data?
★      Have I educated my team on the importance of data encryption and
how to implement it correctly?
★      Do I perform regular security assessments and penetration testing to
identify vulnerabilities?
★      Is encryption automatically applied to all user data, or do I rely on
manual processes?
★      Do I use end-to-end encryption for communication channels within
my application?
★      Am I aware of and compliant with relevant data protection
regulations such as GDPR or CCP A?
★      Have I documented my encryption policies and procedures?
★      Do I have a response plan in place for potential data breaches?
★      Am I monitoring access to encrypted data to detect and prevent
unauthorized access?
★      Do I update my encryption methods as new technologies and
standards emer ge?
★      Have I considered the performance impact of encryption on my
system and optimized it accordingly?
★      Do I use hardware security modules (HSM) to manage encryption
keys?
★      Do I provide users with clear information about how their data is
protected?
★      Have I engaged with a data security consultant to review and improve
my encryption practices?
★      Do I perform regular reviews of third-party services to ensure they
meet my encryption standards?
★      Am I prepared to handle encryption key management lifecycle,
including rotation and revocation?
★      Do I implement encryption for all types of data, including structured
and unstructured data?
★      Have I tested my encryption implementation to ensure data integrity
and security?
★      Do I prioritize user data security as a core aspect of my product
development process?
Insufﬁcient Access Contr ols
Imagine you are the owner of a huge mansion with dozens of rooms, each
of them containing valuables and private documents. For some insane
reason, you give every visitor a master key that would access every single
room. The butler , the gardener , the pool cleaner - they all get to roam
around everywhere in the place.
You would not want to do that, would you? Letting absolute strangers into
all your private areas and before all your possessions is silly! you would be
courting disaster - things could disappear , become damaged, or end up in
the wrong hands. In effect, that is what you do with the SaaS product if you
do not put proper controls over user data and system functions in place. It is
a scenar io where you are unnecessarily exposing yourselves to a lot of
nightmares if each user accoun t is set to supreme admin-leve l privileges
regardless of the role.
Why is it a Hazard?
When any user can access and manipulate any data or system functionality
they want, it exponentially increases the risks of:
●         Data leaks and breaches from accidental overwrites or deletions
●         Malicious data tampering by bad actors
●         Unauthorized visibility into private user info
●         Security lapses from users abusing elevated privileges
Even fat-ﬁngering an incorrect data change could have been catastrophic in
nature if users had access far beyond their needs.
Imperva Cloud WAF Security Incident (2019)
The Imperva Cloud WAF Security Incident of 2019 serves as a stark
reminder of the critical importance of access controls in web product design
and manageme nt. Imperva, a cybersecurity company providing Web
Application Firewall services, found itself in the uncomfortable  position of
being the victim of a signiﬁcant security breach due to insufﬁcient access
controls.
At the heart of the incident was an internal compute instance in AWS that
Imperva had created for testing and development purposes. This instance,
however , was set up with excessively permissive privileges, violating the
principle of least privilege. More alarmingly , it had unrestricted access to
production databases containing sensitive customer data. The situation was
further exacerbated by poor credential management, as an administrative
API key for this powerful test instance was left exposed. The lack of proper
segregation between development/test and production environments
completed the perfect storm of security oversights.
The consequences of these lapses were severe. An attacker managed to
access a database snapshot containing a wealth of sensitive information,
including email addresses, hashe d and salted passwords, API keys, and SSL
certiﬁcates for a subset of Imperva's customers. This breach forced
Imperva's Cloud WAF customers to undertake the cumbersome  process of
changing their user account passwords and implementing new SSL
certiﬁcates.
For Imperva, a company whose business is built on trust in its cybersecurity
expertise, the reputational damage was signiﬁcant. The incident likely
eroded custome r conﬁdence and led to increased scrutiny from customers,
partners, and the broader cybersecurity community . The ﬁnancial impact
was also substantial,  with costs incurred from incident response, customer
support, and potential loss of business.
The Solutions to Batten Down the Hatches
The classic ﬁx is the implem entation of role-based access controls to
constrain what they can view and manipulate in line with their role and
requirements.
RBAC is a famous access control methodology , providing access based on
predeﬁned roles within an organization. Think of this like assigning
different colored hats to employees based on their job duties.
Suppose you are a software company with developers, testers, and
managers; all these roles would have been created in RBAC. Next, you give
speciﬁc access rights to those roles: the developers will be able to write and
modify code; testers may have  all testing environments at their disposal;
and the manager will be able to review reports on the progress of the
project.
Now , what happ ens when somebody joins is that you simply assign them a
proper role and they inherit all the permissions tied together in that role. No
manic setting of individual permissions for every single user. This RBAC
scheme is really helpful for organizations that appear to have coherent,
fairly stable job roles and responsibilities. it is easy to deal with and
understand its meaning, so rightly it ﬁnds quite signiﬁcant applications in
business circles.
There is also Attribute Based Access Control (ABAC), which involves
access control by user attribute—job title, location, device—the list
continues. In sum, it is way more ﬂexible than those rigid roles.
ABAC  is far more dynamic and ﬂexible. While in ABAC, the access is
granted based on attributes associated with users, resources, and
environments; in RBAC, it is based on predeﬁned roles.
Consider this in regard to a boun cer at some swanky club: they really do not
care what your job title might be, but they do care about your age, whether
you are a VIP, and your dress code. You deﬁne policies using such attributes
in ABA C. For example, "Grant access to the ﬁnancial reports if the
department is Finance, their job level is Manager or above, and they are
accessing from a company IP  address."
This allows rather granular , context-aware access control. Permissions can
be changed automatically whenever the situation changes, without requiring
manual intervention. To that regard, ABAC is particularly suited for large,
complex organiz ations with a varied user base and access requir ements that
could change with time. Though it does require attention to planning and
management of attribute deﬁnitions and their policies.
Now , this is an older access control method, and that is Access Control
Lists, or ACL.
ACL  is a set of simple  tables specifying which users or groups may, or may
not, do which actions on which objects. Imagine a shared drive with folders
for every department. The access control list for the "HR" folder might
include:
●         Alice, HR manager: Read, Write, Delete
●         Bob, HR assistant: Read, Write
●         Carol, marketing manager: Read
●         Everybody else: No access
ACLs are simple and easy to understand. They are still very much in use
nowadays, in ﬁle systems and other types of network devices.
This makes ACL  rather awkwar d to administer on a big scale. If a new user
is to be added, someone has to update all relevant ACLs by hand. As the
number of users and resources grows, ACLs become more complex and
error -prone.
There is one more access control framework which is not so recommended
but used in some peculiar cases, it is Discretionary Access Control (DAC).
DAC is almos t like allowing the kids to make their own rules. DAC lets
resource owners manage access to their resources as per their whim and
fancy . In the DAC system, if Alice creates a document, she can decide who
may read it, who can edit it, or who can delete it. She can share it with Bob,
offfer Carol view-only access, and completely hide it from Dave.
DAC is ﬂexible and easy to use, imitating real-life concepts of ownership
and sharing. It is successfully implemented in collaborative environments -
such as Google Docs, by users who would like to share resource s. However ,
this DAC might  be quite risky , since it solely depends on many resource
owners' judgme nt. Less experienced users may overshare sensitive data or
grant access to the wrong people.
In summary , each access control method has its own strengths and
weaknesses:
●         RBAC is best for organizations with well-deﬁned, stable roles and
responsibilities. it is easy to manage but can be rigid.
●         ABAC offers dynamic, ﬁne-grained access control based on
attributes. it is ﬂexible but requires careful policy management.
●         ACLs are simple and widely used but can become unwieldy at scale.
●         MAC provides strict, centralize d control but can be inﬂexible and
create bottlenecks.
●         DAC is ﬂexible and intuitive but relies on the judgment of individual
users.
When choosing an access control system for your SaaS product, consider
factors like:
●         The size and complexity of your or ganization
●         The nature of your user base and their access needs
●         The sensitivity of your data and resources
●         The regulatory requirements in your industry
●         The scalability and maintainability of the system
In many cases, a combination of both might be correct. You could, for
example, extend RBAC with ABAC, which would enable you to perform
ﬁner granularity and more dynamic access control within the roles assigned
by the former .
The key is to strike a balance between security and usability .
You want users to have all possi ble access to the resources that will let them
do their jobs, but nothing more, and for accidental or malicious data
breaches to be minimized.
This really is access control - getting it right spells security and success for
your SaaS product. You do not want to end up like the mansion owner who
gave out master keys to everyone! Take time to understand the options that
exist out there; select methods that will suit your needs and implement them
with good sense.
Your users will feel satisﬁed that their data is absolutely safe and secure
behind highly protected doors.
Self-Evaluation

current access needs?

access security?

personnel only?

user access?

access control policies?
★      Do I conduct regular access control training for employees?

interest?

control vulnerabilities?

incidents?

strategy?

policies?

functioning correctly?

current security standards?

regulatory requirements?
★      Do I have a process for verifying user identities before granting
access?
★      Are privileged accounts subject to stricter access control measures?
★      Do I use temporary access controls for contractors and temporary
employees?

applications?
★      Do I have a process for detecting and responding to unauthorized
access attempts?

my or ganization grows?

assessments?

practices?
