relevant stakeholders?
★      Do I use access control to protect both digital and physical assets?
★      Have I assessed the potential impact of access control failures on my
organization?
★      Do I involve key stakeholders in the development and implementation
of access control policies?
★      Are access control measures regularly updated to address emer ging
threats?
★      Do I use role-based access control (RBAC) in combination with other
access control methods for enhanced security?
Poor  Session Management
Imagine this: You interact with your bank's website using a library's
computer . You are done, and in a rush, you forget to log out. There you go,
leaving, and the next guy sitting now has access to all your sensitive
ﬁnancial information. Yikes! That is the kind of nightmare that can happen
with a SaaS product if it has bad session management. This will act like
leaving the front door wide open for anybody to just waltz right in if user
sessions are allowed to remain active indeﬁnitely .
Why it is a Hacker's Dr eam Come True
Inadequate session management makes it easier to have perfect visibility
into unauthorized access. Here are a few ways this can happen:
The Forgetful User :  A user logs in with a shared device, gets distracted,
and forgets to log out. The next user on the same device now gets an open
sesame for snooping around in the previous user's account.
The Sneaky Session Hijacker : A hacker who is able to steal a user's
session ID by any number of ways, like cross-site scripting or packet
snifﬁng, could use this ID and pose as the user to hijack their session
without needing to know the login details.
The Idle Accou nt Inﬁltrator : A user leaves his or her device for some
considerable duration without logging out. Some curious colleague or
family member who comes across such an open session will get to have a
look at personal data.
In all of these cases, poor session management can be the cause of eliciting
sensitive data into the wrong hands. it is a potential privacy nightmare if
any of these occur .
LinkedIn AutoFill Featur e Vulnerability of 2018
LinkedIn, the professional networking giant, offered a feature called
AutoFill that allowed third-party  websites to let users quickly populate form
ﬁelds with information from their LinkedIn proﬁles. This feature was
designed to enhance user experience by streamlining form completion
across the web. However , a signiﬁcant vulnerability in this feature's
implementation led to a severe security risk.
The core of the problem lay in how LinkedIn managed user sessions and
permissions for this feature. When a user logged into Linked In, the site
would set a session cookie. This cookie was then used to authenticate the
user for the AutoFill feature on third-party websites. Howeve r, LinkedIn
failed to implement proper controls to ensure that only authoriz ed websites
could access this feature and that users had explicitly consen ted to share
their data.
The vulnerability was discovered by security researcher Jack Cable, who
found that any website could abuse this feature to silently harvest user data
without their knowledge or consent. An attacker could create a malicious
website with hidden form ﬁelds and use LinkedIn's AutoFill feature to
populate these ﬁelds with the user's personal information, including name,
email address, location, and even phone number .
What made this vulnerability particularly concerning was its ease of
exploitation and the potential for large-scale data harvesting. An attacker
could theoretically harvest data from thousands of LinkedIn users who
visited a compromised website, all without their knowledge or consent.
The consequences of this vulnerability were far-reaching. It exposed
LinkedIn users to potential privacy breaches and identity theft. For
LinkedIn, it represented a signiﬁcant breach of user trust and raised
questions about the company's commitment to data privacy and security .
The incident also highlighted the risks associated with features designed to
enhance user experience without adequately considering security
implications. In response to the discovery , LinkedIn quickly took action.
They restricted the AutoFill functionality to only work with whitelisted
websites that had been approved by LinkedIn. They also implemented
additional user consent mechan isms to ensure users were aware of when
and how their data was being shared.
The "No Mor e Snoozing" Solution
Now , to keep these session management snafus at bay, proper controls will
have to be in place. The important ones among these include:
Session Timeouts : These sessions  will automatically expire after a certain
period of inactivity . Much like when you stop using your phone; it gets
locked after some minutes. If any user is idle for too long, his session will
be terminated, which means he will have to log in again.
Automatic Logouts : No user sessio ns should be left open forever , even if
the user is extraordinarily active. It involves the introduction of maximum
session duration after which they log out automatically . They can log back
in whenever for more time if they wish.
Trusted Devices : Think  about adding a feature for 'trusted' devices.
Provided they log in from a device they have used before and trusted, you
could extend their session duration or even keep them logged in for a
number of days. Like giving them almost a VIP pass for their favorite
devices!
Reasonable Session Durations : This should give you the middle ground
between security and convenien ce in the right session time. So, you may
want to use 30-minute timeouts for a banking app but less sensitive project
management tools would be comfortable with a 2-hour timeout.
"Remember  Me" Functionality : The "Rememb er Me" function is when a
user has logged in and the session information is stored in a cookie. This
allows them to close their browser or navigate away from the site without
being required to log in each and every time they come back. it is kinda like
a "Fast Pass" that allows them to cruise on by the login line!
The User  Experience Balancing Act
it is important to keep in mind  that security should not be taken to the
detriment of the lousy user experience. However , on apps storing highly
sensitive data, like ﬁnancial or medical information, users usually easily put
up with additional security layers.
Think about this: if you are using a banking application, would not you feel
much better about using such an app if it had rigid session controls and
logged you out automatically after some period of no activit y? In these
cases, people would understan d that this little hassle is worth peace of
mind.
For less sensitive applications,  however , it is still important to ﬁnd that
sweet spot between security and convenience. Trusted devices, reasonable
session durations, and "Remember Me" functionality are some of the
techniques to achieve that.
Poor session management is like leaving your house keys under the
welcome mat, an invitation to bad guys. But by putting in smart session
controls that are attuned to the sensitivity of your app's data, along with
convenient "Remember Me" features, you can block unauthorized access
without losing your mind - or your users.
Keep those sessions under control, but remember about unique security
requirements or user expectations of your app.
Self-Evaluation
★      Do I implement session timeouts to automatically log users out after a
period of inactivity?
★      Is there an automatic logout feature after a maximum session
duration?
★      Do I use "Remember Me" functionality for trusted devices while
maintaining security?
★      Are session durations set reasonably based on the sensitivity of the
application?
★      Do I educate users about the importance of logging out from shared
or public devices?
★      Do I monitor and audit session activity for any suspicious behavior?
★      Are sessions terminated immediately after a user logs out or changes
their password?
★      Do I implement secure methods for session ID generation and
management?
★      Are session IDs transmitted over secure channels (e.g., HTTPS)?
★      Do I use unique session IDs for each login session to prevent session
ﬁxation attacks?
★      Are session IDs stored securely (e.g., in secure, HTTP-only cookies)?
★      Do I implement measures to prevent session hijacking, such as
regenerating session IDs upon login?
★      Are idle sessions logged out automatically to prevent unauthorized
access?
★      Do I notify users about unusual session activities or logins from new
devices?
★      Are session management practices compliant with relevant security
standards and regulations?
★      Do I provide users with the ability to view and manage their active
sessions?
★      Are there measures in place to detect and respond to session-related
attacks (e.g., brute force, session replay)?
★      Do I implement proper access controls to restrict session-related
functionalities to authorized users?
★      Are session management policies reviewed and updated regularly to
address emer ging threats?
★      Do I balance security and user experience when designing session
management features?
★      Do I use session management best practices for both web and mobile
applications?
★      Are session tokens invalidated immediately upon logout to prevent
reuse?
★      Are session management practices tested regularly as part of security
audits and assessments?
★      Do I have a process in place for handling session-related incidents
and breaches?
★      Are users prompted to re-authenticate for sensitive actions within the
application?
★      Do I implement measures to prevent Cross-Site Request For gery
(CSRF) attacks?
Inadequate User  Education
Imagine this: you are about to set off on a wild hiking adventure deep in the
heart of the wilderness with a backpack full of granola bars, your trusty
walking stick by your side, and a map that looks like it was drawn by a
toddler . As you take to the trail, you suddenly realize there is just one little
problem: you have no idea how to actually navigate using this map! you
turned it upside down, squinted at it sideways, folded it up, still just a
confusing mess of squiggly lines.
Well, that is exactly what your users are doing when you are not taking the
time to educate  them in best practices around data security: They move
through a forest full of phishing traps, password pitfalls, and Wi-Fi wolves -
without a hint on how to take care of themselves!
Why it is a Disaster  Waiting to Happen
When you are leaving your users uneducated on data security , these kinds
of hilarious scenarios (by hilarious I mean ﬂat-out terrifying) can happen:
The Phishing Victim : Your user gets an email suppos edly from your entity;
in reality, however , the mail is from a rather sneaky hacker after stealing his
or her login information. Your poor user, if not aware of the red ﬂags to
look out for , falls for the trap and hands over the keys to his account!
The Password Recycler : This guy thinks he is slick, running around using
the same password for each and every account he's got. Yep, "IluvCats123"
might be easy to remember , but when one of those other accounts gets
hacked, suddenly the hackers have a master key to all their accounts!
The Overshar er: Maybe  this well-meaning, clueless user intended to share
something super -conﬁdential with their team but accidentally did so with
the entire internet instead. Oops, hope that data leak scandal looks good on
the front page of the news!
The Update Neglecter : This user treats software updates like optional
suggestions, blissfully ignoring all those pesky notiﬁcations. Meanwhile,
their outdated version of your app is like a neon sign inviting hackers to
come on in and make themselves at home.
The Insecure Wi-Fi Surfer : This user logs into your app from his local
coffee shop using their free Wi-Fi, not realizing that said netw ork is about
as secur e as a screen door on a submarine. The hacker sipping the latte next
to him will be more than happy to intercept his login credentials!
See what I mean? When your users are left in the dark about data security , it
is like sending them into a mineﬁeld without a map or a metal detector .
Boom, boom, boom!
The "Knowledge Is Power" Solution
But fear not, you will be able to turn your users from clueless victims into
savvy security superheroes. Here is how:
Clear  Security  Guidelines : Provide users with a survival guide with
clearly understa ndable tips on how to create strong passwords, recognize
phishing attemp ts, and deal with sensitive data like an expert. Then, make it
fun and engaging, and sprinkle some internet memes in there for good
measure!
Regular  Training : Provide regular security training events, which would
be less "snooze fest" and more  "action-packed thriller ." Think interactive
challenges or gamiﬁed tutorials - or maybe a few explosions. (just kidding,
please do not blow anything up).
Contextual Tips: Sprinkle helpful security tips throughout your app like a
trail of breadcrumbs leading to safety . When a user creates a weak
password, hit them with a witty reminder . When they are about to share a
ﬁle, make sure they double-check that recipient list!
Encouraging Reporting : Make your users feel like the Sherlock Holmes of
cybersecurity . Get them to report everything ﬁshy that crosses their screens.
Provide a hotline, give sweet rewards, and see how your users can become
your very watchdogs of security .
Bug Bounty Bonanzas : Take a page from the high-tech playbook and
implement a bug bounty progra m - essentially , you will be inviting a small
army of security  researchers to poke and probe your system until they turn
up those really pesky vulnerabilities. Sort of like hide-and-seek, where the
winners get cash prizes instead of bragging rights.
Instead of teaching security by lecturing at your users, turn security
education into an epic adventur e. You will turn your users from a liability
into your ﬁrst line of defense against the dark side of technology .
Self-Evaluation
★      Do I provide clear and understandable security guidelines for users?
★      Are there regular training sessions or events to educate users on data
security best practices?
★      Do I make security training engaging and interactive to maintain user
interest?
★      Are there contextual security tips embedded within the app to guide
users in real-time?
★      Do I encourage users to report suspicious activities and provide easy
ways for them to do so?
★      Is there a bug bounty program in place to incentivize security
researchers to ﬁnd vulnerabilities?
★      Do I educate users on how to create strong, unique passwords for
their accounts?
★      Are users informed about the dangers of phishing and how to
recognize phishing attempts?
★      Do I provide guidelines on how users should handle and share
sensitive data?
★      Are users reminded to keep their software and applications up to
date?
★      Do I educate users about the risks of using public Wi-Fi for accessing
sensitive information?
★      Are there rewards or recognition programs for users who contribute to
maintaining security?
★      Do I make use of internet memes or humor to make security tips more
relatable and memorable?
★      Are users trained on the importance of logging out from shared or
public devices?
★      Do I provide examples of common security threats and how users can
avoid them?
★      Are there regular assessments or quizzes to test users' knowledge of
security practices?
★      Do I provide resources or links to additional security information for
interested users?
★      Are users informed about the consequences of poor security
practices?
★      Do I provide support for users who have experienced a security
incident?
★      Are there clear instructions for users on how to recover from a
security breach?
★      Do I make it easy for users to contact support if they suspect their
account has been compromised?
★      Are there regular updates or newsletters to keep users informed about
new security threats and best practices?
★      Do I use real-life examples or case studies to illustrate the importance
of data security?
★      Are users encouraged to use multi-factor authentication (MF A) for
added security?
★      Do I provide tips on how users can securely store their passwords?
★      Are users informed about the importance of regular password
changes?
★      Do I provide guidance on how users can recognize and avoid
malicious websites?
★      Are there measures in place to prevent users from reusing passwords
across multiple accounts?
★      Do I educate users on the importance of securing their personal
devices?
Storing Sensitive Data Without Necessity
Sometimes companies keep the data that does not have practic al utility for
them, they store it as if it were some junk. Overloading private info is like
having a house that if a single ﬁre burns, all the house will collapse. Think
of a toy company that wants the kids' address, date of birth, and even their
favorite colors, though, they only need an email in order to mail them a
receipt. This sensitive information could make the extra sensitive
information could turn into big trouble if it falls into the wrong hands!
Facebook-Cambridge Analytica Data Scandal
The Facebook-Cambridge Analytica data scandal is a well-kno wn story in
which the personal data of users were put in danger , or were
exhibited/exhibited beyond the reason of collection or authorization. In
2018, the political consulting ﬁrm Cambridge Analytica was accused of
improperly using the personal data of up to 87 million Facebook users
without their permission. This was reported by the New York Times, The
Gaurdian, and The Observer
Such a situation was created because of the fact that Facebook the big social
network at that time allowed a Cambridge University scholar to collect data
from users and their connected people through a personality quiz app in
2013-2014, Moreover , he breached Facebook policies. The data were
composed of the categories of user identities, locations, interest s, and more
- inform ation beyond which was not common to be gathered and transferred
to third parties such as Cambridge Analytica.
The information is then used to construct fully detailed dossiers of
American voters and then present custom advertisement to them during
political campaigns-for instance, the Donald Trump 2016 presidential race
through Faceboo k. It was later found out Facebook was not checking the
access third-party apps had to user data and that the concern about user
privacy and personal informati on abuse was legitimate saw by users in
many countries.
Facebook has been faced with a lot of issues like investigations, the wrath
of the public, and ﬁnes that are epic in size from the federal Trade
Commission for the data leaked in this scandal. The FTC charged Facebook
with the allegation of lying to users about security provisions.
The Legal Lowdown: Privacy Laws You Need to Know
Fascinatingly , the current situation is complicated with serious legal issues.
Speciﬁcally , a number of regulations that continue to spoil matters. The
following are a couple of the main ones:
GDPR : The General Data Protection Regulation is a European law that
allows users to choose how much data is used that is about them. If you
have any Europeans among your users, you are required to be more
sensitive to the kinds of data you collect and how you use it. In other words,
do not collect data that you do not need!
CCP A: The California Consumer Privacy Act is similar to GDPR and is
like the American relative of the law. It is designed speciﬁcally for
California reside nts and guarantees them the right to know what personal
data companies have been collecting about them and the right to ask such
companies to delete that data. If you have users in the Golden State, be sure
that you have no non-essential information!
These are just two examples, but there are privacy laws popping up all over
the world. The bottom line? If you are collecting sensitive user data, you
need to be upfro nt about what you are collecting, why you need it, and how
you will use it. And if you do not need it, do not keep it!
So, how can you avoid the pitfalls of data hoarding and stay on the right
side of privacy laws?
The answer is simple: embrace data minimalism!
Data minimalism means only collecting and storing the sensitive data you
absolutely need to make your product work. it is like decluttering your
house - you keep the essentials and get rid of the rest.
Here are a few tips to help you become a data minimalist:
Take a Data Inventory: Check what kind of data is collected while making
yourself the question of if you truly need the very data introduced into your
system. If you want to get rid of it, you should remove it from your system!
Get Consent: In case you necessitate collectin g sensitive data, ensure user
consent is acquired at the ﬁrst step. Describe the reason behind you
requiring the data that you need and how you will use it. Roll out the red
carpet to be able to say No to the users who do not feel at ease sharing their
data. Make sure that you are not storing data that is no longer needed by
establishing expiration dates for data. Thus, delete it when its use has
ended. Thus, Breach can be reduced and data will be maintained at a fresh
and relevant state.
Set Data Expiration Dates: Like milk in a fridge is expiring, data too
becomes less relevant with time. Put expiration time on the sensitive data
and erase it if you do not use it anymore. This way it will reduce the chance
of a breach and guarantee you have fresh and useful data.
Encrypt Everything:  You can use encryption techno logy to store the data
securely if it is sensitive. That is, the hacker cannot have access to the
information even if it is obtained. it is just like putting your cash and
jewelry in a security box - it provides an extra layer of protection.
Train Your Team: Be sure that each member of your company knows the
signiﬁcance of data minimalism . Instruct them in the ﬁrst place not to
collect, store and delete conﬁde ntial data. You are less likely to hoard data
when it is easy for everyone to follow the policy . When your team is
collectively on the same page, it is simpler to control data hoarding.
While there are plenty of cautionary tales out there, there are also
companies that are doing data minimalism right.
DuckDuckGo's No-T racking Policy
Unlike other search engines like Google and Bing that keep track of what
you search online, DuckDuckG o is different. They amass your personal
data and information to sell ads targeted just for you. But DuckDuckGo
does things dif ferently .
DuckDuckGo is the only search engine that guarantees its promises to take
care of all your privacy concerns when you browse with them by declining
to use or share your personal data. Thus, even if you make your searches via
DuckDuckGo, the engine will not create a product personality for you such
as your place of stay , what you like, or how you browse.
Basically , other search ﬁrms collect enormous amounts of user data to run
targeted ads and also they take the chance to resell that information to other
corporations. Contrariwise, DuckDuckGo stands against such a practice.
What they say is they do not require to intrude into your personal space and
collect personal info just to give you useful search results.
So, through DuckDuckGo, your searches are private- a totally blank slate.
The engine merely retrieves and gives you the most suitable website links
based on your keywords. There is no data tracking and secret monitoring
through the form of proﬁling occurring on the background.
DuckDuckGo is one of the companies that after a user has made a search
request do not keep nor track the user's personal information. They will not
track your data, nor dig up your info to sell it to others. Just simple and
transparent search results without any spying on the users' part.
For the many internet users who worry about their online privacy ,
DuckDuckGo can become a great relief in that it does not store their private
information, as many of the mainstream search engines do. One can search
for whatever they want, without  being kept on the hook of a tech company
that makes a data proﬁle of you and your interests without your consent.
Self-Evaluation
★      Do we regularly assess the necessity of the data we collect?
★      Are we transparent with users about what data we collect and why?
★      Do we obtain explicit consent from users before collecting sensitive
data?
★      Are our data collection practices compliant with GDPR and CCP A
regulations?
★      Do we have a policy for setting data expiration dates and regularly
purging outdated information?
★      Do we use encryption to protect sensitive data at rest and in transit?
★      Is our team trained on the principles and importance of data
minimalism?
★      Do we conduct regular data audits to identify and eliminate
unnecessary data?
★      Are we minimizing the amount of personally identiﬁable information
(PII) we collect?
★      Do we have a clear process for users to request deletion of their data?
★      Are our third-party data-sharing practices clearly outlined and strictly
regulated?
★      Do we have a robust system for monitoring and securing stored data?
★      Are we regularly updating our data security measures to address new
threats?
★      Do we limit access to sensitive data to only those who absolutely
need it?
★      Are we proactive in educating users about their privacy rights and
how their data is used?
★      Do we have a breach response plan in place, and is our team trained
to handle data breaches ef fectively?
★      Are we using anonymization techniques where possible to protect
user privacy?
★      Do we regularly review and update our privacy policy to reﬂect
current practices and legal requirements?
★      Are we vigilant about avoiding the collection of data beyond what is
necessary for our services?
★      Do we provide users with easy-to-understand information about their
data privacy options?
★      Are we conducting regular security audits to ensure the integrity and
security of our data storage systems?
★      Do we avoid storing sensitive data on unsecured or shared devices?
★      Are we minimizing the use of cookies and tracking technologies that
collect user data?

are processed promptly and thoroughly?

on new data protection laws and regulations?

users?
★      Are we providing adequate resources and training for our team on
data protection and privacy best practices?
★      Do we regularly review and update our data handling and storage
practices to improve security?
★      Are we employing data masking techniques to protect sensitive
information during development and testing?
★      Do we encourage a culture of data privacy and protection within our
organization?
Not Having a Data Br each Response Plan
Imagine this: you are comfy at home, absorbed in the show you love, and
suddenly , the ﬁre alarm blares. You jump, heart pounding, unsur e of what to
do next. So, what is your plan if a ﬁre sparks, do you grab a bucket of water
or dial 911? Or perhaps you would scramble about in fear? This is exactly
the type of mayhem a company feels when there is a cyber -attack with no
security plan in place. It feels like they're laid bare naked in the middle of
the city square - embarrassing, disorderly and potentially disastrous. Now ,
picture the confusion that unfolds in a customer's head when they ﬁnd out
their beloved brand is a disaster and has no contingency plan.
Why Failing to Plan is Planning to Fail
Every single moment matters when a security breach occurs. The more time
you take to respond is directly proportional to the scale of damage. This is
the reaso n why a breach respons e plan is mandatory and not having it is like
playing a losing game: your employees might wander aimlessl y without a
clearly outlined plan. Besides humans running in all directions, you would
discover that, over the period, the losses would accrue. The way you react
to a data breach can either glorify your reputation or be the cause of your
downfall. Peopl e's trust in you collapses faster than you can reassure them
by the word if you are not fast enough.
Some privacy laws require businesses to report any data breach within a
certain timeframe after discovery . You can be blamed if the detection takes
too long, especially without a plan. The attack's damage can be signiﬁcantly
worse as time passes. The aftermath might include an investigation, legal
action, lost business, and a considerable ﬁnancial impact if not managed
well.
A data breach is like a dumpster ﬁre - if you do not have a plan to put it
out, it will just keep spreading and causing more damage.
Equifax's Epic Fail
In 2017, one of the three main credit reporting agencies in the USA,
Equifax, had to deal with an enormous data breach that had an impact on
almost 143 million people by revealing their personal information. The
information comprises birth dates, Social Security numbers - the type of
sensitive data that can seriously af fect you if it falls into the wrong hands.
Almost every time something as big as this occurs, the ﬁrm should react
rapidly , tell people as it is, and manage it correctly , is not that so? Well,
Equifax, in essence, did the opposite!
They , however , did not inform anyone till around ﬁve month s later, the
moment they gotten to know about it. But it had already happened when
they declared it.
But that is not all! When the company ﬁnally did let people know , they
introduced this website that was confusing a bit, and the people were
curious if the site even legit. Moreover , the company's Twitter account
accidentally made a tweet with the link to the fake and phishin g version of
the site. Moreover , Equifax was reported to have been using weakly secured
(default passwo rds like "admin") systems many a time. it is neither
professional nor responsible of them.
Obviously , the situation was a mess from start to ﬁnish. Equifax got
investigations, lawsuits, and had to pay for damages for up to 425 million
dollars for the hacker breach affected. It was an example of how not to
handle a catastrophe. Instead of being honest, transparent, and caring for the
customers, Equifax was engaged in making more and more mistakes,
hiding, and, above all, being clumsy .
Creating a data breach response plan is like setting up a lifeboa t before you
sail into the sea - it is a crucial safety measure that you never expect to be
called for, but if you are, it could be what prevents failure. This is a detailed
set of the guidelines for creating an ef fective plan:
Build A Taskfor ce
Choose people with different skills and specialties like IT, legal, PR, and
customer service and clearly state what role each team mem ber will be
taking in case a breach occur s. Furthermore, everyone should be well
versed in the means of contacting each other swiftly and securely while a
team leader can be appointed to manage the response body .
Draft a Handbook
The ﬁrst step should be to establish the various kinds of data breaches that
could be under the threat (e.g., ransomware attack, phishing scam, insider
threat) and then write a respons e plan for every breach type that includes
actions to be taken quickly to contain the breach and avert furth er damage,
alerting appropriate ofﬁcials and departments, reaching out to the affected
users and society , probing into the root of the breach by collecting evidence
and ﬁnally setting up plans on how to avoid future breaches. Make sure that
the playbook is always availabl e to the entire team, even when the regular
systems are down and consider developing checklists and ﬂowcharts to
make the playbook user -friendly during a distressing period.
Practice Makes You Perfect
On your schedul e, carry out the regular preparation exercises, by which, the
procedures record in real life mode and the software acts as the phony one
phishing bolt, invented press inquiries, and breakdown of computer
capabilities gets at the human weakest link. You should, in addition, learn
about non-technical issues, such as intrusion detection, and set the
appropriate development policy .
Keep It Fr esh
Fix a time by the end of every year, and update the methodology versions to
coincide with the upcoming modiﬁcations or new threats to the company -
this may also change the schedule. You are directing the resolving of the
latest threat through the plan by the implementation of new and different
possibilities. One possible way is asking for input or even hiring the outside
expertise to evaluate your team's approach in a completely dif ferent way .
Besides a carefully developed plan, companies must include several
fundamental tools and resources in the data breach response toolkit:
1. In the event of a breach, your usual communication channels may
be compromised. Prepare secure, alternative channels (for
instance, encrypted messaging apps, and dedicated phone lines)
in advance.
2. Regular , secure backups of the critical data may be a major relief
if various types of attacks, e.g., ransomware are decrypted or/an d
destroyed. Remember that both backups should be kept ofﬂine
and tested frequently .
3. There are various software tools provided for you to facilitate and
automate your breach respon se process; these include the
identiﬁcation of potential incidents to communicating with
stakeholders.
4. Being well-acquainted with the contact details for your legal and
PR team as well as considering having pre-prepared messaging
templates and holding statements would be beneﬁcial.
With all the tools and resources in place, you can be sure that you are able
to apply your response plan quickly and efﬁciently when a breach happens.
Nobody wishes to dwell on the topic of data breaches, but the bitter truth is
that they occur . And when they do, you certainly do not want to be
unprepared. A data breach response plan that has been put in place and
regularly practic ed at quarterly intervals is not only the way for you to
efﬁciently mitigate the damage and remain your users' trust but you might
even emer ge even stronger in the end.
Self-Evaluation
★      Do we have a formal data breach response plan in place?
★      Is our data breach response plan regularly reviewed and updated?
★      Have we identiﬁed and documented the types of data breaches that
could impact our or ganization?
★      Do we have a dedicated team or task force responsible for handling
data breaches?
★      Are roles and responsibilities clearly deﬁned for each team member in
the event of a data breach?
★      Have we established secure and swift communication channels for
use during a breach?
★      Do we conduct regular drills or simulations to test our breach
response plan?
★      Are our employees trained to recognize and report potential security
incidents?
★      Do we have a clear protocol for containing and mitigating the damage
from a data breach?
★      Are there procedures in place for notifying af fected individuals and
relevant authorities promptly?
★      Have we established guidelines for collecting and preserving
evidence during a data breach investigation?
★      Do we have pre-prepared messaging templates for communicating
with stakeholders during a breach?
★      Are we using encryption and secure backups to protect sensitive data?
★      Have we identiﬁed and documented our critical data assets and their
locations?
★      Do we have alternative communication methods prepared in case our
usual channels are compromised?
★      Are we regularly testing and updating our backup systems to ensure
they are ef fective?
★      Do we have access to legal and PR experts to help manage the
aftermath of a breach?
★      Are we aware of the legal requirements for reporting data breaches in
all the jurisdictions where we operate?
★      Do we have a process for analyzing the root cause of a data breach
and implementing measures to prevent future incidents?
★      Are we regularly reviewing and updating our security policies and
procedures to address new threats?
★      Have we conducted a risk assessment to identify potential
vulnerabilities in our systems?
★      Do we have monitoring tools in place to detect and respond to
security incidents quickly?
★      Are we using up-to-date security software to protect against known
vulnerabilities and threats?
★      Do we have a plan for restoring systems and data after a breach?
★      Are we maintaining an inventory of all data and systems that could be
targeted in a breach?
★      Do we have a process for documenting and learning from past breach
incidents?
★      Are we engaging with external experts to evaluate and improve our
breach response plan?
★      Have we established clear criteria for determining the severity of a
data breach?
★      Do we have a communication plan for informing our customers about
data breaches transparently and promptly?
★      Are we committed to maintaining user trust by being proactive and
transparent in our approach to data security?
Overlooking Third-Party Integrations
Let us say that you are in the process of putting together your dream home.
It has taken you several months to design each and every room perfectly , to
choose the right materials, and to make sure that everything is at your
convenience. However , later when you want to put in the electrical wiring
you decide to hire the cheapest  contractor on Craigslist without checking
their credentials or references.
That is pretty much the same as trusting third-party services with your
software as a service (SaaS) without thoroughly checking their security
certiﬁcations. Integrating a third -party service into your software is trusting
a primar y piece of your system to a provider and if they have holes or bad
habits, your system can be collapsed.
The Risks in the "Set It and Forget It" Philosophy
In this age, of the Internet of Things, and global interconn ection, the
integration of third-party service s with SaaS systems is almost a mandatory
requirement. Whether it is a payment processor , marketing automation tool,
or a customer support platform,  these integrations can bring a lot of value
and additional features to your product. But they can also open the door to
newly arising risks. These are just some of the ways that disregarding third-
party security can come back to haunt you:
The Back Door  Attack
A third-party service with which you are integrated is the one that got
breached, and the attackers may use that account to access your system too.
It is like leaving your door half open hoping no one will break in.
The Compliance Disaster
Many sectors are ruled out by the strict regulations on data security and
privacy (healthcare HIPAA, ﬁnancial services PCI). If the third-party
integrations are not compliant with these standards, you might end up being
on the hook for some signiﬁcant penalties and legal trouble in the future.
The Reputation Br eaker
Even if you are not the culpable party in a security breach, if a hacker
managed to ﬁnd the weak spot in one of your third-party integrations, the
blame will still be on you. Clients will not question the identity of the
culprit - they will only know that they were compromised due to your
product.
The Partner  Pickle
If you are not cautious regarding the veriﬁcation of your third-party
integrations, you might end up wagering with an enterprise that brandishes
a backgr ound of security issues or sleazy acts. That is a pickle you probably
do not want to be in the middle of when the stuff hits the blades. Integrating
with third-party services is similar to inviting strangers into your house.
Make sure you know them and that they will not deceive you.
The "T rust, But Verify" Way
Additionally , you should be able to harness the merits of third-party
integrations without much ado. The solution is by using the "trust, but
verify" approach. Here is what that looks like in real terms:
Investigation As If You Were CIA: Always study the company's historical
records of how securely they keep information as if you were in the CIA.
Check their privacy policy , peruse for hints  and breaches in the past and
decide whether to work with them or not. If there seems to be some
negative air around a company , it is best to be vigilant and ﬁnd a different
partner .
Get It in Writing: After having chosen a solution for their integration,
make sure to have it down in print and clear that it shows the responsibility
and liability of each partner . Do not take the path of least resistance. Make a
special agreement in writing.
Test, Test, Test: Prior to launching a new third- party integration into your
live environmen t, be certain to give it a complete check for bugs or
compatibility issues. it is wrong to wait until a breakdown happens due to
the impr opriety of your customers' data leaking through the system to know
of the problem.
Monitor  Like A Hawk:  As soon as a third-party service has passed the
vetting process does not mean you are done with it. Check the integration
for any signs of being manipulated by cybercriminals or the product's
disrupted activity . Also, have a plan in place to quickly switch off the
service if necessary .
Stay in the Loop:  Regular ly inspe ct the actions of your third-party partners
and check the industry news for any updates. If a breach occurs or when
they change their policies, you need to know the reasons immediately to
take the right measures.
By keeping these actions in practice, you can form a reliable network of
third-party integrations that embellish your product without causing any
risks to your security . It is similar to having a team of vetted, reliable
contractors working on your ideal house - them being there will ensure you
have no worries about your security .
The Tragic Tale of Target's Br each
It was a data breach of a massive scale and was a wake-up call on the front
of supply chain cybersecurity . It occurred in 2013, just before the holidays
when the laxity in the vendor  management of Target was exploited by
criminals who entered the systems.
Initially , the cybercriminals' entry point looked quite deceiving having
chosen a small HVAC company that was contracted with Target's networks.
They used the vendor credentials, the cybercriminals sneaked malware onto
Target's payment systems virtually undetected.
Despite being shown the present suspicious activity by the cyber -attack tool
twice, Target was too sluggish to act. Stuck in the trafﬁc of incipient threats
through their networks the sensitive data dramatically leaked per-second
payments cards , names, addresses, and phone numbers of 110 million
customers before the danger was contained.
The damage was indeed seriou s. Target's sales dropped by half and they
were moving into the key Christ mas quarter when the market was alienated
by the time the scandal broke out. They dealt with a deluge of lawsuits, with
the amount of settlements being over $18.5 million and the costs of
recovery being higher than $250 million to compensate for their mistakes.
During the ruction, the Target CEO and CIO were forced out of their
positions amidst denouncing speeches about their weak points  on security
and nonchalant responses. Wha t they once thought of as one of the top
retailers had come to light as a complete failure of the new normal.
After the breach, it became clearly visible that each vulnerable node, the
corporation shou ld protect every possible entry to the syste ms. The ﬂaw in
Target's tech partner monitoring , due to weak practices, was the bridge for
the attack. Even though cybers ecurity standards have to be surveyed and
executed in the entire supply chain, the Target break had made it a
remarkable thing. The gateways that can used by cyber -attackers to get into
the core of an or ganization are third-party vulnerabilities.
For Target, the act of breaking  the security was a lesson that was very
expensive and affected the brand, not only of their own fortresses but also
of every partner , contractor , and vendor's security posture. Any leakage that
happens in one area can rapidly change into a systemic crisis with the
current interconnectedness.
In the world of SaaS, which moves at incredible speed, it is very tempting
to just keep on working and adding new features or integrations to your
product. Yet, when it comes to security , there is no such thing as going too
fast.
You need to exhaustively check third-party integrations and permanently
oversee their security practice s is a never -ending thing for businesses.
Nevertheless, it is essential to be done as compared to the potential cost of a
breach.
Therefore, do not be like the company that is cheating when it comes to
third-party security. Be the company that constructs a protective barrier
consisting of secure partnerships and unbreakable integrations.
Actually , I need to carry out a security check on some contractors

concerns with third-party providers?
★      Are we continuously seeking feedback from our third-party providers
to improve our security practices?
Inadequate Backup and Recovery Pr ocesses
Visualize this: you are putting mega effort into a huge project that has taken
a long time. you are on a roll, coding like a computer , when suddenly ... your
computer dies. You restart it in a panic, only to see that whatev er you were
doing is gone. Poof. You cannot ﬁnd it anywhere.
Above is a kind of a bad dream scenario that can occur if you are not fully
prepared with a proper data backup and recovery policy . Believe me, it is
not just a person al productivity killer but it can really be a business-ending
catastrophe.
The Risks of Flying Without a Net
For a SaaS product, data is everything. it is the lifeblood of your product,
the key to your customers' success, and the foundation of your business. So,
what happens when that data goes missing or gets corrupted? Nothing good,
that is what.
Here are just a few of the ways that inadequate backup and recovery can
come back to haunt you:
1. The Ransomwar e Reckoning:  Suppose a hacker succeeds in
breaking in your system and, as a result, you are attacked by a
nasty ransom. All your data is suddenly encrypted and kept
hostage until you pay a big ransom. If you do not have the most
recent backups,  you may have no other choice but to pay the
ransom or regret the loss of your data permanently .
2. The Hardwar e Headache:  Regardless of how good your servers
and infrastructure are, failures in hardware do occur . Disks go
bad, memory modules are damaged, and power supplies are
overheated. Without good backups, a single ﬂawed component
could erase key data and leave your business in the lurch.
3. The Human Error Horr or: it is true. We are all prone to making
mistakes. But small yet critical data management errors can result
in big problems. Errors such as a ﬁle getting accidentally delete d,
overwritten ﬁles, and messed up updates can all be reasons for
missing data if you are not quick to back up the data.
4. The Compliance Conundrum:  The rest of the world's industries
have very strict rules around  things like data retention and
disaster recovery (by the way, HIPAA and GDPR). If you are not
doing it properly and cannot get your data back immediately , you
could be ﬁned with the law and incur heavy penalties.
Running a SaaS business without proper backup and recovery is as good as
walking a tightrope without the availability of a safety net. Naturally , you
may succeed without any setbacks, but once a mistake is made, then it is
game over .
The "Save Early , Save Often" Strategy
Thus, the questi on arises of how to guard your data (and your company)
from the dangers of suboptimal backup and recovery? The absolute
requirement is to adopt an attitude about the importance of saving data early
and often. Here is what that looks like in practice:
1. Backup Religiously:  A successful backup schedule that copies
and saves all your important data regularly is crucial. You might
want to backup data daily , hourly , or even in real-time, depending
on which of your data changes and the amount of aggregate data
you will accept as waste in a scenario of day-dreaming.
2. Follow the 3-2-1 Rule:  An excellent method of data backup is to
have three copies of your data, on two separate media, with one
backup kept elsewhere. This will ensure that even if one backup
fails or is destroyed, you still have other copies to rely on.
3. Test Your Backups:  (Psst...see if they work or not.) Generally
speaking, it is wonderful to have backups, but, if you do not have
the resources to restore them, then they are virtually useless. The
most important  thing is to regularly check the process of
restoration that only then can you be sure that the process is
feasible and you can get your data back safely and quickly .
4. Automate Whe re You Can: From the point of view of the best,
manual backups  are generally better than no backups, but the
problem is that they are compl etely subject to human error and
the fact that there can be inconsistencies with them. In as far as it
is possib le, automate the backup process, be it through scripts, or
using tools, or managed services. Set and for get it (but control it).
5. Documented (and tried) Recovery Plan:  It is commonly said
that time ﬂies, but, in the case of a catastrophe, you will be the
only one who is stuck, and your ﬁrst response is scrambling to
ﬁnd the right way to recover  the data. Create an elabora te
recovery plan that states how each step should be taken, by
whom, and in what order . And the same as with the restores, be
sure to test your recovery plan to make sure it actually works out.
In practice, the approach of combining these strategies togeth er will help
you to take the myth out of backups. Thus, even in the embarrassing
situation that you get it wrong (face it, something always goes wrong), you
can quickly recover and get back to your busy day . 
Still not convinced that inadequate backup and recovery is a big deal? let us
take a look at a real-world example of what can happen when  companies
skimp on their data protection:
The CodeSpaces Catastr ophe :
During the time period of June 2014, CodeSpaces, a code hosting company ,
was the target of a very severe and complex cyber -attack that, in fact, made
it close down forever .
The ﬁrst step was a distributed denial of service (DDoS) attac k that it the
servers of the service to buckle  under the load. The dark side of the moon
was this- the unhackneyed aggressor had already managed to snap the
authorization Key to the Amazon Web Services contro l panel of
CodeSpaces.
The trespasser then set off ransom notes, in which he demanded that
CodeSpaces paid the amount needed to shut down the DDoS  attack. But
CodeSpaces said no and the hacking caused them to have to ﬁght back
again by changing the passwords so that the hacker would not be able to
take any further action.
Once through the old way, the attacker launched an all-out extermination
operation - erasing CodeSpaces' backup data, storage buckets, machine
instances, and even their off-site backups. The corrosive deletion incident
wiped out practically all of CodeSpaces' data and backups.
Because they felt that there was hardly a single other road to travel,
CodeSpaces decided to leave the industry permanently , being the victim of
a quick but unfortunate operatio n. The matter was made worse by the fact
of a mention of the unbearable price of the customer recovery and refund.
The event, in fact, manifested the imprudence involved in the lack of a very
solid support and backup system, particularly in times when cloud facilities
are lent. Cloud providers are not enough as a backup source without disaster
recovery plans in place.
The security professionals claim ed that the event was the major driver for a
multi-layered backup plan that was fought for against organized attacks
such as with the CodeSpaces pair. The argument here was that merely
possessing geographic diversity of your data to beat the situatio n, though is
no longer completely accurate.
The CodeSpace s debacle was a deep and shocking reminder to the world
about what a cyber -attack can lead to, and on the other hand, the supreme
importance of cyber -related robust data backup and recovery processes. The
CodeSpaces catastrophe is a unique and painful lesson that the company has
learned, never can undo until today .
Self-Evaluation
★      Do we have a documented data backup policy in place?
★      How frequently do we backup our critical data?
★      Are backups scheduled to occur automatically or manually?
★      Do we follow the 3-2-1 rule for data backups (three copies, on two
different media, with one of f-site)?
★      Have we tested our backup process to ensure data can be restored
successfully?
★      Is our backup data encrypted to protect sensitive information?
★      Do we have a disaster recovery plan that outlines procedures for
restoring data in various scenarios?
★      Have we identiﬁed and prioritized our critical systems and data for
backup and recovery?
★      Are there redundancies in place for our backup systems to prevent
single points of failure?
★      How quickly can we recover data in the event of a data loss incident?
★      Are our backup and recovery processes compliant with relevant
industry regulations and standards?
★      Do we regularly review and update our backup and recovery policies
based on changing business needs?
★      Have we allocated sufﬁcient resources (time, personnel, budget) to
maintain ef fective backup and recovery operations?

recovery?

compromise?

with technology advancements and security threats?

disruptions to our backup systems?

or issues related to backup and recovery promptly?

vulnerabilities in our backup and recovery processes?
