unauthorized access to backup data?
★      Do we maintain an inventory of all backup media and storage
locations, including of f-site backups?
★      How do we prioritize which data to recover ﬁrst in the event of a
widespread outage or disaster?
★      Are our backup and recovery strategies aligned with our overall
business continuity plan?
Chapter  10: Using Metrics and Analytics
There is no gr eat genius without some touch of madness - Aristotle
When it came to the user engag ement metrics, the ﬁrst task was to deﬁne
your goals and then to choose those metrics that not only are compatible
with your goals but also give you the data to manually act on it. It is of
course important to take the measurements that involve comparing or
setting a benchmark, which in turn will allow you to monitor systematically
your performance throughout the time. let us discuss why these metrics are
so impo rtant and how we shou ld properly and efﬁciently implement the
monitoring process.
Daily Active Users (DAU) / Monthly Active Users (MAU):
Daily Active Users (DAU) : The number of unique users who engage with
your product in a single day .
Monthly Active Users (MAU) : The number of unique users who engage
with your product in a single month.
These metrics help gauge the regular use and popularity of your product.
They are key indicators of user engagement and retention. DAU  measur es
the day-to-day activity and stickiness of your product. It helps you
understand how many users ﬁnd your product valuable enough to use it
daily . On the other hand, MAU  provides a broader view of user engagement
over a longer period. It shows how many users are consistently engaging
with your product monthly .
let us say you have a task management app called "T askMaster".
On May 1st, 2024, you have 500 unique users who logged into TaskMaster
and performed any activity (e.g., creating tasks, checking off completed
tasks, viewing their task list).
Your DAU for May 1st, 2024, is 500.
Throughout May 2024, you have 5,000 unique users who logged into
TaskMaster at least once and performed any activity .
Your MAU for May 2024 is 5,000.
How to Use These Metrics :
Track DAU and MAU over time to spot trends. Are your DAU and MAU
increasing, stabl e, or declining? This can indicate the overall health of your
product.
Calculate the ratio of DAU to MAU to understand the stickiness of your
product; this is called the Engagement Ratio. A higher ratio indicates that a
large portion of your monthly users are also daily users.
For instance, if your DAU is 500 and your MAU is 5,000:
This means that 10% of your monthly users are also daily users.
Imagine you notice that your MAU is high, but your DAU is relatively low.
This might indic ate that users ﬁnd value in your product but do not need to
use it daily .
Customer  Lifetime Value (CL TV)
Customer Lifetime Value (CLTV) is a metric that estimates the total
revenue a business can expect from a single customer account throughout
their entire relationship with the company . It helps businesses understand
the long-term value of their customer relationships.
CLTV helps businesses make informed decisions about marketing spend,
customer acquisition, and retention strategies. By knowing how much
revenue a customer is likely to bring, businesses can allocate resources
more efﬁciently . Understanding CLTV helps in assessing the overall
proﬁtability of customer segme nts and optimizing the customer journey to
maximize revenue.
Average Revenue Per  User  (ARPU)
Average Revenu e Per User (ARPU) is a key performance indicator (KPI)
that measures the average amount of revenue generated per user or account
over a speciﬁc period. ARPU provides insight into the revenue contribution
of each user and is crucial for understanding the ﬁnancial health  and growth
potential of a SaaS business.  ARPU helps businesses understand the
revenue generate d by each user, which is essential for pricing strategies and
revenue forecast ing. It indicates the value that each customer brings to the
business, helping to identify high-value customer segments. ARPU trends
over time can indicate the effectiveness of upselling, cross-selling, and
customer retention strategies. It allows companies to compare their
performance against industry standards or competitors.
How to calculate ARPU?
ARPU is calculated by dividing the total revenue generated in a speciﬁc
period by the number of active users during that period.
Let us say a SaaS product, "ProjectX", provides project management
software. In the month of April, ProjectX generated $100,000 in revenue
and had 2,000 active users.
This means that, on average, each active user generated $50 in revenue for
ProjectX in April.
What factors could inﬂuence ARPU?
Pricing Strategy : Different pricing tiers (e.g., basic, premium, enterprise)
can af fect ARPU. Higher -priced plans typically increase ARPU.
Product Value: Enhanced features and better product offerings can justify
higher prices, leading to increased ARPU.
Customer  Mix: The proportion of high-paying customers versus those on
lower -tier plans impacts ARPU.
Discounts and Promotions : While these can attract more users, they might
lower ARPU if not managed carefully .
Upselling and Cross-selling : Effective strategies in these areas can boost
ARPU by encouraging existing customers to spend more.
Imagine "ProjectX" introduces a new feature for premium users in May.
This feature is highly valued, and many users upgrade from the basic to the
premium plan.
Total Revenue in May : $150,000, Number  of Active Users : 2,500
In May, ProjectX’ s ARPU incre ased to $60. This rise indicates that the new
premium featur e successfully  encouraged users to upgrade, thereby
increasing the average revenue per user .
Tips to impr ove ARPU
Encourage custo mers to purchase higher -tier plans or addition al
products/services.
Continuously improve the product to justify higher pricing and
attract premium customers.
Customize offers and promotions to meet the speciﬁc needs of
different customer segments, encouraging them to spend more.
Maintain and improve customer satisfaction to reduce churn and
retain high-value users.
Regularly review and adjust pricing strategies based on market
trends, and customer feedback.
Net Pr omoter  Scor e (NPS)
Net Promoter Score (NPS) is a metric that measures customer  loyalty and
satisfaction by asking users how likely they are to recommend your product
or service to others. NPS is based on a simple question: "On a scale of 0 to
10, how likely ar e you to r ecommend our pr oduct to a friend or colleague? "
NPS provides insight into how loyal and satisﬁed customers are with your
product. A high NPS often correlates with positive word-of-mouth, lower
churn rates, and higher customer lifetime value. It helps identify promoters
who can become brand advocates and detractors who need attention to
improve their experience. Companies can compare their NPS against
industry standards or competitors to gauge their performance.
How to Calculate NPS
NPS is calculated by subtracti ng the percentage of detractors from the
percentage of promoters. The respondents are categorized as follows:
Promoters (scor e 9-10) : Loyal enthusiasts who will keep buying and refer
others, fueling growth.
Passives (scor e 7-8): Satisﬁed but unenthusiastic customers who are
vulnerable to competitive of ferings.
Detractors (score 0-6): Unhappy customers who can damage your brand
and impede growth through negative word-of-mouth.
NPS=%Promoters−%Detractors
Suppose a SaaS company , "TechSoftABC," surveys 100 customers about
their likelihood to recommend the product:
●         60 customers are promoters (score 9-10)
●         30 customers are passives (score 7-8)
●         10 customers are detractors (score 0-6)
NPS=60%−10%=50
Or 

TechSoftABC’ s NPS is 50, indicating a positive customer sentiment. The
NPS score of 50 suggests that a signiﬁcant portion of their customer base is
very satisﬁed and likely to recommend their product. However , they still
need to address the concerns of the 10% detractors to further improve their
score and overall customer satisfaction.
TIPS to impr ove NPS:
●         Continuously improve and add new features based on custom er
feedback to meet their needs better .
●         Provide excellen t customer service to quickly resolve issues and
enhance user experience.
●         Regularly surve y customers to stay updated on their satisfaction
levels and address any emer ging issues promptly .
●         Proactively engage with customers to ensure they are getting the most
value from your product.
●         Ensure that all teams within the company are aligned toward s
providing exceptional customer value.
Customer  Satisfaction Scor e (CSA T)
Customer Satisfaction Score (CSA T) is a metric that measures the level of
satisfaction customers have with a product, service, or speciﬁc interaction.
It is typically measured through a survey where customers are asked to rate
their satisfaction on a scale, often from 1 to 5 or 1 to 10.
CSAT provides immediate insig ht into how customers feel about speciﬁc
aspects of your product or service. High CSAT scores are linked to higher
customer retenti on rates, as satisﬁed customers are more likely to continue
using the product. CSAT highlights areas needing improvement, enabling
targeted efforts to enhance customer satisfaction. CSAT scores can be
benchmarked over time or against industry standards to gauge performance.
How to Calculate CSA T
CSAT is usually calculated as the percentage of respondents who indicate
satisfaction with the product or service. The formula is:
Suppose a SaaS product, "AppEaseXYZ," surveys its customers after a
support interaction. They use a scale of 1 to 5, where 1 is "Very
Dissatisﬁed" and 5 is "V ery Satisﬁed." Out of 200 responses:
150 customers rate their satisfaction as 4 or 5 (Satisﬁed), 50 customers rate
their satisfaction as 1, 2, or 3 (Dissatisﬁed)
AppEaseXYZ’ s CSAT is 75%, indicating that 75% of their customers are
satisﬁed with their support interactions.
Steps to Measur e and Impr ove CSA T
Design the Survey : Create  a simple, clear survey that asks customers to
rate their satisfaction with a speciﬁc aspect of your product or service.
Collect Data : Send the surve y at appropriate touchpoints, such as after a
customer support interaction, after a feature is used, or periodically .
Analyze Results : Review the feedback to identify trends and areas for
improvement.
Act on Feedback : Implement changes based on the feedback to improve
the areas causing dissatisfaction.
Communicate Changes : Let customers know what improvements have
been made based on their feedback to show that their input is valued.
Consider our example of AppEaseXYZ,   a company providing a customer
support platform. They decide to measure CSAT to understand customer
satisfaction with their support team.
Survey Question : "How satisﬁed are you with the support you received?"
Response Options : Very Dissatisﬁed (1), Dissatisﬁed (2), Neutral (3),
Satisﬁed (4), Very Satisﬁed (5)
Responses :
100 customers rate 5 (V ery Satisﬁed)
50 customers rate 4 (Satisﬁed)
30 customers rate 3 (Neutral)
10 customers rate 2 (Dissatisﬁed)
10 customers rate 1 (V ery Dissatisﬁed)
AppEaseXYZ’ s CSAT score of 75% suggests that a majority of their
customers are satisﬁed with the support they receive, but there  is room for
improvement to increase this percentage.
Now imagine this company receives feedback that their support response
times are too slow. To address this, they implement a new ticke ting system
to prioritize urgent issues and train their support staff to handle inquiries
more efﬁciently . They then survey customers again and see an increase in
their CSAT score from 75% to 85%, indicating improved customer
satisfaction due to these changes.
Engagement Scor e
Engagement Score is used to measure how actively and effectively users are
interacting with a SaaS product.  It evaluates user behaviors and actions that
indicate a deepening relationsh ip with the product, such as frequency of
use, feature adoption, and interaction with core functionalities.
High engagement scores correla te with lower churn rates, as engaged users
are more  likely to continue using the product. Engaged users derive more
value from the product, which can lead to higher customer satisfaction and
loyalty . Tracking engagement scores helps identify which features are most
valuable to users, guiding product development and improvement. Engaged
users are more likely to upgrad e to premium plans, purchase add-ons, and
recommend the product to others, driving revenue growth.
How to Calculate Engagement Scor e?
The calculation of an engageme nt score can vary depending on the speciﬁc
product and the behaviors that indicate engagement. Typically , it involves
tracking multiple user activities and assigning weights to them based on
their important
Let us consider a project management tool called "TaskMaster ." The
company tracks several key user actions:
●         Login Fr equency : How often users log in.
●         Task Cr eation : Number of tasks created.
●         Project Completion : Number of projects completed.
●         Collaboration : Number of comments or interactions with team
members.
●         Featur e Usage : Usage of advan ced features like Gantt charts or time
tracking.
Each of these actions is assigned a weight based on its signiﬁcance:
●         Login Fr equency : 10%
●         Task Cr eation : 20%
●         Project Completion : 30%
●         Collaboration : 20%
●         Featur e Usage : 20%
Now imagine a user named Jane has the following activities in a month:
●         Logs in 20 times (2 points per login)
●         Creates 50 tasks (1 point per task)
●         Completes 5 projects (5 points per project)
●         Makes 30 comments (0.5 points per comment)
●         Uses advanced features 10 times (1 point per use)
First, we calculate the raw points for each activity:
Login Fr equency : 20 logins×2 points per login=40 points
Task Cr eation : 50 tasks×1 point per task=50 points
Project Completion : 5 projects×5 points per project=25 points
Collaboration : 30 comments×0.5 points per comment=15 points
Featur e Usage : 10 uses×1 point per use=10 points
Total Raw Points: 40+50+25+15+10=140 points
Next, we apply the weights to each activity to calculate the weighted points:
Login Fr equency  (10% weight): 40 points×0.10=4
Task Cr eation  (20% weight): 50 points×0.20=10
Project Completion  (30% weight): 25 points×0.30=7.5
Collaboration  (20% weight): 15 points×0.20=3
Featur e Usage  (20% weight): 10 points×0.20=2
Finally , we add up all the weighted points to get Jane’ s overall engagement
score: 4+10+7.5+3+2=26.5
So, Jane’ s engagement score for the month is 26.5. This score helps
TaskMaster understand how engaged Jane is with the product based on her
activities and the importance of those activities.
How to impr ove Engagement Scor e?
●         Identify Key Actions : Determ ine which user actions best represent
engagement with your product.
●         Assign Weights : Assign weights to each actio n based on its
importance to overall engagement.
●         Track User  Activities : Use analytics tools to track these key actions
for each user .
●         Calculate Scor es: Compute engagement scores regularly to monitor
changes over time.
●         Analyze and Act: Analyz e the scores to identify patterns and areas
for improvement. Use this data to inform product development,
customer success strategies, and marketing ef forts.
Consider our example TaskMaster , a project management SaaS company .
They notice that users with higher engagement scores are less likely to
churn. They decide to focus on improving the engagement score of new
users during their ﬁrst month by:
●         Encouraging frequent logins through reminder emails.
●         Providing tutorials on how to create and manage tasks.
●         Highlighting the beneﬁts of completing projects.
●         Promoting collaboration features through in-app tips.
●         Offering advanced feature webinars to demonstrate their value.
Suppose TaskM aster identiﬁes that users who attend their weekly webinar
on advan ced features tend to have a higher engagement score. They decide
to make the webinar more prominent in their onboarding emails  and in-app
notiﬁcations. As a result, they see an increase in the average engagement
score of new users from 20 to 30 within the ﬁrst month,  leading to
improved retention rates.
User  Growth Rate
User Growth Rate is a crucial metric for SaaS products as it measures the
rate at which new users are signing up for the product over a speciﬁc
period. This metric helps produ ct owners understand how quickly they are
expanding their user base, which is essential for scaling and long-term
success.
A stead y or increasing user growth rate signiﬁes that your product is
appealing to new customers and that your marketing efforts are effective.
Knowing your user growth rate can help in forecasting future growth and in
making informed decisions about scaling your infrastructure and resources.
High growth rates are attractiv e to investors, as they show potential for
rapid expansion and proﬁtability .
How to Calculate User  Growth Rate?
To calculate the User Growth Rate, you follow these steps:
1. Identify the Number  of Users at the Start and End of the
Period : Determ ine the number of users at the beginning and end
of the time period you are measuring.
2. Calculate the Growth : Subtract the number of users at the start
from the number of users at the end to ﬁnd the growth in users.
3. Calculate the Growth Rate : Divide the growth in users by the
number of users at the start of the period and then multiply by
100 to get a percentage.
Let us go throug h an example to understand this metric better . We can go to
our previous example TaskMaster , which provides project management
software. You want to calculat e the User Growth Rate for the month of
April.
Number  of Users at the Start of April : 1,000 users, Number  of Users at
the End of April : 1,200 users
Growth = Number of Users at End − Number of Users at Start
Growth=1,200−1,000=200
In this example,  TaskMaster had a User Growth Rate of 20% for April. This
means the user base grew by 20% over the month. A high growth rate
indicates that the product is attracting new users effectively , which is a
positive sign of business health and market demand.
Session Duration
Session Duratio n reﬂects the average time users spend on the product in a
single session. This metric helps businesses understand how engaging and
valuable their product is to users. A longer session duration generally
indicates that users ﬁnd the product useful and are spending more time
interacting with it, which can be a positive sign of user enga gement and
satisfaction.
Longer session durations often indicate higher user engagement, suggesting
that users ﬁnd the product usefu l and are willing to spend more time on it.
This metric can highlight how user-friendly and engaging your product is. If
users are spending a lot of time in your product, it often means they are
ﬁnding value and navigating it easily. Short session durations may indicate
problems such as poor user experience, lack of engaging content, or
technical issues that cause users to leave early . Tracking sessi on duration
before and after releasing new features or updates can help measure the
impact of those changes on user engagement.
How to Calculate Session Duration?
To calcu late the average session duration, you need to measu re the total
time users spend in the produc t and the number of sessions over a given
period.
Let us say you run a SaaS product called EduLearnX which is an online
learning platform. Now how to calculate average session duration for the
month of May
Total Duration of All Sessions in May : 50,000 minutes
Number  of Sessions in May : 10,000 sessions
In this example , EduLearnX’ s users spent an average of 5 minutes per
session on the platform in May. This indicates how much time, on average,
users engage with the platform each time they log in.
Bounce Rate
Bounce rate indicates the perce ntage of users who visit a site or app and
then leave without taking any further action. In other words, these users
"bounce" away after viewing only one page or screen. This metric helps
businesses understand how effectively their landing pages or initial user
experiences are engaging visitors.
A high bounce rate can indicate that users are not ﬁnding the initial page
engaging or relevant enough to continue exploring. It reﬂects on the
usability and appeal of the landing page or entry point. A confu sing layout
or irrelevant content can drive users away . It helps assess the effectiveness
of marketing campaigns. If users bounce after clicking an ad, the ad might
be misleading or targeting the wrong audience. Search engines consider
bounce rate when ranking page s. A high bounce rate can negatively affect
SEO, reducing or ganic trafﬁc.
Now imagine your product has a total bounces of 1,500 from a total entries
of 4,500 in a given month.
This suggests that about one-third of the users who visited the homepage
left without taking any further action. If you feel 33.33% bounce rate is too
high you might want to implement some optimization strategies as below:
●         Impr oving Page Load Speed : Optimize images and reduce server
response time.
●         Content Optimization : Ensure that the landing page content matches
what was promised in ads and search results.
●         User  Experience Enhancements : Simplify the layout and make
navigation intuitive.
●         Targeted Marketing : Adjust marketing campaigns to ensure they
attract the right audience.
●         Effective CTAs: Place prominent and engaging CTAs that encourage
users to explore further .
Metrics like Average Revenue Per User (ARPU), Net Prom oter Score
(NPS), Customer Satisfaction Score (CSA T), Engagement Score, User
Growth Rate, Session Duratio n, and Bounce Rate each offer a unique
perspective on how your users interact with your product. By tracking and
analyzing these metrics, you can identify strengths and weaknesses in your
product offering, improve user retention, and ultimately drive revenue
growth.
Chapter  11: Return on Investment (ROI) Analysis
In any investment, you expect to have fun and make money - Michael
Jordan
Most Software as a Service (SaaS) companies have a hard time proving the
value of design and ﬁnances the investment in numerical terms and
ﬁnancial measur es. This is the part where ROI analysis is needed. ROI, a
ﬁnancial metric, refers to the proﬁt and efﬁciency of an investment made. In
the case of SaaS design, ROI analysis implies the tasks of computing the
possible outcom e of a design investment and comparing it to the costs of
that design being implemented in the ﬁeld for instance.
let us say a SaaS  company that wants to design a new user onboarding ﬂow.
They are convinced that by simp lifying the onboarding process and making
it more intuitive and engaging, they can get more users to sign up and pay
for their services. The way the return on investment on this design
investment would be calculated is as follows:
The company would ﬁrst estimate the following:
The redesign project's cost, including the hours and material
required from the design team and so many others.
The projected rise in the number of users who complete the
onboarding ﬂow and become paying customers is expected from
user research, testing, and benchmarking.
The average lifetime value (LTV) of a paying customer , which
represents the total amount of revenue that a customer is expecte d
to generate over the course of their relationship with the
company .
The expected duration of the beneﬁts, which is the length of time
that the company predicts for increased onboarding completion
rates and customer L TV referring to the redesign.
Using this data, the company will be able to gauge the beneﬁt of the
redesign project by the help of the following formula:
ROI = (Expected Beneﬁts - Cost of Investment) / Cost of Investment
Another illustrat ion of the same  problem is as follows: A redesign project is
expected to cost $50,000 and the company has been promised a 20%
increase in onboarding completion rates, meaning they will add 100 more
paying customers a month. The paying customer average LTV is set to
$1,000, and the required period of effectiveness that the beneﬁts are
expected to last, in 12 months, so the ROI formula would be:
Expected Beneﬁ ts = 100 custo mers/month x $1,000 LTV x 12 months =
$1,200,000
Cost of Investment = $50,000
ROI = ($1,200,000 - $50,000) / $50,000 = 23
According to these results the ROI that we get from the redesign project is
23 which implies that for each dollar invested in this project one may
expect $23 in additional revenue to be realized in the next 12 months. This
is a simp le exam ple of the use of the ROI analyze method to count the value
of design in the context of a software as a service.
To most accurately determine  the likelihood of a higher ROI, SaaS
companies need to use data-driven approaches that measure the balance
between the expected value (beneﬁts) and the resources spent through the
ROI form ula. However , why is the issue of design ROI so vital for the SaaS
companies? There are some points to consider:
1. Prioritization and resour ce allocation:  SaaS businesses often struggle
to cove r the cost of design resources, and thus have to set the most
economical investment in a competitive manner . The ROI analysis will
easily enable companies, to make the best choice of where  to allocate
resources thus economize.
2. Justiﬁ cation and buy-in: Design is still often just a 'nice- to-have' rather
than a cricial business function  in many SaaS organizations. It is often
necessary for design leaders to have a ﬁnancial perspective for the value of
design in order to justify investment and get the blessing from top
management and other stakeholders.
3. Ongoing reﬁne ment and enhanceme nt: SaaS companies are quite
active  in the market, where  rapid upgrades are  essential. Evaluating the
inﬂue nce of design additions and trials through ROI helps the se businesses
pinpoint are as for improving outcomes. Consequently , the y get more
chances to e nhance the user inte rface, a key success factor .
4. Our focus is on pleasing our custome rs: To wrap it up, the secret to
the  success of an SaaS company  lies in its devotion to hitting the ir business
goals. These goals could be  growing proﬁts, keeping clients, and broade -
ning their market stake. The  ROI research indicates that ﬁrms that inte -
ntionally tie design expe nses to their net income  often end up ﬁnding
harmony in all parts of their company .
Even though ROI measurement is of essential importance, it is not only the
only option for software startups, but also has issues and limitations. Ones
with All measures are intangible and not easy to quantify , for example,
customer satisfaction increases from design, brand loyalty , word-of-mouth
referrals, etc. Also, the input and assumptions used for the ROI calculations
can be complicated and subject to uncertainty , especially for the long-term
estimation of the design changes’  effects on the user behavior and revenue.
Focusing Solely on Aesthetic Design:
Fully focusing on the aesthetic design brings out a very sensitive issue. A
nice UI can certainly hook the user towards UX at ﬁrst sight, but it does not
at all ensure a positive UX and long-term success. In fact, aesthetics alone
become a real issue sometimes because other aspects attached to the design,
such as usability , accessibility , and functionality .
When only looks matte r in designing something, a business can cre ate a
beautiful produ ct. Yet, if it is hard to use  or has too many repetitive ste ps,
users may reject it.
Think about a SaaS software that is well designed in looks and unique . So
unique that its menu and icons are une xpectedly differe nt. This change was
interesting to obse rve. Howeve r, use rs struggled to spot the feature s they
neede d. They also could not learn how to use the m to achieve what they
wante d. This increases support queries, increases user churning , and ﬁnally
lowers ROI.
In some cases, companies might place more emphasis on visual design and
pay too little attention to the functionality aspect that needs to be developed
and polished within a given budget and time frame. As a result, the product
may end up as being attractive but without the necessary strong
functionality the user actually needs.
Yahoo! Homepage Redesign
Back in 2012, Yahoo! was a big name on the internet. Lots of people used it
every day to check their email, read news, and search for things online. But
Yahoo! thought their website was starting to look old-fashioned. They
wanted to make  it look cool and work better , especially for younger people
who were used to other websites like Facebook.
So, Yahoo! decided to give their homepage a big makeover . They changed
almost everything about how it looked and worked:
1. They made the page look less cluttered by removing some stuf f.
2. They added bigger pictures and videos to make it look more
exciting.
3. They created a new way to show news that was supposed to be
more personal for each user .
4. They changed how people could search for things on Yahoo!.
5. They also tried to make it easier for people to share things on
social media.
At ﬁrst, Yahoo!  thought people would love these changes. But that is not
what happened. Instead, many people who had been using Yahoo! for years
got confused. They could not ﬁnd things where they used to be. The new
design was so dif ferent that it felt like using a completely new website.
This confusion led to a big problem for Yahoo!. People started using it less:
●         About 19 millio n fewer people visited the Yahoo! homepage each
month.
●         About 14 million fewer people used Yahoo! Mail.
●         About 26 million fewer people used Yahoo! to search for things
online.
These numbers might sound big, and they are! It means that for every 100
people who used to use Yahoo!, about 20 to 30 of them stopped.
This was really bad for Yahoo! as a business. You see, Yahoo! makes most
of its money by showing ads to people who use their website. When fewer
people use the website, fewer people see the ads, and Yahoo! makes less
money . Some experts who study these things said Yahoo! might lose
between $500 million and $1.5 billion because of this mistake. That is more
money than most of us can even imagine!
There were a few good things that happened. More people started using
Flickr , which is like Instagram  but owned by Yahoo!. Also, more people
were using Yahoo! on their phones. But these good things were not enough
to make up for all the people who stopped using the main Yahoo! website.
Yahoo! tried to ﬁx things after they saw how many people were unhappy .
They made some more changes to make the website a bit more like it used
to be. This helped a little, but many people had already found other
websites to use instead.
The big lesson from this story is that when you have something that lots of
people use every day, you need  to be very careful about changing it. It is
usually better to make small changes over time, rather than changing
everything all at once. It is also really important to ask your users what they
think before making big changes.
A single focus on design's outward appearance can skew and bias user
responses and needs. Interestingly , this may also cause businesses to
undervalue the importance of the user experience lying underneath. An
attractive user interface may support user adoption, but these users might be
few. Others may realize soon these new visuals as a superﬁcial  ﬁx, leading
them to ignore it and walk away . Importantly , if the visual appe al is created
without considering the design's usability and the functionality being
delivered, there is a risk of producing an aesthetic product that does not
meet people's real needs. As a result, customers may feel let down and
become unfaithful.
This may create  a false feeling of satisfaction, which will lead companies to
invest more in just UI improvements rather than solving real usability or
functionality problems. Eventu ally, the product may have a very nice
appearance, yet it is unable to be the best solution to the real users' need that
continues to plummet happines s. On the other hand, this will prevent it
from fulﬁlling its purpose, thus, reducing users' trust and satisfaction.
In this way, it is necessary for companies to use a data-driven strategy to
analyze ROI to make sure they come up with the right design decisions that
are not only input but also business-focused. For that matter , the clear
metrics and KPIs shall be put in place relative to the improvement of each
of the requested results in design will strengthen the design basis.
A case in point would be user engagement metrics, task comp letion rates,
support tickets, and the user retention rate that display whether design
changes really brought about a difference. Continuous monitoring of data
and comparing it with the standards set beforehand will help the companies
decide where to put their design resources for the highest return on
investment.
Self-Evaluation
★      Am I balancing aesthetic design with usability and functionality in
my product?
★      Have I conducted user testing to ensure that our aesthetic choices do
not hinder usability?
★      Are we prioritizing solving real user problems over simply making
the product look attractive?
★      Do I have metrics in place to measure the impact of design changes
on user engagement and retention?
★      Am I considering accessibility in our design decisions?
★      Have we identiﬁed the core functionalities that users need, and are
these prioritized in the design?
★      Are we making gradual design changes rather than dramatic
overhauls that might confuse users?
★      Do we have a process for gathering user feedback on both aesthetics
and usability?
★      Am I tracking task completion rates to ensure our design supports
efﬁcient user workﬂows?
★      Have we considered how our design choices might impact dif ferent
user segments dif ferently?
★      Are we using data-driven decision making for design changes, or
relying solely on aesthetic preferences?
★      Do I have a clear understanding of our users' mental models and how
our design aligns with these?
★      Are we monitoring support tickets to identify potential usability
issues stemming from design choices?
★      Have we set clear KPIs for design improvements that go beyond just
visual appeal?
★      Am I considering the learning curve for users when implementing
new design elements?
★      Do we have a process for testing design changes with a subset of
users before full implementation?
★      Are we balancing the needs of new users with those of existing,
familiar users in our design decisions?
★      Have we considered how our design choices might impact the
product's performance (e.g., load times)?
★      Are we ensuring that our branding and aesthetic choices align with
our product's core functionality?
★      Do we have a clear rationale for each design decision that goes
beyond "it looks good"?
★      Are we considering how our design might need to evolve as our
product scales or adds new features?
★      Have we analyzed competitor designs not just for aesthetics, but for
usability and functionality?
★      Are we ensuring that our design supports the primary user ﬂows and
does not distract from them?
★      Do we have a process for reconciling conﬂicting feedback on
aesthetics vs. usability?
★      Are we considering the impact of our design choices on dif ferent
devices and screen sizes?
★      Have we implemented analytics to track how users interact with
different design elements?
★      Do we have a style guide that ensures consistency not just in
aesthetics, but in functionality across the product?
★      Are we considering the emotional impact of our design choices
beyond just visual appeal?
★      Have we established a process for regular design audits that consider
both aesthetics and usability?
Under estimating Onboarding and Training Costs:
One of the most common mistakes that SaaS companies make  when they
are calculating the costs of the onboarding and training process is that they
forget to include the ﬁnancial requirements for these programs in their book
of accou nts, which consequentl y skews their ROI analysis and may have
undesirable consequences for their business.
User-onboarding, or user training by other name, is the way new users are
introduced to a particular technology and getting them accustomed to its
features and advantages. The main purpose for the efﬁcient operation of
user onboarding is that it is the ﬁrst impression of the product.
User training is a continuous and subtle process. It is the supply of ongoing
training and support to guide users and let them know  about the
functionality and features of the product. Why would users want to keep
training? Software-as-a-service is subject to changes over time, with new
features being introduced, and a product owner may want to guide users
towards best practices by letting them know how to use the product
effectively , thus decreasing pressure on the customer support team.
If the expenses that these implic ations arise are not estimated properly , then
the result of the decision regarding the design investments would be over-
optimist. The company woul d make decisions based on the lower
operational costs but omit the facts that the onboarding and training are the
biggest hidden costs which are causing the failure of the objectives.
Self-Evaluation
★      Have I fully accounted for all onboarding and training costs in our
ﬁnancial projections?
★      Am I considering onboarding as a crucial ﬁrst impression of our
product?
★      Do we have a comprehensive strategy for continuous user training
beyond initial onboarding?
★      Have I factored in the costs of updating training materials as our
product evolves?
★      Am I allocating sufﬁcient resources for creating and maintaining user
documentation?
★      Have we considered the cost of personalized onboarding for dif ferent
user segments?
★      Am I accounting for the time and resources needed to develop
interactive tutorials or walkthroughs?
★      Have we factored in the cost of creating video tutorials or webinars
for user training?
★      Am I considering the potential impact of poor onboarding on
customer support costs?
★      Have we budgeted for tools or platforms speciﬁcally for user
onboarding and training?
★      Am I accounting for the cost of gathering and analyzing user
feedback on our onboarding process?
★      Have we considered the cost of A/B testing dif ferent onboarding
approaches?
★      Am I factoring in the potential revenue impact of a well-executed vs.
poorly executed onboarding process?
★      Have we considered the cost of providing multilingual onboarding
and training materials?
★      Am I accounting for the cost of training our own team members who
will be involved in user onboarding?
★      Have we considered the cost of integrating onboarding analytics
tools?
★      Am I factoring in the cost of personalizing the onboarding experience
based on user data?
★      Have we considered the cost of creating and maintaining a knowledge
base or help center?
★      Am I accounting for the potential cost of increased churn if
onboarding is not ef fective?
★      Have we considered the cost of providing live chat support during the
onboarding process?
★      Am I factoring in the cost of creating and updating F AQ sections?
★      Have we considered the cost of gamiﬁcation elements in our
onboarding process?
★      Am I accounting for the cost of user research to continuously improve
our onboarding process?
★      Have we considered the cost of creating onboarding email sequences?
★      Am I factoring in the cost of in-app guidance tools or features?
★      Have we considered the cost of providing phone support during
onboarding for certain user tiers?
★      Am I accounting for the cost of creating and maintaining a user
community or forum for peer support?
★      Have we considered the cost of conducting regular onboarding audits
and improvements?
★      Am I factoring in the cost of creating contextual help within the
product?
★      Have we considered the potential long-term ROI of investing in a
robust onboarding and training process?
Failur e to Account for  Long-T erm User  Retention:
Many businesse s often ignore design tasks and prioritize  short-term
victories. This could mean maki ng clie nts purchase a product or sign up for
a service. The  importance of these ste ps is clearly acknowledged. Howe ver,
the disre gard for use r loyalty in the long term could lead to inaccurate -
calculation of returns on investment, le ss success for the product.
User retention is the most important driving factor for a successful SaaS
product to keep its users engaged and maintain their activeness  over a long
period of time. Long-term success and proﬁtability are dependen t largely on
user retention in the subscription-based SaaS business. Here is why:
1. Recurring Revenue: The blood of SaaS businesses is recurring
revenue. On the contrary , traditional software which got revenue
from one-time purchases, SaaS ﬁrms depend only on periodic
subscription fees to remain in the business and grow it. The more
the user is subscribed the more amount of revenue they bring in
during their lifetime.
2. Lower  Acquisit ion Costs: It is very expen sive to attract new
users. SaaS companies have to spend large amounts of money on
marketing, advertising, and sales to attract new customers. At the
same time bear in mind that the retention of an existing user is
less expensive than attracting a new customer . In fact, some
studies claim that it costs up to ﬁve times more to obtain a new
customer than to retain the current one.
3. Increased Customer  Lifetime Value: The customer lifetime
value (CLV) is the main indicator of a SaaS busines s,
demonstrating the total sum that a customer is forecasted to bring
in for the company throughout his/her relationship there. By
acquiring new customers SaaS companies get more proﬁt from
those users and thus, the customer lifetime value grows.
Augmenting user retention, even by a hardly tangible percentage,
has a signiﬁcant inﬂuence on CL V and total revenue increasing.
4. Positive Word-of-Mouth:  Once your product is in the hands of
happy custome rs, it is most likely that they will be your
advocates and recommend it to their colleagues, friends, and
family . Elevation of this social phenomenon by word-of-mou th
will signiﬁcantl y stimulate the interest of more new users to
enroll in the application of the former product line, thereb y
obtaining organic growth and low acquisition costs will be
another consequence of this referral network.
If a company focuses solely on metrics like conversion rates or initial sign-
ups, it is easy to for them to overestimate the value of certain design
decisions, and underestimate the importance of the factors that contribute to
long-term retention. For examp le, imagine a SaaS company that redesigns
its onboarding ﬂow to optimize for quick sign-ups. The new design may
indeed boost initial conversion rates, but if it fails to properly educate and
engage users, those users may churn quickly , leading to a high turnover rate
and low lifetime value.
In this case, although there might be initial beneﬁts such as increased
conversion rates, it is quite likely that such positive outcomes would not
lead to the long-term success of the company . Also, the return on
investment in the redesign may be much smaller than was ﬁrst thought.
By paying less attention to the long-term retention of users who are in the
early stages of user growth who cannot be retained for long periods without
successful intervention is a strong contributor to high churn rates and proﬁts
sustainability . Churn, or the percentage of users who cancel their
subscription or stop using the product, is a crucial measurement for SaaS
businesses. Even the smallest increases in this sensitive metric have the
power to demolish the chances of companies to make more money or to be
proﬁtable.
Suppose a SaaS company havin g 10,000 users and charging a $50 monthly
subscription fee. In the case of a monthly churn rate of 5% at the company ,
they will experi ence a loss of 500 users per month, which is a monthly
revenue loss of $25,000. A 300,000-dollar vanish into thin air is the span of
12 months.
If a company makes a product that is intended to make it easy for people to
sign up or be initially attracted right away without taking long-term care or
offering help, it produces anno yance and dissatisfaction in the users with
time. This could result in bad feedback, poor word-of-mouth, and the
company's reputations to be damaged. In an era where people use social
media and the internet to refer to the numerous traditional people that a
disappointed few can really make it difﬁcult for a company to bring and
restrain customers.
Users who are satisﬁed in the long term are much more likely to purchase
upgrades, additional products or services, and the provision of valuable
feedback and insights that are instrumental in shaping the future  direction of
the product.
Self-Evaluation
★      Am I prioritizing long-term user retention in our product design and
strategy?
★      Have I calculated the lifetime value (L TV) of our users and factored
this into our ROI calculations?
★      Do we have metrics in place to track user retention over time?
★      Am I considering the impact of design decisions on both short-term
conversion and long-term retention?
★      Have we implemented strategies to reduce churn rates?
★      Am I balancing the focus between acquiring new users and retaining
existing ones?
★      Do we have a system in place to gather and act on feedback from
long-term users?
★      Have I considered how our onboarding process impacts long-term
retention?
★      Are we providing ongoing value to users beyond the initial sign-up
period?
★      Do we have a strategy for re-engaging users who show signs of
decreased activity?
★      Am I factoring in the cost savings of retaining users versus acquiring
new ones?
★      Have we implemented features or programs that encourage long-term
engagement?
★      Do we have a system for identifying and addressing common reasons
for user churn?
★      Am I considering how product updates and new features impact long-
term user satisfaction?
★      Have we developed a strategy for encouraging and leveraging
positive word-of-mouth from satisﬁed users?
★      Are we providing adequate ongoing support and education to help
users get long-term value from our product?
★      Do we have a system for tracking and analyzing user behavior over
time to predict potential churn?
★      Have I considered implementing a loyalty or rewards program for
long-term users?
★      Are we ef fectively communicating the ongoing value of our product
to existing users?
★      Do we have a strategy for upselling or cross-selling to existing users
in a way that provides additional value?
★      Have I considered the impact of pricing strategies on long-term
retention?
★      Are we regularly auditing and optimizing our user journey to ensure it
supports long-term engagement?
★      Do we have a process for gathering and acting on cancellation
feedback?
★      Are we ef fectively leveraging data analytics to identify patterns in
user retention and churn?
★      Do we have a strategy for turning long-term users into product
advocates?
★      Have I considered the role of community building in supporting long-
term user retention?
Misalignment with Business Goals and KPIs:
Not connecting design with the larger business scenario can result in the
design crew not achieving the business objectives and Performance KPIs.
This could be due to not having enough data, or perhaps getting it wrong. A
scenario like this could indeed have harmful impacts. These might lead to
incorrect ROI calculations, wasted resources, and missed growth
opportunities.
Designing and developing new features and functionalities can consume a
lot of market hours, cash, and resources. In situations where these tasks lack
coordination with business objectives and key performance  indicators,
resources may be wasted. Thes e resources could have been used on more
important projects.
For exam ple, imagine a SaaS company that invests heavily in designing and
developing a new social media integration feature. They do not care if it
aligns with the overall business goal or target market. If the feature does not
turn out to be as effective as expected in the increase of the user base, the
company is missing attracting prospective clients and new clients.
Teams building features that the company does not support might overlook
better opportunities that could boost the business. If the design team focuses
only on creating functions for current users and ignores the needs of
potential new tar gets, they might miss out on future growth and revenue.
Through setting  speciﬁc goals and KPIs, every part of a business can align
its efforts with realizing awareness, marketing plans, and business
objectives. This means creating deﬁnite, measurable goals for important
numbers. Thing s like how many  users we bring in, how engaged they are,
how many stick  around, and how much we are growing in proﬁts. These
should be shared by top personnel. Finally , make sure everyone in the team
and all stakeholders understand these goals.
The design department should partner together with product managers to
recognize the market they are targeting, the user needs, and business
requirements for each feature or functionality . They should also team up
with data analysts and user researchers to elicit user behavior and
preferences, as well as validate design decisions by means of user testing
and feedback.
Suppose a design team is engag ed in a new onboarding ﬂow project for a
SaaS product, they must build a success criteria of their work. For instance,
the increased onboarding proce ss completion rate of users, the time that
users spend before encountering their ﬁrst Aha and the improvement of user
retention rates in the long run.
Through the establishment of clear success indicators and the monitoring of
the progress of the design teams with these indicators, it is ensured that
design team initiatives are aligned with the goals of the company and create
meaningful impact in business through their activities.

★      Have I clearly communicated our business goals and KPIs to the
design team?
★      Are our design decisions directly tied to speciﬁc business objectives?

business goals?

our tar get market needs?

metrics?

and data analysis teams?

these guiding our design decisions?

align with market needs?

initiatives impact business goals?

the highest potential business impact?

business value?

on business performance?

investments?

in our design decisions?

teams and business stakeholders?

decisions?

not meeting business objectives?

consider business implications?

design ef forts with business goals?

business impact?

design changes?

and revenue streams?

teams to discuss goals and metrics?
★      Are we balancing user needs with business requirements in our design
process?
★      Do we have a system for capturing and acting on user feedback in
relation to our business goals?
★      Am I encouraging the design team to propose ideas that could open
new business opportunities?
★      Have we established a process for post-launch analysis of design
changes against business metrics?
★      Are we considering how our design decisions impact dif ferent
segments of our user base and business?
★      Do I regularly share business performance data with the design team
to inform their decision-making?
How to do Design ROI analysis?
Design Return on Investment (ROI) analysis is a methodical approach to
evaluating the effectiveness of design investments by measuring the
tangible and intangible beneﬁ ts they deliver relative to the resources
invested. Here is a framework for conducting a Design ROI analysis:
Deﬁne Objectives and Metrics: Clearly articulate the objectives of the
design initiative, whether it is improving user experience, increasing
conversion rates , or reducing support costs. Identify relevant metrics that
align with the objectives, such as user satisfaction scores, conversion rates,
retention rates, revenue growth, or cost savings.
Establish Basel ine Metrics: Determine the current performance metrics
related to the design initiative before implementing any changes. This
provides a benchmark for measuring the impact of design interventions.
Collect baseline data on key performance indicators (KPIs) such as user
engagement, task completion rates, and customer satisfaction levels.
Quantify Design Investments: Calculate the resources invested in the
design initiative, including design team salaries, tooling costs, research
expenses, and development time. Assign monetary values to design-related
activities and resources to quantify the total investment in the design
initiative.
Measur e Design Impact: Monitor the performance metrics identiﬁed in
step 1 after implementing the design changes. Measure any changes in user
behavior , conversion rates, or other relevant KPIs. Use A/B testing, user
surveys, analyti cs tools, and other data sources to gather quantitative and
qualitative data on the impact of design interventions.
Calculate ROI:  Calcul ate the ROI of the design initiative using the
following formula:
ROI = (Expected Beneﬁts - Cost of Investment) / Cost of Investment
Determine the total beneﬁts derived from the design improvements, such as
increased reven ue, cost savings, or productivity gains. Subtract the total
costs invested in the design initiative from the total beneﬁts to calculate the
net beneﬁt. Divide the net beneﬁ t by the total costs and express the result as
a percentage to determine the ROI.
Consider  Intangible Beneﬁts: In addition to quantiﬁable beneﬁts, consider
the intangible beneﬁts of design improvements, such as improved brand
perception, increased customer loyalty , and enhanced user satisfaction.
While intangibl e beneﬁts may be more challenging to measure, they
contribute to the overall value proposition of design investments and should
be considered in the ROI analysis.
Communicate Findings: Present the ﬁndings of the Design ROI analysis
to key stakeholders, including executives, product managers, and design
team members. Clearly commu nicate the impact of design investments on
business outcomes and demonstrate the value of design in achieving
strategic objectives. Use data visualization techniques and storytelling to
effectively communicate complex ﬁndings and insights from the ROI
analysis.
Segment Analysis by Design Initiatives:  Break down the ROI analysis by
individual design initiatives or features to identify which areas yield the
highest return on investment. This allows for targeted optimization efforts
and resource allocation based  on the performance of speciﬁc design
interventions.
Account for Design Lifecycle Costs: Consider the full lifecycle costs of
design initiative s, including ongoing maintenance, updates, and support.
Factor in the long-term costs  of sustaining design improvements to
accurately assess their overall ROI.
Benchmark Against Industry Standards:  Compare the ROI of design
investments against industry benchmarks and best practices to gauge
performance relative to peers. Benchmarking provides context for
interpreting ROI metrics and identifying areas for improvement.
Factor  in Risk and Uncertainty: Acknowledge the inherent risk and
uncertainty associated with design investments, particularly for innovative
or experimental initiatives. Use sensitivity analysis and scenario  planning to
assess the potential impact of uncertainty on ROI projections.
Account for Opportunity Costs: Consider the opportunity costs of
investing resources in design initiatives compared to alternative uses of
those resources. Evaluate the potential ROI of different investment options
to inform resource allocation decisions.
Account for External Factors:   Recognize the inﬂuence of external factors
such as market trends, competitive landscape, regulatory changes, and
economic conditions on the ROI of design investments. Adjust ROI
projections accordingly to account for external uncertainties and dynamics.
Engage Cross-F unctional Teams: Collaborate with cross-functiona l teams
beyond the design department, including product management, engineering,
marketing, and customer suppo rt, to ensure alignment of objectives and
perspectives in the ROI analysis process.
Incorporate Qualitative Insig hts: Supplement quantitative ROI metrics
with qualitative insights from user feedback, usability testing, and customer
interviews. Qualitative data provides nuanced insights into user perceptions
and preferences that may not be captured by quantitative metrics alone.
Consider  Long-T erm Strategic Impact: Evaluate the long-term strategic
impact of design investments on brand equity , customer loyalty , market
positioning, and innovation capabilities. Assess how design initiatives
contribute to overarching business goals and strategic objectives.
Iterate on Measur ement Framework: Continuously reﬁne the
measurement framework for Design ROI analysis based on evolving
business needs, changing market dynamics, and advancements in design
methodologies. Adapt metrics and methodologies to ensure relevance and
accuracy over time.
The Design ROI analysis framework provides a systematic approach to
evaluating the impact of design investments on business outcomes. By
following this comprehensive framework, or ganizations can:
●          Deﬁne clear objectives and metrics aligned with strategic goals.
●          Establish baseli ne metrics to measure the current state of
performance.
●          Quantify design  investments and measure the impact of design
interventions.
●          Calculate ROI using a formula that considers both costs and beneﬁts.
●          Consider both tangible and intangible beneﬁts of design
improvements.
●          Segment analys is by design initiatives to identify areas for
optimization.
●          Account for design lifecycle costs, risk, and uncertainty .
●          Benchmark against industry standards and best practices.
●          Factor in opportunity costs and external factors inﬂuencing ROI.
●          Engage cross-functional teams and incorporate qualitative insights.
●          Consider the long-term strategic impact of design investments.
●          Iterate on the measurement framework and invest in data
infrastructure for accurate analysis.
ROI Calculation Example:
Let us consider a hypothetical scenario where implementing UX
improvements leads to a 10% increase in conversion rates for a SaaS
product with 10,000 monthly users and an average monthly subscription fee
of $50.
●          Current Monthly Revenue: 10,000 users * $50 = $500,000
●          10% Increase in Conversion Rate: $500,000 * 0.10 = $50,000
●          Estimated Monthly Revenue After UX Improvements: $500,00 0 +
$50,000 = $550,000
●          Additional Monthly Revenue Due to UX Improvements: $50,000
Based on this calculation, the SaaS product could expect an additional
$50,000 in monthly revenue as a result of implementing UX improvements.
Over a year, this could result in a signiﬁcant return on investm ent, making
the upfront investment in UX improvements worthwhile.
Let us imagine you have a popular SaaS product with 10,000 monthly
subscribers. And each of those users pays an average of $50 per month for
your service. Doing some simple math, that equals $500,000 in monthly
revenue coming in. Not too shabby!
But what if I told you there is a way to increase that revenue by 10%
every month?
Just by making some smart improvements to your product's user experience
(UX).
Here is how those numbers could look:
Your current $500,000 monthly revenue is the baseline. A 10% boost thanks
to better  UX means an extra $50,000 per month (because $500,000 x 0.10 =
$50,000).
So, if we add that $50,000 to your original $500,000, you are suddenly
looking at $550,000 in monthly revenue. Just by making some UX tweaks!
it is kind of like hiring an amaz ing new marketer who brings in 10% more
customers each month, except you do not even have to add headcount. Just
make your product easier and more enjoyable to use.
Now let us look at the bigger picture. Over a whole year, that is an
additional $600, 000 in revenue ($50,000 extra per month x 12 months). A
cool $600K boost is nothing to sneeze at!
And remember , these numbers are based on just a 10% lift. Who knows,
maybe improving UX could unlock an even bigger increase in conversions
and sales? The possibilities are exciting.
Of course, making those UX enhancements does require some work and
investment upfront. But if the potential payof f is anywhere close to this
example, that upfront cost could easily pay for itself in just a few months
after launching the improvements.
it is like renovat ing your kitchen. Sure, it costs money upfront. But having
an amazing new kitchen also increases your home's value and makes
cooking/living better everyday . A smart investment!
The same goes for UX improv ements - spend a little now for big returns
down the line. When the numbe rs point to such a positive ROI, the case for
great UX is pretty clear .
Those awesome  results and extra money will only happen if the UX
changes are actually good and helpful for your users. If the changes make
things worse and more confusing, you could end up losing customers and
making less money instead!
That is why I wrote this whole book - to teach you the right way to improve
your product's UX so you get the positive results you want.
So, follo w the UX best practic es in this book. Do the research, get user
feedback, and optimize their experience. If you do it right, you will be well
on your way to boosting conversions and fattening up those monthly
revenues!
